**Adapted Text:**

*Embroidered Traditions from Morocco to Afghanistan* is the first exhibition at the Art Institute of Chicago to focus entirely on the artistic and cultural traditions of the SWANA region—an acronym for Southwest Asia and North Africa. The show highlights the area’s distinctive beauty and iconic visual styles in a striking way. Drawing mainly from the museum’s own extensive collection of textiles, jewelry, and personal adornment, the exhibition features more than 70 works created between the 1700s and 1900s. Most of these pieces have never been shown to the public before. The display also includes four rare face veils on loan from a private collector based in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

CuratorialText: Among the many other highlights are elaborately decorated garments from Syria, Yemen, Niger, and Egypt; silver and coral “treasure necklaces” from Morocco’s Anti-Atlas Mountains; richly embroidered linen panels from the Art Institute’s exceptional collection of Ottoman Turkish textiles; and a wedding blanket embroidered by the Marsh Arab (Ahwari) peoples of Iraq.
persona2_adapted: Among the many other highlights are elaborately decorated garments from Syria, Yemen, Niger, and Egypt; silver and coral “treasure necklaces” from Morocco’s Anti-Atlas Mountains; richly embroidered linen panels from the Art Institute’s exceptional collection of Ottoman Turkish textiles (that is, textiles from the period of the Ottoman Empire, which ruled much of the Middle East and North Africa from the 1300s to the early 1900s); and a wedding blanket embroidered by the Marsh Arab (Ahwari) peoples of Iraq.

CuratorialText: Barbara Hammer’s experimental film Sanctus depicts human bodies at risk. It features archival moving X-rays taken by doctor and filmmaker James Sibley Watson Jr. (1894–1982) of people making simple gestures and engaging in everyday activities: applying lipstick, drinking liquids, shaking hands, and shaving. Hammer rephotographed the original film and spliced the images together in a visceral montage set to overwhelming requiems by Neil B. Rolnick. She further transformed the X-rays with flashes of light, hand-painted colors, and other visual effects and by speeding up, looping, and reversing the movements. In presenting images captured by medical technology that uses large amounts of radiation, Hammer underscored her idea that “science advances through the demise of animal, including human, subjects in experiments” and that imaging, and even treatment, can cause harm.
persona2_adapted: Barbara Hammer’s experimental film *Sanctus* shows human bodies in vulnerable situations. The film uses archival moving X-rays taken by doctor and filmmaker James Sibley Watson Jr. (1894–1982), which capture people performing simple gestures and everyday actions: applying lipstick, drinking, shaking hands, and shaving. Hammer re-photographed the original footage and edited the images together in a powerful sequence set to overwhelming musical compositions (requiems) by Neil B. Rolnick. She further altered the X-rays with flashes of light, hand-painted colors, and other visual effects, as well as by speeding up, looping, and reversing the movements. By presenting images created through medical technology that relies on large amounts of radiation, Hammer emphasized her idea that “science advances through the demise of animal, including human, subjects in experiments” and that imaging—and even medical treatment—can cause harm.

CuratorialText: Sanctus emerged from Hammer’s initial engagement with medical themes, an interest incited in the early 1980s by the AIDS epidemic. Over the course of her 50-year career, Hammer worked in photography, 16mm film, and video with an unflinching focus on queer communities and on her encounters within the American medical system.
persona2_adapted: Sanctus grew out of Hammer’s early interest in medical subjects, sparked in the 1980s by the AIDS crisis. Throughout her 50-year career, Hammer worked in photography, 16mm film, and video, consistently focusing on queer communities and her own experiences with the American healthcare system.

CuratorialText: Step back in time and immerse yourself in the spiritual, domestic, and chivalric worlds of the Middle Ages and Renaissance. The Deering Family Galleries of Medieval and Renaissance Art, Arms, and Armor present nearly 700 objects from the museum’s rich holdings of art from 1200 to 1600: monumental altarpieces, exquisite jewelry, and the beloved arms and armor collection.
persona2_adapted: Step back in time and explore the spiritual, everyday, and knightly worlds of the Middle Ages and Renaissance. The Deering Family Galleries of Medieval and Renaissance Art, Arms, and Armor display nearly 700 objects from the museum’s extensive collection of art created between 1200 and 1600: large-scale religious paintings made for church altars, finely crafted jewelry, and the popular collection of weapons and armor.

CuratorialText: The sumptuous display begins with a large room featuring architectural sculpture, treasury objects, and early painting that convey the austere sanctity of earlier medieval art. The centerpiece of the gallery is the meticulously conserved Ayala Altarpiece, commissioned in 1396 by the chancellor of Castile for his family’s funerary chapel in northern Spain. Spanning nearly 24 feet across and eight feet high, the painted wood altarpiece has undergone extensive treatment over the last three years and makes its restored debut in brilliant form. Two linked vaulted galleries showcase altarpieces and altarpiece fragments from Italy, Spain, and northern Europe, including the Art Institute favorite Saint George and the Dragon by Bernat Martorell. Each is displayed to suggest its original religious space, highlighting the church as a center of public life.
persona2_adapted: The exhibition opens with a grand room filled with architectural carvings, objects used in church treasuries, and early paintings that reflect the solemn, sacred quality of medieval art. At the heart of the gallery is the carefully restored Ayala Altarpiece—a large painted wooden panel commissioned in 1396 by the chancellor of Castile for his family’s burial chapel in northern Spain. Measuring nearly 24 feet wide and eight feet tall, this altarpiece has undergone extensive conservation work over the past three years and is now on view for the first time since its restoration, looking remarkably vivid. Two connected arched galleries display altarpieces and fragments from Italy, Spain, and northern Europe, including the Art Institute’s beloved painting *Saint George and the Dragon* by Bernat Martorell. Each piece is arranged to evoke the religious setting for which it was originally made, emphasizing the church as a central gathering place in community life.

CuratorialText: The spaces that follow are more intimate, focusing on the texture of late Gothic and Renaissance life. One gallery presents luxury goods and the accessories of feasting, while another displays works of art for the bedchambers of Tuscany’s merchant elite. Everyday objects from northern Europe, along with jewelry and items of personal display, complete the domestic picture of the period.
persona2_adapted: The rooms ahead feel more personal, offering a closer look at daily life during the late Gothic and Renaissance periods. One gallery features fine luxury items and the tools used for festive dining, while another shows artworks made for the bedrooms of Tuscany’s wealthy merchant class. Everyday objects from northern Europe, together with jewelry and accessories meant for personal adornment, round out the domestic scene of the era.

CuratorialText: An elegant rotunda introduces the collection of arms and armor with equipment for the bodyguards of four rulers. From here, the space opens to a gallery filled with weaponry and armor dominated by two armored figures on horseback—one dressed for battle, the other for sport—and two armed and costumed figures engaged in foot combat. Finally, a gallery lined with cabinetry features the art of the sword, luxury firearms, and equipment for the hunt extending through the Baroque era.
persona2_adapted: An elegant circular hall welcomes visitors to the arms and armor collection, featuring equipment used by the personal bodyguards of four rulers. From this starting point, the space opens into a gallery filled with weapons and protective gear, where two armored figures on horseback stand out—one outfitted for actual battle, the other for sporting events—alongside two armed and costumed figures engaged in combat on foot. The journey concludes in a gallery lined with display cabinets that showcase the craftsmanship of swords, high-end firearms, and hunting equipment, spanning the artistic period known as the Baroque era.

CuratorialText: The new installation expands the display of art of this fascinating period sixfold, premiering several recently conserved works and many significant new acquisitions—an elegant late Gothic breastplate and backplate, a rare 15th-century German portrait, and an outstanding group of 17th- and 18th-century swords. Subtle changes in the architecture of the galleries propel visitors through time and from religious to secular works, while digital labels provide an interactive multimedia experience. At the heart of this display are the gifts of major collectors whose donations serve as the collection’s foundation: the Ayala Altarpiece and Saint George and the Dragon from Charles Deering, Sienese and Florentine gold-ground paintings and early Northern works given by Martin Ryerson, a terracotta altarpiece by Florentine Benedetto Buglioni and a South German carved and painted Saint Catherine from Kate Buckingham, and George F. Harding’s impressive collection of arms and armor. All come together in the new Deering Family Galleries, offering a transporting experience to a time that continues to enchant.
persona2_adapted: The new installation expands the display of art from this fascinating period six times over, showcasing several recently preserved works and many important new additions—an elegant late Gothic chest and back plate for armor, a rare 15th-century German portrait, and an outstanding group of swords from the 1600s and 1700s. Subtle changes in the gallery architecture guide visitors through time, moving from religious to everyday objects, while digital labels offer an interactive multimedia experience. At the heart of this display are the gifts of major collectors whose donations form the collection’s foundation: the Ayala Altarpiece and Saint George and the Dragon from Charles Deering; paintings with gold backgrounds from Siena and Florence, along with early Northern European works given by Martin Ryerson; a clay altarpiece by Florentine artist Benedetto Buglioni and a carved and painted Saint Catherine from South Germany donated by Kate Buckingham; and George F. Harding’s impressive collection of weapons and armor. All come together in the new Deering Family Galleries, offering a transporting experience to a time that continues to captivate.

CuratorialText: Jill Mulleady paints by breathing new life into unseen histories. Drawing from art-historical, cinematic, and literary sources, her memorable images can haunt the viewer long after encountering them. This exhibition—Mulleady’s first solo show at a major museum—brings together approximately 20 paintings by the artist.
persona2_adapted: Jill Mulleady creates paintings by reviving overlooked stories from the past. She draws inspiration from art history, film, and literature, crafting images that can linger in the viewer’s mind long after they are seen. This exhibition—Mulleady’s first solo show at a major museum—brings together about 20 of her paintings.

CuratorialText: Mulleady uses various methods including staining, and at times incorporating materials such as copper, to achieve fragile, luminous surfaces that feel impossibly thin yet visually deep, holding tension between flatness and depth. Not limited to canvas or linen, she employs a range of supports, including glass and velvet. The resultant works balance different degrees of transparency and opacity and can be looked at and, sometimes, seen through.
persona2_adapted: Mulleady works with a range of techniques—such as staining her materials or adding elements like copper—to create surfaces that appear delicate and glowing. These surfaces feel almost impossibly thin, yet they also seem to have great visual depth, creating a tension between flatness and a sense of space. Rather than sticking to traditional materials like canvas or linen, she also uses glass and velvet as her base. The resulting pieces play with varying levels of transparency and opacity; some can be viewed from the front, while others can even be seen through.

CuratorialText: Mulleady’s paintings often depict scenes that are enigmatic and dangerous, showing figures at risk or under threat—from appetite, predation, or compulsion. While these ambiguous scenes may imply narratives, they likewise refuse tidy resolutions. Her imagery draws actively on painting lineages—allegory and the Baroque, Symbolist unease, the grotesque—recasting genres and canonical images for the present. In contrast to the purity and rationality of 20th-century modernism, Mulleady prioritizes a tradition in which chaos, instinct, and history drive art making.
persona2_adapted: Mulleady’s paintings often show scenes that feel mysterious and dangerous, with figures who appear to be at risk or under threat—whether from hunger, being hunted, or an uncontrollable urge. Although these open-ended scenes might hint at a story, they also refuse to offer neat or satisfying endings. Her imagery actively draws on older traditions in painting—such as using symbolic figures to convey deeper meanings (allegory), the dramatic lighting and emotion of the Baroque period, the unsettling mood of Symbolist art, and the exaggerated, distorted forms of the grotesque. In doing so, she reworks these classic styles and well-known images for today’s world. Unlike the clean, orderly, and logical approach of 20th-century modernism, Mulleady favors a tradition where chaos, instinct, and history are the driving forces behind making art.

CuratorialText: As Mulleady has said, she works with existing languages in order to invent a new one, and her staged tableaux are particularly resonant within the Art Institute galleries, as they rhyme with many works in the collection that have informed the artist.
persona2_adapted: As Mulleady has explained, she works with existing visual languages—the familiar styles, symbols, and conventions of art history—in order to invent a new one of her own. Her carefully composed, staged scenes feel especially meaningful within the Art Institute galleries, because they echo or mirror many works in the collection that have influenced the artist.

CuratorialText: After decades as a representational painter, in her seventies Alma Thomas turned to abstraction, creating shimmering, mosaic-like fields of color with rhythmic dabs of paint that were often inspired by forms from nature. The artist had been fascinated with space exploration since the late 1960s, and her later paintings often referenced America’s manned Apollo missions to the moon. Although she had never flown, Thomas began to paint as if she were in an airplane, capturing what she described as shifting patterns of light and streaks of color. “You look down on things,” she explained. “You streak through the clouds so fast… . You see only streaks of color.”
persona2_adapted: After spending many years painting recognizable subjects—people, places, and objects—Alma Thomas, in her seventies, began making abstract works. These pieces are built from small, rhythmic dabs of paint that create shimmering, mosaic-like fields of color, often inspired by shapes found in nature. Thomas had been fascinated by space exploration since the late 1960s, and her later paintings frequently reference America’s manned Apollo missions to the moon. Even though she had never flown, she started to paint as if she were looking down from an airplane, capturing what she described as shifting patterns of light and streaks of color. “You look down on things,” she explained. “You streak through the clouds so fast… . You see only streaks of color.”

CuratorialText: Starry Night and the Astronauts evokes the open expanse and celestial patterns of a night sky, but despite its narrative title, the work could also be read as an aerial view of a watery surface, playing with our sense of immersion within an otherwise flat picture plane. The viewer is immersed not only in the sense of organic expanse that this painting achieves, however, but also in an encounter with Thomas’s process: the surface here is clearly constructed stroke by stroke. Meanwhile, the glimpses of raw canvas between each primary-colored mark seem as vivid as the applied paint itself—almost as if the composition were backlit. Thomas relied on the enlivening properties of color throughout her late-blooming career. “Color is life,” she once proclaimed, “and light is the mother of color.” This painting was created in 1972, when the artist was eighty. In the same year, she became the first African American woman to receive a solo exhibition at a major art museum, the Whitney Museum of American Art in New York City.
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

*Starry Night and the Astronauts* suggests the vastness and patterned beauty of a night sky. Yet, despite its story-like title, the painting can also be seen as an overhead view of a watery surface. This double meaning plays with how we experience depth in what is otherwise a flat, two-dimensional image. The viewer is drawn not only into the sense of organic space the painting creates, but also into an awareness of how the artist, Alma Thomas, worked: the surface is clearly built up, one brushstroke at a time. The glimpses of raw canvas that appear between each bright, primary-colored mark feel just as vivid as the paint itself—almost as if the whole composition were lit from behind. Thomas relied on the life-giving power of color throughout her career, which blossomed later in life. “Color is life,” she once said, “and light is the mother of color.” This painting was made in 1972, when the artist was eighty years old. That same year, she became the first African American woman to have a solo exhibition at a major art museum, the Whitney Museum of American Art in New York City.

CuratorialText: Perhaps the most famous depiction of a bedroom in Western art history, this vibrant painting documents Vincent van Gogh’s sleeping quarters in his beloved “Yellow House” in Arles, France. The composition exists in three versions, the first of which Van Gogh conceived in October 1888, a month after he moved into the home. In a letter to his brother Theo, he described having painted “the walls pale lilac, the floor in a broken and faded red, the chairs and the bed chrome yellow, the pillows and the sheet very pale lemon green, the bedspread blood-red, the dressing table orange, the washbasin blue, the window green.” With its bold colors, thick and broken brushwork, and sharply receding lines, the picture might suggest a nervous energy. But the artist understood it as a calming and restful image.
persona2_adapted: Perhaps the most famous image of a bedroom in Western art, this vivid painting shows the room where Vincent van Gogh slept in his beloved “Yellow House” in Arles, France. The scene exists in three versions, the first of which van Gogh created in October 1888, a month after moving into the home. In a letter to his brother Theo, he described having painted “the walls pale lilac, the floor in a broken and faded red, the chairs and the bed chrome yellow, the pillows and the sheet very pale lemon green, the bedspread blood-red, the dressing table orange, the washbasin blue, the window green.” With its strong colors, thick and uneven brushstrokes, and sharply angled lines that seem to pull the room inward, the picture might suggest a restless energy. But the artist saw it as a calm and soothing image.

CuratorialText: The painting in the Art Institute’s collection is Van Gogh’s second version of the scene, made nearly a year after the first, in September 1889. He produced a third, smaller version at the same time as a gift for his mother and sister.
persona2_adapted: This painting in the Art Institute’s collection is Van Gogh’s second version of this scene, created nearly a year after the first, in September 1889. Around the same time, he made a third, smaller version as a gift for his mother and sister.

CuratorialText: This meditating Buddha comes from the coastal town of Nagapattinam in southern India, which was, as a result of settlers from Srivijaya (Indonesia), one of the few places where Buddhism was still flourishing in the twelfth century. The Buddha—with his elongated earlobes, the wheel marks on his palms, the urna between his brows, and the cranial protuberance covered with snail-shell curls—is seated in the posture of meditation, with his hands resting on his lap (dhyanamudra), wearing a seemingly diaphanous monastic garment. As in other images from Nagapattinam, a flame emerges out of the Buddha’s cranial protuberance, probably signifying wisdom. This monumental granite sculpture originally would have graced a monastic site at Nagapattinam, which is also well known for its Buddhist bronzes. The Tamil inscription covering its back is no longer legible.
persona2_adapted: This meditating Buddha comes from the coastal town of Nagapattinam in southern India. Because of settlers from Srivijaya (in what is now Indonesia), Nagapattinam was one of the few places where Buddhism was still thriving in the twelfth century. The Buddha is shown in the classic meditation pose, with his hands resting on his lap. He has several traditional features: elongated earlobes, wheel-shaped marks on his palms, a small dot (often called the urna) between his eyebrows, and a bump on his head covered with spiral curls that resemble snail shells. He wears a monastic robe that appears almost transparent. As in other sculptures from Nagapattinam, a flame rises from the bump on his head, likely representing wisdom. This large granite statue originally would have stood in a monastery complex in Nagapattinam, a site also famous for its Buddhist bronze works. A Tamil inscription on the back of the sculpture is now too worn to read.

CuratorialText: Many Mansions is the first in Kerry James Marshall’s series of five large-scale paintings depicting public housing projects in Chicago and Los Angeles such as Rockwell Gardens, Wentworth Gardens, or, as in Many Mansions, Stateway Gardens. Struck by the absurdity of the term “garden” to describe these failed solutions to low-income housing, Marshall was inspired to represent the profound contradictions of living in such an environment. Many Mansions is filled with ironic and startling juxtapositions of the real and artificial—from the unnaturally cheerful landscape to the three haunting, ebony-skinned figures dressed in nostalgic Sunday best. The title of the painting, visible on the red ribbon above, is a variation on Christ’s oft-quoted remark found in John [14:2]: “In my Father’s house there are many mansions.”
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

*Many Mansions* is the first painting in Kerry James Marshall’s series of five large-scale works that depict public housing projects in Chicago and Los Angeles—places like Rockwell Gardens, Wentworth Gardens, or, as in this painting, Stateway Gardens. Marshall was struck by the oddness of calling these failed attempts at low-income housing “gardens,” and this inspired him to explore the deep contradictions of living in such an environment. The painting is filled with ironic and startling contrasts between what is real and what is artificial: from the unnaturally cheerful landscape to the three haunting figures with deep black skin, dressed in nostalgic Sunday best. The title, visible on the red ribbon above, is a variation on a well-known saying of Christ from the Gospel of John (14:2): “In my Father’s house there are many mansions.”

CuratorialText: In Ferris Bueller’s Day Off, Ferris’s best friend Cameron Frye intensely studies this nearly ten-foot-wide painting during a scene set at the Art Institute. Featuring people of every age and social class on the banks of the River Seine, A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884 has captivated visitors ever since its arrival at the museum in 1924.
persona2_adapted: In *Ferris Bueller’s Day Off*, Ferris’s best friend, Cameron Frye, stares intently at this nearly ten-foot-wide painting during a scene set at the Art Institute. The work shows people of all ages and social classes gathered along the banks of the River Seine. Ever since it arrived at the museum in 1924, *A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884* has fascinated visitors with its detailed, almost pointillist style—a technique where tiny dots of color blend together to create the image.

CuratorialText: If we, like Cameron, come closer to the painting, figures and forms dissolve into dots and dashes of complementary colors laid side by side, characteristic of Seurat’s pointillist technique. Many smaller painted and drawn sketches and several larger canvases, in which Seurat laid out the parameters for the landscape and figures, led up to this majestically composed scene. Seurat returned to the work two years after its start date, amplifying the silhouettes of some figures and adding others. Some of these, like the monkey on the leash, seem so integral to the final composition that it is hard to imagine them as add-ons, but others, like the man carrying a rolled newspaper in the furthest distance, are barely noticeable. To the artist, however, every decision was essential to his aim of making a painting of modern life equivalent to a classical Greek frieze.
persona2_adapted: If we, like Cameron, step closer to the painting, the figures and shapes break down into small dots and dashes of opposite colors placed next to each other. This is a hallmark of Seurat’s technique, called pointillism, where he built images from tiny, separate touches of paint rather than blending them on the canvas. Before reaching this grand, carefully arranged scene, Seurat created many smaller painted and drawn studies, as well as several larger canvases, to work out the details of the landscape and the people in it. He returned to the painting two years after starting it, making some figures’ outlines stronger and adding new ones. Some of these additions, such as the monkey on a leash, now feel so essential to the final image that it’s hard to believe they weren’t there from the beginning. Others, like the man holding a rolled newspaper far in the background, are barely noticeable. Yet for Seurat, every choice mattered in his goal: to create a painting of modern life that carried the same weight and dignity as a classical Greek frieze—a long, sculpted panel that tells a story in a single, balanced composition.

CuratorialText: Laboring at a backstrap loom in this painting is Luz Jiménez, a master weaver and Nahua, one of the largest Indigenous groups in Mesoamerica. With expertise and dexterity, Jiménez threads weft through warp, slowly building her intricately patterned textile, the completed portion resting on her lap. Diego Rivera, a leading modernist painter who came to prominence in the years after the Mexican Revolution (1910–20), promoted a vision of Mexican national identity rooted in Indigenous and folk cultures, distinct from the legacies of Spanish colonialism. By centering Jiménez in Weaving, Rivera claimed her traditions as part of his own.
persona2_adapted: At work on a backstrap loom in this painting is Luz Jiménez, a master weaver and a member of the Nahua people—one of the largest Indigenous groups in Mesoamerica. With skill and steady hands, Jiménez threads the horizontal weft through the vertical warp, slowly building her richly patterned textile, the finished part resting on her lap. Diego Rivera, a leading modernist painter who rose to fame in the years after the Mexican Revolution (1910–20), promoted a vision of Mexican national identity rooted in Indigenous and folk traditions, separate from the legacy of Spanish colonial rule. By placing Jiménez at the center of *Weaving*, Rivera claimed her cultural traditions as part of his own.

CuratorialText: This monumental painting is Gustave Caillebotte’s largest, most ambitious work and has been a visitor favorite since the Art Institute acquired it in 1964, well before the artist’s contributions to the Impressionist movement were widely recognized. Caillebotte captured a busy intersection of Paris that was only a short walk from his home. In fact, his paintings of Paris rarely depict areas beyond his familiar neighborhoods.
persona2_adapted: This monumental painting is Gustave Caillebotte’s largest and most ambitious work. It has been a favorite among visitors since the Art Institute acquired it in 1964—long before the artist’s role in the Impressionist movement was widely appreciated. Caillebotte captured a busy Parisian intersection just a short walk from his home. In fact, his paintings of Paris rarely show areas beyond the neighborhoods he knew well.

CuratorialText: Caillebotte demonstrated his affinity with the Impressionists through his subject of modern urban life but approached these settings with a more Realist technique. The dramatic cropping of the scene may have been inspired by the newly popularized medium of photography; the two central figures seem to be walking straight into the viewer as something captures their attention outside of the frame. At the time, artists rarely attempted to depict people in motion; Caillebotte carefully placed each of the background figures to evoke the feeling of bustling city streets. When he started exhibiting with the Impressionists, Caillebotte also focused on portraying the atmosphere of his scenes; here, instead of explicitly painting raindrops to indicate the weather, he suggests it through the incorporation of newly available retractable umbrellas and the subtle reflections on the wet cobblestones.
persona2_adapted: Caillebotte shared the Impressionists' interest in depicting modern city life, but he painted these scenes with a more detailed, realistic style. The way the image is sharply cut off at the edges may have been influenced by photography, which was becoming popular at the time. The two people in the center seem to be walking directly toward us, as if something outside the picture has caught their attention. In this era, artists rarely tried to show figures in movement; Caillebotte carefully arranged each person in the background to create the sense of a busy, lively street. When he began showing his work alongside the Impressionists, he also focused on capturing the mood or feeling of a scene. Here, rather than painting raindrops directly to show it's raining, he suggests the weather through the use of newly available umbrellas that can be opened or closed, and through the subtle reflections on the wet cobblestones.

CuratorialText: Richard Hunt created Hero Construction in 1958, one year after graduating from the School of the Art Institute of Chicago. The sculpture is composed of found objects—old pipes, bits of metal, and automobile parts—that the artist discovered in junkyards and on the street. Using a torch like a paintbrush, Hunt welded these elements into a form that is abstract yet recognizable.
persona2_adapted: Richard Hunt created *Hero Construction* in 1958, just one year after finishing his studies at the School of the Art Institute of Chicago. The sculpture is made from found objects—old pipes, scraps of metal, and car parts—that the artist collected from junkyards and the street. Using a torch as if it were a paintbrush, Hunt fused these pieces together through welding, shaping them into a form that is abstract yet still recognizable.

CuratorialText: Hunt was fascinated with mythology, and Hero Construction can be related to other sculptures past and present, from ancient statues of Greek heroes to modern monuments of statesmen. The modest but monumental stance of the figure suggests a contemporary hero who maintains strength in the face of the uncertainties and dangers of the present age.
persona2_adapted: Hunt was deeply interested in mythology, and *Hero Construction* can be linked to other sculptures from different eras—ranging from ancient Greek statues of heroes to modern monuments of political leaders. The figure’s pose, though unassuming, carries a sense of grandeur, suggesting a modern-day hero who holds onto strength even when confronted with the uncertainties and dangers of our time.

CuratorialText: The Mexicas, or Aztec Empire, ruled central Mexico from 1428 until 1521. This monument uses glyphs to record the history of the Mexica universe up to 1503. On the back, 1 Rabbit represents the beginning of time. On the front, the four previous cosmic eras are inscribed in the corners, while the current cosmic era 4 Movement appears in the center. 1 Crocodile (above) and 11 Reed (in the square frame) indicate the date July 15, 1503, when the ninth emperor Moctezuma Xocoyotzin was coronated. Just as these glyphs recurred throughout the Mexica capital, Tenochtitlan, they can also be found in Chicago: 4 Movement appears in a mural at the 18th Street “L” stop and on manhole covers in Pilsen—a neighborhood that has long been home to Chicago’s vibrant Mexican community.
persona2_adapted: The Mexicas—also known as the Aztec Empire—ruled central Mexico from 1428 until 1521. This stone monument uses picture-symbols, called glyphs, to record the history of the Mexica universe up to the year 1503. On the back of the monument, the symbol "1 Rabbit" marks the beginning of time. On the front, the four previous cosmic eras are carved into the corners, while the current era—named "4 Movement"—appears in the center. The symbols "1 Crocodile" (above) and "11 Reed" (inside the square frame) together represent the date July 15, 1503, the day the ninth emperor, Moctezuma Xocoyotzin, was crowned. Just as these symbols once appeared throughout the Mexica capital, Tenochtitlan, they can also be found in Chicago today: "4 Movement" appears in a mural at the 18th Street “L” stop and on manhole covers in Pilsen—a neighborhood that has long been home to Chicago’s vibrant Mexican community.

CuratorialText: A palpable energy and sense of movement enliven Nightlife, Archibald Motley’s portrayal of a crowded cabaret in the South Side neighborhood of Bronzeville in Chicago. With stylized figures, an array of diagonal lines, and heightened colors keyed to shades of magenta and violet, the artist captured the exuberance of city dwellers out on the town. Motley created a network of gestures and glances among the people, drawing attention to the various social interactions that animate the scene. The composition is an exploration of artificial lighting. Motley was inspired, in part, to paint Nightlife after having seen Edward Hopper’s Nighthawks (1942.51), which had entered the Art Institute’s collection the prior year.
persona2_adapted: A lively energy and sense of motion bring *Nightlife* to life. In this painting, Archibald Motley shows a crowded cabaret in Bronzeville, a neighborhood on Chicago’s South Side. Using simplified figures, many diagonal lines, and bright colors focused on shades of magenta and violet, the artist captured the excitement of city residents enjoying a night out. Motley created a web of gestures and looks among the people, highlighting the different social interactions that make the scene feel active. The painting also explores the effects of artificial light. Motley was partly inspired to paint *Nightlife* after seeing Edward Hopper’s *Nighthawks* (1942.51), which had entered the Art Institute’s collection the year before.

CuratorialText: Although influenced by Abstract Expressionist artists in New York in the early 1950s, Joan Mitchell did not prioritize self-expression: her often exuberant abstractions were “about landscape, not about me,” she once explained. Mitchell painted large, light-filled canvases animated by loosely applied skeins of bright color—here infused with the energy of a large metropolis. The title suggests a relationship between the painting’s network of pigments and the nerves or arteries of an urban space. The sense of spontaneity conveyed in City Landscape, however, belies Mitchell’s methods. Unlike many of her contemporaries, who were dubbed “action painters,” Mitchell worked slowly and deliberately. “I paint a little,” she said. “Then I sit and look at the painting, sometimes for hours. Eventually, the painting tells me what to do.”
persona2_adapted: Although Joan Mitchell was influenced by artists in New York during the early 1950s who were part of the Abstract Expressionist movement, she did not focus on expressing her own emotions. Her lively, non-representational works were, as she once said, “about landscape, not about me.” Mitchell created large, bright paintings filled with loosely applied strands of vivid color—here, those colors seem charged with the energy of a big city. The title suggests that the painting’s network of pigments might resemble the nerves or arteries of an urban environment. However, the spontaneous feel of *City Landscape* hides the careful process behind it. Unlike many of her peers, who were called “action painters” for their fast, energetic techniques, Mitchell worked slowly and thoughtfully. “I paint a little,” she explained. “Then I sit and look at the painting, sometimes for hours. Eventually, the painting tells me what to do.”

CuratorialText: Around 1920 Georgia O’Keeffe painted a number of oils exploring, as she later recalled, “the idea that music could be translated into something for the eye.” In Blue and Green Music, O’Keeffe’s colors and forms simultaneously suggest the natural world and evoke the experience of sound. She was drawn to the theories of the Russian Expressionist painter Vasily Kandinsky, who, in his 1912 text Concerning the Spiritual in Art, argued that visual artists should emulate music in order to achieve pure expression free of literary references.
persona2_adapted: Around 1920, Georgia O’Keeffe created a series of oil paintings based on what she later described as “the idea that music could be translated into something for the eye.” In *Blue and Green Music*, her use of color and shape both hints at the natural world and brings to mind the sensation of hearing sound. O’Keeffe was interested in the ideas of the Russian painter Vasily Kandinsky, who was part of the Expressionist movement—a style that emphasizes emotional experience over realistic depiction. In his 1912 book *Concerning the Spiritual in Art*, Kandinsky argued that visual artists should take inspiration from music to create pure expression, free from references to stories or everyday objects.

CuratorialText: Henri Matisse considered Bathers by a River to be one of the five most “pivotal” works of his career, and with good reason: it facilitated the evolution of the artist’s style over the course of nearly a decade. Originally, the work was related to a 1909 commission by the Russian collector Sergei Shchukin, who wanted two large canvases to decorate the staircase of his Moscow home. Matisse proposed three pastoral images, though Shchukin decided to purchase only two works, Dance II and Music (both State Hermitage Museum, Saint Petersburg).
persona2_adapted: Henri Matisse considered *Bathers by a River* to be one of the five most “pivotal” works of his career, and for good reason: it marked a major turning point in the artist’s style, which evolved over nearly a decade. The painting began as part of a commission from a Russian collector named Sergei Shchukin in 1909. Shchukin wanted two large canvases to decorate the staircase of his home in Moscow. Matisse originally proposed three peaceful, countryside scenes, but Shchukin decided to buy only two of them: *Dance II* and *Music* (both now housed at the State Hermitage Museum in Saint Petersburg).

CuratorialText: Four years later, Matisse returned to this canvas, the rejected third image, altering the idyllic scene and changing the pastel palette to reflect his new interest in Cubism. He reordered the composition, making the figures more columnar, with faceless, ovoid heads. Over the next years, Matisse transformed the background into four vertical bands and turned the formerly blue river into a thick black vertical band. With its restricted palette and severely abstracted forms, Bathers by a River is far removed from Dance II and Music, which convey a graceful lyricism. The sobriety and hint of danger in Bathers by a River may in part reflect the artist’s concerns during the terrible, war-torn period during which he completed it.
persona2_adapted: Four years later, Matisse returned to this canvas—the one that had been rejected as the third image—and reworked the peaceful scene. He changed the soft, pastel colors to reflect his growing interest in Cubism, a style that breaks objects into geometric shapes. He reorganized the composition, making the figures more like columns, with faces simplified into featureless, oval shapes. Over the next few years, Matisse transformed the background into four vertical strips and turned what was once a blue river into a thick, black vertical band. With its limited color range and highly simplified forms, *Bathers by a River* feels very different from *Dance II* and *Music*, which have a flowing, graceful beauty. The seriousness and subtle sense of threat in *Bathers by a River* may partly reflect the artist’s anxieties during the terrible, war-torn years when he finished it.

CuratorialText: One of four posts sculpted for the palace of the king (ogoga) of Ikere, this evocative image of the Yoruba concept of a divine ruler is a masterwork of Olowe of Ise. The renowned artist’s dynamic style can be distinguished by the interrelationships between figures, their exaggerated proportions, and the use of open space. The sculptor’s diminutive portrayal of the king suggests his dependence on others, and his crown has been emphasized to evoke the spiritual power residing within it. The towering image of the king’s senior wife standing behind him underscores her superior status as a guardian of her husband’s interests, endowed with keen political understanding and supernatural knowledge. She would have placed the crown on the king’s head during his enthronement ceremony. The smaller figures at the king’s feet represent one of his junior wives and the flute-playing trickster god Eshu.
persona2_adapted: One of four carved posts originally made for the palace of the king (ogoga) of Ikere, this striking image of the Yoruba idea of a divine ruler is a masterwork by Olowe of Ise. The famous artist’s energetic style can be seen in how the figures relate to one another, their exaggerated sizes, and the use of empty space around them. The sculptor’s small-scale portrayal of the king suggests his reliance on others, while his crown has been made prominent to hint at the spiritual power it holds. The towering figure of the king’s senior wife standing behind him highlights her higher status as a protector of her husband’s interests, someone with sharp political insight and spiritual knowledge. She would have placed the crown on the king’s head during his coronation ceremony. The smaller figures at the king’s feet represent one of his junior wives and the flute-playing trickster god Eshu.

CuratorialText: About Nighthawks Edward Hopper recollected, “unconsciously, probably, I was painting the loneliness of a large city.” In an all-night diner, three customers sit at the counter opposite a server, each appear to be lost in thought and disengaged from one another. The composition is tightly organized and spare in details: there is no entrance to the establishment, no debris on the streets. Through harmonious geometric forms and the glow of the diner’s electric lighting, Hopper created a serene, beautiful, yet enigmatic scene. Although inspired by a restaurant Hopper had seen on Greenwich Avenue in New York, the painting is not a realistic transcription of an actual place. As viewers, we are left to wonder about the figures, their relationships, and this imagined world.
persona2_adapted:
**About *Nighthawks***  
Edward Hopper once recalled that, “unconsciously, probably, I was painting the loneliness of a large city.” In this scene, set in an all-night diner, three customers sit at the counter across from a server. Each figure seems lost in thought and disconnected from the others.  

The painting is tightly arranged, with few details: there is no visible entrance to the diner, and the streets are empty. Through balanced shapes and the warm glow of the diner’s electric lights, Hopper created a calm, beautiful, yet mysterious scene. Although the work was inspired by a restaurant Hopper saw on Greenwich Avenue in New York, it is not a realistic copy of an actual place. As viewers, we are left to wonder about the people, their connections to one another, and this imagined world.

CuratorialText: The Assumption of the Virgin was the central image of El Greco’s first major commission, an altarpiece for the Church of Santo Domingo el Antiguo, a convent in Toledo, Spain. The painting’s subject, the raising of the Virgin Mary’s body into heaven, relates to the function of the church as the funerary monument of a wealthy noblewoman, Doña Maria de Silva; the iconography of the entire commission focuses on death and salvation.
persona2_adapted: The Assumption of the Virgin was the central painting in El Greco’s first major commission: an altarpiece—a large artwork placed behind an altar—for the Church of Santo Domingo el Antiguo, a convent in Toledo, Spain. The painting shows the Virgin Mary’s body being lifted up into heaven. This subject was chosen because the church also served as the burial monument for a wealthy noblewoman, Doña Maria de Silva. As a result, the overall theme of the commission focuses on death and salvation.

CuratorialText: El Greco used broad, free brushwork, flickering hues, rich color harmonies, and bold figural arrangements to arouse devotional fervor in the viewer and impart a deep sense of faith. The composition is divided into two zones: the earthly sphere of the apostles and the celestial realm of the angels. El Greco masterfully compressed many figures into a tall and narrow format: In the earthly zone, the apostles’ faces and gestures express a range of emotions through their dynamic, agitated gestures and poses. They are so individually considered that it seems El Greco studied actual studio models. In contrast, the angels in the heavenly scene above are calm, graceful, and direct in their focus toward the looming figure of the Virgin Mary as she rises into their realm.
persona2_adapted: El Greco used broad, energetic brushstrokes, shifting colors, and carefully balanced color combinations to stir a deep sense of religious devotion in the viewer. The painting is split into two areas: the earthly world of the apostles below and the heavenly realm of the angels above. El Greco skillfully packed many figures into a tall, narrow space. In the earthly section, the apostles’ faces and hand movements show a wide range of emotions through their lively, restless poses. Each figure seems so individually observed that it appears El Greco worked from real models in his studio. In contrast, the angels in the heavenly scene above are calm, elegant, and all look directly toward the towering figure of the Virgin Mary as she rises into their realm.

CuratorialText: Katsushika Hokusai’s much celebrated series, Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji (Fugaku sanjûrokkei), was begun in 1830, when the artist was 70 years old. This tour-de-force series established the popularity of landscape prints, which continues to this day. Perhaps most striking about the series is Hokusai’s copious use of the newly affordable Berlin blue pigment, featured in many of the compositions in the color for the sky and water. Mount Fuji is the protagonist in each scene, viewed from afar or up close, during various weather conditions and seasons, and from all directions.
persona2_adapted: Katsushika Hokusai’s widely celebrated series, *Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji*, was begun in 1830, when the artist was 70 years old. This masterful series helped make landscape prints popular—a trend that continues to this day. What may be most striking about the series is Hokusai’s extensive use of a newly affordable blue pigment called Berlin blue, which appears in many of the prints, coloring the sky and water. Mount Fuji is the central figure in every scene, whether seen from far away or up close, in different weather and seasons, and from every direction.

CuratorialText: The most famous image from the set is the “Great Wave” (Kanagawa oki nami ura), in which a diminutive Mount Fuji can be seen in the distance under the crest of a giant wave. The three impressions of Hokusai’s Great Wave in the Art Institute are all later impressions than the first state of the design.
persona2_adapted: The most famous image from the series is the "Great Wave" (Kanagawa oki nami ura). In this print, a tiny Mount Fuji appears in the distance beneath the towering curl of a massive wave. The three versions of Hokusai's Great Wave held by the Art Institute were all printed later than the earliest version of the design.

CuratorialText: Pablo Picasso made The Old Guitarist while working in Barcelona. In the paintings of his Blue Period (1901-04), the artist restricted himself to a cold, monochromatic blue palette, flattened forms, and emotional, psychological themes of human misery and alienation related to the work of such artists as Edvard Munch and Paul Gauguin. The elongated, angular figure of the blind musician also relates to Picasso’s interest in Spanish art and, in particular, the great 16th-century artist El Greco. The image reﬂects the twenty-two-year-old Picasso’s personal struggle and sympathy for the plight of the downtrodden; he knew what it was like to be poor, having been nearly penniless during all of 1902.
persona2_adapted: Pablo Picasso painted *The Old Guitarist* while he was living and working in Barcelona. During what is known as his Blue Period (1901–04), he limited himself to a cold, single-color blue palette, flattened the forms in his paintings, and focused on emotional and psychological themes like human suffering and isolation. These concerns were similar to those found in the work of artists such as Edvard Munch and Paul Gauguin. The blind musician’s stretched, angular figure also shows Picasso’s interest in Spanish art, especially the work of the great 16th-century painter El Greco. The image reflects the personal struggles of the twenty-two-year-old Picasso, as well as his sympathy for people who were poor or marginalized. He knew what it was like to be destitute, having been nearly penniless throughout 1902.

CuratorialText: Illustrator Norman Rockwell was one of the most popular artists of the 20th century, beloved in particular for the hundreds of cover images he created for the Saturday Evening Post. The Dugout is the largest of several studies for one such cover, published on September 4, 1948. It was inspired by a springtime doubleheader that he attended during which the hometown Boston Braves swept the visiting Chicago Cubs. The artist created an image contrasting dejected Cubs players and elated Braves fans.
persona2_adapted: Norman Rockwell, an illustrator best known for his many cover images for the *Saturday Evening Post*, was one of the most popular American artists of the 1900s. *The Dugout* is the largest of several preparatory works—essentially practice versions—for one of those covers, which was published on September 4, 1948. Rockwell got the idea after attending a springtime doubleheader (two games played in a row on the same day) in which the hometown Boston Braves defeated the visiting Chicago Cubs in both games. In the image, he sets up a clear contrast: the dejected Cubs players in their dugout versus the elated Braves fans celebrating nearby.

CuratorialText: Rockwell staged the entire scene, which enabled him to take photographs for later reference. He recruited several family members of Braves players to act as fans. In the dugout, he positioned (from left to right) Cubs pitcher Bob Rush, manager Charlie Grimm, catcher Al “Rube” Walker, and pitcher Johnny Schmitz. The figure of the forlorn batboy, however, was not a member of the Cubs; Rockwell paid Braves batboy Frank McNulty $5 to don the Cubs uniform and pose.
persona2_adapted: Rockwell carefully arranged every element of the scene, taking photographs to use as reference material later. He asked several family members of Braves players to pose as fans. In the dugout, he placed (from left to right) Cubs pitcher Bob Rush, manager Charlie Grimm, catcher Al “Rube” Walker, and pitcher Johnny Schmitz. The sad-looking batboy, however, was not actually a member of the Cubs; Rockwell paid Braves batboy Frank McNulty $5 to put on a Cubs uniform and pose for the picture.

CuratorialText: By the time the issue was published, the Cubs had fallen to last place in their league. Rockwell’s cover helped bolster their growing reputation as the “loveable losers,” and it became one of the most recognized sports images of the 20th century.
persona2_adapted: By the time the magazine issue came out, the Cubs had dropped to the bottom of their league standings. Rockwell’s cover image helped strengthen the team’s emerging image as the “loveable losers,” and it went on to become one of the most famous sports pictures of the 1900s.

CuratorialText: The monumental stacks that Claude Monet depicted in his series Stacks of Wheat rose fifteen to twenty feet and stood just outside the artist’s farmhouse at Giverny. Through 1890 and 1891, he worked on this series both in the field, painting simultaneously at several easels, and in the studio, refining pictorial harmonies. In May 1891, Monet hung fifteen of these canvases next to each other in one small room in the Galerie Durand-Ruel in Paris. An unprecedented critical and financial success, the exhibition marked a breakthrough in Monet’s career, as well as in the history of French art. In this view, and in nearly all of the autumn views in the series, the conical tops of the stacks break the horizon and push into the sky. But in most of the winter views, which constitute the core of the series, the stacks seem wrapped by bands of hill and field, as if bedded down for the season. For Monet, the stack was a resonant symbol of sustenance and survival. He followed this group with further series depicting poplars, the facade of Rouen Cathedral, and, later, his own garden at Giverny. The Art Institute has the largest group of Monet’s Stacks of Wheat in the world.
persona2_adapted:
The towering stacks of wheat that Claude Monet painted in his *Stacks of Wheat* series stood about fifteen to twenty feet tall, just outside the artist’s farmhouse in Giverny. Between 1890 and 1891, Monet worked on this series both outdoors—painting at several easels at once to capture changing light—and indoors, where he fine-tuned the colors and composition. In May 1891, he hung fifteen of these paintings side by side in a single small room at the Galerie Durand-Ruel in Paris. The exhibition was an unprecedented success, both critically and financially, marking a turning point in Monet’s career and in French art history.

In this particular painting, and in nearly all the autumn scenes from the series, the cone-shaped tops of the stacks rise above the horizon line, pushing into the sky. But in most of the winter scenes—which form the heart of the series—the stacks appear wrapped by bands of hills and fields, as if settling in for the season. For Monet, the stack was a powerful symbol of nourishment and endurance. He followed this group with other series featuring poplars, the front of Rouen Cathedral, and later, his own garden at Giverny. The Art Institute holds the largest collection of Monet’s *Stacks of Wheat* in the world.

CuratorialText: In 1886 Vincent van Gogh left his native Holland and settled in Paris, where his beloved brother Theo was a dealer in paintings. Van Gogh was apparently inspired by the energy of the city and by his introduction to Impressionism, and he created at least twenty-four self-portraits during his two-year stay in the French capital.
persona2_adapted: In 1886, Vincent van Gogh left his homeland of Holland and moved to Paris, where his beloved brother Theo worked as an art dealer. The city's vibrant energy and his first encounters with Impressionism—a style of painting that focuses on capturing fleeting moments of light and color—seem to have deeply inspired him. During his two years in the French capital, he produced at least twenty-four self-portraits.

CuratorialText: This early example is modest in size and was painted on prepared artist’s board rather than canvas. Its densely dabbed brushwork, which became a hallmark of Van Gogh’s style, reﬂects the artist’s response to Georges Seurat’s revolutionary pointillist technique in A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884. What was for Seurat a method based on the cool objectivity of science became in Van Gogh’s hands an intense emotional language. The surface of the painting dances with particles of color—intense greens, blues, reds, and oranges. Dominating this dazzling array of staccato dots and dashes are the artist’s deep green eyes and the intensity of their gaze. “I prefer painting people’s eyes to cathedrals,” Van Gogh once wrote to Theo. “However solemn and imposing the latter may be—a human soul, be it that of a poor streetwalker, is more interesting to me.” From Paris, Van Gogh traveled to the southern town of Arles for fifteen months. At the time of his death, in 1890, he had actively pursued his art for only five years.

Its surface is covered in dense, dabbed brushstrokes—a technique that would become a signature of Van Gogh’s style. This approach was inspired by Georges Seurat’s revolutionary method in *A Sunday on La Grande Jatte—1884*, a technique known as pointillism, where tiny dots of color are placed side by side to blend in the viewer’s eye. What for Seurat was a cool, scientific system became, in Van Gogh’s hands, a way to express intense emotion. The painting’s surface seems to vibrate with particles of color—vivid greens, blues, reds, and oranges. Dominating this shimmering field of short, staccato marks are the artist’s deep green eyes and the power of their gaze. “I prefer painting people’s eyes to cathedrals,” Van Gogh once wrote to his brother Theo. “However solemn and imposing the latter may be—a human soul, be it that of a poor streetwalker, is more interesting to me.” From Paris, Van Gogh traveled south to the town of Arles for fifteen months. By the time of his death in 1890, he had actively pursued his art for only five years.

CuratorialText: In American Gothic, Grant Wood directly evoked images of an earlier generation by featuring a farmer and his daughter posed stiffly and dressed as if they were, as the artist put it, “tintypes from my old family album.” They stand outside of their home, built in an 1880s style known as Carpenter Gothic. Wood had seen a similar farmhouse during a visit to Eldon, Iowa. When it was exhibited at the Art Institute in 1930, the painting became an instant sensation, its ambiguity prompting viewers to speculate about the figures and their story. Many understood the work to be a satirical comment on midwesterners out of step with a modernizing world. Yet Wood intended it to convey a positive image of rural American values, offering a vision of reassurance at the beginning of the Great Depression.
persona2_adapted: In *American Gothic*, Grant Wood directly called to mind images of an earlier generation by showing a farmer and his daughter standing stiffly and dressed as if they were, in the artist’s words, “tintypes from my old family album.” They stand outside their home, built in an 1880s style called Carpenter Gothic—a type of architecture that uses decorative wooden details to imitate medieval stone buildings. Wood had seen a similar farmhouse during a visit to Eldon, Iowa. When the painting was exhibited at the Art Institute in 1930, it became an instant sensation. Its unclear meaning led viewers to wonder about the figures and their story. Many saw the work as a satirical comment on midwesterners who seemed out of step with a modernizing world. Yet Wood intended it to convey a positive image of rural American values, offering a vision of reassurance at the start of the Great Depression.

CuratorialText: Little did Claude Monet know that the water garden he created three years after buying his Giverny, France, property in 1890 would become his primary inspiration over the next two and a half decades. These paintings, numbering around 250, mark Monet’s artistic journey from more straightforward depictions of the pond spanned by the wooden Japanese bridge to the monumental and near-abstract series on the water lily theme he made in preparation for his murals at the Musée de l’Orangerie in Paris between 1914 and 1926.
persona2_adapted: Claude Monet probably had no idea that the water garden he built three years after buying his property in Giverny, France, in 1890 would become his main source of inspiration for the next twenty-five years. These paintings—about 250 in total—trace Monet’s artistic development from fairly straightforward views of the pond with its wooden Japanese bridge to the large-scale, nearly abstract series on the water lily theme that he created in preparation for his wall paintings at the Musée de l’Orangerie in Paris, completed between 1914 and 1926.

CuratorialText: Water Lilies, one of a group of paintings on the subject made between 1903 and 1908, comes at the midpoint of Monet’s developing style and spatial experimentations. The nearly square format underscores his move away from painting the conventional zones of land, sky, and water to focus solely on the water’s surface. Clusters of water lilies at bottom left and top right frame a watery path, while the water’s surface reflects trees and clouds. Although the dreamlike quality of floating forms might seem to be a natural development for the artist, he considered these works “an obsession,” a sentiment borne out by technical examinations on the Art Institute’s canvas, revealing many significant changes made to the painting in progress.
persona2_adapted: Water Lilies is part of a series of paintings on this theme that Monet created between 1903 and 1908. This work marks a middle point in the evolution of his style and his experiments with space. The painting is nearly square, which highlights his shift away from the traditional approach of depicting separate areas like land, sky, and water. Instead, he focuses entirely on the surface of the water. Clusters of water lilies in the lower left and upper right create a kind of watery pathway between them, while the water's surface reflects trees and clouds above. Although the floating forms give the painting a dreamlike quality that might seem like a natural step for the artist, Monet himself described these works as "an obsession." Scientific analysis of the Art Institute's canvas supports this feeling, revealing that he made many significant changes to the painting as he worked on it.

CuratorialText: Pierre-Auguste Renoir considered the Restaurant Fournaise in Chatou (a village along the Seine River) the perfect spot to enjoy nature and good company away from the urban distractions of Paris. His painting Two Sisters (On the Terrace), which he exhibited at the Impressionist exhibition of 1882, is among the last of his paintings set there.
persona2_adapted: Pierre-Auguste Renoir saw the Restaurant Fournaise in Chatou—a village along the Seine River—as an ideal place to enjoy nature and good company, far from the busy distractions of Paris. His painting *Two Sisters (On the Terrace)*, which he showed at the 1882 Impressionist exhibition (a group show for artists who focused on capturing light and everyday scenes), is one of the last works he created in that location.

CuratorialText: Sometimes, as in his most famous work on this subject, Luncheon of the Boating Party (1882; Phillips Collection, Washington, DC), Renoir used his close friends as models. For Two Sisters, however, despite the title of the painting, he paid unrelated models to create this intimate and perfectly balanced pairing of youth and nature. The older girl, wearing the blue flannel of a female boater’s outfit, sports a luxurious red hat, but it is her younger companion who captivates. Her oversize headdress composed of a floral bouquet is echoed in the brightly colored skeins of yarn in the basket that she gently touches. As was typical for the artist, Renoir painted the faces of his models with controlled and precise brushwork creating a porcelain-like surface that contrasts brilliantly with the diaphanous, impressionist backdrop of river, boats, and blossoming trees just beyond the railing of the terrace.
persona2_adapted: Sometimes, as in his most famous work on this subject, *Luncheon of the Boating Party* (1882; Phillips Collection, Washington, DC), Renoir used his close friends as models. For *Two Sisters*, however, despite the painting’s title, he paid unrelated models to create this intimate and perfectly balanced pairing of youth and nature. The older girl, wearing the blue flannel of a female boater’s outfit, sports a luxurious red hat, but it is her younger companion who captivates. CuratorialText: Willem de Kooning was a central figure in Abstract Expressionism, an art movement that espoused the painterly actions of the artist as a sign of his or her emotions. De Kooning completed Excavation in June 1950, just in time for it to be exhibited in the twenty-fifth Venice Biennale. His largest painting up to that date, the work exemplifies the Dutch-born innovator’s style, with its expressive brushwork and distinctive organization of space into sliding planes with open contours. According to the artist, the point of departure for the painting was an image of women working in a rice field in Bitter Rice, a 1949 Neorealist film by Italian director Giuseppe de Santis. The mobile structure of hooked calligraphic lines defines anatomical parts—bird and fish shapes, human noses, eyes, teeth, necks, and jaws—that seem to dance across the painted surface, revealing the particular tension between abstraction and figuration that is inherent in de Kooning’s work. The original white pigment has yellowed over the years, diluting somewhat the flashes of red, blue, yellow, and pink that punctuate the composition. Aptly titled, the painting reflects de Kooning’s technically masterful painting process: an intensive building up of the surface and scraping down of its paint layers, often for months, until the desired effect was achieved.
persona2_adapted:
Willem de Kooning was a key figure in Abstract Expressionism, a movement in which artists used bold, physical brushwork to express their inner emotions. He finished *Excavation* in June 1950, just in time for it to be shown at the twenty-fifth Venice Biennale, a major international art exhibition. At that time, it was the largest painting he had ever made, and it shows his distinctive style: energetic, visible brushstrokes and a way of arranging space into sliding, overlapping shapes with open outlines.

The painting was inspired, de Kooning said, by a scene from *Bitter Rice*, a 1949 Italian film by director Giuseppe de Santis, showing women working in a rice field. Across the canvas, hooked, line-like forms suggest body parts—bird and fish shapes, human noses, eyes, teeth, necks, and jaws—that seem to move and dance. These forms create a constant push and pull between abstract shapes and recognizable figures, a tension that runs through much of de Kooning’s work.

Over time, the original white paint has yellowed, softening the flashes of red, blue, yellow, and pink that stand out across the composition. The title *Excavation* is fitting: it reflects de Kooning’s intense process of building up layers of paint and then scraping them back down, often over many months, until he achieved the effect he wanted.

CuratorialText: White Crucifixion is the first in Marc Chagall’s series of compositions that feature Jesus as a Jewish martyr and dramatically call attention to the persecution and suffering of Jews in 1930s Germany at the hands of the National Socialist Party. Chagall stressed Jesus’s religious identity by depicting him and the biblical figures above him in traditional Jewish garments. The surrounding images show the devastation of pogroms, violent attacks against Jewish communities often organized or sanctioned by local governments. Combining the Crucifixion with contemporary events, Chagall’s painting links the martyred Jesus with the Jewish people being persecuted across Europe and implicitly compares the Nazis with Jesus’s tormentors.
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

*White Crucifixion* is the first in a series of works by Marc Chagall that show Jesus as a Jewish martyr. The painting powerfully draws attention to the persecution and suffering of Jews in 1930s Germany under the National Socialist Party. Chagall emphasizes Jesus’s Jewish identity by depicting him—and the biblical figures above him—wearing traditional Jewish clothing. The scenes surrounding the crucifixion show the destruction caused by pogroms: violent attacks on Jewish communities that were often organized or approved by local governments. By combining the image of the Crucifixion with current events, Chagall connects the martyred Jesus to the Jewish people being persecuted across Europe. In doing so, he implicitly compares the Nazis to those who tormented Jesus.

CuratorialText: The Child’s Bath is a tender portrayal of familial closeness, a subject that Mary Cassatt explored throughout her career. The caregiver’s cheek brushing the child’s shoulder, her encircling embrace, and the child’s pudgy hand on her knee suggest an emotional bond between the two. Captivated by a large exhibition of Japanese prints in Paris in 1890, Cassatt set out to produce a series of color prints influenced by Japanese aesthetics. She then continued her investigation across media, culminating in this bold composition, with its dramatically flattened picture plane, decorative patterning, and bright palette.
persona2_adapted: The Child’s Bath is a gentle depiction of family intimacy, a theme Mary Cassatt returned to again and again in her work. The caregiver’s cheek lightly touching the child’s shoulder, her arms wrapped around the child, and the child’s chubby hand resting on her knee all suggest a deep emotional connection between them. After being inspired by a major exhibition of Japanese prints in Paris in 1890, Cassatt began creating a series of color prints that reflected Japanese artistic styles. She then continued exploring this approach in other mediums, resulting in this bold composition—notice how the space appears compressed, almost flat, with decorative patterns and bright colors throughout.

CuratorialText: René Magritte painted Time Transfixed at a moment when he was attempting to elicit “poetic secrets” through his works. With both philosophy and psychology in mind, the artist challenged himself to juxtapose two images in such a way that they would suggest in the viewer’s mind a third un-pictured thing. Here he connected two highly disparate objects—a train and a fireplace—in a composition that became one of his most iconic works. Although the fireplace is domestic and the train is industrial, they are linked by the smoke. Magritte understood that viewers would connect the locomotive’s billowing exhaust with the plumes of smoke that travel up chimneys, uncovering the painting’s poetic secret.
persona2_adapted: René Magritte painted *Time Transfixed* at a time when he was trying to uncover what he called “poetic secrets” through his art. Drawing on ideas from both philosophy and psychology, he set himself a challenge: to place two images side by side in a way that would make the viewer imagine a third, unseen thing. In this painting, he brought together two very different objects—a train and a fireplace—in a composition that became one of his most famous works. Even though the fireplace belongs to the home and the train belongs to the industrial world, they are connected by smoke. Magritte knew that viewers would link the train’s thick exhaust with the smoke rising from a chimney, and in doing so, would discover the painting’s hidden poetic meaning.

CuratorialText: One of the most avant-garde aspects of the Impressionists was their choice of subject matter, which frequently included scenes derived from modern, industrial Paris, from iron bridges to exhibition halls to train sheds. The train station at Saint-Lazare would have been a familiar, meaningful sight to Claude Monet in the 1870s. The terminal linked Paris to Normandy, where the artist developed his technique of painting outdoors in the 1860s. It was also the point of departure for the towns and villages west and north of Paris that the Impressionists frequently visited. Monet completed eight of his twelve known paintings of the Gare Saint-Lazare in time for the third Impressionist exhibition, in 1877, probably displaying them in the same gallery.
persona2_adapted: One of the most daring choices made by the Impressionists was what they decided to paint. Instead of traditional subjects, they often turned to scenes from modern, industrial Paris—iron bridges, exhibition halls, and train sheds. For Claude Monet in the 1870s, the Saint-Lazare train station would have been a familiar and meaningful place. This station connected Paris to Normandy, where Monet had developed his technique of painting outdoors in the 1860s. It was also the departure point for the towns and villages west and north of Paris that the Impressionists frequently visited. Monet completed eight of his twelve known paintings of the Gare Saint-Lazare in time for the third Impressionist exhibition in 1877, and he likely displayed them together in the same gallery.

CuratorialText: Monet chose to focus his attention here on the glass-and-iron train shed, where he found an appealing combination of artiﬁ cial and natural effects—the rising steam of locomotives trapped within the structure and the daylight penetrating large, glazed sections of the roof, for instance. Monet’s depictions of the station inaugurated what was to become for him an established pattern of painting a speciﬁc motif repeatedly in order to capture subtle and temporal atmospheric changes, as in his famous series of stacks of wheat. But the Saint-Lazare paintings also represented his last attempt to capture urban life: from this point on in his career, Monet largely devoted himself to landscapes.
persona2_adapted: Monet focused here on the train shed, a structure made of glass and iron. He was drawn to the way artificial and natural elements came together in this space—for example, the steam rising from the locomotives, trapped inside the building, and the daylight filtering through the large glass sections of the roof. By painting the Saint-Lazare station repeatedly, Monet began a practice that would become central to his work: capturing the same subject over and over to record subtle, temporary changes in light and atmosphere. This is the same approach he later used in his well-known series of paintings of haystacks. However, the Saint-Lazare paintings also marked his final effort to depict city life. After this point, Monet turned almost entirely to painting landscapes.

CuratorialText: Salvador Dalí, Surrealism’s most publicized practitioner, created monstrous visions of a world turned inside out, which he made even more compelling through his extraordinary technical skills. When the Art Institute acquired Inventions of the Monsters in 1943, the artist wrote his congratulations and explained.
persona2_adapted: Salvador Dalí, the most widely known artist associated with Surrealism—a movement that explored dreamlike, irrational imagery—created disturbing visions of a world turned upside down. He made these visions even more striking through his exceptional technical ability. When the Art Institute acquired *Inventions of the Monsters* in 1943, the artist wrote to congratulate the museum and offered an explanation of the work.

CuratorialText: “According to Nostradamus the apparition of monsters presages the outbreak of war. The canvas was painted in the Semmering mountains near Vienna a few months before the Anschluss [the 1938 political union of Austria and Germany] and has a prophetic character. Horse women equal maternal river monsters. Flaming giraffe equals masculine apocalyptic monster. Cat angel equals divine heterosexual monster. Hourglass equals metaphysical monster. Gala and Dalí equal sentimental monster. The little blue dog is not a true monster.”
persona2_adapted: According to Nostradamus, the appearance of monsters foreshadows the outbreak of war. This painting was created in the Semmering mountains near Vienna just a few months before the Anschluss—the 1938 political union of Austria and Germany—and carries a prophetic quality. In the work, horse-women represent maternal river monsters; a flaming giraffe stands for a masculine apocalyptic monster; a cat-angel embodies a divine heterosexual monster; an hourglass symbolizes a metaphysical monster; and the figures of Gala and Dalí themselves become a sentimental monster. The little blue dog, however, is not considered a true monster.

CuratorialText: Inventions of the Monsters has an ominous mood. It is rife with threats of danger, from the menacing fire in the distance to the sibylline figure in the foreground with an hourglass and a butterfly, both symbols of the inevitability of death. Next to this figure sit Dalí and his wife and muse, Gala. With his native Catalonia embroiled in the Spanish Civil War, the artist surely felt great anxiety over a world without a safe haven, a world that indeed had allowed for the invention of monsters.
persona2_adapted: Inventions of the Monsters carries a dark, unsettling atmosphere. The painting is filled with signs of danger, from the threatening fire in the background to the mysterious figure in the front holding an hourglass and a butterfly—both objects that suggest the certainty of death. Beside this figure sit Dalí and his wife and muse, Gala. With his home region of Catalonia caught up in the Spanish Civil War, the artist must have felt deep anxiety about a world with no safe place left, a world that had, in fact, allowed for the creation of monsters.

CuratorialText: Shiva, one of the most important Hindu divinities, is here depicted as the Lord of the Dance (Nataraja), an iconic image in Indian art. Shiva’s cosmic dance sets in motion the rhythm of life and death; it pervades the universe, as symbolized by the ring of fire that is filled with the loose, snakelike locks of the god’s hair.
