One pair of his arms balances the flame of destruction and the hand drum (damaru) that beats the rhythm of life while another performs symbolic gestures: the raised right hand means “fear not,” and the left hand (gajahasta) pointing down toward his raised left foot signifies release from the ignorance that hinders realization of the ultimate reality. Shiva is shown perfectly balanced, with his right leg planted on the demon of darkness (Apasmara), stamping out ignorance. The tiny figure of the personified river goddess, Ganga, is caught up in his matted, flowing locks. Shiva was believed to break the fall of the great Ganges River as it descends from the Himalayas by standing beneath the waters, which divide over his hair, becoming the seven holy rivers of India. This classic bronze comes from the Chola period in the south of India. Icons such as this were carried in procession during religious ceremonies.
persona2_adapted: Shiva, one of the most important gods in Hinduism, appears here as the Lord of the Dance (Nataraja), a famous image in Indian art. His cosmic dance sets the rhythm of life and death; it fills the entire universe, as shown by the ring of fire around him, which is crowded with the loose, snake-like strands of his hair. One pair of his arms holds the flame of destruction and the hand drum (damaru) that beats the rhythm of life, while the other pair makes meaningful gestures: his raised right hand means “do not fear,” and his left hand, pointing down toward his lifted left foot, signals freedom from the ignorance that blocks understanding of the ultimate truth. Shiva is shown perfectly balanced, with his right foot planted on the demon of darkness (Apasmara), crushing ignorance. The small figure of the river goddess Ganga is caught in his tangled, flowing hair. According to belief, Shiva stood beneath the great Ganges River as it fell from the Himalayas, breaking its descent; the waters divided over his hair and became the seven holy rivers of India. This classic bronze sculpture comes from the Chola period in southern India. Objects like this were carried in processions during religious ceremonies.

CuratorialText: Albert Bierstadt is best remembered for his paintings of the American West, but during his early career, the ambitious artist also created New England landscapes, particularly of the White Mountains, as seen here. When this painting was first exhibited in 1863, critics declared it Bierstadt’s “best work” and praised the artist’s deft contrast of “light and shade,” which brought a heightened realism to the image. Bierstadt’s affinity for the White Mountains mirrored a growing interest in the region as one of America’s premier tourist attractions. Photographs of the area from the 19th century suggest that Bierstadt utilized landscape elements from the Flume, a popular New Hampshire tourist site, in this imaginary composition.
persona2_adapted: Albert Bierstadt is best known for his paintings of the American West, but early in his career, this ambitious artist also created landscapes of New England—especially the White Mountains, as shown here. When this painting was first displayed in 1863, critics called it Bierstadt’s “best work” and praised his skillful use of contrast between light and dark, which gave the image a striking sense of realism. Bierstadt’s connection to the White Mountains reflected a growing public interest in the region as one of America’s top tourist destinations. Nineteenth-century photographs of the area suggest that Bierstadt borrowed landscape features from the Flume, a popular New Hampshire tourist site, to create this imagined scene.

CuratorialText: In this large-scale canvas, one of several Paul Gauguin sent back to France in 1897, women of varying ages appear to go about daily life within a colorful, stylized outdoor environment. Like much of the artist’s work, this painting combines observed detail with artistic fantasy to create a dreamlike scene whose narrative resists simple interpretation. The work’s deliberately provocative title enhances this effect, leaving us to wonder if the “you” refers to someone in the painting or to the viewer.
persona2_adapted: In this large-scale painting—one of several Paul Gauguin sent back to France in 1897—women of different ages seem to be going about their daily routines in a colorful, deliberately simplified outdoor setting. Like much of the artist’s work, this scene blends what he actually observed with imagined elements, creating a dreamlike atmosphere whose story is not easy to pin down. The title, which is intentionally meant to provoke or unsettle, adds to this effect: it leaves us wondering whether the “you” refers to someone inside the painting or to us, the viewers.

CuratorialText: Motivations for travel throughout history have been almost as varied as the means of travel. Whether people traveled alone or in groups, on foot, on animals, in caravans, on ships, or using modern modes of transportation like planes, trains, and automobiles, art can capture how and why we move through the world.
persona2_adapted: Motivations for travel throughout history have been almost as varied as the means of getting from place to place. Whether people journeyed alone or in groups, on foot, on animals, in caravans, on ships, or using modern modes of transportation like planes, trains, and automobiles, art can capture both how and why we move through the world.

CuratorialText: Marco Polo (1254–1324) was a Venetian merchant who spent about seventeen years in China—from around 1274 to 1292—during the reign of the Mongol emperor Khubilai Khan (reigned 1260–94), founder of the Yuan dynasty. In 1276, Khubilai’s armies conquered the Chinese capital at Hangzhou with minimal destruction. Sometime after this, Marco Polo visited the city and its West Lake.
persona2_adapted: Marco Polo (1254–1324) was a merchant from Venice who lived in China for roughly seventeen years, from about 1274 to 1292. This was during the rule of the Mongol emperor Khubilai Khan (who reigned from 1260 to 1294), the founder of the Yuan dynasty. In 1276, Khubilai’s armies took control of the Chinese capital, Hangzhou, causing very little damage. Sometime after this event, Marco Polo traveled to the city and visited its famous West Lake.

CuratorialText: Travel is a common theme in traditional Chinese landscape painting. From ancient times, the emperors of China maintained a network of roads and waterways that unified their vast realm and facilitated the movement of people and goods. Owing to this imperial association, images of ordinary travelers on the empire’s roads and rivers connoted the benevolent rule of an enlightened sovereign and evoked an era of peace and prosperity. Rather than documenting the hardships or actual circumstances of any particular journey, images of anonymous characters traveling or working together capture some of the noteworthy sights and scenes a traveler might encounter along the way. Simultaneously, they promote the fundamental ideals of a harmonious society and a well-ordered state.
persona2_adapted: Travel is a common subject in traditional Chinese landscape painting. Since ancient times, China’s emperors built and maintained a system of roads and waterways that connected their vast territory, making it easier for people and goods to move around. Because of this connection to imperial power, images of ordinary people traveling on the empire’s roads and rivers suggested that a wise ruler was in charge, and brought to mind a time of peace and prosperity. Rather than showing the difficulties or real details of any specific journey, these paintings of unnamed figures traveling or working together capture some of the interesting sights and scenes a traveler might see along the way. At the same time, they promote the basic ideals of a harmonious society and a well-ordered state.

CuratorialText: One of the most enduring motivations for travel is religious devotion. Nearly every major religion—Christianity, Judaism, Islam, Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, and Sikhism, among others—has a tradition of pilgrimage: intentional travel to a site of spiritual significance. Christian pilgrims traveled to the Holy Land to walk in the footsteps of Christ or visited more local destinations connected to the lives or relics of the saints. In Islam, pilgrimage (hajj) to Mecca is one of the principal duties of all believers. Other holy sites were also visited on a regular basis. Over many centuries, hundreds of Chinese, Japanese, and Korean pilgrims braved countless dangers to visit places where the Historical Buddha lived and taught. Pilgrims would sometimes bring gifts, called votive offerings, with them as a donation to the destination site.
persona2_adapted: One of the most powerful reasons people have traveled throughout history is religious devotion. Nearly every major religion—Christianity, Judaism, Islam, Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, and Sikhism, among others—has a tradition of pilgrimage: a journey made on purpose to a place that holds spiritual meaning. Christian pilgrims traveled to the Holy Land to follow in the footsteps of Christ, or visited more local sites connected to the lives or remains of saints. In Islam, the pilgrimage to Mecca, known as the hajj, is one of the essential duties for all believers. Other holy places were also visited regularly. Over many centuries, hundreds of pilgrims from China, Japan, and Korea braved countless dangers to visit locations where the Historical Buddha lived and taught. Sometimes, pilgrims would bring gifts, called votive offerings, as a donation to the place they were visiting.

CuratorialText: Trade was another common motivation for travel and occurred in many forms, but the most famous trade route in history was the Silk Road, the overland trade routes that linked the prosperous Tang empire (618–907) with Central, West, and South Asia. On the Silk Road, foreign merchants joined Buddhist missionaries, diplomatic envoys, translators, craftsmen, entertainers, and other skilled immigrants in a wealthy, worldly environment that offered a ready market for exotic imports, including silver and gold objects, delicate glassware, and even grape wine.
persona2_adapted: Trade was another common reason people traveled, and it took many different forms. However, the most famous trade route in history was the Silk Road—a network of land routes that connected the wealthy Tang empire (618–907) with Central, West, and South Asia. Along the Silk Road, foreign merchants traveled alongside Buddhist missionaries, government representatives, translators, skilled craftspeople, performers, and other immigrants with specialized talents. Together, they created a rich and internationally connected environment, where there was a strong demand for exotic goods from abroad—such as objects made of silver and gold, delicate glassware, and even grape wine.

CuratorialText: In the twentieth century, travel offered a key means for diplomacy and cultural exchange. Alice Roosevelt Longworth (1884–1980), Theodore Roosevelt’s daughter, embarked on a tour of Asia accompanying William Howard Taft’s goodwill mission in 1905. This voyage is immortalized in a series of photographs but also through diplomatic gifts that dignitaries, such as the emperor of Japan, bestowed on Roosevelt.
persona2_adapted: In the twentieth century, travel became an important tool for building relationships between countries and sharing cultures. Alice Roosevelt Longworth (1884–1980), the daughter of President Theodore Roosevelt, joined a goodwill mission led by William Howard Taft in 1905, traveling through Asia. This journey is remembered not only through a series of photographs but also through the official gifts she received from foreign leaders, such as the emperor of Japan, as a gesture of diplomacy.

CuratorialText: Adventurers, archaeologists, and scholars have long been traversing the globe to conduct research and to explore foreign lands. In the early twentieth century, leisure travel among Europeans and North Americans also became increasingly common. Fascinated by the cultural heritage of Asia and the Middle East, tourists began to expand their horizons by venturing there.
persona2_adapted: Travelers, researchers, and historians have spent centuries journeying across the world to study and discover unfamiliar places. By the early 1900s, more Europeans and North Americans were also traveling for pleasure. Drawn by the rich cultural traditions of Asia and the Middle East, tourists began to broaden their experiences by visiting these regions.

CuratorialText: Artists who traveled played a pivotal role in documenting and popularizing their visions of the distant places they visited. Their interactions with other artists and the things they experienced on their journeys contributed to the dispersal of different artistic styles and motifs.
persona2_adapted: Artists who traveled played a key role in recording and sharing their personal impressions of the faraway places they visited. The exchanges they had with other artists, as well as the sights and experiences from their journeys, helped spread different artistic styles and recurring themes across regions.

CuratorialText: While travel has mostly positive connotations in popular culture today, it’s important to remember the darker sides of travel as well. Not all travel is undertaken by choice: people can be forced to travel as a result of political exile, as well as due to war, famine, disease, economic hardship, environmental disaster, and colonialism. Even when travel is voluntary, it can be dangerous, and this was particularly true in ages past. The physical act of traveling exposed people to potential injuries and diseases, the natural elements were unpredictable and threatened modes of transportation, and there was an inherent unpredictability to being on the road far from home. The recognition of these dangers shaped stories and cultural beliefs.
persona2_adapted: While travel today is often seen as something positive—a chance for adventure or relaxation—it's worth acknowledging that movement across the world has not always been a choice or a pleasure. Many people have been forced to leave their homes due to political exile, war, famine, disease, economic hardship, environmental disaster, or colonialism. Even when travel is voluntary, it can be risky, and this was especially true in earlier centuries. Simply being on the road exposed travelers to injury and illness; unpredictable weather could damage or destroy ships, carts, or other means of transport; and there was always an element of uncertainty in being far from familiar surroundings. These dangers shaped the stories people told and the beliefs they held about the world.

CuratorialText: Objects themselves have rich histories and often undertook journeys of their own, whether through trade, gift giving, or collecting. For conservators, the journey of an item is embedded within its material presence; marks, scars, repairs, and other interventions describe the actions of others who have contributed to its present state. The process of conservation involves the synthesis of material evidence and its placement in time, not just to understand an item’s past but to envision its future preservation. Anthropologists have described the trajectories of material items in terms of their lives, biographies, or itineraries, but such notions are intuitively familiar to conservators, whose actions are fundamentally additive to an item’s journey, both materially and through time.
persona2_adapted: Objects themselves have rich histories and often traveled from place to place—through trade, as gifts, or by being collected. For conservators—the specialists who care for and preserve objects—an item’s journey is written into its physical condition. Marks, scratches, repairs, and other signs of handling tell the story of everyone who has touched it and shaped what it looks like today. The work of conservation involves piecing together these physical clues and understanding when they occurred, not only to learn about the object’s past but also to plan how to keep it safe for the future. Anthropologists have described the paths objects take in terms of their lives, biographies, or journeys, but these ideas feel naturally familiar to conservators, whose actions always add a new chapter to an object’s story—both in its material form and across time.

CuratorialText: Individual travelers carried things with them that were essential for the journey itself. Clothing and equipment suitable to traversing long distances sometimes set them apart from local residents, and travelers carried personal effects and accessories, such as small books, drinking flasks, and protective amulets, to make the journey easier.
persona2_adapted: Individual travelers brought along items that were necessary for the journey itself. The clothing and gear suited to covering long distances sometimes made them stand out from local people, and they carried personal belongings and small accessories—such as pocket-sized books, drinking flasks, and protective charms—to make the trip more manageable.

CuratorialText: Travelers brought objects acquired on their journeys home with them as well. Holy objects acquired by pilgrims allowed them to bring the blessings of their pilgrimage back from their destination. Images made during travel—whether sketches, paintings, or photographs—captured the experience of the journey for future reflection. Souvenirs purchased during a trip also offered travelers a way of commemorating a journey and gave them something tangible on which to reflect. The advent of commercial global travel networks in many cultures and contexts was accompanied by a flood of photographs, drawings, postcards, mementos, and other means of recording scientific and sentimental experiences abroad. Perhaps the most recognizable artifact of travel over the past 150 years is the picture postcard. These were collected as mementos or mailed to family and friends from abroad, accruing the additional prestige of a foreign stamp and postmark.
persona2_adapted: Travelers also brought back objects they had collected during their journeys. For pilgrims, sacred items they acquired allowed them to carry the spiritual benefits of their pilgrimage home from the destination. Images created while traveling—whether sketches, paintings, or photographs—preserved the experience of the journey for later reflection. Souvenirs purchased along the way gave travelers a way to remember their trip and provided something physical to look back on. As commercial global travel networks expanded across many cultures and time periods, this led to an outpouring of photographs, drawings, postcards, keepsakes, and other ways of recording both scientific observations and personal experiences abroad. Perhaps the most familiar travel item from the past 150 years is the picture postcard. People collected these as keepsakes or mailed them to family and friends from other countries, which added the extra appeal of a foreign stamp and postmark.

CuratorialText: Most travel we think of involves physically moving between places, but virtual travel (colloquially, “armchair travel”) that takes place in the mind spans time and cultures and is facilitated by books and works of art. Through focused contemplation of a travel account, a map, a cherished souvenir, or images of far-off places, memory and imagination combine to transport someone mentally and to simulate the experience of travel.
persona2_adapted: Most of the travel we think about involves physically moving from one place to another. But there is also a kind of travel that happens in the mind—often called "armchair travel"—which can stretch across different time periods and cultures. This mental journey is made possible by things like books, maps, treasured souvenirs, or images of distant locations. When we focus deeply on these objects, our memory and imagination work together to mentally carry us away, creating an experience that feels like real travel.

CuratorialText: While it may not be the first thing we think of when looking at a work of art, environmental factors have shaped everything about it, from how it was made, to how it has been perceived and appreciated, to how it has been preserved for today’s audiences. Dive into the complex and interconnected relationships between art and the environment—from images of the natural world to recycling ancient jades—to see our collections in a whole new way.
persona2_adapted: When we look at a work of art, we might not immediately think about the environment. Yet environmental factors have influenced nearly every aspect of it—how it was created, how people have understood and valued it over time, and how it has been kept safe for us to see today. This exhibition explores the many ways art and the environment are connected, from depictions of the natural world to the reuse of ancient jade objects. It invites you to see our collections from a fresh perspective.

CuratorialText: While environmental conditions differ from place to place, contact with nature has been a universal human experience. It’s therefore not surprising that natural motifs—plants, animals, water, the sky—adorn works of art across all cultures and are a consistent source of inspiration and meaning.
persona2_adapted: While environments vary from one place to another, connecting with nature is something all people share. So it makes sense that images of plants, animals, water, and the sky appear in art from every culture, serving as an ongoing source of inspiration and meaning.

CuratorialText: Depictions of landscapes can serve as valuable documentation of specific places, but they can also evoke strong emotions. Images of the natural world can remind us of the beauty and power of nature, evoking feelings of tranquility, wonder, awe, and contemplation.
persona2_adapted: Depictions of landscapes do more than simply record what a particular place looks like—they can also stir powerful emotions. Images of the natural world have the ability to remind us of nature’s beauty and force, prompting feelings of calm, curiosity, amazement, and reflection.

CuratorialText: From the earliest Japanese poetry anthology, compiled in the eighth century, to the present day, the beauty of the Japanese landscape has been celebrated in literature and art. At first, landscapes were not an independent subject but were incorporated into the backgrounds of Buddhist paintings, seasonal subjects (shikinami-e), and paintings of famous scenic or sacred sites (meisho-e). Beginning around the thirteenth century, possibly through the influence of the introduction of new modes of ink painting from China and Korea, landscape became an independent subject in Japanese painting. Landscapes of Japan were most often, but not exclusively, painted in the full color mode, which effectively expressed the seasonal changes that are inextricably linked with natural imagery in Japanese poetry and art. Ink painting was usually chosen for varied and dramatic landscapes of China, which most Japanese artists understood through their study of imported paintings, and later, of painting manuals as well. Whether rendered in rich, deep colors or in subtle tones of ink, landscapes by Japanese artists are imbued with a sense of intimate understanding and appreciation of the transient beauty and spiritual significance of the natural world.
persona2_adapted: From the earliest collection of Japanese poetry, compiled in the eighth century, to the present day, the beauty of Japan's landscapes has been celebrated in both literature and art. At first, landscapes were not treated as a subject on their own; instead, they appeared as backgrounds in Buddhist paintings, works depicting the changing seasons, and paintings of famous scenic or sacred places. Around the thirteenth century, possibly influenced by new ink-painting techniques introduced from China and Korea, landscape became an independent subject in Japanese painting. Japanese landscapes were most often—though not always—painted in full color, a style that effectively captured the seasonal changes so closely tied to natural imagery in Japanese poetry and art. Ink painting, on the other hand, was usually chosen for varied and dramatic Chinese landscapes, which most Japanese artists came to know through studying imported paintings and, later, painting manuals. Whether rendered in rich, deep colors or in subtle shades of ink, Japanese landscape paintings convey a deep sense of intimate understanding and appreciation for the fleeting beauty and spiritual meaning of the natural world.

CuratorialText: Travel to misty mountain valleys and foggy ocean beaches through the eyes of great Japanese artists from the sixteenth century on, accompanied by the sounds of the shakuhachi (bamboo flute), in this exploration of nature as an inspiration for Japanese art and music, guided by the virtuoso Kurahashi Yodo II and curator Frank Feltens.
persona2_adapted: Step into misty mountain valleys and foggy ocean beaches as seen through the eyes of great Japanese artists from the 1500s onward. In this exploration, you’ll discover how nature has inspired both Japanese art and music, guided by the virtuoso (master performer) Kurahashi Yodo II and curator Frank Feltens. The experience is accompanied by the sounds of the shakuhachi, a traditional bamboo flute.

CuratorialText: For Chinese scholar-officials, viewing landscape paintings not only provided aesthetic delight, but a landscape setting was often vested with meaning, which could range from providing a clue about the season of the painting to conveying more complex meanings. Seeing an image of figures in a remote landscape often referred to the key cultural and political concept of eremitism. The literati, or scholar class, believed in the Confucian notion that government service was a high moral duty. However, if the emperor was corrupt, or if a scholar was faced with serving a dynasty founded by usurping foreign rulers then, following the tradition of eremitism, a true gentleman refused government office. Instead, he became a mountain recluse, or at least assumed the demeanor of a hermit or rustic fisherman, to protest the corruption of moral order. Vested with meaning as a symbol of ethical reclusion, landscape motifs had poignant significance.
persona2_adapted:
For Chinese scholar-officials, looking at landscape paintings was more than just a visual pleasure—it was a way to understand deeper ideas. The setting of a landscape could hint at simple things, like the season, but it could also carry more complex messages. For example, when a painting showed people in a remote, mountainous area, it often referred to a key cultural and political idea called eremitism—the practice of withdrawing from society as a form of protest.

The scholar class, known as the literati, followed Confucian teachings, which held that serving the government was a high moral duty. However, if the emperor was corrupt, or if a scholar was asked to serve a dynasty founded by foreign invaders who had seized power, then a true gentleman would refuse that role. Following the tradition of eremitism, he would instead become a mountain recluse, or at least take on the appearance of a hermit or a simple fisherman, as a way of protesting the breakdown of moral order. Because of this, landscape elements in paintings were filled with meaning—they stood for ethical withdrawal and carried deep emotional weight.

CuratorialText: Careful observation of nature did not only translate to depictions of real places. Artists used their knowledge of the natural world to create imagined landscapes as well.
persona2_adapted: Artists didn't just use their close study of nature to paint real locations. They also drew on their understanding of the natural world to invent entirely fictional landscapes.

CuratorialText: A full appreciation of Chinese art includes embracing the category of “scholar’s rocks,” alternatively called “spirit stones.”  Scholar’s rocks consist of wondrously shaped and textured rocks, often of limestone, that are collected for placement in a designed garden or are brought indoors for display in a scholar’s study. The size can range from twelve feet or more to tiny enough to be enclosed in a fist. Philosophic and religious texts as well as poetry have a long history of singling out mountains and rocks for special consideration. A saying from the time of Confucius (551 BCE–479 BCE) notes, “The wise take joy in water; the humane take joy in mountains.” At the same time, Daoists pictured mountains as sanctuaries or paradises for immortal beings and used minerals in elixirs for infinite life. Placement of evocative rocks in gardens and homes helped turn a mundane space into a paradise, or at least into an environment conducive to cultivating personal longevity or to the pursuit of eternal life.
persona2_adapted: A full appreciation of Chinese art includes understanding the category of "scholar's rocks," also known as "spirit stones." These are unusually shaped and textured rocks, often made of limestone, collected either for display in a designed garden or indoors in a scholar's study. Their size can range from over twelve feet tall to small enough to fit in a fist. For centuries, philosophical and religious writings, as well as poetry, have given special attention to mountains and rocks. A saying from the time of Confucius (551–479 BCE) observes, "The wise take joy in water; the humane take joy in mountains." Meanwhile, Daoists viewed mountains as sanctuaries or paradises for immortal beings and used minerals in elixirs meant to grant eternal life. Placing evocative rocks in gardens and homes helped transform an ordinary space into a paradise—or at least into an environment that encouraged personal longevity or the pursuit of eternal life.

CuratorialText: In Chinese tradition, the educated class delighted in rocks as tangible embodiments of the powers of creation and cosmic transformation, and they sometimes rhapsodized about them as eternally faithful “friends.” Gazing upon them could encourage imaginary travel deep into nature. Chinese scholars, poets, painters, and emperors all engaged in collecting rocks, and painters often studied the rocks they collected, treating them as both muse and model for their landscape paintings.
persona2_adapted: In Chinese tradition, educated people treasured rocks as physical symbols of the forces that create and transform the universe. They sometimes spoke of them with great emotion, calling them eternally loyal “friends.” Looking at these rocks could inspire the mind to wander deep into nature, as if taking an imaginary journey. Scholars, poets, painters, and even emperors in China all collected rocks, and painters often studied the ones they owned, using them both as a source of inspiration and as a model for their landscape paintings.

CuratorialText: From intimate courtyards to monumental temple, tomb, and pleasure gardens, Asia has been central to the development of cultivated landscapes. The earliest known garden—the biblical Garden of Eden—may have been located in West Asia at the confluence of the Tigris and the Euphrates in present-day Iraq. The very word “paradise” is derived from the walled orchard gardens and hunting parks of ancient Iran, referred to as pardis. This type of enclosed garden became an integral part of Persian architectural landscape for centuries and also often included pavilions to provide shade and relief from the sun. According to written sources, the earliest gardens in China, dating to the Zhou period (ca. 1050–256 BCE), consisted of enclosed hunting grounds reserved for the royal elite. Chinese gardens were often characterized by carefully positioned rocks and pools intended to recreate microcosms of nature at large. In Japan, gardens followed a more naturalistic design and incorporated rolling hills and languid ponds to underline harmony between humans and their surroundings. In the arid landscapes of West and South Asia, one of the most common garden plans depended on a series of interconnected pools and axial watercourses.
persona2_adapted:
From small, private courtyards to grand temples, tombs, and pleasure gardens, Asia has played a central role in shaping how humans design and interact with nature. The earliest known garden—the biblical Garden of Eden—may have been located in West Asia, where the Tigris and Euphrates rivers meet in what is now Iraq. The very word "paradise" comes from the walled orchard gardens and hunting parks of ancient Iran, which were called *pardis*. This style of enclosed garden became a key feature of Persian architecture and landscape design for centuries, often including small buildings called pavilions that offered shade and relief from the sun.

According to written records, the earliest gardens in China date back to the Zhou period (around 1050–256 BCE). These were enclosed hunting grounds reserved for royalty and the elite. Chinese gardens often featured carefully placed rocks and pools, designed to create miniature versions of the natural world. In Japan, gardens followed a more natural-looking design, with rolling hills and calm ponds that emphasized harmony between people and their surroundings. In the dry landscapes of West and South Asia, one of the most common garden layouts relied on a series of connected pools and straight water channels.

CuratorialText: Throughout history, art has served as a reflection of and response to environmental change. Sometimes this change is deliberately brought about through human beings adapting to and altering their surrounding environment. Cultural landscapes—including the ways that humans have built on, altered, and imagined natural environments—play a significant role in how we understand the world we inhabit.
persona2_adapted: Throughout history, art has shown us how people both reflect on and respond to changes in the environment. Sometimes these changes are intentional, resulting from humans adapting to and reshaping the world around them. The idea of "cultural landscapes" helps us understand this: it refers to the ways people have built upon, transformed, and even imagined natural spaces. These landscapes are important because they shape how we make sense of the world we live in.

CuratorialText: Works of art can bear witness to the environmental changes that have taken place throughout recorded history. Depictions of animals from regions where those animals no longer exist, for example, can tell us about migrations or extinctions.
persona2_adapted: Works of art can serve as evidence of the environmental changes that have occurred throughout recorded history. For instance, images of animals from areas where those animals are no longer found can reveal information about their migrations or extinctions.

CuratorialText: Humans have always had an impact on the environments where they live, but the scale of that impact dramatically increased with industrialization. In the United States and Europe, factories and advances in transportation sprung up to create a range of consumer goods and ways to travel with new ease and speed. Largely coal powered, these machines spewed pollution that artists such as Whistler sought to visualize in paint.
persona2_adapted: Humans have always shaped the environments they inhabit, but the scale of that influence grew dramatically with industrialization. In the United States and Europe, factories and new transportation systems emerged, producing a wide range of consumer goods and making travel faster and easier. These machines were mostly powered by coal, and they released pollution that artists like Whistler tried to capture and represent in their paintings.

CuratorialText: Similarly, the infusion of Western technology into Japan from the beginning of the Meiji era (1868–1912) spurred widespread visual documentation of the new developments. Railroad transportation, telegraphic communication, gas-fueled lighting, steam-powered river and ocean vessels, and new materials and designs for bridges and buildings were crammed into the Japanese landscape over two decades.
persona2_adapted: Similarly, the introduction of Western technology into Japan from the start of the Meiji era (1868–1912) led to a broad effort to visually record these new developments. Over just two decades, the Japanese landscape was filled with railroads, telegraph systems, gas lighting, steam-powered ships for rivers and oceans, and new materials and designs for bridges and buildings.

CuratorialText: Contemporary artists are increasingly exploring the impact of climate change on the natural world and human communities, whether their goal is to raise awareness about the urgent need to address climate change, to highlight the social and political issues that are bound up in environmental degradation, or to envision alternative futures and inspire hope and action.
persona2_adapted: Contemporary artists are increasingly examining how climate change affects both the natural world and human communities. Some aim to raise awareness about the urgent need to address this crisis, while others focus on the social and political issues tied to environmental damage. Still, others imagine different possible futures, seeking to inspire hope and encourage action.

CuratorialText: The creation of art itself impacts and is impacted by the environment. Stones and precious metals need to be mined. Wood needs to be harvested. Clay is extracted from the earth. Plants need to be gathered. Many works of art make use of animal products, such as parchment, feathers, horn, and ivory. Different production techniques require burning fuel sources for melting, firing, and glazing. The gathering of raw materials and their transformation into art objects is not without impact on the environment and has at times come at the expense of the exploitation of natural resources and people.
persona2_adapted: The process of making art both affects and is affected by the natural world. Stones and precious metals must be taken from mines. Wood has to be cut down. Clay is dug from the ground. Plants need to be collected. Many artworks use materials that come from animals, such as parchment (treated animal skin for writing or painting), feathers, horn, and ivory. Different methods of production require burning fuels to melt metals, fire clay, or apply glazes. Gathering these raw materials and turning them into art objects has a real effect on the environment, and at times this has come at the cost of overusing natural resources and exploiting people.

CuratorialText: While recycling may seem like a very recent phenomenon, materials have long been reused in the creation of works of art, whether due to the scarcity of materials or to the value placed on an existing object. For example, since jade was such a prestigious medium and a valuable commodity in ancient China, broken or out-of-fashion articles were not discarded but were instead recycled to make new objects. This appears to be especially true in the late Shang dynasty (ca. 1250–1050 BCE). Archaeologists working at the late Shang capital of Anyang have found that, possibly due to the scarcity of resources at a time of high demand, earlier Stone Age (ca. 5000–1700 BCE) objects were being transformed into decorated jewelry and other items that held more interest and meaning to the Shang people. All three pendants shown here were made by cutting up a special kind of Neolithic square tube called a cong, which had lost its ritual significance by Shang times. It is noteworthy that the recycled cong used here represent different Stone Age cultures dispersed across north and south China.
persona2_adapted: While recycling might seem like a modern idea, artists have actually reused materials for centuries—sometimes because new materials were hard to find, and sometimes because an existing object was already valued. In ancient China, for example, jade was a highly prized material, so broken or outdated items were not thrown away but were instead remade into new objects. This practice seems to have been especially common during the late Shang dynasty (around 1250–1050 BCE). Archaeologists working at the late Shang capital of Anyang have discovered that, possibly because resources were scarce at a time of high demand, earlier Stone Age objects (from about 5000–1700 BCE) were being turned into decorated jewelry and other items that held more meaning for the Shang people. The three pendants shown here were all made by cutting up a type of Neolithic square tube called a *cong*, which had lost its original ritual importance by Shang times. Interestingly, the recycled *cong* used here come from different Stone Age cultures spread across northern and southern China.

CuratorialText: Environmental conditions can significantly impact the preservation of works of art. Factors such as temperature, humidity, light, and pollution affect the chemical and physical properties of materials in different ways, causing damage and degradation to objects over time. On a larger scale, major environmental changes such as temperature shifts and more destructive weather patterns can threaten heritage sites around the world. Conservators play a pivotal role in safeguarding the objects in the museum’s collections. Read about conservation and scientific research initiatives at the National Museum of Asian Art. The Smithsonian Institution acknowledges climate change as the existential threat of our time. Learn more about the Smithsonian’s sustainability initiatives.
persona2_adapted: The environment around us can have a major effect on how well works of art hold up over time. Things like temperature, humidity, light, and pollution each interact with the materials in artworks in different ways, slowly causing them to break down or become damaged. On a broader scale, larger environmental changes—such as shifting temperatures and more extreme weather patterns—can put entire historic sites around the world at risk. Specialists known as conservators play a key role in protecting the objects in the museum’s collections. You can read more about conservation and scientific research efforts at the National Museum of Asian Art. The Smithsonian Institution views climate change as the most urgent challenge of our time. Learn more about the Smithsonian’s sustainability initiatives.

CuratorialText: A color of vitality, passion, good fortune, and celebration, red has undeniable energy. It catches our eye and stirs our blood, forcing us to take notice. From glittering stones to ruddy earth, from insects to minerals, learn about various ways that people have made and understood red through examples drawn from across the museum’s collections.
persona2_adapted: Red is a color full of life—it stands for energy, strong emotion, good luck, and celebration. It naturally grabs our attention and quickens our pulse, making us stop and look. In this exhibit, you’ll explore the many ways people have created and interpreted red, using materials as different as sparkling gems, reddish soil, insects, and minerals. The examples come from across the museum’s collections, showing how this powerful color has been used and understood in different times and places.

CuratorialText: Several metal compounds (that include iron, mercury, or lead) form commonly used historic colored pigments. These compounds may be found, mined, or produced. Iron compounds in earth pigments can have different colors. Red iron earths are readily found or can be manufactured and have been discovered in many applications, including pottery and paints.
persona2_adapted: Several metal-based substances—such as those containing iron, mercury, or lead—were commonly used in the past to create colored pigments. These substances could be found in nature, mined from the ground, or manufactured. For example, iron compounds found in earth pigments can produce a range of colors. Red iron earths, which occur naturally or can be made artificially, have been used in many different ways, including in pottery and paints.

CuratorialText: Similarly, mercury-based pigments are made from grinding the red mineral cinnabar. Synthetic versions have long been produced and are referred to as vermilion. This deep red pigment, though toxic, was used in burial ceremonies, incorporated in lacquers, and adorns paintings and documents.
persona2_adapted: Similarly, pigments made from mercury are created by grinding the red mineral cinnabar. For centuries, artificial versions of this pigment have also been manufactured, known as vermilion. Despite its toxicity, this rich red color was used in burial rituals, mixed into lacquers, and applied to paintings and manuscripts.

CuratorialText: While red lead has a naturally forming mineral equivalent (minimum), it was manufactured far more commonly for use as a pigment. In general, lead compounds were heated under conditions favorable to produce the desired colored material and minimizing the formation of others.
persona2_adapted: While red lead does occur naturally as a mineral (called minimum), it was much more often produced artificially for use as a coloring material. Typically, lead compounds were heated under specific conditions that encouraged the creation of the desired color while limiting the formation of other, unwanted substances.

CuratorialText: Red copper oxide or cuprite is found as a degradation product on bronzes and forms by the reaction of the copper from the bronze and the atmosphere.
persona2_adapted: Red copper oxide, also known as cuprite, is a substance that forms when the copper in bronze reacts with the air. It appears as a type of corrosion, or natural chemical change, on bronze objects over time.

CuratorialText: Plants and animals can also be sources of reds used in artworks. Madder root and safflower each may have their color extracted, purified, and prepared for use in objects such as textiles and prints. The conditions in which the dye or lake is prepared can change the final color. Scale insects (such as lac and cochineal) are another source. Such insects were harvested, with the red color extracted and prepared for use as a pigment or dye. When possible, determining the colorants used in artworks can shed light on historical trade routes due to the limited native habitat of these insects.
persona2_adapted: Plants and animals can also provide the reds used in artworks. For example, the roots of the madder plant and the petals of safflower can be processed to extract their color, which is then purified and prepared for use in objects like textiles and prints. The way this dye or pigment is prepared can affect the final shade. Certain scale insects, such as those that produce lac and cochineal, are another source. These insects were collected, and their red color was extracted and turned into a pigment or dye. When possible, identifying the specific colorants used in artworks can reveal information about historical trade routes, since these insects lived only in certain regions.

CuratorialText:
Dried cochineal insects. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution
Red also occurred naturally in the form of precious and semi-precious stones, such as rubies, garnets, spinels, and carnelians. Prized for their color and rarity, they were valuable gifts and were often worn by elites or used to adorn luxury items.
persona2_adapted:
Dried cochineal insects. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution

Red also appeared in nature as precious and semi-precious stones, including rubies, garnets, spinels, and carnelians. Valued for their vivid color and scarcity, these stones were given as important gifts, worn by the upper classes, or used to decorate high-status objects.

CuratorialText: Rubrication, the practice of writing portions of a text in red ink, helped visually underscore certain information and became particularly common in European manuscripts after the seventh century. Red was sometimes used for the angular Kufic script in in copies of the Qur’an made between the eighth and eleventh centuries CE to indicate vocalization; it stood out next to black- or brown-inked calligraphy. Red was also commonly used in the paste for the inked seals that were added to finished works of Chinese painting or calligraphy; it contrasted against black ink writing and images. The seal might give the name of the artist or his studio, the collector, or other private individuals, and it occasionally expresses a favorite brief saying.
persona2_adapted: In European manuscripts after the seventh century, scribes began using red ink for certain parts of the text—a practice called rubrication—to help emphasize important information. Similarly, in copies of the Qur’an made between the eighth and eleventh centuries CE, red was sometimes used for a specific angular style of writing known as Kufic script, in order to show how words should be pronounced. This red stood out clearly against the black or brown ink used for the main calligraphy. Red also appeared in the paste used for the inked seals that were added to finished Chinese paintings or calligraphy works. These seals, which contrasted with the black ink of the writing and images, might include the name of the artist or his studio, the collector, or other private individuals. Occasionally, they would also feature a short, favorite saying.

CuratorialText: Love, passion, importance—red carries many positive associations today, and has throughout history. In ancient Egypt, pharaohs were depicted in red due to its association with royalty and passage through the afterlife. In China, red is auspicious—associated with life-generating energy (the sun, blood, and fire)—and is the color of celebrations and prosperity.
persona2_adapted: Red has long been linked to ideas like love, passion, and importance—and these meanings stretch back through history. In ancient Egypt, pharaohs were shown wearing or surrounded by red because the color was tied to royalty and the journey into the afterlife. In China, red is considered lucky or favorable. It is connected to life-giving forces such as the sun, blood, and fire, and it remains the color of celebrations and good fortune.

CuratorialText: Red is still associated with festivities and happiness in Chinese culture today. Learn more about Lunar New Year, when families decorate windows with red paper cuttings and adorn doors with couplets expressing auspicious wishes. Watch and learn how Chinese paper cuttings are made, from the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.
persona2_adapted: Red continues to carry strong associations with celebration and joy in Chinese culture today. Explore the traditions of Lunar New Year, a time when families decorate their windows with red paper cuttings and hang couplets on their doors—poetic phrases that express hopeful wishes for good fortune. You can also watch a demonstration of how these paper cuttings are made, presented by the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.

CuratorialText: Red can carry more negative associations as well, such as with warfare, rage, and destruction. The color of blood, there is a visceral quality to red that resonates uniquely with the human psyche. Haunted by the traces that the human body leaves behind, contemporary artist Chiharu Shiota’s 2015 installation “Over the Continents” amassed personal memories through an accumulation of nearly four hundred individual shoes. Collected by the artist, each shoe came with a note from the donor describing lost individuals and past moments. Almost four miles of red yarn wrapped around the shoes and attached to a single point in the museum’s Sackler pavilion, evoking the artist’s attention to the fundamental ties that connect humans to one another and the world.
persona2_adapted: Red can also carry more negative meanings, such as war, rage, and destruction. As the color of blood, it has a powerful, gut-level effect on the human mind that is unlike any other color. Haunted by the marks the human body leaves behind, contemporary artist Chiharu Shiota created a 2015 installation called “Over the Continents.” In this work, she gathered personal memories by collecting nearly four hundred individual shoes. Each shoe came with a note from the person who donated it, describing lost loved ones or past moments. The artist then wrapped the shoes with almost four miles of red thread, connecting them all to a single point in the museum’s Sackler pavilion. This arrangement highlights the artist’s focus on the basic ties that link people to one another and to the world.

CuratorialText: Whether it’s in the form of growing plants, precious stones, or deliberately blended pigments, there’s a human impulse to surround ourselves with green in the art we create, the environments we inhabit, and the spaces we build. Green evokes fertile landscapes, growth and prosperity, and even health and wellness. Learn about how green has been made and understood across various cultures and traditions represented in the museum’s collections.
persona2_adapted: Whether it appears in the form of growing plants, precious stones, or carefully mixed paints, there is a human tendency to surround ourselves with green—in the art we make, the places we live, and the spaces we design. Green brings to mind fertile landscapes, growth and abundance, and even health and well-being. Discover how green has been created and interpreted across different cultures and traditions represented in the museum’s collections.

CuratorialText: While there aren’t many plant sources of green dyes in the art world, some Indian paintings use beetle wings to mimic shiny green emeralds. In very special works of art, emeralds could be carved to make unique objects, or were made into jewelry.
persona2_adapted: While green dyes derived from plants are relatively uncommon in art, certain Indian paintings achieve the effect of shimmering green emeralds by using beetle wings instead. In particularly rare or important works, actual emeralds might be carved into distinctive objects or set into jewelry.

CuratorialText: The green patina on copper and bronze objects occurs as the copper corrodes. Patina occurs naturally when copper comes into contact with salty or acidic surroundings. See, for instance, how a range of corrosion products affects the surface color of the ancient Chinese bronze ritual vessel below: most of the surface is covered with a smooth, tin oxide-stained pale green with copper compounds, but there are also numerous scattered small patches of dull red cuprite, and the bright, powdery green seen here and there represents copper chloride. Explore this object in 3D.
persona2_adapted: The green coating that forms on copper and bronze objects is the result of the metal corroding. This coating, called a patina, develops naturally when copper is exposed to salty or acidic environments. For example, look at the ancient Chinese bronze ritual vessel below: you can see how different types of corrosion create varied colors on its surface. Most of the surface has a smooth, pale green tint, stained by tin oxide and mixed with copper compounds. Scattered across it are small patches of dull red, caused by a copper mineral known as cuprite. And here and there, you’ll notice bright, powdery green spots, which come from copper chloride. You can explore this object in 3D.

CuratorialText: Copper corrosion products can be taken advantage of to create man-made copper green pigments; more commonly, however, the green mineral malachite can be ground to make paints. Malachite is often used in paintings in China.
persona2_adapted: Copper corrosion can be harnessed to produce artificial green pigments, but a more common method is to grind the green mineral malachite into paint. Malachite appears frequently in Chinese painting traditions.

CuratorialText:
Fine-ground malachite. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution
Copper, together with lead, is often the basis of green glazes in ceramics. Mixtures of copper with yellow particles of lead tin oxide or lead antimony oxide are also used for green enamels.
persona2_adapted:
Fine-ground malachite. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution

Copper, often combined with lead, is a common ingredient in the green glazes used on ceramics. To create green enamels—a type of glassy coating—copper is also mixed with yellow particles, such as lead tin oxide or lead antimony oxide.

CuratorialText: “Celadon” refers to a large family of high-fired ceramics. Their glazes are colored by small percentages of iron oxide. When fired in an oxygen-deprived, or reducing, atmosphere, the glazes turn variable shades of bluish green, traditionally described with the French word céladon. The word is commonly believed to have been derived from a seventeenth-century French drama, L’Astrée, in which a shepherd named Céladon appears dressed in a gray-green robe. Some modern scholars prefer the term “green ware,” but the poetic French name is more likely to remind a reader that celadon ceramics can exhibit a broad range of colors, from sky blue to sea green.
persona2_adapted: "Celadon" describes a broad category of ceramics that are fired at very high temperatures. Their glazes get their color from small amounts of iron oxide. When these pieces are fired in a kiln with little oxygen—a process called a reducing atmosphere—the glazes turn into various shades of bluish green. This color range is traditionally referred to by the French word *céladon*. The term is thought to come from a seventeenth-century French play, *L’Astrée*, where a shepherd named Céladon wears a gray-green robe. While some modern scholars prefer the more straightforward term "green ware," the poetic French name better reminds us that celadon ceramics can range widely in color, from sky blue to sea green.

CuratorialText: Jade was the first true luxury medium to emerge in ancient China. Although the term is used to refer to a range of hard, beautifully figured stones, most Chinese Neolithic, Bronze Age, and early imperial period examples have been scientifically identified as nephrite, geologically different from the bright, apple green jadeite used in much later times. Composed of interlocked, fibrous crystals and occurring as pebbles and boulders in a range of colors (due to iron, chromium, manganese, or similar materials fused in the rock), nephrite is an incredibly hard stone. It cannot be carved or even scratched by metal but instead must be ground into shape using quartz and other tough minerals. These qualities and its rarity inspired ancient Chinese people to value it very highly and to reserve its use for special purposes, such as for jewelry and ceremonial and ritual objects.
persona2_adapted: Jade was the first truly luxurious material to appear in ancient China. While the word "jade" is often used to describe several types of hard, beautifully patterned stones, most examples from China's Neolithic period, Bronze Age, and early imperial era have been scientifically identified as nephrite. This is a different mineral from the bright, apple-green jadeite that became popular much later. Nephrite is made up of tightly interlocked, fibrous crystals and is found as pebbles and boulders in a variety of colors—caused by traces of iron, chromium, manganese, or similar elements fused within the rock. It is an extremely hard stone. It cannot be carved or even scratched by metal tools; instead, it must be shaped by grinding it with quartz and other tough minerals. Because of these qualities and its rarity, ancient Chinese people valued nephrite very highly and reserved it for special uses, such as jewelry and objects for ceremonies and rituals.

CuratorialText: According to his close friend Agnes Meyer, when Charles Lang Freer was ill near the end of his life, he often held a piece of jade in his hand, believing with an almost religious faith in the comforting and restorative powers of the stone. Freer collected ancient Chinese jades in large numbers, when most other Western collectors showed little interest. Ultimately, there were more than 500 pieces in his collection, spanning more than 5,000 years.
persona2_adapted: According to his close friend Agnes Meyer, near the end of his life, when Charles Lang Freer was ill, he often held a piece of jade in his hand. He believed—with an almost religious conviction—that the stone had the power to comfort and restore him. Freer amassed a large collection of ancient Chinese jades at a time when most other Western collectors showed little interest in them. In the end, his collection included more than 500 pieces, spanning over 5,000 years of history.

CuratorialText: Green is so prevalent in the flora of the natural world that the color easily conjures lush environments teeming with plants. Life, growth, and regeneration are all concepts that come to mind in the context of verdant landscapes. In Southeast Asia, during the ninth through the thirteenth century, constructing cities required carving out spaces from the verdant, tropical environment. The jungle had to be systematically cultivated; water had to be diverted into reservoirs, moats, and canals; and the landscape had to be carefully tended. When population centers shifted due to political, ecological, and religious changes, the tropical landscape readily encroached on the built environment, transforming once urban landscapes back into jungle. This environment informed Southeast Asian artists: on temple walls and sculptures, plants and flowers grow on thick stalks, vines curlicue, and sprays of foliage transform surfaces into lush landscapes.
persona2_adapted: Green is so common in the plants of the natural world that the color easily brings to mind rich environments full of vegetation. Life, growth, and renewal are all ideas that come to mind when we think of lush, green landscapes. In Southeast Asia, between the ninth and thirteenth centuries, building cities meant carving out spaces from the dense, tropical environment. The jungle had to be carefully managed; water had to be redirected into reservoirs, moats, and canals; and the landscape had to be constantly maintained. When population centers shifted due to political, ecological, or religious changes, the tropical landscape quickly reclaimed the built areas, turning once-urban spaces back into jungle. This environment shaped the work of Southeast Asian artists: on temple walls and sculptures, plants and flowers grow on thick stems, vines twist in spirals, and bursts of leaves turn surfaces into lush, plant-filled scenes.

CuratorialText: Set in a garden, the image depicts a group of angels, referred to as huris or paris in Persian, entertaining their queen by playing music, dancing, and serving food and wine. The drawing's lively mood echoes other Persian representations of outdoor banquets or bazm (a form of celebration with wine and music) ceremonies, with the exception that this fine drawing features heavenly creatures--a favorite subject matter in sixteenth-and seventeenth-century Iran and Turkey--instead of the princely figures seen in illustrations to lyrical poetry by Hafiz and Assar.
persona2_adapted: Set in a garden, the image shows a group of angels—called *huris* or *paris* in Persian—entertaining their queen with music, dancing, food, and wine. The lively mood of the drawing resembles other Persian scenes of outdoor banquets or *bazm* (a type of celebration involving wine and music). However, this fine drawing stands out because it features heavenly beings instead of the royal figures typically found in illustrations of love poetry by Hafiz and Assar. Such angelic subjects were especially popular in sixteenth- and seventeenth-century Iran and Turkey.

CuratorialText: Referred to as peri winged celestial creatures are among some of the most popular subjects in sixteenth-century Ottoman and Persian drawings and paintings. The exquisitely rendered composition typifies a distinct style of Ottoman drawing that combines sweeping, calligraphic lines with soft, feathery strokes, as is evident in the minute designs on the figure's robe, cup, and bottle. Light washes of color add a sense of volume and texture to the form of this heavenly being.
persona2_adapted: In sixteenth-century Ottoman and Persian art, winged celestial beings—often called *peri*—were among the most popular subjects in drawings and paintings. This beautifully detailed composition is a classic example of a distinct Ottoman style, which blends sweeping, calligraphic lines with soft, feathery strokes. You can see this especially in the tiny patterns on the figure’s robe, cup, and bottle. Light washes of color give the heavenly being a sense of shape and texture, making it feel more three-dimensional.

CuratorialText: Four-part inro in form of flattened rectangle with rounded corners (probably derived from the coin called ryo). The inset rim of each of the three compartments fits into the base of the compartment above it or into the lid. The four components are secured by a blue-green silk cord passing through two holes in each of the elements, tied in a bow below and secured by the bead (ojime) above and atttached to the toggle (netsuke) at the end.
persona2_adapted: This small, four-part container (called an *inro*) is shaped like a flattened rectangle with rounded corners—a form likely inspired by a type of coin known as a *ryo*. Each of its three inner compartments has a raised edge that fits snugly into the base of the one above it, or into the lid. The four pieces are held together by a blue-green silk cord that passes through two holes in each section. The cord is tied in a bow at the bottom, secured by a small bead (called an *ojime*) above, and attached to a carved toggle (known as a *netsuke*) at the end.

CuratorialText: Box, small cylindrical, with cover of equal size with the box proper. Clay: hard, grayish. Glaze: pale greenish-gray celadon, with faint lilac tints. Decoration: inlaid in white and black under glaze: medallion of two flying cranes surrounded by geometric borders.
persona2_adapted: This small cylindrical box comes with a lid that matches the body in size. It is made of hard, grayish clay and covered in a pale greenish-gray glaze with faint hints of lilac. The decoration is created using an inlay technique, where white and black patterns are set into the clay before the glaze is applied. The main design features a medallion showing two flying cranes, surrounded by geometric borders.

CuratorialText: A straight-sided cup which angles inward about three-quarters of the way toward a flattened bottom. The walls of the cup are very thin and this type of ware has been referred to as egg-shell ware. The fabric is a pinkish beige color. The inside of the cup is unpainted. The details on the outside of the cup are painted in brownish-black and red on an upainted background. The same motif of a red lotus bud shape outlined in brownish-black and tied with ribbons of the same brownish-black color is repeated seven times around the cup. The ornament color is repeated seven times around the cup. The ornament extends from the edge of the cup to about the middle of the cup. Within the cup are remains of a black powdery substance.
persona2_adapted:
This cup has straight sides that gently angle inward about three-quarters of the way down to a flattened base. Its walls are exceptionally thin—so much so that this style of pottery has sometimes been called "egg-shell ware." The material itself is a pinkish beige color.

The inside of the cup is left unpainted. On the outside, the decoration is painted in brownish-black and red against the natural, unpainted surface. A single design—a red lotus bud shape outlined in brownish-black and tied with ribbons of the same dark color—is repeated seven times around the cup. This ornament runs from the rim down to about the middle of the cup.

Inside the cup, traces of a black, powdery substance remain.

CuratorialText: This is one of several paintings of shop fronts that Whistler completed while in St. Ives. Sympathetic critics singled it out for enthusiastic praise when Whistler exhibited it in London in 1884. One declared, it "is probably the most perfect little thing of its kind that was ever wrought by an artist who has learnt how to see….It is without detail, without apparent labour, without dramatic interest; but it is exquisite in colour, faultless in tone, and its well-considered mystery has, at least, the interest of suggestiveness."
persona2_adapted: This is one of several paintings of shop fronts that Whistler completed while in St. Ives. Supportive critics singled it out for enthusiastic praise when Whistler exhibited it in London in 1884. One declared that it "is probably the most perfect little thing of its kind that was ever wrought by an artist who has learnt how to see….It is without detail, without apparent labour, without dramatic interest; but it is exquisite in colour, faultless in tone, and its well-considered mystery has, at least, the interest of suggestiveness."

CuratorialText:
Plate with a flat rim and peacock in the center. Clay: buff-colored. Glaze: transparent over blue, green, red and black painted over a white paste. Decoration: Peacock amidst flowers in the center with a scroll of half-palmettes on the rim; eight blossoms on the exterior.
Greek letters on label: (^o^) and No. 48.
persona2_adapted: This plate has a flat rim and features a peacock at its center. It is made from buff-colored clay, with a transparent glaze that covers painted decorations in blue, green, red, and black, applied over a white paste. The central design shows a peacock surrounded by flowers, while the rim is decorated with a scrolling pattern of half-palmettes—a motif resembling half of a fan-shaped leaf. On the outside of the plate, there are eight blossoms. A label on the piece includes Greek letters and the number 48.

CuratorialText: Molded Yue-ware vessels like this were made in large quantities, but their function is unclear. They may have held a candle, or perhaps scholars dipped their writing brushes in the water-filled interior. In the Jin dynasty (265-420 CE), mythical lions, with exaggeratedly curled manes, and winged chimeras were protective images; huge stone examples guarded important tombs.
persona2_adapted: Molded Yue-ware vessels like this one were produced in large numbers, but their exact purpose remains uncertain. They might have held a candle, or perhaps scholars used the water-filled interior to rinse their writing brushes. During the Jin dynasty (265-420 CE), mythical lions—with their highly curled manes—and winged chimeras served as protective symbols; large stone versions of these creatures stood guard at important tombs.

CuratorialText: A pair of blue and white molded porcelain beaker vases [F1992.47.1 and .2] decorated with freely drawn feather, foliate scroll and scattered flowerheads, each with a leaf or conch shell "emblem" mark on base. Part of a five-piece garniture set with three jars.
persona2_adapted: A pair of blue-and-white porcelain vases, shaped like tall, flared drinking cups and made from molded clay. Their surfaces are decorated with loosely painted feathers, curling leaf patterns, and scattered flower blossoms. On the bottom of each vase is a small mark shaped like a leaf or a conch shell—a type of symbol or "emblem" used by the artist or workshop. These vases were originally part of a five-piece decorative set, which also included three jars.

CuratorialText:
The Kiyotaki Waterfall is located along the route of the Tokaido, the great road passing through the eastern provinces of Japan and linking the city of Edo (now Tokyo), seat of the Tokugawa shogunate, with Kyoto, the capital and home of the emperor.
In this print from the series, Shokoku taki meguri (Famous Waterfalls in Various Provinces), the Kiyotaki Waterfall descends in branching rivulets over a sheer, rock face. In the foreground, pilgrims ascend from a teahouse toward a shrine dedicated to Kannon, a Buddhist deity associated with compassion. The remoteness and altitude of this scene is emphasized by the bands of clouds at the lower left.
persona2_adapted:
The Kiyotaki Waterfall lies along the route of the Tokaido, the great highway that ran through Japan’s eastern provinces, connecting Edo (now Tokyo)—the seat of the Tokugawa military government—with Kyoto, the imperial capital and home of the emperor.

This print is part of a series called *Shokoku taki meguri* (Famous Waterfalls in Various Provinces). Here, the waterfall cascades in branching streams down a steep, bare rock face. In the foreground, pilgrims climb up from a teahouse toward a shrine dedicated to Kannon, a Buddhist figure representing compassion. The scene’s sense of remoteness and high altitude is reinforced by the bands of clouds in the lower left corner.

CuratorialText: Small dish with pleated rim. Clay: buff pottery. Glaze: transparent glaze with olive greenish tone (Ofuke glaze), large irregular cracks stained brown; glaze deeper green in tone where thick. Interior of footrim glazed; glazed wiped off outside of footrim and surrounding base. Decoration: pleating on narrow everted rim. The form of this dish with fluted rim distantly echoes the shape of Chinese Longquan-ware celadon dishes.
persona2_adapted: Small dish with a ruffled edge. Made from light brown clay. The surface is covered in a clear glaze with an olive-green tint—this type of glaze is called Ofuke glaze. The glaze has large, uneven cracks that have turned brown over time, and it looks deeper green in areas where it was applied more thickly. The inside of the dish's small base ring is glazed, but the glaze was wiped off the outside of that ring and the surrounding bottom. The decoration is the ruffled pattern along the narrow, outward-turned rim. The shape of this dish, with its grooved rim, loosely recalls the style of Chinese celadon dishes from the Longquan kilns.

CuratorialText:
The Khamsa, or Quintet, by Nizami ranks among the great masterpieces of Persian literature. The romance of Khosraw and Shirin, one of the five poems that constitute the Khamsa, narrates the adventures of the Sasanian king Khusraw Parviz and the Armenian princess Shirin. Attributed to the early fifteenth century, the Freer Khamsa is one of the earliest known illustrated copies of this work.
Khosraw at the Gate of Shirin's Castle is among the most poignantly expressive manuscript paintings of the fifteenth century. Displeased with Khosraw's behavior and his marriage to another woman, Shirin has withdrawn to her castle. Following the death of his queen, Khosraw repeatedly attempts to see Shirin, who finally yields to his request. Shielded by the royal umbrella and standing on precious brocades and carpets, the king gestures in supplication toward Shirin, who gazes down from a window in her castle. Although the text specifies that Khosraw visited Shirin at dawn in the dead of winter, the artist has situated the reconciliation in a lush garden setting softly illuminated by a waning moon. The elegant, mustachioed Khosraw in these paintings bears a striking resemblance to known depictions of the Timurid patron Baysunghur Mirza (1399-1434).
persona2_adapted:
The *Khamsa* (or *Quintet*) by Nizami is one of the great masterpieces of Persian literature. One of its five poems, the romance of Khosraw and Shirin, tells the story of the Sasanian king Khusraw Parviz and the Armenian princess Shirin. The Freer *Khamsa*, believed to date from the early 1400s, is among the earliest known illustrated copies of this work.

The painting *Khosraw at the Gate of Shirin's Castle* is one of the most emotionally powerful manuscript illustrations of the 1400s. Displeased with Khosraw’s behavior and his marriage to another woman, Shirin has withdrawn to her castle. After his queen dies, Khosraw tries repeatedly to see Shirin, who eventually agrees. Protected by the royal umbrella and standing on expensive brocades and carpets, the king gestures pleadingly toward Shirin, who looks down from a castle window. Although the story says Khosraw visited Shirin at dawn in the middle of winter, the artist has placed the reconciliation in a lush garden, softly lit by a waning moon. The elegant, mustachioed Khosraw in these paintings closely resembles known portraits of the Timurid royal patron Baysunghur Mirza (1399–1434).

CuratorialText: Set in the London home of Whistler's brother-in-law Seymour Haden, this painting was originally titled The Morning Call. The woman reflected in the mirror at the left is Whistler's half sister Deborah Haden. The seated girl is his niece, Annie Haden. The standing woman, Miss Isabella Boott, was connected to the Haden family by marriage. Instead of trying to create a convincing illusion of three-dimensional space, Whistler adopted a color scheme and sharply angled perspective that emphasize the flatness of the picture plane.
persona2_adapted: Set in the London home of Whistler's brother-in-law, Seymour Haden, this painting was originally called *The Morning Call*. The woman reflected in the mirror on the left is Whistler's half-sister, Deborah Haden. The seated girl is his niece, Annie Haden. The standing woman, Miss Isabella Boott, was connected to the Haden family through marriage. Rather than trying to create a realistic sense of depth, Whistler used a carefully chosen color palette and a sharply tilted viewpoint that draw attention to the painting's flat surface.

CuratorialText: Popular tales and lively images played a role in transmitting beliefs and values to the Jain community. The Shripalarasa is a narrative that recounts the wondrous adventures of the Jain Prince Shripala, who travels to distant lands in search of trade and wealth. In this unusually large and vivacious painting, the artist represents the Prince's ship as a British vessel. The inscriptions scattered across the painting, which repetively identify parts of the ship, indicate a fascination with seafaring. The depiction of the Union Jack lends immediacy to a story that took place long before the British came to India.
persona2_adapted: Popular stories and lively pictures helped pass down beliefs and values within the Jain community. The *Shripalarasa* is a tale that follows the amazing adventures of the Jain Prince Shripala, who travels to faraway lands in search of trade and wealth. In this unusually large and energetic painting, the artist shows the Prince's ship as a British vessel. Notes written across the painting, which repeatedly label parts of the ship, suggest a strong interest in seafaring. The presence of the Union Jack makes a story feel immediate and real, even though it takes place long before the British arrived in India.

CuratorialText: Funeral vase: tall, slender, on hollowed, spreading feet. Wood stand. Clay: soft, light-weight, gray-white. Glaze: thin, transparent green celadon; close brown crackle. Areas of stain and of over-baked wrinkled glaze. Decoration: hieratic figures and a dragon supporting the sun-disk - in applied relief, under glaze.
persona2_adapted: This tall, slender vase stands on a hollowed, flared base, which is set into a wooden stand. It is made of a soft, lightweight, gray-white clay.
