This cylindrical drinking cup is the masterpiece of a Maya vase painter known today as the Metropolitan Master. It contains one of the finest surviving portraits of a god from the Classic Maya period. The young rain god, named Chahk, is shown mid-stride, lifting his left foot and extending his right leg forward, with his toes gracefully pointed. The underside of each leg is marked with a scale pattern, suggesting a shimmering, wet aquatic creature. He wears a complex loincloth made of knotted cotton, typical of his costume, and the back panel twists in a way that seems to reference a fish tail.

Chahk’s necklace is quite unusual: the collar is made of dangling eyeballs, and the chest piece is shaped like an upside-down water jar marked with the hieroglyph for darkness, with what looks like a small serpent emerging from its mouth. His other jewelry—on his ankles and wrists—may be jade or another precious material, and his headdress is a wild tangle of water plants. The typical shell earrings accent a barbel (a fleshy whisker-like growth) extending from his nostril and projecting from his chin. The artist’s brushstrokes emphasize his human-like qualities: the distinguished profile of his face, the point of his elbow, his ankle joints, and even his fingernails and toenails.

In his right hand, he grips the wooden handle of a shining stone axe, and in his left hand, he holds a stone that seems alive. The rain god actively engages with a giant, jawless creature, likely representing a *witz*—the spirit of a mountain. His leg crosses in front of the creature’s upper lip, while his left arm passes behind it. It is as if the rain personified must perform a ritual combat dance with an animate mountain to set the events shown here in motion. The mountain monster has a feathered eyelid (a feature seen on crocodilians in Maya art), a jagged tooth, and liquid or vegetation spilling out onto the ground at Chahk’s feet. The mountain’s head is covered with grape-like markings that signify it is a stony place.

Most significant about this animal-like mountain is the character reclining on top of it: a supernatural baby jaguar. The baby jaguar’s face is clearly that of a god, and it contrasts sharply with Chahk’s more human-like appearance. The square eye is a marker of divinity in Maya art, and the overbite with a shark-like tooth also indicates that this reclining figure is a peer of Chahk and the sun god, both of whom are sometimes shown with this protruding tooth. The ears, hands, feet, and tail are masterfully painted to look like those of a jaguar. In fact, all signs point to this creature being an infant jaguar, known from other portrayals of the same reclining infant and from the hieroglyphic symbol found in some royal names. The baby jaguar has a similar knotted hairstyle and plant-like headdress as Chahk, and it flails about as if restlessly searching for stability.

Almost touching the baby jaguar is a frightening creature of the night. It has a skeletal head marked with bone sutures and two protruding eyeballs, an insect-like shell, the swollen belly of a corpse, and spindly legs with knobby knees. This is likely a death god, a resident of the Maya underworld who plays a role in the myth of the baby jaguar’s birth. He dances too, lifting his left foot and extending his right. His outstretched arms hold tense hands that seem to reach for the baby jaguar or the hieroglyphic caption hovering above him. The death god wears an elaborate back-rack made of textiles, bone elements, and protruding eyeballs.

The death god has two creepy companions. Floating above the ground is a firefly, shown as a skeletal cyclops with a single central eye in the form of *akbal*, the hieroglyph for darkness. Three protruding eyeballs crown its head, and the artist delicately rendered its insect-like hind legs and abdomen. It holds a cigar or torch—a Maya artistic convention for signifying a creature of the night: the light of a cigar being smoked in the darkness mimics the natural flickering of mating bioluminescence in lightning bugs. Below the firefly is a mischievous dog with a spotted tail and ears. It pants behind the dancing death god and raises up on its hind legs as if begging for food or playing.

The text made up of eight hieroglyph blocks floating above the baby jaguar is unclear in meaning. The owner is named with a royal title, "k’uhul chatan winik," used during the Classic Period in certain places. The overarching theme of this vessel is the necessary interaction between life-giving rains and rotting death—the contrasts needed to produce life, represented here by the baby jaguar god. The death god and his companions are present because when organic matter decays and rains fall, life begins in that fertilized space. All of this occurs on top of a mythological mountain at the center of the Maya world. The wash of color used by the artist on the lower part of the vessel may represent the steamy breath that emerges from caves to create clouds and produce rain. Chahk, in all his glory, emerges from and interacts with the cave as he celebrates the birth of the baby jaguar. The death god’s pose seems to show that the baby jaguar was snatched from the clutches of death itself.

CuratorialText: Paired male and female figures are common subjects in the ceramics of the western Mexico peoples of Jalisco, Colima, and Nayarit. Named for the states in which the works have been discovered in quantity in deeply buried, multichambered shaft tombs, the ceramic sculptures show aspects of daily and ritual life, from ballgame representations to feasting ceremonies to deceptively simple family scenes. While subject matter was relatively consistent throughout the entire region, style distinctions existed. The figures here are in Nayarit's Ixtlán del Río style, which is characterized by thin, pliant limbs, and stout, cylindrical torsos. Gender differences center on attire and activity, but both sexes wear numerous ornaments on ears and nose. Here the male figure wears a conical hat, a shoulder cloth, and a loincloth. He holds a drum in his right hand and raised drumstick in the other. His companion wears a shoulder cloth and skirt, and her right arm is draped around his shoulder.
persona2_adapted:
A pair of male and female figures is a common subject in the ceramics made by ancient peoples from western Mexico, in the regions now known as Jalisco, Colima, and Nayarit. These clay sculptures get their name from the states where many of them have been found, buried deep inside multi-chambered tombs that were dug into the earth. The figures show scenes from both daily life and religious ceremonies—everything from ballgame players to feasting rituals to family moments that may look simple at first glance but carry deeper meaning.

Although the subject matter was fairly consistent across the region, the style of the figures varied from place to place. The two figures shown here are in the Ixtlán del Río style from Nayarit. This style is known for thin, flexible-looking limbs and short, cylindrical bodies. The differences between male and female figures are mainly in what they wear and what they are doing, but both men and women wear many ornaments on their ears and noses. In this piece, the male figure wears a pointed hat, a cloth over one shoulder, and a loincloth. He holds a drum in his right hand and a raised drumstick in the other. His companion wears a shoulder cloth and a skirt, and her right arm is draped around his shoulder.

CuratorialText: This Wari bottle seamlessly merges the body of an anthropomorphized feline with that of a flask-like vessel. Combining in-the-round, low-relief, and painted elements, this ceramic is both visually and technically intricate. The depth of its earthy tones and its balance of volumes brilliantly illustrate the ability of Wari artists to fuse Andean mastery of color and sculpture in the art of pottery. Standing upright with its arms against its body, the feline’s posture de-emphasizes its animality. The figure stares defiantly at the viewer with its eyes wide-open, a threatening stance amplified by its upright ears, gaping fanged mouth, and dotted eyebrows. The animal’s paws are partially humanized, depicted with digits rather than claws and topped with a grey wristband. The clawed fingers grab a fantastical creature, commonly known as the "humped animal" in scholarship (Menzel 1964; Knobloch 1983), on each side (one side is damaged, perhaps from prolonged exposure to salts in the ground). The two humped animals were painted in brick red with a yellow outline on raised roundels. This color scheme is reversed on the two figures on the circumference of the bottle, whose elongated bodies fit closely the curvature of the vessel’s chamber. Geometric designs, such as dotted circles and rhombuses with appendages, complement the painted decoration of the vessel’s body. A row of orange, brick red, and grey chevrons crown the bottle’s spout, a feature common to Wari ceramics. The highly burnished orange slip (a suspension of clay mixed with water, often with added pigments) on this bottle provides a smooth and glossy background that accentuates the raised roundels and feline’s legs on the ceramic. The white highlights stand out on this surface, in particular the eyes, fangs, and claws of the multiple figures—lethal attributes that suggest that this object abounds with dangerous wildlife. By bringing together naturalistically-rendered animal features, hybrid supernatural figures, and abstract geometric motifs, this bottle invokes a complex microcosm of bodies and forms, which the viewer must examine from a distance as well as up-close. The imagery unfolds as the viewer handles, turns, and angles the vessel. This bottle comes from a looted elite burial from the Ingenio Valley in the Nasca drainage on the south coast of present-day Peru (Tello 1917; Menzel 1968). From around 600 to 1000 CE, the Wari ran an empire that expanded throughout most of the Central Andes, albeit with varying degrees of presence and control. Wari culture was born out of the interactions between the Huarpa, a local group of the central highlands in present-day Ayacucho, and the Nasca people on the South Coast. Its empire bridged multiple regions, cultures, and artistic practices. Indeed, this bottle features techniques and motifs inherited from the Nasca repertoire, from its polychromy to motifs such as the humped animal and the ventrally extended creature, but they are combined with elements taken from northern traditions, such as the modeled feline. An almost identical bottle was excavated at the site of Wilkawain, in the northern highlands of Peru (Paredes Olvera 2016: fig. 16). Archaeologists believe that it was imported there, possibly from the Wari heartland. Whether the bottles from Wilkawain and the Ingenio Valley were both made in the same workshop and then exported to different regions remains unknown. While other Wari bottles have a similarly flattened body, this model differs in that it has to be oriented sideways in order for the viewer to face the feline, adapting the form of the container to the figure. Although it is possible that this bottle was never used and only destined to be deposited in a burial, it could have once contained liquid to be poured on the ground or ingested, perhaps maize- or red peppercorn- beer (Sayre et al. 2012). The vessel’s small size suggests that it was meant for personal devotional practice. As one held this bottle featuring multiple powerful beings in one’s hands, the viewing experience would have evolved, especially as the bottle was tilted upside-down to pour liquid from the bottle’s spout. Would this physical manipulation have prompted in the user sensations of fear and respect for these entities, or instead a sense of control over them?

The design combines three-dimensional carving, shallow relief, and painted details, making the piece both visually striking and technically complex. The rich, earthy colors and balanced proportions show how Wari artists skillfully brought together Andean traditions of color and sculpture in their pottery.

Standing upright with its arms held against its body, the feline figure downplays its animal nature. These two humped animals were painted in brick red with a yellow outline on raised circular panels. A row of orange, brick red, and grey zigzag lines crowns the bottle’s spout, a common feature in Wari ceramics. CuratorialText: The imagery on this plate represents the earliest known depiction of a well-known episode from the story of Bahram Gur or Bahram V, the Sasanian king (r. 420 –438). The story seems to have been popular for centuries—both in Sasanian times and under the Islamic rule that followed—but was only recorded in the Shahnamah, or Book of Kings, by Firdausi in the early eleventh century A.D., centuries after this plate was created. The Shahnamah recounts how Bahram Gur, challenged to feats of archery by his favorite musician, Azadeh, changed a male gazelle to a female and a female to a male and pinned the hind leg of an animal to its ear. This plate directly refers to the story of Bahram Gur because the horns of the male have been shot off with an arrow, transforming his appearance into that of a female, while the two arrows embedded in the head of the female make her appear to have horns like those of a male gazelle. This plate was likely hammered into shape and then decorated to highlight its varied surface contours and colors, making its human and animal forms stand out in relief. It was partially gilded using a mixture of mercury and gold, which could be painted onto the surface. Large numbers of gold and silver vessels made during Sasanian rule in Iran and Mesopotamia have survived, demonstrating an ancient Near Eastern tradition that spread westward to the Hellenistic and Roman world, where vessels made of precious metals also became symbols of power and prestige. These objects, presumably official state products, were presented as royal gifts to persons of importance within Sasanian Iran and beyond its borders.

This story appears to have remained popular for centuries—both during the Sasanian period and under later Islamic rule—but it was only written down in the *Shahnamah*, or *Book of Kings*, by the poet Firdausi in the early eleventh century AD, hundreds of years after this plate was made.

The *Shahnamah* tells how Bahram Gur, challenged to a display of archery by his favorite musician, Azadeh, transformed a male gazelle into a female and a female into a male, and pinned the hind leg of an animal to its ear. This plate directly refers to that story: the male gazelle’s horns have been shot off with an arrow, changing its appearance to look like a female, while two arrows embedded in the female gazelle’s head make it appear to have horns like a male.

CuratorialText: This solid-cast sculpture is one of a pair of nearly identical images of a hero or a demon wearing the upturned boots associated with highland regions, his power enhanced by the mighty horns of the ibex on his head and the body and wings of a bird of prey draped around his shoulders. It was created at the time the first cities emerged in ancient Sumer. A new world view conceived of human figures in realistic terms, through accurate proportions and highly modeled forms with distinctive features. The blending of human and animal forms to visualize the supernatural world and perhaps to express shamanistic beliefs, however, is more characteristic of the contemporary arts of Proto-Elamite Iran, where a remarkable tradition of metalworking developed during this period.
persona2_adapted: This solid-cast sculpture is one of a pair of nearly identical figures depicting either a hero or a demon. The figure wears upturned boots, a style linked to highland regions, and its power is emphasized by the large horns of an ibex on its head, along with the body and wings of a bird of prey draped across its shoulders. The piece was created during the time when the first cities were emerging in ancient Sumer. At that period, a new way of seeing the world led artists to represent human figures more realistically—using accurate body proportions, highly detailed forms, and distinctive facial features. However, the combination of human and animal forms to represent the supernatural world—and possibly to express shamanistic beliefs—is more typical of the art from Proto-Elamite Iran, a contemporary culture. This region developed a remarkable tradition of metalworking during this same era.

CuratorialText: This fragmentary piece takes the form of the face of a roaring lion. It was found in a storeroom at Fort Shalmaneser, a royal building at Nimrud that was probably used to store booty and tribute collected by the Assyrians while on military campaign. During the final defeat of Assyria at the end of the seventh century B.C., the royal complexes at Nimrud were sacked and burned, and the lion was likely blackened through exposure to fire at this time. The flat back suggests that this piece may have originally been attached to a piece of wooden furniture such as the arm of a chair. The right cheek, eyebrows, and incised muzzle are detailed with incised dots and the eyes are deeply carved for now-lost semiprecious stone or colored glass inlays. A pattern of painted white triangles outlined with raised ribs is preserved on the right side at the back of the face, perhaps to represent a mane. Scenes of the Assyrian king hunting lions are depicted on royal reliefs from the Assyrian palaces. Because it shares the same subject matter, this piece has been attributed to an Assyrian style. Built by the Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II, the palaces and storerooms of Nimrud housed thousands of pieces of carved ivory. Most of the ivories served as furniture inlays or small precious objects such as boxes. While some of them were carved in the same style as the large Assyrian reliefs lining the walls of the Northwest Palace, the majority of the ivories display images and styles related to the arts of North Syria and the Phoenician city-states. Phoenician style ivories are distinguished by their use of imagery related to Egyptian art, such as sphinxes and figures wearing pharaonic crowns, and the use of elaborate carving techniques such as openwork and colored glass inlay. North Syrian style ivories tend to depict stockier figures in more dynamic compositions, carved as solid plaques with fewer added decorative elements. However, some pieces do not fit easily into any of these three styles. Most of the ivories were probably collected by the Assyrian kings as tribute from vassal states, and as booty from conquered enemies, while some may have been manufactured in workshops at Nimrud. The ivory tusks that provided the raw material for these objects were almost certainly from African elephants, imported from lands south of Egypt, although elephants did inhabit several river valleys in Syria until they were hunted to extinction by the end of the eighth century B.C.

This fragmentary piece is shaped like the face of a roaring lion. It was discovered in a storage room at Fort Shalmaneser, a royal building in the ancient city of Nimrud. This fort was likely used to hold the spoils of war and tribute that the Assyrians collected during their military campaigns. When the Assyrian Empire fell at the end of the seventh century B.C., the royal buildings at Nimrud were looted and set on fire. The lion’s darkened surface probably resulted from exposure to that fire.

The flat back of the piece suggests it was originally attached to a piece of wooden furniture, perhaps the arm of a chair. The right cheek, eyebrows, and the lines around the muzzle are decorated with small carved dots. The eye sockets are deeply carved, likely to hold precious stones or colored glass that have since been lost. On the right side, near the back of the face, a pattern of white triangles outlined with raised ridges has survived, possibly meant to represent a mane.

Scenes of Assyrian kings hunting lions appear on the stone carvings that decorated the walls of Assyrian palaces. Because this piece shares that same subject—a lion—it is considered to be in an Assyrian style. The palaces and storerooms at Nimrud, built by the Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II, once held thousands of carved ivory objects. Most of these ivories were used as decorative inlays on furniture or as small luxury items like boxes.

While some of these ivories were carved in a style similar to the large stone reliefs in the Northwest Palace, most of them show images and styles linked to the art of North Syria and the Phoenician city-states. Ivories in the Phoenician style are recognizable by their use of imagery borrowed from Egyptian art, such as mythical creatures called sphinxes and figures wearing the crowns of Egyptian pharaohs. They also feature elaborate carving techniques, including openwork (where parts of the design are cut out) and inlays of colored glass. North Syrian style ivories, on the other hand, tend to show figures that are stockier and placed in more active scenes. These are carved as solid panels with fewer added decorative details.

Still, some pieces don’t fit neatly into any of these three styles. Most of the ivories were probably collected by Assyrian kings as tribute from weaker states or as loot from conquered enemies, though a few may have been made in workshops at Nimrud itself. The ivory tusks used as raw material almost certainly came from African elephants, imported from lands south of Egypt. Elephants did once live in several river valleys in Syria, but they were hunted to extinction there by the end of the eighth century B.C.

CuratorialText: Of the many legacies left by the ancient civilizations of southern Mesopotamia, the invention of writing is paramount. At the end of the fourth millennium B.C., written language developed in the region, first as pictographs and then evolving into abstract forms called cuneiform. The pictographs, like the ones on this tablet, are called proto-cuneiform and were drawn in the clay with a pointed implement. Circular impressions alongside the pictographs represented numerical symbols. Cuneiform (meaning wedge-shaped) script was written by pressing a reed pen or stylus with a wedge-shaped tip into a clay tablet. Clay, when dried to a somewhat hardened state, made a fine surface for writing, and when fired the records written on it became permanent. Early writing was used primarily as a means of recording and storing economic information. This tablet most likely documents grain distributed by a large temple, although the absence of verbs in early texts makes them difficult to interpret with certainty. In addition to the writing that appears on this tablet, the imagery of the cylinder seal, which was incompletely impressed on both faces and the edges of the tablet before it was inscribed, also records information. This seal apparently has not survived. The seal impression depicts a male figure guiding two dogs on a leash and hunting or herding boars in a reed marsh. He is the so-called priest-king, a male figure who can be identified by his dress and pose. Here he appears in his role as the good shepherd who protects flocks from wild predators.
persona2_adapted:
Of the many achievements left by the ancient civilizations of southern Mesopotamia, the invention of writing is the most important. Around 3000 B.C., written language developed in this region, first as simple picture symbols and then evolving into more abstract forms known as cuneiform. The picture symbols on this tablet are called proto-cuneiform, and they were drawn into the clay using a pointed tool. Small circular marks next to the pictures represented numbers.

Cuneiform (meaning "wedge-shaped") writing was made by pressing a reed pen—a stylus with a wedge-shaped tip—into a clay tablet. When clay was dried until it became somewhat hard, it made an excellent surface for writing. If the clay was then fired (baked), the records written on it became permanent.

Early writing was mainly used to record and store economic information. This tablet most likely documents grain distributed by a large temple, though because early texts lack verbs, they are difficult to interpret with complete certainty.

In addition to the writing on this tablet, the image from a cylinder seal also records information. This seal was rolled across both faces and the edges of the tablet before the writing was added, but it appears the seal itself has not survived. The seal impression shows a male figure guiding two dogs on a leash and hunting or herding wild boars in a reed marsh. This figure is known as the priest-king, a male character who can be identified by his clothing and posture. Here, he appears in his role as the good shepherd—someone who protects flocks from wild predators.

CuratorialText:
In ancient Mesopotamia, a cylinder-shaped seal could be rolled on a variety of objects made of clay. When seals were impressed on tablets or tablet cases the seal impressions served to identify the authority responsible for what was written in the documents, much as a signature does today. When seals were impressed on sealings — lumps of clay that were used to secure doors and the lids of storage jars— the seal impressions served to identify their owner and protect against unauthorized opening. Many cylinder seals have survived because they were made of durable materials, particularly stone, but also metal and fired clay. Perforated through the middle like a bead, seals were also believed to have apotropaic, or protective, functions and were worn as jewelry or pinned on garments.

The modern impression of a seal is shown here so that the entire design can be seen. The scene is composed of two basic groupings that form an overall continuous design unified by the stylized landscape setting. In one group, two tall trees flank a hunter grasping an ibex. Above the man a cuneiform inscription gives the name of the seal owner, Balu-ili, who was a court official, and his profession, cupbearer. In the other group, ibexes stand on mountains, facing each other and flanking three trees. This seal was made during the Akkadian period, when the iconography used by the seal engraver expanded to include a variety of new mythological, thematic, and narrative subjects. Here, the already ancient motif of the hunt is given a new setting in a clearly defined landscape. If there was a relationship between this imagery and the inscribed text, its meaning is no longer understood.

Sacred and secular ideas, fundamental to the beliefs of ancient Mesopotamian peoples, were visualized in the miniature images carved on the seals. The carver used intaglio, a technique in which the forms were cut into the stone, to create the raised impression. The challenge of the seal carver was to create a design that would maintain its balance and clarity when rolled out only half its length on a small surface or twice its length on a larger surface. On the left and right edges of the impression one can see how the rolled out design begins to repeat. Unlike much of the art of the ancient Near East, which survives only in a fragmentary state, cylinder seals are in the unique position of appearing almost exactly as they would have looked to the ancient people who used them.
persona2_adapted:
In ancient Mesopotamia, people used small cylinder-shaped seals that could be rolled across objects made of clay. When rolled onto clay tablets or their protective cases, the seal’s imprint acted like a signature does today—it showed who had the authority to write or approve the document. When pressed onto lumps of clay used to seal doors or jar lids, the imprint identified the owner and helped prevent unauthorized opening. Many of these cylinder seals have survived because they were made from long-lasting materials, especially stone, but also metal and fired clay. Like beads, they were pierced through the center, and people believed they had protective, or apotropaic, powers. They were often worn as jewelry or pinned onto clothing.

The image shown here is a modern impression of a seal, made so the full design can be seen clearly. The scene is made up of two main groups that together form a continuous pattern, held together by a stylized landscape. In one group, two tall trees frame a hunter who is grabbing an ibex (a type of wild goat). Above the man, a cuneiform inscription—an ancient writing system using wedge-shaped marks—gives the name of the seal’s owner, Balu-ili, who was a court official, and his job title, cupbearer. In the other group, ibexes stand on mountains, facing each other with three trees between them. This seal was made during the Akkadian period, a time when the images used by seal carvers expanded to include many new mythological, thematic, and narrative subjects. Here, the already ancient theme of the hunt is placed in a clearly defined landscape. If there was any connection between this imagery and the written text, its meaning is now lost.

Both sacred and everyday ideas, central to the beliefs of ancient Mesopotamian people, were brought to life in the tiny images carved onto these seals. The carver used a technique called intaglio, where the design is cut into the stone, so that when rolled, the raised pattern appears. The challenge for the seal carver was to create a design that would remain balanced and clear whether it was rolled only halfway across a small surface or twice its length across a larger one. On the left and right edges of the impression, you can see where the design begins to repeat. Unlike much of the art from the ancient Near East, which survives only in broken pieces, cylinder seals are unique because they appear almost exactly as they would have to the people who used them long ago.

CuratorialText: This type of figurine known as an eye idol, made of stone and having incised eyes, has been excavated at Tell Brak, where thousands were found in a building now called the Eye Temple. They were probably dedicated there as offerings. Many are incised with multiple sets of eyes, others with jewelry, and still others with representations of "children"—smaller eyes and body carved on the body of the larger idol. Wide eyes demonstrate attentiveness to the gods in much of Mesopotamian art.
persona2_adapted: This type of small stone figurine, called an eye idol, was carved with simple, incised eyes. Archaeologists have uncovered thousands of them at a site called Tell Brak, in a building now known as the Eye Temple. People likely left them there as offerings. Many of these idols have several sets of eyes carved into them, while others are decorated with jewelry. Some even show what seem to be "children"—smaller eyes and bodies carved onto the larger figure. In much of the art from ancient Mesopotamia, wide eyes were a way of showing devotion and attentiveness to the gods.

CuratorialText: Some of the most elaborate representations of females in the art of the ancient Near East are images of divine and cult figures whose association with certain aspects of life made them essential to the welfare of humanity. Fertility, procreation, and the growth of crops and livestock were among the basic concepts identified with female divinities. Representations of nude females in clay, stone, and metal are the simplest and most obvious expression of these concepts, and such figures appear throughout antiquity in many regions. This striking example in clay from the South Caspian region of northwestern Iran is hollow and probably served as a cult vessel as well as a sacred image.
persona2_adapted: Some of the most detailed depictions of women in ancient Near Eastern art show divine or religious figures. Because these figures were linked to essential aspects of life, people believed they were crucial to human well-being. Ideas like fertility, childbirth, and the successful growth of crops and livestock were among the basic concepts associated with female deities. Simple statues of nude women—made from clay, stone, or metal—are the most direct and obvious way these ideas were expressed. Such figures appear across many regions throughout the ancient world. This striking clay example, from the South Caspian region of northwestern Iran, is hollow inside. It likely served both as a sacred image and as a vessel used in religious rituals.

CuratorialText: Distinctive clay female figures like this one were produced at sites belonging to the Halaf culture. This example displays a strong stylization with an emphasis on the sexual features. She sits with her large thighs extended, supporting her breasts with her arms; neither hands nor feet are shown. Her head is missing; in other figures of this type when the head is intact, it is elongated into a large noselike projection but otherwise is featureless. Remains of paint may represent jewelry. The meaning of such representations is unknown but may be connected with fertility. The stylized depiction of the nude female form remained an artistic convention in northern Syria, Anatolia, and the Aegean for several millennia.
persona2_adapted: This clay female figure comes from a cultural group known as the Halaf culture, which produced similar distinctive figurines at several of their settlements. The figure is highly stylized, meaning the artist simplified and exaggerated certain features rather than aiming for a realistic appearance. In particular, the sexual characteristics are emphasized. She is seated with her large thighs extended outward, and her arms support her breasts. Neither hands nor feet are shown. Her head is missing, but in other figures of this type that have survived intact, the head is elongated into a large, noselike projection, with no other facial features. Traces of paint may indicate that she once wore jewelry. We do not know exactly what these figures meant to the people who made them, but they may have been connected to ideas of fertility. This simplified way of depicting the nude female body became a lasting artistic tradition in northern Syria, Anatolia, and the Aegean region for thousands of years.

CuratorialText: Furniture inlaid with carved ivory plaques was highly prized by the Assyrian kings. During the ninth to seventh centuries B.C., vast quantities of luxury goods, often embellished with carved ivory in local, Syrian, and Phoenician styles, accumulated in Assyrian palaces, much of it as booty or tribute. This object belongs to a group of plaques depicting animals and stylized plants. They were made by master carvers in a delicate openwork technique characteristic of Phoenician ivory carving. However, the style and subjects depicted have close parallels on stone relief sculptures from Tell Halaf, in northern Syria, and a debate exists over which tradition produced these fine panels.
persona2_adapted: Furniture decorated with carved ivory panels was highly valued by Assyrian kings. Between the ninth and seventh centuries B.C., large amounts of luxury goods—often adorned with carved ivory in local, Syrian, and Phoenician styles—filled Assyrian palaces, much of it taken as war spoils or given as tribute. This piece belongs to a set of panels showing animals and stylized plants. They were crafted by master carvers using a delicate technique that cuts through the ivory to create a lace-like pattern, a method typical of Phoenician ivory work. However, the style and subjects closely resemble those found on stone relief carvings from Tell Halaf in northern Syria, leading scholars to debate which artistic tradition actually produced these fine panels.

CuratorialText: This statuette group, carved in the round, was found with five other statuettes with similar imagery. The six statuettes were excavated in and just below two arched niches built into the wall of a room at Fort Shalmaneser, a royal building at Nimrud that was probably used to store booty and tribute collected by the Assyrians while on military campaign. Those found just below the niches could have fallen when the palaces were destroyed during the the final defeat of Assyria at the end of the seventh century B.C. Originally, these objects were attached to long ivory plinths and exhibited in the arched niches, perhaps arranged in a procession of foreigners bringing different animals and animal skins as tribute to the Assyrian king. A frontally facing male, striding to the right with his lower body in profile, grasps the horns of an oryx (a species of desert-dwelling antelope) that strides behind him. The male figure’s eyes, necklace, and armlet were carved to receive colored glass or semiprecious stone inlays. He wears a short kilt belted with a long sash, embroidered with decoration including two uraei (mythical, fire-spitting serpents), zig-zags, wavy lines, small squares, rosettes, diamonds, and circles. A monkey, whose fur is rendered with short incisions, sits erect on his left shoulder and grasps his short curly hair. Although his right arm does not survive, it was probably extended to support the elaborately patterned leopard skin draped over his right shoulder. Built by the Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II, the palaces and storerooms of Nimrud housed thousands of pieces of carved ivory. Most of the ivories served as furniture inlays or small precious objects such as boxes. While some of them were carved in the same style as the large Assyrian reliefs lining the walls of the Northwest Palace, the majority of the ivories display images and styles related to the arts of North Syria and the Phoenician city-states. Phoenician style ivories are distinguished by their use of imagery related to Egyptian art, such as sphinxes and figures wearing pharaonic crowns, and the use of elaborate carving techniques such as openwork and colored glass inlay. North Syrian style ivories tend to depict stockier figures in more dynamic compositions, carved as solid plaques with fewer added decorative elements. However, some pieces do not fit easily into any of these three styles. Most of the ivories were probably collected by the Assyrian kings as tribute from vassal states, and as booty from conquered enemies, while some may have been manufactured in workshops at Nimrud. The ivory tusks that provided the raw material for these objects were almost certainly from African elephants, imported from lands south of Egypt, although elephants did inhabit several river valleys in Syria until they were hunted to extinction by the end of the eighth century B.C.

This fort was likely used to store the spoils of war and tribute collected by the Assyrians during their military campaigns. The statuettes found just below the niches may have fallen when the palaces were destroyed during Assyria’s final defeat at the end of the seventh century B.C.

Originally, these objects were attached to long ivory bases and displayed inside the arched niches, possibly arranged to look like a procession of foreigners bringing different animals and animal skins as tribute to the Assyrian king. A monkey, its fur indicated by short carved lines, sits upright on his left shoulder and grasps his short, curly hair. Although his right arm is missing, it was probably extended to support the elaborately patterned leopard skin draped over his right shoulder.

The palaces and storerooms of Nimrud, built by the Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II, once housed thousands of carved ivory objects. Most of these ivories were used as inlays for furniture or as small precious items like boxes. While some were carved in the same style as the large stone reliefs that lined the walls of the Northwest Palace, the majority show images and styles linked to the arts of North Syria and the Phoenician city-states. Phoenician-style ivories are recognizable by their use of Egyptian-inspired imagery—such as sphinxes and figures wearing pharaonic crowns—and by elaborate carving techniques like openwork (cutting through the material to create a pattern) and colored glass inlay. North Syrian-style ivories, on the other hand, tend to show stockier figures in more dynamic poses, carved as solid plaques with fewer added decorative elements. However, some pieces don’t fit neatly into any of these three categories.

Most of the ivories were probably collected by Assyrian kings as tribute from vassal states or as booty from conquered enemies, though some may have been made in workshops at Nimrud itself. The ivory tusks used as raw material almost certainly came from African elephants, imported from lands south of Egypt—even though elephants did live in several river valleys in Syria until they were hunted to extinction by the end of the eighth century B.C.

CuratorialText:
This bronze monumental statue is one of the very few nearly complete Roman bronze statues of the third century preserved today. It is a reminder that many of the disembodied portrait heads displayed in these galleries were once attached to freestanding sculptures, whose original appearances were undoubtedly quite different. Portraits of the emperor served the highly important function in imperial propaganda of presenting the ruler to the public and of projecting the personality with which he wished to be perceived. Leadership and military strength are evoked in the heroic nudity of the figure and in the pose, which recalls the famous statue of Alexander the Great with the Lance by Lysippos. In contrast to the idealized body, the portrait head represents the emperor with brutish realism. Trebonianus likely would have cradled a parazonium, or short sword, in his left arm, and held a spear in his upraised right hand.
The statue has undergone several campaigns of restoration since its discovery in the early nineteenth century and was examined in great detail as part of the conservation treatment in preparation for its display here. Visual examination inside and out, combined with x-radiography, made possible the clear identification of ancient and restored areas, which are indicated in the accompanying illustration. At least three-quarters of the statue is ancient. Despite the discrepancy in scale, the head belongs to the body. Although the mantle draped over his left shoulder is a modern restoration, cast edges beneath it confirm that the statue had a similar embellishment in antiquity. The left foot with its elaborately decorated open-fronted boot appears to be ancient but may not belong.

Leadership and military strength are suggested by the figure’s heroic nudity and by its pose, which recalls a famous statue of Alexander the Great holding a lance, created by the sculptor Lysippos. In contrast to the idealized body, the portrait head shows the emperor with a rough, almost brutal realism. As part of the conservation work done in preparation for its display here, it was examined in great detail. Visual inspection inside and out, combined with X-ray imaging, made it possible to clearly identify which parts are ancient and which are restored—these are marked in the accompanying illustration. CuratorialText: Eirene, the daughter of Zeus and Themis, was one of the three Horai (Seasons), maidens closely associated with the fertility of the earth and the nurturing of children. The original bronze was erected in the Agora (marketplace) of Athens between 375/374 and 360/359 B.C. Rarely can an ancient monument be dated so exactly. We know from literary sources that the cult of Eirene was introduced to Athens in 375/374, and six recently found Panathenaic amphorae dated to 360/359 show an image of the statue. The Greek traveler Pausanius saw the work in the Agora in the second century A.D. and reported that it was by the sculptor Kephisodotos. Eirene was represented as a beautiful young woman wearing a peplos and himation (cloak), holding a scepter in her right hand, and carrying the young child Ploutos (the personification of wealth) and a cornucopia on her left arm. The figure brings to mind images of Demeter, the major goddess of agricultural plenty and the mother of Ploutos.
persona2_adapted: Eirene, the daughter of Zeus and Themis, was one of the three Horai (the goddesses of the seasons), maidens closely linked to the fertility of the earth and the care of children. The original bronze statue was set up in the Agora (the central public square and marketplace) of Athens between 375/374 and 360/359 B.C. It is rare for an ancient monument to be dated with such precision. We know from ancient writings that the worship of Eirene was introduced to Athens in 375/374, and six recently discovered Panathenaic amphorae (ceramic jars used as prizes in a major Athenian festival) from 360/359 show an image of the statue. The Greek traveler Pausanius saw the work in the Agora in the second century A.D. and reported that it was made by the sculptor Kephisodotos. Eirene was shown as a beautiful young woman wearing a peplos and himation (a type of tunic and cloak), holding a scepter in her right hand, and carrying the young child Ploutos (the personification of wealth) and a cornucopia (a horn-shaped symbol of abundance) on her left arm. The figure brings to mind images of Demeter, the major goddess of agricultural plenty and the mother of Ploutos.

CuratorialText: Many private summer villas were located along the coast near Naples. One of the most sumptuous must have been the villa built by Agrippa, friend of the emperor Augustus and husband of his daughter, Julia. It stood overlooking the Bay of Naples from a spot near the modern town of Boscotrecase. The villa was partially excavated between 1903 and 1905 after its accidental discovery during work on a railway. Wall decorations that still survived in four bedrooms were removed. The Metropolitan Museum acquired sections from three rooms and the Archaeological Museum at Naples received the rest. Agrippa died in 12 B.C. and his son, Agrippa Postumus became the villa's proprietor in 11 B.C. as inscriptions found there indicate. The frescoes must have been painted during renovations begun at the time. Painted by artists working for the imperial household, they are among the finest existing examples of Roman wall painting. The so-called Black Room was one of a sequence of bedrooms facing south toward the downward slope of the mountain and the sea. The source of light was a wide doorway giving onto a terrace or promenade. This ambiguous and sophisticated decoration is a masterpiece of the so-called third style of Roman wall painting, which flourished during the reign of Augustus. The theme is a playful rendition of architectural motif. A low red dado serves as the base from which a skeleton of thin white columns appears to rise against a black background. There almost weightless columns support pavilions, candelabra, tripods, and a narrow cornice that runs around the room. They were embellished with jewel-like decorations. On the back wall tiny swans, the bird of Apollo, patron god of Augustus, perch improbably on threadlike spirals, and yellow panels with Egyptianizing motifs must have brought to mind the recent annexation of Egypt after the death of Cleopatra in 30 B.C. This architectural scheme creates almost no sense of depth or volume. The black walls behind appear at once to be flat and to dissolve into limitless space. Tiny landscape vignettes float like islands in the middle of this blackness. Burnished to a high polish, these walls must have appeared magical indeed when illuminated by lamps at night.
persona2_adapted:
Along the coast near Naples, many wealthy Romans built summer villas. One of the most luxurious must have been the villa of Agrippa, a close friend of Emperor Augustus and the husband of his daughter, Julia. It stood on a hillside overlooking the Bay of Naples, near the modern town of Boscotrecase. Workers accidentally discovered the villa during railway construction, and it was partially excavated between 1903 and 1905. The wall decorations that still survived in four bedrooms were removed. The Metropolitan Museum acquired sections from three rooms, while the Archaeological Museum in Naples received the rest.

Agrippa died in 12 B.C., and inscriptions found at the site indicate that his son, Agrippa Postumus, became the villa's owner in 11 B.C. The frescoes were likely painted during renovations that began around that time. Created by artists working for the imperial household, they are among the finest surviving examples of Roman wall painting.

The so-called Black Room was one of a series of bedrooms facing south, toward the mountain slope and the sea. Light entered through a wide doorway that opened onto a terrace or walkway. This room’s decoration is both puzzling and sophisticated—a masterpiece of what is known as the "third style" of Roman wall painting, which flourished during Augustus’s reign. The theme is a playful take on architectural design. A low red base, or dado, runs along the bottom of the wall. From it, a delicate framework of thin white columns seems to rise against a black background. These nearly weightless columns support small pavilions, candelabra, tripods, and a narrow ledge that runs around the room, all decorated with jewel-like details.

On the back wall, tiny swans—birds sacred to Apollo, the patron god of Augustus—perch improbably on threadlike spirals. Yellow panels decorated with Egyptian-style motifs would have reminded viewers of Rome’s recent annexation of Egypt after Cleopatra’s death in 30 B.C. This architectural design creates almost no sense of depth or solidity. The black walls behind appear both flat and as if they dissolve into endless space. Tiny landscape scenes float like islands in the middle of this blackness. Polished to a high shine, these walls must have seemed truly magical when lit by lamps at night.

CuratorialText:
From the villa of Agrippa Postumus at Boscotrecase, the Mythological Room (19)
Landscape with Perseus and Andromeda This fresco from the Imperial villa at Boscotrecase depicts two consecutive events from the myth of Perseus and Andromeda. Perseus is about to rescue Andromeda from the ketos, a snaky sea monster painted in a brilliant blue-green palette. The creature raises his head with gigantic open jaws and frightful teeth toward Andromeda, who stands with outstretched arms in the center of the panel. One hand appears to be chained to the crag; the other elegantly placed on the rocks. Perseus flies in from the left with his lyre in one hand, winged shoes on his feet, and a windblown cloak over his shoulder. In the upper right portion of the fresco, he is greeted by Andromeda's grateful father, a scene that alludes to the myth's happy endingâ€“the marriage of hero and princess. The fortunes of love and the ever-present sea are the themes that link this fresco and that of Polyphemus and Galatea (20.192.17) from the same cubiculum at Boscotrecase. The translucent blue-green background of both frescoes also unifies the disparate episodes combined in each painting, and must have brought a sense of coolness to the room.
persona2_adapted:
From the villa of Agrippa Postumus at Boscotrecase, the Mythological Room (19)

**Landscape with Perseus and Andromeda**

This wall painting, from a grand Roman country house at Boscotrecase, shows two moments from the story of Perseus and Andromeda. In the first scene, Perseus is about to rescue Andromeda from the *ketos*—a sea monster with snaky features, painted in vivid blue-green tones. The creature lifts its head, jaws wide open with terrifying teeth, toward Andromeda, who stands in the center of the panel with her arms stretched out. One hand appears chained to the rocky cliff; the other rests gracefully on the stone. Perseus flies in from the left, carrying a lyre (a stringed instrument), wearing winged shoes, and with a windblown cloak over his shoulder.

In the upper right part of the painting, we see a second scene: Perseus being welcomed by Andromeda’s grateful father. This hints at the story’s happy ending—the marriage of the hero and the princess. The themes of love’s fortunes and the ever-present sea connect this fresco to another one in the same room, *Polyphemus and Galatea* (20.192.17). The translucent blue-green background of both paintings also helps unify the different events shown in each, and would have brought a sense of coolness to the room.

CuratorialText: This noble image of a woman brings to mind the philosopher Aristotle's description of commonly held beliefs about the dead: "In addition to believing that those who have ended this life are blessed and happy, we also think that to say anything false or slanderous against them is impious, from our feeling that it is directed against those who have already become our betters and superiors" (Of the Soul, quoted in Plutarch, A Letter to Apollonius 27). Larger than life and seated on a thronelike chair, this figure assumes almost heroic proportions.
persona2_adapted: This noble portrait of a woman recalls the philosopher Aristotle’s description of how people commonly think about the dead: “In addition to believing that those who have ended this life are blessed and happy, we also think that to say anything false or slanderous against them is impious, from our feeling that it is directed against those who have already become our betters and superiors” (Of the Soul, quoted in Plutarch, A Letter to Apollonius 27). Larger than life and seated on a chair that resembles a throne, this figure takes on an almost heroic stature.

CuratorialText: A stylized octopus flanked by fish covers each side of this stirrup jar, named for the shape of its handles. Mycenaean pottery often reflects Minoan-inspired themes and techniques that originated on the island of Crete. Animated marine motifs adopted from Minoan art began to appear on Mycenaean vessels in about 1500 BCE and were initially rendered in a very naturalistic manner. Here, the symmetrical composition and abstract depiction of the sea creatures are characteristically Mycenaean. Such jars were commonly used to transport liquids.
persona2_adapted: A stylized octopus, with fish on either side, decorates each side of this stirrup jar—so named because its handles resemble the shape of a stirrup. The pottery of the Mycenaean culture often shows themes and techniques inspired by the Minoan civilization, which originated on the island of Crete. Around 1500 BCE, lively sea-life designs borrowed from Minoan art began to appear on Mycenaean vessels, at first depicted in a very lifelike way. In this piece, however, the balanced arrangement and the more abstract rendering of the sea creatures are typical of Mycenaean style. Jars like this were commonly used for transporting liquids.

CuratorialText:
On the shoulder, women dancing between musicians playing flute and lyre
On the body, wedding procession. This is the earliest and most complete known representation of an Attic wedding. The bridal couple with the best man behind them sit in a cart drawn by two donkeys. A mule cart with four guests follows. Other members of the procession are on foot. The woman in the lead holds two torches, indicating that the scene takes place at night. The procession heads toward the bridegroom's house where a woman, probably the mother of the groom, awaits. The architecture of the house is carefully indicated; the white columns of the porch may be painted wood.
persona2_adapted:
On the shoulder of the vase, women dance between musicians playing the flute and the lyre, a stringed instrument similar to a small harp.

On the main body of the vase, a wedding procession is shown. This is the earliest and most complete known depiction of a wedding from ancient Attica (the region around Athens). The bride and groom, with the best man behind them, sit in a cart pulled by two donkeys. A mule cart carrying four guests follows behind. Other members of the procession walk on foot. The woman leading the group holds two torches, which tells us the scene takes place at night. The procession is heading toward the groom's house, where a woman—likely his mother—waits for them. The house's architecture is shown in careful detail; the white columns of the porch may have been made of painted wood.

CuratorialText: This highly ornate and extremely well-preserved Roman marble sarcophagus came to the Metropolitan Museum from the collection of the Dukes of Beaufort and was formerly displayed in their country seat, Badminton House in Gloucestershire, England. An inscription on the unfinished back of the sarcophagus records that it was installed there in 1733. In contrast to the rough and unsightly back, the sides and front of the sarcophagus are decorated with forty human and animal figures carved in high relief. The central figure is that of the god Dionysos seated on a panther, but he is somewhat overshadowed by four larger standing figures who represent the four Seasons (from left to right, Winter, Spring, Summer, and Fall). The figures are unusual in that the Seasons are usually portrayed as women, but here they are shown as sturdy youths. Around these five central figures are placed other Bacchic figures and cultic objects, all carved at a smaller scale. On the rounded ends of the sarcophagus are two other groups of large figures, similarly intermingled with lesser ones. On the left end, Mother Earth is portrayed reclining on the ground; she is accompanied by a satyr and a youth carrying fruit. On the right end, a bearded male figure, probably to be identified with the personification of a river-god, reclines in front of two winged youths, perhaps representing two additional Seasons. The sarcophagus is an exquisite example of Roman funerary art, displaying all the virtuosity of the workshop where it was carved. The marble comes from a quarry in the eastern Mediterranean and was probably shipped to Rome, where it was worked. Only a very wealthy and powerful person would have been able to commission and purchase such a sarcophagus, and it was probably made for a member of one of the old aristocratic families in Rome itself. The subjects - the triumph of Dionysos and the Seasons - are unlikely, however, to have had any special significance for the deceased, particularly as it is clear that the design was copied from a sculptor's pattern book. Another sarcophagus, now in the Hessisches Landesmuseum in Kassel, Germany, has the same composition of Dionysos flanked by the four Seasons, although the treatment and carving of the figures is quite different. On the Badminton sarcophagus the figures are carved in high relief and so endow the crowded scene with multiple areas of light and shade, allowing the eye to wander effortlessly from one figure to another. One must also imagine that certain details were highlighted with color and even gilding, making the whole composition a visual tour de force. Very few Roman sarcophagi of this quality have survived. Although the Badminton sarcophagus lacks its lid, the fact that it was found in the early eighteenth century and soon thereafter installed in Badminton Hall means that it has been preserved almost intact and only a few of the minor extremities are now missing.
