CuratorialText:
On the neck, obverse, battle of centaurs and Lapiths; reverse, youths and women
Around the body, Amazonomachy (battle between Greeks and Amazons). The ancient Greeks almost never depicted contemporary or historical events in art. Thus, while literary works of the fifth century B.C. make clear that the Greeks understood the magnitude of their victory in the Persian Wars, there was no concern among artists to illustrate major events or personalities. Instead, their preference was for grand mythological battles between Greeks and eastern adversaries, notably Amazons. The Amazons were a mythical race of warrior women whose homeland lay far to the east and north. The most celebrated Amazonomachies in Athens during the first half of the fifth century were large-scale wall paintings that decorated the Theseion and the Stoa Poikile (the Painted Portico). Their influence was considerable and underlies the representation here and on the adjacent krater, 06.286.86.
persona2_adapted:
On the neck of the vase, one side shows a battle between centaurs (creatures that are half-human, half-horse) and Lapiths (a mythical Greek tribe). The other side shows young men and women. Around the body of the vase is an Amazonomachy—a battle between Greeks and Amazons, a mythical race of warrior women whose homeland was thought to lie far to the east and north.

The ancient Greeks almost never depicted real historical events or famous people in their art. Even though writers in the fifth century B.C. made it clear that the Greeks understood how important their victory in the Persian Wars was, artists did not try to show major battles or leaders. Instead, they preferred grand mythological battles between Greeks and eastern enemies, especially the Amazons.

In Athens during the first half of the fifth century B.C., the most famous Amazon battle scenes were large wall paintings that decorated two important buildings: the Theseion (a temple dedicated to the hero Theseus) and the Stoa Poikile (the Painted Portico, a public colonnade). These paintings had a major influence on later art, and they are the inspiration for the scene on this vase, as well as on the nearby krater (a large mixing bowl for wine), numbered 06.286.86.

CuratorialText: Approximately 230 paintings in the collection of the Rijksmuseum are still lifes. As early as the 16th century, Flemish painters specialized in this genre. These are arranged displays featuring, for example, shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, but also flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. Life literally stands still for a moment. Separate specializations soon emerged within the genre. For instance, Rachel Ruysch primarily painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed creating arrangements with food and eating and drinking utensils. Another variant, the vanitas still life, which often depicts a skull, refers to the transience of life and the material world.
persona2_adapted:
Approximately 230 paintings in the Rijksmuseum’s collection are still lifes. This type of painting—which shows arranged objects rather than people or landscapes—was already a specialty of Flemish artists as early as the 1500s. In a still life, you might see carefully composed displays of shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, as well as flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. As the name suggests, life literally stands still for a moment.

Soon, artists began to focus on specific subjects within the genre. For example, Rachel Ruysch concentrated on painting flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda were known for their arrangements of food, drinking vessels, and utensils. Another type, the vanitas still life—which often includes a skull—was meant to remind viewers of the fleeting nature of life and the emptiness of material possessions.

CuratorialText: Orange above! With the coronation of King William I (1772-1843), the Netherlands became a monarchy for the first time in 1815. This first king is the son of the last Stadtholder, William V, who had fled to England years earlier. King William I has canals and roads constructed in the Netherlands and invests in trade, shipping, and industry. However, with his sights firmly set on the economy, William I pays less attention to other affairs of state. Discontent in the Southern Netherlands grows. The population rises in revolt, and in 1831, the secession of Belgium becomes a reality. The king resists, but attempts to regain the territory come to nothing. After years of struggle, William I recognizes independent Belgium in 1839. Disappointed, he abdicates the throne a year later, and his son, William II, takes over.
persona2_adapted: Orange above! With the coronation of King William I (1772-1843), the Netherlands became a monarchy for the first time in 1815. This first king was the son of the last Stadtholder—a historical title for a leader or governor of the Dutch Republic—William V, who had fled to England years earlier. King William I had canals and roads built across the Netherlands and invested in trade, shipping, and industry. However, with his attention firmly fixed on the economy, he paid less attention to other matters of state. Discontent grew in the Southern Netherlands. The population rose in revolt, and in 1831, Belgium broke away and became an independent country. The king resisted, but his attempts to reclaim the territory failed. After years of struggle, William I finally recognized an independent Belgium in 1839. Disappointed, he gave up the throne a year later, and his son, William II, took over.

CuratorialText: The extensive costume collection of the Rijksmuseum, the oldest in the Netherlands, tells a wealth of stories. From women's and men's to children's clothing: these pieces reveal stories about the wearer and their taste, the methods of the makers, and local and global influences. Many garments are custom-made for a special occasion, while others showcase the technological advancements of ready-to-wear fashion. From the Frisian branch of the Orange dynasty in the 17th century to Yves Saint Laurent: the collection features highlights of Dutch and international fashion. The collection is a remarkable reflection of fashion developments throughout the centuries.
persona2_adapted: The Rijksmuseum’s extensive costume collection—the oldest in the Netherlands—holds a rich array of stories. Spanning clothing for women, men, and children, these pieces reveal details about the wearer’s personal taste, the techniques of the makers, and the local and global influences that shaped them. Many garments were custom-made for special events, while others highlight the technological advances that made ready-to-wear fashion possible. From the 17th-century Frisian branch of the Orange dynasty to the designs of Yves Saint Laurent, the collection showcases key moments in both Dutch and international fashion. Together, these items offer a remarkable window into how fashion has evolved over the centuries.

CuratorialText: At the end of the 19th century, a new art style emerges. It is a reaction by artists who rebelled against the historicism prevailing at the time, which harked back to the styles of previous centuries. The new art, aptly named Art Nouveau, is characterized by natural forms, such as flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The emphasis lies on flowing lines, geometric shapes, and asymmetrical compositions. With Paris, Brussels, and Munich as centers, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement in Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from approximately 1890 until the beginning of the First World War in 1914, Art Nouveau became visible in all fields of the arts.
persona2_adapted: In the late 1800s, a new artistic style emerged. It was a direct response from artists who rejected the dominant trend of their time—historicism, which simply meant copying the styles of earlier centuries. Instead, these artists wanted something fresh and original. This new style, fittingly called Art Nouveau (French for "new art"), drew inspiration from nature: flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. Its key features included smooth, flowing lines, geometric shapes, and compositions that were deliberately unbalanced or asymmetrical. With major creative hubs in Paris, Brussels, and Munich, Art Nouveau quickly became an international movement, spreading across Western Europe and into the United States. This period lasted from about 1890 until the start of World War I in 1914, and during that time, Art Nouveau influenced nearly every form of art and design.

CuratorialText: Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. Trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, he also developed into a renowned painter. Goltzius followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem. There, together with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he introduced the Mannerism of Bartholomäus Spranger to the Netherlands. The style is recognizable by muscular bodies, twisted poses, and small heads in intricate compositions. During a trip through Italy in 1590-91, Goltzius was inspired by the work of the Italian masters. Back in the Netherlands, he left Mannerism behind and opted for a more classicist style with classical proportions in his work. His technically complex engravings, in particular, were admired throughout Europe. Goltzius's oeuvre provides a fine reflection of the developments in Dutch painting around 1600.
persona2_adapted: Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. Trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, he also became a well-known painter. Goltzius followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem. There, together with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he introduced a style called Mannerism—brought from Bartholomäus Spranger—to the Netherlands. This style is easy to spot: it features muscular bodies, twisted poses, and small heads, all arranged in complex, crowded compositions. During a trip through Italy in 1590-91, Goltzius was inspired by the work of Italian masters. When he returned to the Netherlands, he moved away from Mannerism and adopted a more classicist approach, using balanced proportions inspired by ancient art. His technically demanding engravings, in particular, were admired across Europe. Goltzius's body of work offers a clear reflection of the shifts in Dutch painting around 1600.

CuratorialText: Rembrandt van Rijn (1606-1669) decided at a young age that he wanted to become a painter. He first apprenticed with Jacob van Swanenburgh in his hometown of Leiden and subsequently continued his training with Pieter Lastman in Amsterdam. Rembrandt allowed himself to be unconstrained by rules and experimented freely with daring compositions, paint handling, and rendering of textures. He enhanced the drama in his depictions with strong light-dark contrasts. His early works are painted quite precisely; later, his brushstrokes became increasingly loose. He regularly received important commissions, including his most famous and largest painting, The Night Watch. Instead of a static portrait, Rembrandt painted a lively moment in which all the figures are depicted in action. In addition to this masterpiece, the Rijksmuseum holds 24 other paintings, 64 drawings, and approximately 1,300 prints by Rembrandt in its collection.
persona2_adapted: Rembrandt van Rijn (1606-1669) decided at a young age that he wanted to become a painter. He first trained with Jacob van Swanenburgh in his hometown of Leiden and later continued his studies with Pieter Lastman in Amsterdam. Rembrandt refused to be limited by traditional rules and experimented freely with bold arrangements of figures and objects, the way he applied paint, and how he showed different surfaces and textures. He heightened the sense of drama in his scenes by using strong contrasts between light and dark. His early works are painted quite precisely; later, his brushstrokes became increasingly loose and visible. He regularly received important commissions, including his most famous and largest painting, *The Night Watch*. Instead of creating a static, posed portrait, Rembrandt captured a lively moment in which all the figures are shown in action. In addition to this masterpiece, the Rijksmuseum holds 24 other paintings, 64 drawings, and about 1,300 prints by Rembrandt in its collection.

CuratorialText: Although Johannes Vermeer (1632-1675) is now world-famous, little is known about him. It is clear, however, that the native of Delft spent his days as an art dealer, father of a large family, and, naturally, a painter. His early work includes a Biblical and a mythological scene. Other early works reveal influences from the Utrecht Caravaggists. Vermeer primarily paints interiors and tronies (portrait-like studies of people). His most famous works are the intimate genre pieces, snapshots of everyday life: a lady reading a letter, a woman engaged in a household chore. All the scenes share a serene calm, which sometimes even feels somewhat mysterious. Vermeer is admired for his exceptional use of light, powerful colors, and incredible precision. During his lifetime, he rose to a certain fame and commanded high prices for his paintings. Afterward, Vermeer fell into obscurity and was not rediscovered until the mid-19th century.
persona2_adapted: Although Johannes Vermeer (1632–1675) is now world-famous, surprisingly little is known about his life. What is clear is that he was born in Delft, worked as an art dealer, raised a large family, and, of course, painted. His early work includes a scene from the Bible and one from classical mythology. Other early pieces show the influence of a group of painters known as the Utrecht Caravaggists—artists who, like the Italian master Caravaggio, used dramatic contrasts of light and shadow. Vermeer mostly painted indoor scenes and what are called *tronies*: studies of people that resemble portraits but are not meant to depict specific individuals. His most famous works are intimate scenes of everyday life—a lady reading a letter, a woman doing a household chore. These are sometimes described as "genre" paintings, meaning they show ordinary people in ordinary settings. All of these scenes share a quiet calm, which can even feel a bit mysterious. Vermeer is admired for his remarkable handling of light, his vivid colors, and his extraordinary attention to detail. During his lifetime, he achieved a certain level of fame and could charge high prices for his paintings. After his death, however, his work was largely forgotten, and it was not rediscovered until the mid-19th century.

CuratorialText: Rachel Ruysch (1664-1750) is one of the most successful artists of her time. Her flower still lifes are very detailed. Roses, poppies, carnations, and irises: all almost indistinguishable from the real thing. Her work bears witness to a vast knowledge of nature. Ruysch acquires this knowledge at the Amsterdam Botanical Garden, where her father, Frederik Ruysch, teaches as a professor. She also studies his collection of prepared flowers and insects. She learns the tricks of the trade from still-life painter Willem van Aelst. In 1701, Ruysch becomes the first woman to join the Hague artists' society Pictura. From 1708 to 1716, she works as court painter for the German Elector John William of the Palatinate, a particularly honorable position. He is by no means the only admirer of her work. Ruysch's flower still lifes are collected by enthusiasts far beyond the national borders.
persona2_adapted: Rachel Ruysch (1664–1750) stands among the most accomplished artists of her era. Her flower still lifes—paintings of carefully arranged bouquets—are extraordinarily detailed. Roses, poppies, carnations, and irises appear nearly indistinguishable from the real thing. Her work reveals a deep understanding of the natural world. Ruysch developed this knowledge at the Amsterdam Botanical Garden, where her father, Frederik Ruysch, was a professor. She also studied his collection of preserved flowers and insects. She learned the techniques of her craft from Willem van Aelst, a painter who specialized in still lifes. In 1701, Ruysch became the first woman admitted to Pictura, the artists' society in The Hague. From 1708 to 1716, she served as court painter to John William, Elector of the Palatinate—a German ruler—a particularly prestigious role. He was far from her only admirer. Collectors across Europe eagerly acquired her flower still lifes, drawn to their beauty and precision.

CuratorialText: Thérèse Schwartze (1851-1918) was born into the artistic family of painter Johann Georg Schwartze and grew into a talented portrait painter, draftsman, and printmaker. Trained in her father's studio and after art education in Munich and Paris, she develops into a true talent. When her father dies prematurely, the 23-year-old Schwartze becomes the new breadwinner of the family. Her style is powerful, with rapid brushstrokes and large areas of color, which make her portraits full of character yet flattering. Schwartze's work is well received, and she becomes increasingly famous both at home and abroad. Around the year 1900, Schwartze is the most successful portraitist in the Netherlands, and she portrays members of the Royal Family. She works quickly and creates hundreds of portraits in pastel and oil paint during her career, of which the Rijksmuseum holds a number of artist portraits in its collection.
persona2_adapted: Thérèse Schwartze (1851-1918) was born into a family of artists—her father, Johann Georg Schwartze, was a painter—and she grew up to become a highly skilled portraitist, as well as a draftsman and printmaker. After training in her father’s studio and studying art in Munich and Paris, she developed into a true talent. When her father died unexpectedly, the 23-year-old Schwartze became the family’s main earner. Her painting style is bold and energetic, marked by quick brushstrokes and broad areas of color. This approach gives her portraits a strong sense of personality while still being flattering to her subjects. Schwartze’s work was well received, and she grew increasingly famous both in the Netherlands and abroad. By around 1900, she was the most successful portrait painter in the country, and she painted members of the Dutch royal family. She worked quickly, producing hundreds of portraits in pastel and oil paint over the course of her career. The Rijksmuseum now holds a number of her portraits of fellow artists in its collection.

CuratorialText: Anyone walking through Amsterdam towards the end of the 19th century has a good chance of encountering George Hendrik Breitner (1857-1923). For days on end, the painter roams through his beloved Amsterdam with a sketchbook. He calls himself 'the painter of the people' and primarily draws and paints 'ordinary' city dwellers. Breitner also uses his camera to capture life. He subsequently applies his typical photographic style, in which figures partially fall outside the frame, to his paintings as well. This style, combined with thick brushstrokes and smudges, is not understood by everyone. Journalists are critical of his work, but enthusiasts love it. To this day, Breitner remains one of the most famous Impressionists of his time.
persona2_adapted: If you were walking through Amsterdam in the late 1800s, you would have had a good chance of spotting George Hendrik Breitner (1857–1923). Day after day, the painter wandered through his beloved city with a sketchbook in hand. He called himself "the painter of the people," focusing mainly on drawing and painting everyday city dwellers. Breitner also used a camera to capture scenes from daily life. He then brought a photographic quality to his paintings—one in which figures are sometimes cut off by the edge of the frame, as if caught mid-motion. This style, combined with thick, visible brushstrokes and smudged areas, wasn’t always well received. Journalists often criticized his work, but his fans loved it. Even today, Breitner remains one of the most famous Impressionists—artists who aimed to capture fleeting moments and the effects of light—of his era.

CuratorialText: He drew extensively and received his first painting lessons from Anton Mauve, a cousin by marriage and a renowned painter. In his early years, Van Gogh primarily drew and painted many peasant scenes in dark colors. After a brief period of study in Antwerp, Van Gogh left for Paris, where he was influenced by Impressionism and Pointillism. He developed a style characterized by short brushstrokes and bright, unmixed colors applied side by side. In 1888, he moved to sunny Southern France. Van Gogh struggled with mental health issues, for which he was hospitalized. However, this did not prevent him from continuing to paint. It was only after his death, in 1890, that his work reached the general public and Van Gogh became world-famous.
persona2_adapted: He drew extensively and received his first painting lessons from Anton Mauve, a relative by marriage and a well-known artist. He developed a style marked by short brushstrokes and bright, unmixed colors applied side by side. In 1888, he moved to sunny Southern France. Van Gogh struggled with mental health issues, for which he was hospitalized. However, this did not stop him from continuing to paint. It was only after his death, in 1890, that his work reached the general public and Van Gogh became world-famous.

CuratorialText: Born into a Utrecht family of silversmiths, the career path of Adam van Vianen (1568/69-1627) was determined early on. Around 1590, Van Vianen must have completed his master's examination, as he worked independently from that moment on and joined the blacksmiths' guild. His imaginative objects tell a story through lobe-like ornaments and forms. In 1614, Van Vianen received a commission from the Amsterdam guild to create a jug in memory of his own brother Paulus, who was also a silversmith and had passed away shortly before. The jug became a tribute to his brother's work and creativity. In this jug, the ornament plays the leading role for the first time.
persona2_adapted: Born into a family of silversmiths in Utrecht, Adam van Vianen (1568/69–1627) had his future career largely set from an early age. Around 1590, he likely passed the examination required to become a master craftsman, since from that point on he worked independently and joined the local blacksmiths' guild. The objects he created are highly imaginative, telling a story through their curved, lobe-like shapes and decorative forms. In 1614, the Amsterdam guild commissioned Van Vianen to create a jug in memory of his own brother Paulus, also a silversmith, who had died shortly before. The jug became a tribute to his brother’s work and artistic vision. For the first time in this piece, the ornament itself—rather than the object's function or subject—takes center stage.

CuratorialText: Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. Trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, he also developed into a renowned painter. Goltzius followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem. There, together with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he introduced the Mannerism of Bartholomäus Spranger to the Netherlands. During a trip through Italy in 1590-91, Goltzius was inspired by the work of the Italian masters. Back in the Netherlands, he left Mannerism behind and opted for a more classicist style with classical proportions in his work. His technically complex engravings, in particular, were admired throughout Europe. Goltzius's oeuvre provides a fine reflection of the developments in Dutch painting around 1600.
persona2_adapted: Hendrick Goltzius (1558-1617) was born in Germany. Trained as a draftsman, engraver, and glass painter, he also became a well-known painter. Goltzius followed his teacher Dirck Volkertsz. Coornhert to Haarlem. There, together with Karel van Mander and Cornelis Cornelisz. van Haarlem, he introduced a dramatic, highly stylized artistic approach—called Mannerism—to the Netherlands, inspired by the work of Bartholomäus Spranger. This style is easy to spot: it features muscular bodies, twisted poses, and small heads, all arranged in complex, crowded compositions. During a trip through Italy in 1590-91, Goltzius was inspired by the work of Italian Renaissance masters. When he returned to the Netherlands, he moved away from Mannerism and adopted a more restrained, balanced style, one that emphasized classical proportions—meaning figures were more naturally proportioned and harmonious. His technically demanding engravings, in particular, were admired across Europe. Goltzius’s body of work offers a clear reflection of the shifts taking place in Dutch painting around 1600.

CuratorialText: Visual artist and photographer Viviane Sassen (1972) visited the village of Pikin Slee, deep in the rainforest of Suriname, in 2012. Here, people live without running water, electricity, roads, or the internet. But however remote the village may be, the influence of the modern world is clearly noticeable. For instance, this community also possesses mobile phones and plastic household items. The photo series that Sassen created in Pikin Slee captures the beauty of everyday life in abstract compositions. Largely in black and white, but with the colorful splashes characteristic of Sassen's earlier work. In 2013, the Pikin Slee series was added to the collection of the Rijksmuseum as a modern addition to the collection of photographs of Suriname, which until then had been primarily historical in character.
persona2_adapted: Visual artist and photographer Viviane Sassen (born 1972) visited the village of Pikin Slee, deep in the rainforest of Suriname, in 2012. The people there live without running water, electricity, roads, or the internet. Yet despite the village’s remoteness, the influence of the modern world is clearly visible. For example, this community also has mobile phones and plastic household items. The photo series Sassen created in Pikin Slee captures the beauty of everyday life through compositions that are not realistic but instead emphasize shape, pattern, and form—what art experts call "abstract." Most of the images are in black and white, but they include the bursts of color that are a hallmark of Sassen’s earlier work. In 2013, the Pikin Slee series was added to the Rijksmuseum’s collection, serving as a modern addition to the museum’s photographs of Suriname, which until then had been mostly historical in nature.

CuratorialText: William of Orange (1533–1584) was an ambitious nobleman who rose to become the leader of the Revolt and was revered as the 'father of the fatherland'. At the age of 11, William inherited the French principality of Orange and received the title 'Prince of Orange'. He led a turbulent life with an impressive career. Philip II, the son of Charles V, appointed William as Stadtholder of Holland, Zeeland, and Utrecht, the most important Dutch provinces. The two men clashed. Philip strived for an empire with central authority and one religion: Roman Catholicism. William, on the other hand, stood for freedom of religion and conscience for everyone. This struggle cost him his life on July 10, 1584, from a gunshot.
persona2_adapted: William of Orange (1533–1584) was an ambitious nobleman who rose to become the leader of the Dutch Revolt and was honored as the 'father of the fatherland.' At age 11, he inherited the French territory of Orange and gained the title 'Prince of Orange.' His life was marked by turmoil and an impressive career. Philip II, the son of Emperor Charles V, appointed William as Stadtholder—a kind of governor—of Holland, Zeeland, and Utrecht, the most important provinces in the Dutch region. The two men came into conflict. Philip sought to build an empire with centralized control and a single religion: Roman Catholicism. William, in contrast, championed freedom of religion and personal conscience for everyone. This struggle ultimately cost him his life on July 10, 1584, when he was shot.

CuratorialText: Who is that girl in the painting by Simon Maris, anyway? Little is known about her. However, it is clear that around the year 1906, she modeled for Simon Maris in his studio at Keizersgracht 498. She was about 12 years old at the time. This is evident from photographs Maris took of the young lady in his studio. Documents have been preserved in Simon Maris's archive in which he refers to her as 'Isabella'. Some other paintings Maris made of the girl were also given this name. Nevertheless, it is not certain that this young lady was really named 'Isabella'. It is possible that Maris invented this name for her. Or perhaps she used a pseudonym herself, as models often did at that time.
persona2_adapted: Who is that girl in the painting by Simon Maris, anyway? We know very little about her. What we do know is that around 1906, she worked as a model for Maris in his studio at Keizersgracht 498. At the time, she was about 12 years old. This information comes from photographs Maris took of her in that same studio. In Maris’s personal archive, documents refer to her as “Isabella,” and several other paintings he made of her also carry that name. Still, we cannot be sure that this was her real name. It’s possible Maris invented it. Or perhaps she used a false name, as models often did in that period.

CuratorialText: Amalia of Solms (1602-1675) played a crucial role in increasing the prestige of the House of Orange and the international positioning of the Netherlands. She serves as a sounding board and advisor to her husband, Stadtholder Frederik Hendrik. When he falls ill, she assumes some of his duties and charts a course that heralds the end of the Eighty Years' War. Three years after her husband's death, her son passes away, marking the beginning of the first stadtholderless era. To safeguard the future of the House of Orange, Van Solms exerts national and international influence. She uses art as a political tool. Her collection of paintings, furniture, and Asian objects competes with other European courts. In doing so, she firmly establishes the Netherlands, with the House of Orange at its head, as a European center of art and trade.
persona2_adapted: Amalia of Solms (1602-1675) was a key figure in raising the status of the House of Orange and strengthening the Netherlands' position on the world stage. She acted as a trusted advisor and sounding board for her husband, Stadtholder Frederik Hendrik—a Stadtholder being a kind of national leader or governor in the Dutch Republic at the time. When he fell ill, she took on some of his responsibilities and helped steer a course that led to the end of the Eighty Years' War. Three years after her husband's death, her son also died, which triggered the start of a period known as the first stadtholderless era—a time when the country had no single Stadtholder in charge. To protect the future of the House of Orange, Amalia worked to build influence both within the Netherlands and abroad. She used art as a political tool, assembling a collection of paintings, furniture, and objects from Asia that could rival those of other European royal courts. In doing so, she helped establish the Netherlands—with the House of Orange at its center—as a major European hub for both art and trade.

CuratorialText: Between 1650 and 1672, there was no Stadtholder. The most powerful citizens governed the country. As Grand Pensionary of Holland, Johan de Witt rose to become the most influential politician. He cunningly plays England and France, the Republic's enemies, against each other. Nevertheless, these countries, together with the prince-bishoprics of Münster and Cologne, attack the Netherlands in 1672. His brother, Cornelis de Witt, oversees the military activities as a deputy of the States General. As the foreign troops gain ground, the call for the return of the Stadtholder grows ever louder. Cornelis is arrested on suspicion of conspiracy against the Prince of Orange. When Johan visits him in prison, an incited mob drags the brothers out and kills them. Long after their deaths, both are remembered as champions of 'true freedom'.
persona2_adapted: Between 1650 and 1672, the Netherlands had no Stadtholder—a position similar to a head of state or military leader. Instead, the country was governed by its most powerful citizens. Johan de Witt, serving as Grand Pensionary (the highest political office in the province of Holland), became the most influential politician of the era. He skillfully played England and France, the Republic's enemies, against each other to protect Dutch interests. Despite his efforts, in 1672 these countries, along with the prince-bishoprics of Münster and Cologne, attacked the Netherlands. Johan's brother, Cornelis de Witt, oversaw military operations as a representative of the States General, the national assembly. As foreign troops advanced, public demand for the return of a Stadtholder grew louder. Cornelis was arrested on suspicion of plotting against the Prince of Orange, a rival political figure. When Johan visited him in prison, an angry mob dragged both brothers out and killed them. Long after their deaths, they are remembered as champions of what was called 'true freedom'—a vision of government without a single powerful ruler.

CuratorialText: At the beginning of the 19th century, Hortense de Beauharnais (1783-1837) and Louis Napoleon became the first Queen and King of Holland, appointed by Emperor Napoleon Bonaparte. The royal couple had three sons together, including the future Emperor Napoleon III, but it was an unhappy marriage. Queen Hortense was often present at festivities and official occasions and, partly due to her visits to local businesses, became increasingly popular with the people. That was until her husband, displeased with her popularity, forced her into the background. After Napoleon's defeat, she settled in Switzerland without her husband. At her impressive Arenenberg estate, De Beauharnais, herself a talented musician and composer, organized cultural gatherings that were talked about for a long time afterwards.
persona2_adapted: At the start of the 19th century, Hortense de Beauharnais (1783-1837) and Louis Napoleon became the first Queen and King of Holland, a position granted to them by Emperor Napoleon Bonaparte. The royal couple had three sons together—one of whom would later become Emperor Napoleon III—but their marriage was an unhappy one. Queen Hortense frequently appeared at celebrations and official events, and in part because she visited local businesses, she grew increasingly popular with the Dutch people. That changed when her husband, displeased by her popularity, pushed her out of the public eye. After Napoleon’s defeat, she settled in Switzerland without her husband. At her impressive estate in Arenenberg, Hortense—herself a talented musician and composer—hosted cultural gatherings that people talked about for a long time afterward.

CuratorialText: On July 13, 1885, the new Rijksmuseum officially opened its doors after a construction period of nearly ten years. The building was designed by the architect Pierre Cuypers (1827–1921), a prominent member of the Catholic Church. On the exterior of the Rijksmuseum, Cuypers depicted himself, just as the master builders did in the decoration of cathedrals during the Middle Ages. Over the years, many original decorations in the museum have disappeared. During the last renovation, however, Cuypers' work was restored to its former glory. The Rijksmuseum was not Cuypers' only showpiece. In 1889, 'his' Central Station in Amsterdam was unveiled. In addition to these famous projects, Cuypers focused primarily on churches and monuments.
persona2_adapted: On July 13, 1885, the new Rijksmuseum officially opened its doors after nearly ten years of construction. The building was designed by the architect Pierre Cuypers (1827–1921), a prominent member of the Catholic Church. On the exterior of the museum, Cuypers included a depiction of himself, following a tradition from the Middle Ages when master builders would place their own images in the decorations of cathedrals. Over time, many of the museum’s original decorative elements were lost. However, during the most recent renovation, Cuypers' work was restored to its former splendor. The Rijksmuseum was not Cuypers' only major achievement. In 1889, his design for Amsterdam's Central Station was unveiled. Beyond these well-known projects, Cuypers focused mainly on churches and other monuments.

CuratorialText: Willem Drees (1886-1988) made a career in The Hague as a member of the Social Democratic Workers' Party, which would later become the PvdA. The politician climbed a long ladder of various positions: municipal councilor, alderman, Member of Parliament. In the first cabinet elected after the war, Drees was appointed Minister of Social Affairs. With his Drees Emergency Act, a provision for fixed benefits, he helped the elderly out of financial distress. Drees was popular and became Prime Minister in 1948. It was his task to effectively handle the reconstruction of the Netherlands. Drees proved to be the right man in the right place. With a sense of solidarity, the Netherlands was rebuilt. Under Drees's leadership, until 1958, the foundation of the modern Dutch welfare state was laid. To this day, the statesman is appreciated for his efforts for the Netherlands.
persona2_adapted: Willem Drees (1886-1988) built his political career in The Hague as a member of the Social Democratic Workers' Party, which later became the PvdA (Labour Party). He rose through a series of elected and appointed roles: first as a municipal councilor, then as an alderman, and later as a member of Parliament. In the first government elected after World War II, Drees was appointed Minister of Social Affairs. There, he introduced the Drees Emergency Act, a law that provided fixed financial benefits to help elderly citizens escape poverty. Drees was widely popular and became Prime Minister in 1948. His main task was to manage the country's reconstruction after the war. He proved to be exactly the right person for the job. With a strong sense of shared responsibility, the Netherlands was rebuilt under his leadership. During his time in office, which lasted until 1958, Drees helped create the foundations of the modern Dutch welfare state. To this day, he is remembered and respected for his contributions to the nation.

CuratorialText: At the end of the 16th century, there was a preference in Italy for Mannerism, a style full of complex compositions and figures in intricate, twisted poses. Around 1600, the Italian Baroque artist Michelangelo Merisi da Caravaggio broke radically with this art form. His figures are actually very realistic. Wrinkled faces, unkempt hair, dirty feet: gods and saints are depicted as very ordinary people. Caravaggio has a preference for strong light-dark contrasts, dramatic effects, and compositions with a clear structure. His method spreads rapidly throughout Europe. Especially in Utrecht, around 1620, many followers, known as Caravaggists, can be found. Artists such as Gerard van Honthorst, Hendrick ter Brugghen, and Dirck van Baburen travel to Rome to study Caravaggio's style. And although he never visited Italy, Rembrandt was also inspired by Caravaggio's lighting effects.
persona2_adapted: In the late 1500s, Italian artists favored a style called Mannerism, which featured highly elaborate compositions and figures in complex, contorted poses. Around 1600, the Italian painter Michelangelo Merisi da Caravaggio broke sharply with this tradition. His figures are strikingly realistic: gods and saints appear with wrinkled faces, messy hair, and dirty feet—just like ordinary people. Caravaggio was drawn to strong contrasts between light and shadow, dramatic effects, and clearly organized compositions. His approach quickly spread across Europe. In particular, around 1620, many artists in Utrecht—known as the Caravaggists—adopted his style. Painters like Gerard van Honthorst, Hendrick ter Brugghen, and Dirck van Baburen traveled to Rome to study Caravaggio’s methods. And although Rembrandt never visited Italy, he too was influenced by Caravaggio’s dramatic use of light.

CuratorialText: The 15th and 16th centuries are broadly regarded as the period of the Renaissance, a term that literally means 'rebirth'. In Europe, the arts, literature, and sciences flourish due to a new appreciation of the classical antiquity of the Romans and Greeks. Artists and architects seek ideal proportions. They study Roman architecture and adopt formal languages ​​such as round arches, columns, and pediments. The visual arts increasingly focus on a realistic representation of nature and man, using perspective and anatomical studies. Alongside religious themes, secular themes and mythological representations become popular. The Renaissance spreads from Italy throughout Europe, thanks to the rise of printmaking and printing in the mid-15th century.
persona2_adapted: The 15th and 16th centuries are widely seen as the Renaissance, a word that literally means 'rebirth.' Across Europe, the arts, literature, and sciences thrive as people develop a renewed appreciation for the classical cultures of ancient Greece and Rome. Artists and architects search for ideal proportions—harmonious relationships between parts of a design. They study Roman buildings and adopt recognizable features such as rounded arches, columns, and triangular pediments (the low-pitched gables above doorways or windows). In painting and sculpture, there is a growing focus on depicting nature and the human body realistically, using techniques like perspective (creating the illusion of depth on a flat surface) and detailed studies of human anatomy. Alongside religious subjects, secular themes—those not related to religion—and stories from classical mythology become popular. The Renaissance spreads from Italy across the rest of Europe, helped by the rise of printmaking and the printing press in the mid-1400s.

CuratorialText: The period between the First and Second World Wars is dominated by Art Deco, an international art movement in which tradition and industrial progress go hand in hand. Emerging from Art Nouveau, Art Deco also draws inspiration from stylized plant and floral motifs as well as linear, geometric designs. Additionally, artists look to folk art and art forms from Africa, East Asia, and Central America, a source of inspiration that is further fueled by archaeological finds. The term Art Deco is derived from the 1925 World's Fair in Paris, the Exposition Internationale des Arts Décoratifs et Industriels Modernes.
persona2_adapted: The period between the First and Second World Wars is defined by Art Deco, an international design movement that blends tradition with industrial progress. Growing out of the earlier Art Nouveau style, Art Deco also takes inspiration from simplified plant and flower patterns, as well as clean, straight-lined geometric shapes. Artists of this era also look to folk traditions and art forms from Africa, East Asia, and Central America—a source of inspiration further encouraged by new archaeological discoveries. The name "Art Deco" comes from the 1925 World's Fair in Paris, officially called the *Exposition Internationale des Arts Décoratifs et Industriels Modernes* (International Exhibition of Modern Decorative and Industrial Arts).

CuratorialText: From around 1150, increasingly tall churches rose in and around Paris, with Notre-Dame as the most famous example. It is seen as the beginning of Gothic architecture, a style that spread rapidly throughout the rest of Europe and would continue until shortly after 1500. Church walls became airier thanks to tall pointed arch windows fitted with colorful stained glass; the vaults rested on slender columns and were strikingly ribbed, and on the exterior, the masonry was supported by slender flying buttresses with pinnacles. Under the influence of the Gothic style, sculptors also gave their human, animal, and plant figures a stylized appearance with elegant poses, calligraphic drapery, and graceful gestures. Meanwhile, the forms of Gothic architecture also seeped into other art forms, for example, painting, illuminated manuscripts, metalwork, and furniture. The Rijksmuseum possesses numerous ecclesiastical objects in this style, such as painted altars, silver monstrances, wooden prayer beads, and a small nativity scene.
persona2_adapted:
From around 1150, increasingly tall churches began to rise in and around Paris, with Notre-Dame being the most famous example. This marked the beginning of the Gothic style in architecture, a trend that quickly spread across Europe and lasted until shortly after 1500. Church interiors became lighter and more open, thanks to tall, pointed-arch windows filled with colorful stained glass. The ceilings, or vaults, rested on slender columns and featured visible, rib-like lines that added structure and decoration. On the outside, the stone walls were supported by delicate arched supports called flying buttresses, often topped with pointed spires known as pinnacles. 

The Gothic style also influenced sculpture. Artists gave their figures of people, animals, and plants a stylized look, with elegant poses, flowing, almost calligraphic folds in clothing, and graceful gestures. Beyond architecture and sculpture, Gothic forms appeared in other art forms as well—such as painting, illuminated manuscripts (hand-decorated books), metalwork, and furniture. The Rijksmuseum holds many religious objects in this style, including painted altars, silver monstrances (containers used to display the Eucharist), wooden prayer beads, and a small nativity scene.

CuratorialText: The term Impressionism, which means snapshot, was initially used in the early 1870s as a negative label for the work of a group of Parisian painters. Claude Monet, Edgar Degas, and Berthe Morisot depict reality as seen through the eyes of the artist. The French Impressionists are inspired by the realistic style of the French Barbizon School and the work of the slightly older Dutch artist Johan Barthold Jongkind. They work with bright colors and short, loose brushstrokes. They choose their themes from modern life. Due to the sketchy approach, it sometimes appears as if their paintings were created quickly and spontaneously. This often leads critics to say that the artworks are unfinished. From the late 1880s onwards, appreciation for French Impressionism also grows in the Netherlands.
persona2_adapted: The term *Impressionism*—which literally means “snapshot”—was first used in the early 1870s as a dismissive label for the work of a group of Parisian painters. Artists like Claude Monet, Edgar Degas, and Berthe Morisot aimed to capture reality as it appeared through the artist’s own eyes. These French Impressionists drew inspiration from the realistic style of the earlier French Barbizon School (a group of painters who focused on rural landscapes) and from the work of the slightly older Dutch artist Johan Barthold Jongkind. They painted with bright colors and used short, loose brushstrokes—a technique that gives their scenes a sense of movement and immediacy. Their subjects were drawn from modern, everyday life. Because of this sketch-like approach, their paintings often look as though they were made quickly and spontaneously, which led many critics to claim the works were unfinished. By the late 1880s, however, appreciation for French Impressionism began to grow in the Netherlands as well.

CuratorialText: At the end of the 19th century, a new art style emerges. It is a reaction by artists who rebelled against the historicism prevailing at the time, which harked back to the styles of previous centuries. The new art, aptly named Art Nouveau, is characterized by natural forms, such as flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. The emphasis lies on flowing lines, geometric shapes, and asymmetrical compositions. With Paris, Brussels, and Munich as centers, Art Nouveau spread as an international movement in Western Europe and the United States. During this period, which lasted from approximately 1890 until the beginning of the First World War in 1914, Art Nouveau became visible in all fields of the arts.
persona2_adapted: In the late 1800s, a new artistic style emerged. It was a direct response by artists who rejected the dominant trend of the time—historicism, which simply meant copying the styles of earlier centuries. Instead, these artists wanted something fresh and original. This new style, fittingly called Art Nouveau (French for "new art"), drew inspiration from nature, featuring flowers, leaves, animals, and insects. Its key visual traits were smooth, flowing lines, geometric shapes, and arrangements that were deliberately unbalanced or asymmetrical. With major creative hubs in Paris, Brussels, and Munich, Art Nouveau quickly spread as an international movement across Western Europe and the United States. Lasting from about 1890 until the start of World War I in 1914, this style appeared in everything from paintings and architecture to furniture and everyday objects.

CuratorialText: Rembrandt is not only a successful painter, but also exceptionally talented as a draftsman and etcher. The Rijksmuseum collection contains approximately 1,300 prints by his hand, including sometimes multiple copies and different states of a single print. The subjects of his etchings range from biblical and mythological scenes to everyday scenes, landscapes, and (self-)portraits. Rembrandt's development in technique, style, and artistic approach can be clearly seen in these works. His loose drawing style and the high-contrast rendering of light and shadow are unique. The choice to depict the most emotional moments is also remarkable for his time. It makes his work human and relatable. Rembrandt has thus had a major influence on artists of his own time and for a long time thereafter.
persona2_adapted: Rembrandt was not only a highly successful painter, but also an exceptionally gifted draftsman and printmaker. The Rijksmuseum holds around 1,300 prints made by his hand, including multiple copies and different versions of the same print. His etchings cover a wide range of subjects: biblical and mythological stories, everyday life, landscapes, and portraits of himself and others. These works clearly show how Rembrandt’s technique, style, and artistic approach evolved over time. His loose, sketch-like drawing style and the dramatic contrast between light and dark are distinctive features. For his era, it was also unusual to choose the most emotionally charged moments to depict. This choice makes his work feel human and relatable. As a result, Rembrandt had a major influence on artists of his own time and for many years afterward.

CuratorialText: Under the patronage of Augustus the Strong, Elector of Saxony, alchemist Johann Friedrich Böttger succeeded in becoming the first in Europe to produce porcelain in 1708. Not long afterwards, the first European porcelain factory was established in Meissen. The factory played an important role in the dream of August the Strong: a Japanese Palace, in which he wanted to exhibit his collection of Asian and Meissen porcelain. Since the end of the 16th century, porcelain, also known as 'white gold', had been imported from Asia and collected by the wealthy members of European courts and the elite. The fact that porcelain was now also being produced in Europe is one of the most important moments in the history of the European applied arts. The Rijksmuseum holds one of the most important collections of Meissen porcelain, including pieces from the factory's early production from the collection of Margarethe and Franz Oppenheimer.
persona2_adapted: Under the patronage of Augustus the Strong, the Elector of Saxony, the alchemist Johann Friedrich Böttger became the first person in Europe to successfully produce porcelain in 1708. Shortly afterward, the first European porcelain factory was established in Meissen. This factory played a key role in Augustus the Strong’s grand vision: a Japanese Palace where he planned to display his collection of both Asian and Meissen porcelain. Since the late 1500s, porcelain—often called 'white gold'—had been imported from Asia and collected by wealthy members of European courts and the elite. The fact that porcelain could now also be made in Europe marks one of the most significant milestones in the history of European decorative arts. The Rijksmuseum holds one of the most important collections of Meissen porcelain, including early pieces from the factory’s production that once belonged to Margarethe and Franz Oppenheimer.

CuratorialText: The Rijksmuseum has been collecting Asian ceramics since its founding in 1885. The largest part of this is Chinese (approx. 2,300 pieces) and Japanese (approx. 600), plus another 50 pieces from other Asian countries. After all, the Dutch have been importing porcelain from Asia since the founding of the Dutch East India Company (VOC, 1602). From the 17th century onwards, thin-fired porcelain with Chinese blue decorations became immensely popular. Consequently, Chinese and Japanese porcelain play an important role in Dutch interiors. Since 1952, the collection of the Royal Society of Friends of Asian Art (KVVAK) has also been housed in the Rijksmuseum, comprising pieces that are part of art production in Asia: tea ceramics from Japan, Song bowls and Tang funerary sculptures from China, and ceramics from Southeast Asia and Korea.
persona2_adapted:
Since its founding in 1885, the Rijksmuseum has built a large collection of Asian ceramics. The majority comes from China (about 2,300 pieces) and Japan (about 600), with an additional 50 pieces from other Asian countries. This focus is no accident: the Dutch have been importing porcelain from Asia ever since the Dutch East India Company (VOC) was founded in 1602. Starting in the 1600s, a particular type of porcelain—thin, fired at high temperatures, and decorated with blue Chinese designs—became wildly popular. As a result, Chinese and Japanese porcelain came to play a major role in Dutch home interiors.

Since 1952, the museum has also housed the collection of the Royal Society of Friends of Asian Art (KVVAK). This collection includes pieces that represent different traditions of Asian ceramic art: Japanese tea ceramics, Chinese bowls from the Song dynasty and funerary sculptures from the Tang dynasty, as well as ceramics from Southeast Asia and Korea.

CuratorialText: From 1610 onwards, around one hundred thousand pieces of Chinese porcelain were imported into the Netherlands annually. To withstand the Chinese competition, Dutch potters began to refine their products. The earthenware becomes thinner, whiter, and shinier. Blue paintings are applied to a base of white tin glaze, a color scheme strongly associated with Asian porcelain. This earthenware is called 'plateel' or 'faience', but in the 17th and 18th centuries it acquires the name 'Hollants porceleyn'. From 1650 onwards, Delft becomes the center of earthenware production, hence the eventual name 'Delft earthenware'. During the same period, trade in Chinese porcelain comes to a virtual standstill due to the civil war in China (1644-1684). This gives Delft earthenware the opportunity to flourish and gain popularity among the European elite.
persona2_adapted: Between 1610 and the mid-1600s, roughly one hundred thousand pieces of Chinese porcelain were shipped to the Netherlands each year. To compete with this influx, Dutch potters began improving their own ceramics. Their earthenware became thinner, whiter, and shinier. They painted blue designs onto a white background made from a glaze containing tin—a color combination that strongly reminded buyers of Asian porcelain. This type of pottery was called *plateel* or *faience*, but during the 1600s and 1700s, it came to be known as "Hollants porceleyn" (Dutch porcelain). After 1650, the city of Delft became the main center for this kind of earthenware, which is why it is now commonly called Delftware. Around the same time, trade in Chinese porcelain nearly stopped because of a civil war in China (1644–1684). This pause gave Delft earthenware a chance to thrive and become highly desirable among Europe’s wealthy and powerful.

CuratorialText: The bronze collection of the Rijksmuseum contains a collection of small sculptures from the Renaissance, primarily from Italy. A number of these originated in the late 15th century in the university city of Padua. These are small bronze utilitarian objects inspired by classical antiquity: candlesticks, perfume burners, oil lamps, and inkwells, all decorated with mythological figures. Many small workshops cast such luxury utensils in series, but unique sculptures and objects were also made in bronze. A good example is the large candlestick that Verrocchio designed in 1469 for Palazzo Vecchio, the city hall of Florence. Bronze casting in the style of classical antiquity also flourished in other Italian cities, such as Rome and Venice. In the 16th and 17th centuries, these figurines found their way into the studies of humanist scholars, art cabinets of wealthy citizens, and Kunstkammern of princes and nobility.
persona2_adapted: The Rijksmuseum’s collection of bronze works includes small sculptures from the Renaissance, most of them made in Italy. Some of these objects date back to the late 1400s and were created in the university city of Padua. They are small, everyday items made of bronze, inspired by the art and design of ancient Greece and Rome: candlesticks, perfume burners, oil lamps, and inkwells, all decorated with figures from mythology. Many small workshops produced these luxury items in large numbers, but unique sculptures and objects were also cast in bronze. A notable example is the large candlestick designed in 1469 by Verrocchio for the Palazzo Vecchio, Florence’s city hall. The technique of casting bronze in the style of ancient Greece and Rome also flourished in other Italian cities, such as Rome and Venice. During the 1500s and 1600s, these small figures made their way into the private studies of humanist scholars, the art collections of wealthy citizens, and the curiosity cabinets of princes and nobles.

CuratorialText: Originally, artists in seascapes primarily captured historical events at sea, so that posterity could relive the heroic deeds. 'War correspondent' and artist Willem van de Velde I created eyewitness paintings of famous Dutch naval battles. His son Willem was a master at capturing calm or, conversely, stormy seas. In the 18th and 19th centuries, Ludolf Bakhuyzen and Wijnand Nuijen opted for a dramatized depiction of ships in distress. Bakhuyzen even went out to sea to study weather effects up close. Thanks to painters such as Andreas Schelfhout, Louis Meijer, and father and son Schotel, Dutch art abroad became almost synonymous with the seascape. For the artists of the Hague School, the effect of light and cloudy skies played a major role, and they paid more attention to the harsh life of fishermen.
persona2_adapted: Originally, artists who painted the sea focused mainly on recording historical events that took place on the water, so that future generations could imagine or remember heroic moments. One such artist, Willem van de Velde I, worked like a war reporter, creating paintings based on what he saw during famous Dutch naval battles. His son, also named Willem, was especially skilled at painting both calm and stormy seas. In the 1700s and 1800s, artists like Ludolf Bakhuyzen and Wijnand Nuijen chose to depict ships in danger in a more dramatic way. Bakhuyzen even went out to sea to study weather effects up close. Thanks to painters such as Andreas Schelfhout, Louis Meijer, and the father-and-son team Schotel, Dutch art became almost synonymous with seascapes in other countries. For the artists of the Hague School—a group of Dutch painters active in the late 1800s—the way light played across the sky and clouds was very important, and they also focused more on the difficult lives of fishermen.

CuratorialText: In the 17th century, the marksmen of the civic guard were regularly immortalized in group portraits. At that time, the powerful cities themselves were responsible for security. The militia ensured the maintenance of order and security. Every adult man up to the age of 60 is expected to join, although he must be able to purchase his own weaponry. The group portraits are a costly undertaking, as each marksman pays for his own portrait. The earliest marksman portraits date from the 16th century and are rather static. In the course of the 17th century, painters opted for a somewhat looser composition, with Rembrandt's Night Watch and the lively Banquet of the Marksmen by Bartholomeus van der Helst as highlights. Not only marksmen's guilds commission group portraits; other urban, provincial, and national organizations and individuals also commission them.
persona2_adapted:
In the 17th century, members of the civic guard—local volunteer militias responsible for keeping order—were regularly commemorated in group portraits. At the time, powerful cities handled their own security, and every adult man up to age 60 was expected to join the militia, though he had to buy his own weapons. These group portraits were expensive projects, since each guard member paid for his own likeness to be included.

The earliest such portraits date from the 16th century and tend to be quite stiff and formal. Over the course of the 1600s, however, painters began arranging their subjects more naturally and dynamically. Two famous examples are Rembrandt's *The Night Watch* and Bartholomeus van der Helst's lively *Banquet of the Marksmen*. It wasn't only marksmen's guilds that commissioned these group portraits; other city, regional, and national organizations—as well as private individuals—also hired artists to create them.

CuratorialText: Approximately 230 paintings in the collection of the Rijksmuseum are still lifes. As early as the 16th century, Flemish painters specialized in this genre. These are arranged displays featuring, for example, shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals, but also flowers, insects, and sometimes a person in the background. Life literally stands still for a moment. Separate specializations soon emerged within the genre. For instance, Rachel Ruysch primarily painted flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda enjoyed creating arrangements with food and eating and drinking utensils. Another variant, the vanitas still life, which often depicts a skull, refers to the transience of life and the material world.
persona2_adapted: Approximately 230 paintings in the Rijksmuseum’s collection are still lifes. This type of painting, in which artists arrange objects for careful observation, first became a specialty of Flemish painters in the 1500s. These works present carefully composed displays of items such as shells, books, food and drink, tableware, skulls, or dead animals—along with flowers, insects, and occasionally a person in the background. In these scenes, life truly seems to pause for a moment. Soon, artists began focusing on specific subjects within this category. For example, Rachel Ruysch concentrated on painting flowers, while Pieter Claesz and Willem Heda specialized in arrangements of food, drinking vessels, and dining utensils. Another type, known as the vanitas still life, often includes a skull and is meant to remind viewers of the fleeting nature of life and the emptiness of material possessions.

CuratorialText: The Rijksmuseum has an enormous collection of portraits from the late Middle Ages to the present day. In addition to their artistic value, these more than 1,700 portraits give the past a face. They describe what people looked like: what clothing and jewelry they wore, what their hairstyles were like, and sometimes what the interiors looked like at the time. There are also significant differences, as each century emphasizes different aspects. In the Renaissance, portraits seem to become increasingly realistic, whereas artists from the 20th and 21st centuries move away from that realism. Throughout the centuries, governments, organizations, and individuals have commissioned portraits for all kinds of occasions: to confirm profession or status, for a wedding, or as a memorial after someone's death. The Rijksmuseum collection also contains many self-portraits by famous painters such as Jan Steen, Rembrandt, and Van Gogh.
persona2_adapted: The Rijksmuseum holds an extensive collection of portraits spanning from the late Middle Ages to the present day. Beyond their artistic merit, these more than 1,700 portraits give a human face to history. They show us what people looked like—the clothing and jewelry they wore, their hairstyles, and sometimes the interiors of their homes. Yet the portraits also reveal striking differences across time, as each century highlights different priorities. During the Renaissance, for instance, portraits become increasingly lifelike, while artists from the 20th and 21st centuries deliberately move away from that kind of realism. Over the centuries, governments, organizations, and individuals have commissioned portraits for many reasons: to confirm a person’s profession or social standing, to mark a wedding, or to serve as a memorial after death. The Rijksmuseum collection also includes many self-portraits by famous painters such as Jan Steen, Rembrandt, and Van Gogh.

CuratorialText: Genre paintings are paintings depicting scenes from everyday life. They are lively scenes that often take place in kitchens, living rooms, inns, and markets. They capture people in their natural environment: children romping, a family huddled together in the kitchen, or a merchant hawking his wares. The first genre paintings emerged in 16th-century Flemish painting, but in the Netherlands, genre art did not flourish until a century later. The works of Johannes Vermeer, Jan Steen, and Judith Leyster became famous. They often concealed a mischievous content or, conversely, a moral message in their work. The term 'genre paintings' did not emerge until the 19th century, when the popularity of genre art reached a peak. The Rijksmuseum collection also includes works by 19th-century painters such as Jozef Israëls, known for his lively scenes of farmers and fishermen.
persona2_adapted: Genre paintings show scenes from everyday life. These are lively images that often take place in kitchens, living rooms, inns, or markets. They capture people in their natural surroundings: children playing, a family gathered together in the kitchen, or a merchant selling his goods. The first such paintings appeared in 16th-century Flemish art, but in the Netherlands, this type of scene did not become popular until a century later. Works by Johannes Vermeer, Jan Steen, and Judith Leyster became well known. These artists often hid a playful or mischievous meaning in their work—or, on the other hand, a moral lesson. The term "genre paintings" itself was not used until the 19th century, when this kind of art was at the height of its popularity. The Rijksmuseum collection also includes works by 19th-century painters such as Jozef Israëls, who is known for his lively scenes of farmers and fishermen.

CuratorialText: A history piece depicts a memorable historical story, which often originates from a written source. This can be a story from the Bible or mythology, but also events from history. Multiple figures are often visible in these narrative pieces. For instance, around 1530, Jan Jansz. Mostaert depicted a scene from the conquest of America, while in 1824, Jan Willem Pieneman captured The Battle of Waterloo , the largest painting in the Rijksmuseum. In the 17th century, painters also chose Roman scenes as a metaphor for the political situation in the Netherlands. For instance, the Batavian revolt against the Romans, depicted by Ferdinand Bol in the painting Claudius Civilis, refers to the struggle against the Spaniards.
persona2_adapted: A history piece tells a memorable story from the past, usually based on a written source. That source might be a story from the Bible or mythology, but it could also come from actual historical events. These narrative works often include several figures. For example, around 1530, Jan Jansz. Mostaert painted a scene from the conquest of America. In 1824, Jan Willem Pieneman created *The Battle of Waterloo*, the largest painting in the Rijksmuseum. During the 1600s, artists also used Roman scenes as a way to comment on the political situation in the Netherlands. For instance, Ferdinand Bol’s painting *Claudius Civilis* shows the Batavian revolt against the Romans, which was meant to reference the Dutch struggle against the Spanish.

CuratorialText: “We, the people of Indonesia, hereby declare the independence of Indonesia.” By uttering these words, the nationalist leader Sukarno declared his country, Indonesia, independent on August 17, 1945. Former colonizer the Netherlands refuses to recognize that independence and attempts to reoccupy Indonesia. In defense of its independence, Indonesia wages war against the Netherlands on its own soil. From the beginning, Indonesia seeks and receives support from the international community. Under international pressure, the Netherlands is forced to recognize Indonesia's independence in 1949. In 1950, Indonesia becomes a member state of the United Nations, and the international confirmation of independence becomes a reality.
persona2_adapted: “We, the people of Indonesia, hereby declare the independence of Indonesia.” With these words, the nationalist leader Sukarno proclaimed his country’s independence on August 17, 1945. The former colonial power, the Netherlands, refuses to accept this declaration and attempts to reclaim control over Indonesia. In order to defend its newly declared independence, Indonesia fights a war against the Netherlands on its own land. From the very beginning, Indonesia seeks and receives support from other nations around the world. Under growing international pressure, the Netherlands is eventually forced to recognize Indonesia’s independence in 1949. The following year, in 1950, Indonesia becomes a member of the United Nations, marking the international community’s formal acknowledgment of its sovereignty.

CuratorialText: Water is crucial for the development of the Netherlands as a trading nation. The economy grows thanks to the raw materials that enter via rivers and the world's oceans. Consider bulk goods such as grain and timber from the Baltic region, wool from England, wine from France, and raw salt from the Iberian Peninsula. Using these raw materials, the Dutch produce items such as beer, cloth (woolen fabric), fine salt (for the herring trade), weapons, and ships. Many of these products are subsequently exported, often again by water. Cities are important links in this international trade. In the 15th and 16th centuries, Antwerp is the largest port city of the Netherlands. After the (re)conquest by the Spaniards during the Dutch Revolt, Holland, and especially Amsterdam, takes over the role as a staple market. International trade leads to the emergence of a financial market, significant employment, and strong urban growth.
persona2_adapted: Water was essential to the Netherlands' rise as a trading nation. The economy grew because raw materials arrived by river and ocean from around the world. Think of bulk goods like grain and timber from the Baltic region, wool from England, wine from France, and raw salt from the Iberian Peninsula. The Dutch used these materials to produce goods such as beer, cloth (woolen fabric), fine salt (used in the herring trade), weapons, and ships. Many of these products were then exported, often again by water. Cities played a key role in this international trade network. In the 15th and 16th centuries, Antwerp was the largest port city in the Netherlands. After the Spanish (re)conquest during the Dutch Revolt, Holland—and especially Amsterdam—took over as the main trading hub, or "staple market." This international trade led to the development of a financial market, created many jobs, and drove rapid urban growth.

CuratorialText: What is money actually worth? Why does a small piece of metal worth a few euro cents suddenly have a much higher value due to the '2 euro' imprint? Whoever holds the right to mint coins chooses the appearance, the amount of precious metal, and the value. Through trade contacts, gold and silver coins spread across the world for centuries. Coins with little gold or silver circulated primarily locally, because once abroad, the metal value was of paramount importance. The Amsterdam Exchange Bank was for a long time a key hub for the trade in precious metals. As early as the Eighty Years' War, a large portion of the silver that Spain obtained from Spanish America ended up in Amsterdam. Until well into the 18th century, veritable silver fleets sailed from Spain to the Republic. From Amsterdam, money traveled the world, such as to Asia via the VOC.
persona2_adapted: What gives money its value? How can a small piece of metal worth just a few euro cents suddenly be worth far more, simply because it bears the imprint "2 euro"? The authority that controls coin production decides the design, the amount of precious metal used, and the coin's stated value. For centuries, gold and silver coins spread across the globe through trade. Coins that contained little gold or silver mostly stayed within local regions, because when they traveled abroad, the actual metal content mattered most. The Amsterdam Exchange Bank long served as a major center for trading precious metals. As early as the Eighty Years' War, a large portion of the silver that Spain obtained from its American colonies ended up in Amsterdam. Until well into the 1700s, regular silver fleets sailed from Spain to the Dutch Republic. From Amsterdam, money traveled the world—including to Asia, via the Dutch East India Company (VOC).

CuratorialText: William of Orange is regarded as the 'Father of the Fatherland' because of his leading role in the struggle for independence. The House of Orange owes its princely title to the insignificant French city-state of Orange on the Rhône, yet manages to carve out a place for itself among the great European royal houses through its wealth and prestige. The wives of the stadtholders play an important role in these networks. Nevertheless, it seems to be the end for the House of Orange at the end of the 18th century: Stadtholder William V flees to England, and the Batavian Republic is proclaimed. But in 1813, his eldest son returns and becomes King William I. From that moment on, the Netherlands is a 'constitutional monarchy,' and a member of the House of Orange-Nassau has always sat on the throne.
persona2_adapted: William of Orange is known as the 'Father of the Fatherland' because he led the fight for Dutch independence. The House of Orange gets its princely title from a small French city-state called Orange, located on the Rhône River. Despite this modest origin, the family managed to earn a place among Europe's great royal houses through its wealth and influence. The wives of the stadtholders—a Dutch term for leaders who acted as heads of state—played an important role in building these connections. However, by the end of the 18th century, the House of Orange seemed to be finished: Stadtholder William V fled to England, and the Batavian Republic was declared. But in 1813, his eldest son returned and became King William I. From that point on, the Netherlands became a constitutional monarchy—a system where the monarch's power is limited by law—and a member of the House of Orange-Nassau has always held the throne.

CuratorialText: Whose object was it before it came to the museum? The lives, relationships, and circumstances of the owners tell something about the significance of the object. Provenance research reveals stories of pre-war owners and reminds us of the people who were persecuted during the Holocaust. Even if objects were not looted, their provenance provides insight into their history. The Rijksmuseum collection primarily tells the stories of collectors from the period between 1933 and 1945. Research into the collection can therefore not possibly provide a complete picture of all persecuted owners. After all, many everyday objects did not end up in museums or are more difficult to identify. Nevertheless, the objects in the Rijksmuseum remind us of the diversity of pre-war Europe. Provenance research shows that the people persecuted by the Nazis actively contributed to Dutch culture and history. Objects in museums are still the silent witnesses of this today.
persona2_adapted: Whose object was it before it came to the museum? The lives, relationships, and circumstances of the owners tell us something about why the object mattered. Research into an object's history—called provenance—reveals the stories of its pre-war owners and reminds us of the people who were persecuted during the Holocaust. Even when objects were not stolen, their history still offers insight into their past. The Rijksmuseum collection mostly tells the stories of collectors from the period between 1933 and 1945. Because of this, research into the collection cannot give a complete picture of all persecuted owners. Many everyday objects never ended up in museums, or are harder to identify. Still, the objects in the Rijksmuseum remind us of the diversity of pre-war Europe. Provenance research shows that the people persecuted by the Nazis actively contributed to Dutch culture and history. Objects in museums remain the silent witnesses of this today.

CuratorialText: Before the Europeans arrive, centuries-old trade routes already exist around the Indian Ocean. The Dutch East India Company (VOC) forcibly expands these networks and exploits areas such as the Moluccas and Sri Lanka for spices. Batavia (Jakarta) and the Cape of Good Hope become important centers for sugar, coffee, and rice. In the Atlantic region, Dutch merchants trade in products such as sugar and coffee, which leads to the establishment of the Dutch West India Company (WIC). Over time, slave labor and the slave trade take place at all trading posts. Even for a craftsman in the Netherlands, there is money to be made from colonial trade, for example as a supplier of products for the slave trade or for daily life in the colonies. The transport of the products to Europe and their further processing also generates revenue. In this way, colonial trade stimulates the national economy.
persona2_adapted: Before European arrival, trade routes around the Indian Ocean had already existed for centuries. The Dutch East India Company (VOC) aggressively expanded these networks and took control of regions like the Moluccas (in present-day Indonesia) and Sri Lanka to obtain spices. Batavia (modern-day Jakarta) and the Cape of Good Hope (in South Africa) grew into key centers for producing sugar, coffee, and rice. In the Atlantic region, Dutch merchants traded goods such as sugar and coffee, which led to the founding of the Dutch West India Company (WIC). Over time, enslaved labor and the slave trade became part of operations at all trading posts.
