Even a craftsman living in the Netherlands could profit from colonial trade—for instance, by supplying products used in the slave trade or for daily life in the colonies. Transporting these goods to Europe and processing them further also generated income. In this way, colonial trade boosted the national economy.

CuratorialText: The Netherlands was a colonial power for more than 350 years and established trading posts and colonies worldwide in the 17th century, including in Indonesia, South Africa, Sri Lanka, South America, and the Caribbean. Dutch colonial settlement often took place through the conclusion of trade contracts, but also through military force. The Rijksmuseum possesses many (art) objects that were brought to the Netherlands during and after this period, ranging from war booty to items acquired through trade. It is important to be open about the history of this collection and to reflect on the role of the Netherlands in the colonial past. Through research and collaboration with countries of origin, the Rijksmuseum aims to provide a nuanced and diverse picture of this shared past.
persona2_adapted: The Netherlands held colonial power for over 350 years, and during the 17th century, it set up trading posts and colonies around the world—including in Indonesia, South Africa, Sri Lanka, South America, and the Caribbean. Dutch colonial expansion often happened through trade agreements, but also through the use of military force. The Rijksmuseum holds many objects—both artworks and everyday items—that were brought to the Netherlands during and after this period. Some were taken as war booty, while others were acquired through trade. It is important to be open about the history of this collection and to reflect on the Netherlands' role in its colonial past. Through research and collaboration with the countries where these objects originally came from, the Rijksmuseum aims to offer a nuanced and diverse understanding of this shared history.

CuratorialText: For 750 years, Amsterdam has been a city that grows, flourishes, and buzzes. Residents from all corners of the world have made the city what it is today. The first inhabitants of Amsterdam were farmers and fishermen, living along the River Amstel. In the late Middle Ages, Amsterdam grew into a small port city amidst the peatlands. Partly due to the establishment of the trading companies VOC and WIC, the city transformed rapidly into a global metropolis with more than 200,000 inhabitants during the 17th century, where almost every Amsterdammer was directly or indirectly involved in colonial trade. From rope makers to cartographers, from notaries to confectioners. To manage the population growth, a plan was drawn up to expand the city. It was the beginning of the famous canal belt.
persona2_adapted: For 750 years, Amsterdam has been a city that grows, thrives, and hums with activity. People from all over the world have shaped it into the city it is today. The earliest residents were farmers and fishermen who lived along the River Amstel. During the late Middle Ages, Amsterdam developed into a small port town set in the peatlands. In part because of the founding of the VOC and WIC—two major Dutch trading companies—the city quickly transformed into a global hub, home to more than 200,000 people by the 1600s. Nearly every Amsterdam resident at the time was directly or indirectly involved in colonial trade, from rope makers and mapmakers to notaries and candy makers. To manage this rapid population growth, city leaders drew up plans to expand Amsterdam. This marked the beginning of what would become the famous ring of canals.

CuratorialText: The tulip originates from Turkey and was introduced to Europe in the late 16th century. The flower proved to thrive particularly well in the sandy soil of the Dutch coastal region. In the 17th century, traders of tulips and other exotic flowers began publishing 'Florilegia', books with detailed illustrations serving as catalogues of their wares. Enthusiasts of unusual flowers and plants could also turn to painters and draftsmen. Flower still lifes became a popular genre in art. Real flowers wilt, but paintings could serve as durable substitutes. Moreover, these flower still lifes sometimes cost even less than the flowers depicted on the canvas.
persona2_adapted: The tulip originally came from Turkey and was brought to Europe in the late 1500s. It grew especially well in the sandy soil of the Dutch coastal region. During the 1600s, traders who sold tulips and other rare flowers started publishing 'Florilegia'—books filled with detailed illustrations that acted like catalogues of what they had for sale. People who loved unusual flowers and plants could also hire painters or sketch artists. Paintings of flowers, known as flower still lifes, became a popular type of artwork. Real flowers eventually wilt and die, but paintings could last much longer as stand-ins. Interestingly, these flower paintings sometimes cost even less than the actual flowers shown in the picture.

CuratorialText: Throughout history, the role of pets changes significantly, from useful working animals to beloved companions. It was not until between 500 and 1500 that noblemen began keeping dogs for their company. In paintings from the 17th century, these four-legged creatures often appear in that role. For a long time, cats were primarily used as mouse exterminators and had a bad reputation due to their medieval association with witchcraft. It was not until the Victorian era that people accepted cats as pets. A beautiful pedigree cat, such as a British Shorthair or the Persian Longhair, became a status symbol for wealthy citizens. From the 19th century onwards, dogs and cats, and later birds and fish, became everyday pets for all social classes. Artist Henriëtte Ronner-Knip (1821–1909) specialized in painting cats, which brought her fame. She was regularly commissioned to immortalize the beloved pets of aristocratic and bourgeois families.
persona2_adapted: Throughout history, the role of pets has changed dramatically, shifting from useful working animals to beloved companions. It wasn't until the period between 500 and 1500 that noblemen began keeping dogs simply for their company. In paintings from the 1600s, these four-legged creatures often appear in that role. For a long time, cats were mainly used to catch mice and had a bad reputation because, in the Middle Ages, they were linked to witchcraft. It wasn't until the Victorian era—the time of Queen Victoria's reign in the 1800s—that people began to accept cats as pets. A beautiful purebred cat, such as a British Shorthair or a Persian Longhair, became a status symbol for wealthy citizens. From the 1800s onward, dogs and cats, and later birds and fish, became everyday pets for people of all social classes. The artist Henriëtte Ronner-Knip (1821–1909) specialized in painting cats, which brought her fame. Wealthy families from the upper and middle classes regularly hired her to create lasting portraits of their beloved pets.

CuratorialText: Sculpture. From the Middle Ages to the present day, in every shape and size and encompassing a wide range of materials. This expanding online catalog presents a comprehensive overview of the latest scholarly insights into the Rijksmuseum's vast and highly diverse collection of European sculpture. This online catalog includes every work of European sculpture in the Rijksmuseum's art collection. The most recently published catalog of sculpture dates from 1973. Prompting a thorough update of this valuable, but by now outdated reference work was the ever-growing body of art historical research and the long list of acquisitions made in recent decades.   The Rijksmuseum (and its precursors: the Royal Cabinet of Rareities and later the Dutch Museum of History and Art) began acquiring sculpture on a systematic basis only in the last quarter of the nineteenth century. Additions to the collection prior to this time are few in number. One early example is the Dutch government's purchase ofArtus Quellinus's magnificent marble bust portrait of Andries de Graeff in 1817. Initially, the museum's sculpture acquisition policy was primarily oriented to works of national interest. It was not until the twentieth century that this focus gradually began to shift, largely thanks to the efforts of Adriaan Pit (museum director from 1898 to 1917) and Jaap Leeuwenberg (curator of sculpture from 1948 to 1969 and compiler of the 1973 catalogue), who sought to actively acquire international works. As a result, sculptures of non-Netherlandish origin today make up approximately half of the collection. This international scope received an additional impulse when, in 1952, the Dutch government charged the Rijksmuseum with the care of Italian and German works formerly in the possession of Fritz Mannheimer and recovered from Germany after the Second World War. This enhancement of the collection has since served to stimulate the museum's policy of acquiring international works.
persona2_adapted:
**Sculpture at the Rijksmuseum: A Living Collection**

From the Middle Ages to the present day, sculpture has taken countless forms and sizes, made from an extraordinary range of materials. This growing online catalogue offers a broad look at the latest scholarly understanding of the Rijksmuseum's vast and highly varied collection of European sculpture. It includes every European sculpture in the museum's art collection.

The most recent printed catalogue of the sculpture collection was published in 1973. That valuable but now outdated reference work needed a thorough update, driven by the steady increase in art-historical research and the many new acquisitions made over recent decades.

The Rijksmuseum (and its earlier institutions—the Royal Cabinet of Rarities and later the Dutch Museum of History and Art) only began collecting sculpture in a systematic way during the last quarter of the nineteenth century. Before that, very few pieces were added. One early example is the Dutch government's purchase in 1817 of Artus Quellinus's magnificent marble bust of Andries de Graeff.

At first, the museum's approach to acquiring sculpture focused mainly on works of national importance. It was only in the twentieth century that this focus gradually shifted, largely thanks to the efforts of two directors: Adriaan Pit (museum director from 1898 to 1917) and Jaap Leeuwenberg (curator of sculpture from 1948 to 1969, who also compiled the 1973 catalogue). They actively sought to bring in works from other countries. As a result, sculptures from outside the Netherlands now make up about half of the collection.

This international scope received a further boost in 1952, when the Dutch government asked the Rijksmuseum to care for Italian and German works that had once belonged to Fritz Mannheimer and were recovered from Germany after the Second World War. This addition to the collection has since encouraged the museum to continue its policy of acquiring international works.

CuratorialText:
The Rijksmuseum holds a world-class collection of Japanese works on paper: woodblock prints, illustrated books and drawings. Among the almost 4000 prints, more than 800 are exclusive, privately published surimono. This online catalog features 203 surimono donated to the museum between 1991 and 1999 by the collector JHW Goslings (1943-2011). The entries in this catalog are taken from Matthi Forrer's 2013 collection catalog Surimono in the Rijksmuseum Amsterdam.
Poetry and image combined
“Surimono (literally 'printed things') constitute one of the most delicate genres in Japanese printmaking. This genre fascinates because it combines poetry and image and because it presents a pictorial puzzle, which provides the viewer with a particular insight into the intellectual and literary world of the late 18th- and early 19th-century Edo (today's Tokyo). Major artists such asKatsushika Hokusai,Utagawa Kunisada,Toyota HokkeiandYashima Gakutei, to name but a few, provided imagery to accompany the poetic exploits of poetry club members.
persona2_adapted:
The Rijksmuseum holds a world-class collection of Japanese works on paper: woodblock prints, illustrated books, and drawings. Among its nearly 4,000 prints, more than 800 are exclusive, privately published *surimono*—a special type of print made for private occasions rather than public sale. This online catalog features 203 *surimono* donated to the museum between 1991 and 1999 by the collector JHW Goslings (1943–2011). The entries in this catalog are taken from Matthi Forrer's 2013 collection catalog *Surimono in the Rijksmuseum Amsterdam*.

**Poetry and Image Combined**

*Surimono* (literally "printed things") represent one of the most delicate forms of Japanese printmaking. This genre fascinates because it brings together poetry and visual art, and because it presents a kind of visual riddle. Solving this riddle offers the viewer a special window into the intellectual and literary world of late 18th- and early 19th-century Edo (modern-day Tokyo). Major artists such as Katsushika Hokusai, Utagawa Kunisada, Toyota Hokkei, and Yashima Gakutei—to name just a few—created images to accompany the poetic efforts of poetry club members.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Maria Trip (1619-83), sister of Jacobus Trip and later the wife of Balthasar Coymans. Standing, half-length, facing left. Her left arm rests on a banister, holding a fan.
persona2_adapted: Portrait of Maria Trip (1619–83), the sister of Jacobus Trip, who later married Balthasar Coymans. She is shown standing, from the waist up, facing to the left. Her left arm rests on a railing, and she holds a fan.

CuratorialText: He sits with outstretched wings on a cloud placed on a round plinth, holding his right index finger to the mouth of his mischievously smiling face, while reaching with his left hand for an arrow in the quiver lying beside him. His left leg is drawn up, and the foot of his right leg, positioned diagonally in front of him, extends over the round plinth. Beside him lies a flowering rose branch. The quiver filled with arrows displays, among other things, fluting, a wreath of laurel leaves, and godrons. The pedestal, a short column, has a profiled base decorated with a wreath of laurel leaves, a shaft with curved fluting containing culots transitioning into a smooth upper section interrupted by a profiled edge, and furthermore, a profiled, overhanging cover plate decorated with egg-and-dart molding, which is rotatable by means of two brass knobs.
persona2_adapted: He sits on a cloud resting atop a round base, his wings spread wide. With his right index finger pressed to his lips, his face wears a mischievous smile. His left hand reaches for an arrow in the quiver lying beside him. One leg is drawn up, while the other extends diagonally forward, its foot hanging over the edge of the round base. Next to him lies a flowering rose branch. The quiver, filled with arrows, is decorated with vertical grooves, a wreath of laurel leaves, and a pattern of curved, ribbed ornaments. The pedestal—a short column—has a base with a carved profile, also adorned with a laurel wreath. Its shaft features curved grooves that end in leaf-shaped ornaments, then transitions into a smooth upper section interrupted by a raised band. At the top, a projecting cover plate is decorated with a repeating pattern of ovals and darts, and can be rotated using two brass knobs.

CuratorialText: Indoor room with a mother removing lice from her child's hair, known as 'Mother's Duty'. Interior with mother and child sitting in front of a box bed on the right. A high chair on the right; on the left, a dog sits on the tiled floor and looks at the open window in the room behind. Above the door is a small painting of a landscape.
persona2_adapted: In this indoor scene, a mother is removing lice from her child's hair—a task once known as "Mother's Duty." The two sit in front of a box bed on the right side of the room. To the right stands a high chair; on the left, a dog rests on the tiled floor, gazing toward an open window in the room behind. Above the door hangs a small landscape painting.

CuratorialText: This portrait of Alida Assink presents her in her finest attire, depicted from head to toe. That is quite exceptional, as the painter Kruseman typically reserved such a full-length portrait exclusively for royalty. Assink was 23 years old when this likeness was commissioned by her guardian, a wealthy landowner. The rural setting with hunting dog and garden vase harks back to the English portrait style, which Kruseman admired.
persona2_adapted: This portrait of Alida Assink shows her from head to toe, dressed in her finest clothing. That is quite unusual, because the painter Kruseman usually saved this kind of full-length portrait only for royalty. Assink was 23 years old when her guardian—a wealthy landowner—commissioned this painting. The countryside setting, along with the hunting dog and garden vase, reflects the English portrait style, which Kruseman greatly admired.

CuratorialText: The Dordrecht artist Aert Schouman was no scientist, but he was deeply interested in true-to-life depictions of the bird realm. His keen powers of observation are demonstrated by the way in which this red-billed toucan grasps the branch, namely with two, rather than three toes like other species. The landscape airily painted in transparent shades of green and blue evokes the bird’s native regions in Guyana and North Brazil.
persona2_adapted: The Dordrecht artist Aert Schouman was not a scientist, but he had a strong interest in depicting birds as realistically as possible. His sharp observational skills are clear in the way this red-billed toucan grips the branch—using two toes instead of three, as most other birds do. The landscape, painted in light, see-through shades of green and blue, suggests the bird’s natural habitat in Guyana and northern Brazil.

CuratorialText: Still life with roses. Also included are a butterfly and two snails.
persona2_adapted: A still life arrangement of roses, accompanied by a butterfly and two snails, invites the viewer to consider the contrast between beauty and decay, or the fleeting nature of life.

CuratorialText: Here the Duke of Wellington is receiving the message that Prussian forces are coming to his aid. Wellington, commander of the Anglo-Dutch troops, is the central figure in this group portrait of the major players at Waterloo. Lying wounded on a stretcher in the left foreground is the Dutch Crown Prince, later King William II. Originally intended for Wellington, the painting remained in the Netherlands thanks to William I who bought the picture for his son.
persona2_adapted: Here the Duke of Wellington is receiving the message that Prussian forces are coming to his aid. Wellington, the commander of the Anglo-Dutch troops, is the central figure in this group portrait of the major figures at the Battle of Waterloo. In the left foreground, lying wounded on a stretcher, is the Dutch Crown Prince—who would later become King William II. Although the painting was originally intended for Wellington, it stayed in the Netherlands because King William I bought it for his son.

CuratorialText: Of the noblemen who rebelled against Phillip II’s Spanish rule of the Netherlands, William of Orange rose to become the great leader of the Dutch Revolt. He defended his own interests, strove for greater independence and advocated freedom of religion. In 1580 Phillip II put a price on his head, and he was assassinated four years later.
persona2_adapted: Of the noblemen who rebelled against King Philip II of Spain's rule over the Netherlands, William of Orange emerged as the key leader of the Dutch Revolt. He fought to protect his own interests, pushed for greater independence, and supported the idea of religious freedom. In 1580, Philip II placed a bounty on his head, and William was assassinated four years later.

CuratorialText: This still life invites close examination of all the details and elicits marvel at the richness of God’s creation: the colourful flowers, the exotic shells, but certainly also the beautiful insects. At the same time, decay is spreading. Fruit shows the first rotten spots, leaves are eaten away by caterpillars and snails and ants drag a dead fly. The painting is thus also about impermanence.
persona2_adapted: This still life encourages you to look closely at every detail and feel wonder at the abundance of nature: the bright flowers, the unusual shells, and certainly the beautiful insects. At the same time, signs of decay are appearing. Fruit shows the first soft, spoiled spots, leaves are being eaten by caterpillars and snails, and ants are dragging away a dead fly. In this way, the painting also reflects on the idea that all things are temporary.

CuratorialText: Presented here are two squirrel monkeys from Central America, two white sulphur-crested cockatoos from Australia, a grey parrot from Africa and a purple-naped lory – on a chain at the lower left – from Indonesia. Hondecoeter combined these creatures and several other splendid birds in this painting, which was destined for Het Loo, the palace of William III. It hung above the door of the king’s private apartment.
persona2_adapted: Here are two squirrel monkeys from Central America, two white sulphur-crested cockatoos from Australia, a grey parrot from Africa, and a purple-naped lory—shown on a chain at the lower left—from Indonesia. In this painting, Hondecoeter brought together these animals and several other magnificent birds. The work was created for Het Loo, the palace of William III, and originally hung above the door of the king’s private apartment.

CuratorialText: This is a panoramic, sun-drenched river landscape – somewhat hazy in the distance – with sketchily drawn clouds. Two Dutch officers halt at the river, and water their horses next to a herder and his resting cattle. This idyllic scene does not unfold in Italy, but along the Waal between Nijmegen and Cleves, where Cuyp made landscape drawings that he took back to his workshop in Dordrecht.
persona2_adapted: This is a wide, sunlit river landscape, with the distance appearing slightly hazy and the clouds loosely sketched in. Two Dutch officers stop by the river to water their horses, next to a herder and his resting cattle. Although this peaceful scene might look like it takes place in Italy, it actually shows the area along the Waal River between Nijmegen and Cleves. The artist, Cuyp, made drawings of this landscape and brought them back to his studio in Dordrecht.

CuratorialText: Ice entertainment near a village. Winter village scene with many figures skating, playing, and bowling on the ice. On the left, two people have fallen through the ice. In the distance, a drawbridge and a windmill.
persona2_adapted: On a frozen river near a village, a lively winter scene unfolds. Many figures are skating, playing, and bowling on the ice, capturing the spirit of communal outdoor entertainment. To the left, two people have fallen through the ice, adding a note of peril to the otherwise cheerful atmosphere. In the background, a drawbridge and a windmill anchor the scene, giving it a sense of place and everyday life.

CuratorialText: This dolls’ house is exceptionally realistic. All the contents have been made of authentic materials, and the proportions are exactly correct. The fine cabinet, of tortoiseshell decorated with pewter inlays, was made by a cabinetmaker from France, who worked in Amsterdam for several years. Petronella Oortman was married to the Amsterdam merchant Johannes Brandt.
persona2_adapted: This dolls’ house is remarkably lifelike. Every item inside is made from real materials—such as wood, metal, and fabric—rather than painted substitutes, and the scale is perfectly accurate. The elegant cabinet, crafted from tortoiseshell and decorated with pewter inlays (thin pieces of a silver-like metal set into the surface), was built by a French cabinetmaker who spent several years working in Amsterdam. The owner, Petronella Oortman, was married to Johannes Brandt, a merchant from Amsterdam.

CuratorialText: This scene is painted on a copper panel. Its smooth surface was well suited to meticulous and detailed finish. Van Kessel made dozens of such little ‘coppers’ of insects and other animals, which he would arrange in a larger composition. They may have also served as decorative panels for a small cabinet of curiosities.
persona2_adapted: This scene is painted on a copper panel. The metal’s smooth surface allowed the artist to achieve an exceptionally fine and detailed finish. Van Kessel created dozens of these small copper paintings of insects and other animals, which he would then arrange into a larger composition. They may have also been used as decorative panels for a small cabinet of curiosities—a private collection of rare and fascinating objects.

CuratorialText: 18 June 1648: a banquet is taking place at the Amsterdam crossbowmen’s guild. The occasion was the signing of the Treaty of Münster, which marked an end to the war with Spain. The captains of the civic guard company shake hands as a sign of peace, and the drinking horn is passed around. The poem on the drum proclaims the joy of Amsterdam’s armed militia that their weapons can henceforth be laid to rest.
persona2_adapted: 18 June 1648: a banquet is underway at Amsterdam’s crossbowmen’s guild. The event celebrated the signing of the Treaty of Münster, which ended the war with Spain. The captains of the city’s militia shake hands as a gesture of peace, while the drinking horn is passed among the guests. A poem inscribed on the drum expresses the militia’s joy that their weapons can now be set aside.

CuratorialText: The Fishing for Souls: an allegory of the jealousy of the various religions during the Twelve Years' Truce (1609-1621) between the Dutch Republic and Spain. In a river landscape, large groups of people are situated on both banks of the river; several rowboats are on the water. On the horizon, a rainbow. On the left side are the Protestants and their supporters: the Princes of Orange, Maurice and Frederick Henry, Frederick V of the Palatinate, James I, King of England, and the youthful Louis XIII, King of France, with his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat in the foreground sit the Protestant fishermen; their nets are marked with Fides, Spes, and Charitas. On the right side stand the Catholics and their supporters: Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope carried by cardinals. In the small Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the leadership of a bishop.
persona2_adapted:
In this painting, *Fishing for Souls*, the artist uses a symbolic scene to comment on the religious tensions that existed during the Twelve Years' Truce (1609–1621)—a temporary pause in the war between the Dutch Republic and Spain. The image shows a river landscape, with large groups of people gathered on both banks and several rowboats on the water. A rainbow appears on the horizon.

On the left side are the Protestants and their supporters. These include the Princes of Orange, Maurice and Frederick Henry; Frederick V of the Palatinate; James I, King of England; and the young Louis XIII, King of France, accompanied by his mother, Maria de' Medici. In the boat at the front, Protestant fishermen are at work. Their nets are marked with three Latin words—*Fides*, *Spes*, and *Charitas*—which mean Faith, Hope, and Charity, the three core Christian virtues.

On the right side stand the Catholics and their supporters. Among them are Archdukes Albert and Isabella, General Ambrosio Spinola, and the Pope, who is being carried by cardinals. In a smaller Catholic boat, priests are fishing under the direction of a bishop.

The painting is an allegory—a story told through symbols—about the jealousy and rivalry between different religious groups during this uneasy peace.

CuratorialText: Wedding portrait of Isaac Abrahamsz Massa and Beatrix van der Laen, married in Haarlem on April 25, 1622. The couple is seated full-length on a raised platform under a tree. On the left, a thistle; on the right, ivy. On the right in the background, elegant couples are walking in a garden with a fountain at a country house.
persona2_adapted: This portrait commemorates the wedding of Isaac Abrahamsz Massa and Beatrix van der Laen, who were married in Haarlem on April 25, 1622. The couple is shown seated at full length on a raised platform beneath a tree. To the left, a thistle appears; to the right, ivy. In the background on the right, elegantly dressed couples stroll through a garden beside a country house, where a fountain can be seen.

CuratorialText: Figure of painted porcelain. The figure depicts a parrot (macaw) sitting on a tree stump with leaves. The parrot has a blue-scaled neck and head and a white body. The tail and the tips of the wings are painted blue, yellow, and white. A branch with cherries lies beneath the right leg. The figure is unmarked.
persona2_adapted: This small porcelain figure shows a parrot—specifically a macaw—perched on a tree stump with leaves. The bird’s neck and head are painted with blue scales, while its body is white. The tips of its wings and its tail are decorated in blue, yellow, and white. Beneath its right leg lies a branch with cherries. The piece has no maker’s mark or signature, so its origin or artist is unknown.

CuratorialText: Formal long-sleeved kimono for an unmarried young woman (furisode), decorated all over with more than two hundred flying cranes. Black silk crepe (chirimen) with yuzen decoration in white, red, and grey tones, with gold foil detailing. Red silk lining. Single bamboo crest (mon).
persona2_adapted: This formal long-sleeved kimono, known as a *furisode*, was worn by an unmarried young woman. Its entire surface is decorated with over two hundred cranes in flight, a symbol of longevity and good fortune. The kimono is made of black silk crepe—a fabric with a slightly crinkled texture—and features a resist-dyeing technique called *yuzen*, which allows for intricate, hand-painted patterns. The design uses white, red, and grey tones, with delicate accents of gold foil. The inside is lined with red silk. On the back, a single bamboo crest, or *mon*, marks the wearer’s family identity.

CuratorialText: Still life with flowers. A bouquet of flowers in a metal vase standing in a niche. The bouquet contains roses, irises, poppies, a wheat stalk, tulips, and other flowers. A snail, a caterpillar, and a beetle crawl among the flowers and over the niche. Pendant of SK-A-337.
persona2_adapted: A still life painting of flowers. A bouquet of roses, irises, poppies, tulips, and a stalk of wheat sits in a metal vase placed inside a shallow, recessed alcove. Among the flowers and along the edge of the alcove, a snail, a caterpillar, and a beetle crawl. This painting is the companion piece to another work in the museum's collection (SK-A-337).

CuratorialText: Mary Magdalene sitting under a tree with the ointment jar on her lap. The saint is seated in a landscape with mountains and rugged rock formations.
persona2_adapted: Mary Magdalene is shown seated beneath a tree, with an ointment jar resting on her lap—a traditional reference to the biblical story in which she anoints Jesus. She is positioned in a landscape of mountains and rough, jagged rock formations, which frames her figure within a setting that feels both natural and dramatic.

CuratorialText: Portrait of the twenty-year-old Marten Soolmans standing full-length on a checkered tile floor in an undefined space. He is dressed in an expensive black suit with a lace collar, bows, cuffs, and garters, wears a black hat and shoes with rosettes. In his left hand, he holds a glove.
persona2_adapted: A full-length portrait of twenty-year-old Marten Soolmans, shown standing on a checkered tile floor in a setting that is not clearly defined. He wears a costly black suit, complete with a lace collar, decorative bows, cuffs, and garters, along with a black hat and shoes adorned with rosettes. In his left hand, he holds a single glove.

CuratorialText: Portrait of 23-year-old Oopjen Coppit standing full-length on a tiled floor in an otherwise undefined space. She is dressed in an expensive black dress, lace collar and cuffs, and a black veil. She wears precious jewelry. In her right hand, she holds a fan of black ostrich feathers.
persona2_adapted: A full-length portrait of 23-year-old Oopjen Coppit, standing on a tiled floor in a space that is otherwise left undefined—neither indoors nor outdoors. She wears an expensive black dress, a lace collar and cuffs, and a black veil, all of which signal her high social status. She is adorned with precious jewelry, and in her right hand, she holds a fan made of black ostrich feathers.

CuratorialText: Still life with a golden ceremonial goblet, a silver holder on which stands a Chinese dish, an orange, a peeled lemon, grapes, peaches, a lobster, a plate of oysters and a roll.
persona2_adapted: A still life arrangement featuring a golden ceremonial goblet, a silver stand holding a Chinese dish, an orange, a peeled lemon, grapes, peaches, a lobster, a plate of oysters, and a roll.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Marie Fargues, the artist's wife, in Turkish costume. Full-length, seated on a divan. Her head resting on her right hand. On the divan lie a book and a basket containing a mirror, comb, and bonnet. On the floor are an Oriental rug and a small vase with carnations
persona2_adapted: Marie Fargues, the artist's wife, is shown here in a Turkish costume, seated on a low sofa called a divan. She rests her head on her right hand, while a book and a basket—holding a mirror, comb, and bonnet—lie beside her. On the floor, an Oriental rug and a small vase with carnations complete the scene.

CuratorialText: A cheerful violinist holding a birch flask. A laughing man leans out of a window covered by an Oriental rug. In his right hand, he holds up a birch flask filled with wine; in his left hand, a violin with a bow. On his head, a feathered hat.
persona2_adapted: A cheerful violinist holds a birch flask, while a laughing man leans out of a window draped with an Oriental rug. In his right hand, he raises a birch flask filled with wine; in his left, he holds a violin and bow. A feathered hat sits atop his head.

CuratorialText: View of Olinda in Dutch Brazil. On the left, on a hill, a group of people by the ruins of the church. In the foreground, tropical plants, fruits, and animals (armadillo, monkey, sloth, anteater, lizard).
persona2_adapted: This scene captures a view of Olinda, a city in northeastern Brazil during the period of Dutch occupation. On the left, a hill rises where a group of people gather near the ruined remains of a church. In the foreground, the artist has depicted a rich assortment of tropical plants, fruits, and animals native to the region—including an armadillo, a monkey, a sloth, an anteater, and a lizard—offering a vivid sense of the local environment.

CuratorialText: Winter landscape. View of a wide, snow-covered lane leading past a forest. In the foreground, three boys with sleds. Further back, a wagon drawn by two horses stands on the road in front of a farm.
persona2_adapted: A winter scene unfolds along a broad, snow-covered lane that winds past a forest. In the foreground, three boys stand with sleds, while farther down the road, a horse-drawn wagon waits in front of a farmhouse.

CuratorialText: Italian landscape with draftsman. A road through the landscape with a bridge over a mountain stream. On the road, a man with a mule. In the foreground, some seated figures.
persona2_adapted: A scene of the Italian countryside, featuring a draftsman—an artist sketching outdoors—at work. A road winds through the landscape, crossing a bridge over a mountain stream. Along the road, a man leads a mule. In the foreground, a few figures are seated, perhaps resting or taking in the view.

CuratorialText: Still life with turkey pie. Table with a white damask tablecloth on which stand pewter plates with a pie, oysters, lemon, salt and pepper, and olives (?). Also a pewter pitcher, roemer, knife, nuts, bread rolls, a nautilus cup, and a Wan Li bowl with fruit. In the background stands a large pie on which a turkey has been placed.
persona2_adapted: On a table covered with a white damask tablecloth—a type of finely woven linen—rests an array of objects arranged for a still life. There are pewter plates holding a pie, oysters, a lemon, salt and pepper, and what appear to be olives. Also present are a pewter pitcher, a type of wine glass called a roemer, a knife, nuts, bread rolls, a cup made from a nautilus shell, and a bowl from the Chinese Wan Li period, filled with fruit. In the background, a large pie is displayed, topped with a whole turkey.

CuratorialText: The stone bridge. Landscape with a stone bridge over a river or canal. On the water are two small boats with figures. Various people are walking on the road, a wagon on the left. In the middle, a hay barn between trees, a church tower in the distance on the right.
persona2_adapted: A stone bridge spans a river or canal, anchoring this landscape scene. Two small boats carrying figures drift on the water, while several people walk along the road. To the left, a wagon is visible. In the middle ground, a hay barn sits nestled between trees, and in the distance on the right, a church tower rises above the horizon.

CuratorialText: A stone bust of Flora in an ornamental cartouche surrounded by a festoon of various types of flowers, including tulips, roses, delphinium, foxglove, hollyhocks, carnations, and ivy.
persona2_adapted: A carved stone portrait of the goddess Flora sits inside an ornate, decorative frame shaped like a shield or scroll. This frame is surrounded by a garland of flowers—tulips, roses, delphinium, foxglove, hollyhocks, carnations, and ivy—all arranged in a hanging loop.

CuratorialText: The overturned bouquet. A cat, playing with a mousetrap, knocks over a vase containing a bouquet of flowers. In the bouquet: tulips, irises, poppies, and carnations. Flying among the flowers are butterflies, a spider, a dragonfly, and snails.
persona2_adapted: A vase holding a bouquet of flowers has been knocked over by a cat who is playing with a mousetrap. The bouquet includes tulips, irises, poppies, and carnations. Among the flowers, butterflies, a spider, a dragonfly, and snails can be seen moving through the scene.

CuratorialText: The Fall of Man. Adam and Eve standing before the Tree of Knowledge. A snake offers Eve an apple. Surrounding the figures are various animals: monkey, cat, dog, slugs, hedgehog, frog, fox, and owl. In the background on the left, Adam and Eve are warned by God not to eat from the tree.
persona2_adapted: Adam and Eve stand before the Tree of Knowledge, a moment that marks the turning point in the biblical story of humanity's first disobedience. A snake offers Eve an apple, the forbidden fruit that will lead to their expulsion from the Garden of Eden. Around the pair, a variety of animals gather—monkey, cat, dog, slugs, hedgehog, frog, fox, and owl—each perhaps symbolizing the natural world's presence at this pivotal event. In the background to the left, we see God warning Adam and Eve not to eat from the tree, a scene that foreshadows the choice they are about to make.

CuratorialText: View of an orchard with plum blossoms, seen through the branches of a plum tree in the foreground; picnicking visitors in the background; part of the entrance gate on the left in the foreground; against a bright red sky.
persona2_adapted: A plum tree in the foreground frames a view of an orchard in bloom, its branches drawing the eye toward visitors enjoying a picnic in the distance. To the left, part of the entrance gate is visible near the front of the scene. The sky above glows a vivid red, adding a dramatic contrast to the delicate blossoms and leisurely activity below.

CuratorialText: The painting is executed in a pointillist technique. The sitter is positioned on the right, facing the viewer, while studying a book in the light of a kerosene lamp. She is dressed in a pink gown, known as reform clothing, and has her hair simply styled in an updo. The kerosene lamp is partially visible on the table on the left, placed next to a small vase of roses on a small table covered with a floral tablecloth. Flowers in a large vase in the background are visually incorporated into the pattern of the decorative wallpaper.
persona2_adapted: The painting uses a technique called pointillism, in which the artist builds the image from many small dots of color rather than broader brushstrokes. The subject—a woman—sits on the right side of the canvas, facing us as she reads a book by the light of a kerosene lamp. She wears a pink gown, a style known as “reform clothing” (a simpler, more practical alternative to the restrictive fashions of the time), and her hair is pulled back in a neat updo. On the left, the kerosene lamp is partly visible on a small table covered with a floral cloth, beside a small vase of roses. In the background, a larger vase of flowers blends into the pattern of the decorative wallpaper, so that the flowers and wallpaper seem to merge visually.

CuratorialText: Kitten game. A small cat plays with dominoes on a table. There is also an ashtray with a cigar on the table.
persona2_adapted: A small cat is shown playing with dominoes on a table, while an ashtray holding a cigar sits nearby. The scene captures a moment of everyday life, inviting the viewer to consider the quiet, playful interaction between the animal and the objects around it.

CuratorialText: Interior in which a family celebrates the Feast of Saint Nicholas. In the center, a girl holding a John the Baptist doll and a bucket of candy in her arms. On the left, a crying boy who has received a rod in his shoe. Next to the mother with outstretched arms stands a laughing boy with a club. On the floor is a basket of cakes and rolls; by the fireplace, several young people are singing a Sinterklaas song. In the background, an older man and woman.
persona2_adapted: In this indoor scene, a family is celebrating the Feast of Saint Nicholas, a Dutch holiday similar to Christmas. At the center, a girl holds a doll representing John the Baptist and a bucket of candy. To the left, a boy is crying because he received a rod—a traditional punishment for misbehavior—in his shoe instead of a gift. Next to the mother, who has her arms outstretched, stands a laughing boy holding a club. On the floor, there is a basket of cakes and bread rolls. By the fireplace, several young people are singing a Sinterklaas song, a traditional holiday tune. In the background, an older man and woman look on.

CuratorialText: Jeremiah mourning over the destruction of Jerusalem. The old prophet sits on the rocky slope of a mountain, his head resting on his left hand. On a stone beside him lie a golden bowl, a pitcher, and books, sanctuaries from the destroyed temple. Below to the left, the burning buildings of the city and soldiers can be seen.
persona2_adapted: Jeremiah mourns the destruction of Jerusalem. The aging prophet sits on a rocky mountainside, his head resting heavily on his left hand. Beside him, on a stone, rest a golden bowl, a pitcher, and books—objects rescued from the ruined temple. In the lower left of the scene, the burning buildings of the city and the figures of soldiers are visible.

CuratorialText: Still life with fruit, oysters, and a porcelain bowl. On a stone table, a silver plate with oysters, fruit, a porcelain bowl, an inverted roemer, a glowing wick, and a bunch of grapes on a wooden box. Also snails and insects.
persona2_adapted: On a stone table sits a silver plate filled with oysters and fruit, alongside a porcelain bowl, an overturned wine glass (known as a roemer), a glowing wick, and a bunch of grapes resting on a wooden box. Scattered around are snails and insects, adding a sense of life and decay to the scene.

CuratorialText: Painting titled 'View at the Geestbrug'. River landscape with the Trekvliet near Rijswijk, with De Binckhorst Castle in the distance on the left and the Laakmolen on the right. A woman with children stands on the road to the farm, and boats are sailing on the water.
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This painting, titled *View at the Geestbrug*, shows a river landscape along the Trekvliet near Rijswijk. In the distance on the left, you can see De Binckhorst Castle, while the Laakmolen windmill stands on the right. On the road leading to a farm, a woman stands with children, and boats glide across the water.

CuratorialText: The well-stocked kitchen, with Jesus with Martha and Mary in the background. In the kitchen, two maidservants are busy preparing food. One is plucking a chicken, the other is skewering a chicken. To the left, others are sitting by the hearth. In the foreground, all kinds of game and poultry, vegetables, and fruits are laid out. Among these are also various glasses, jugs, pitchers, and plates.
persona2_adapted: In a well-stocked kitchen, a biblical scene unfolds in the background: Jesus visiting Martha and Mary. Two maidservants are busy preparing food—one plucking a chicken, the other skewering it. To the left, others sit by the hearth. In the foreground, an abundant display of game, poultry, vegetables, and fruits is arranged, alongside various glasses, jugs, pitchers, and plates.

CuratorialText: Still life with flowers and fruit. On a plinth stands a stone vase with flowers, fruit, and vines. On the plinth is a glass, and lying are a pineapple, peaches, grapes, and a rose. On the wall hangs a painting of a woman with a flower basket handing a child a flower wreath.
persona2_adapted: On a stone pedestal sits a vase filled with flowers, fruit, and twisting vines. Beside it, a glass rests on the pedestal’s surface, while a pineapple, peaches, grapes, and a single rose lie nearby. Behind them, a painting hangs on the wall, showing a woman with a flower basket reaching out to hand a child a flower wreath.

CuratorialText: Six warships lie on a vast, calm sea. The sails are being hoisted, the anchors weighed. The stern of the ship on the right in the foreground is decorated with the coat of arms of Amsterdam, supported by two standing lions. This likely depicts part of a war fleet preparing for departure. A sloop with dignified figures, probably from the Admiralty, sails past the ships to the sound of trumpets and salutes, while a little further away the last provisions and crew are still being taken on board. The enormous accuracy in the rendering of details—rigging, sails, and cannons—is striking.
persona2_adapted: Six warships rest on a vast, calm sea. Crews are raising the sails and lifting the anchors. The back end of the ship on the right, in the foreground, is decorated with Amsterdam’s coat of arms, held up by two standing lions. This scene likely shows part of a war fleet getting ready to leave. A small boat carrying important figures—probably from the Admiralty, the government body that oversaw the navy—sails past the ships, accompanied by trumpet sounds and gun salutes. Meanwhile, a bit farther away, the last supplies and crew members are still being brought on board. What stands out is the extraordinary precision in how every detail is shown—the ropes, sails, and cannons are all rendered with remarkable accuracy.

CuratorialText: Still life with a hammered bronze vase containing a bouquet of flowers: tulips, roses, anemones, and marigolds. On the tabletop lie a dead frog, a cluster of berries, and a caterpillar.
persona2_adapted: A still life painting shows a hammered bronze vase holding a bouquet of flowers—tulips, roses, anemones, and marigolds. On the table in front of the vase lie a dead frog, a cluster of berries, and a caterpillar.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo (c. 1445-1516). Architect, son of Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo. Bust facing right, with a landscape in the background. A compass and pen lie on the plinth at the bottom of the image. Part, with SK-C-1367, of a diptych.
persona2_adapted: Portrait of Giuliano da Sangallo (c. 1445–1516). He was an architect, the son of Francesco Giamberti da Sangallo. The portrait shows him from the chest up, facing to the right, with a landscape behind him. On the ledge at the bottom of the image rest a compass and a pen. This work, together with SK-C-1367, forms a two-panel painting, known as a diptych.

CuratorialText: An old woman, probably Rembrandt's mother, Neeltgen Willemsdr van Zuydtbroeck (died 1640), presumably depicted as the prophetess Anna. A seated old woman, reading a large book lying open on her lap.
persona2_adapted: An elderly woman, likely Rembrandt's mother, Neeltgen Willemsdr van Zuydtbroeck (who died in 1640), is shown here in the role of the prophetess Anna. She sits reading a large book that lies open on her lap.

CuratorialText: Allegorical musical group. On the left, a man in Oriental clothing and a turban plays a viola da gamba. Next to him stands a young man with a harp. A young woman sits beside a small table with an open songbook on her lap and sings. Behind her, an old woman with a cloth over her head looks on. In the foreground lie musical instruments (lute, violin) and stacks of books. On the wall is a painting of Lot's Flight from Sodom.
persona2_adapted: In this allegorical scene, a group of people is shown making music together. On the left, a man dressed in clothing inspired by the Middle East, wearing a turban, plays a viola da gamba—an early string instrument held between the legs, similar to a cello. Next to him stands a young man with a harp. A young woman sits beside a small table, an open songbook on her lap, and sings. Behind her, an older woman with a cloth covering her head watches the scene. In the foreground, musical instruments like a lute and a violin are placed alongside stacks of books. On the wall hangs a painting depicting Lot's Flight from Sodom, a biblical story about escape and destruction.

CuratorialText: Venus and Adonis. The half-naked Venus and Cupid try to prevent Adonis from going hunting. Two white doves in the lower right. Adonis's quiver hangs from the tree. Adonis has two hunting dogs with him.
persona2_adapted: Venus and Adonis. The partially undressed Venus, along with Cupid, attempts to hold Adonis back from his hunt. In the lower right corner, two white doves appear—a traditional symbol of love and fidelity. Adonis’s quiver, the container for his arrows, hangs from a tree branch, while his two hunting dogs wait beside him.

CuratorialText: The Cannon Shot. A warship in calm with slack sails fires a cannon shot. Two longboats on either side, in the distance another warship with furled sails. Pendant to SK-A-1848.
persona2_adapted: In *The Cannon Shot*, we see a warship at rest on a calm sea, its sails hanging loose and unused. The ship fires its cannon, creating a sudden burst of action in an otherwise still scene. Two longboats sit on either side of the main vessel, while another warship with its sails tightly rolled up appears in the distance. This painting is a companion piece to another work in the collection, numbered SK-A-1848, meaning the two were designed to be viewed together as a pair.

CuratorialText: The word Sufi often conjures up images of Turkish whirling dervishes, turning gracefully round themselves in a mystical ceremony. But outside the world of Islamic mysticism, not much is known of the beliefs and practices in which such ceremonies are rooted, or about the communities that embrace them.
persona2_adapted: The term *Sufi* frequently brings to mind the image of Turkish whirling dervishes, spinning elegantly in a spiritual ritual. Yet, beyond the realm of Islamic mysticism, there is limited understanding of the beliefs and practices that underlie such ceremonies, or of the communities that observe them.

CuratorialText: This display will explore Sufi life and art through a selection of objects that represent Sufi communities in the Middle East, North and Sub-Saharan Africa, and northern India. This mystical movement, which first appeared in the Islamic world during the eighth century AD, has been defined as a pathway to a personal experience with God. Among the British Museum objects on display will be Islamic artefacts imbued with Sufi connotations. Some, such as alms bowls, are linked overtly to dervishes, while others have a more subtle connection, such as calligraphic works, miniature paintings and wall tiles. Sufis come from all walks of life, crossing the boundaries of class, ethnicity and regionality. The display will explore such themes as mystical love, poverty and asceticism, as well as Sufi influence upon the wider societies they belong to.
persona2_adapted: This display explores Sufi life and art through a selection of objects from Sufi communities in the Middle East, North and Sub-Saharan Africa, and northern India. Sufism is a mystical movement within Islam that first emerged in the eighth century AD. It can be understood as a spiritual path toward a personal, direct experience of God. The objects on display come from the British Museum’s collection and include Islamic artifacts that carry Sufi meanings. Some, like alms bowls, are clearly linked to dervishes—members of Sufi orders known for their vows of poverty and devotion. Others have a more subtle connection, such as calligraphic works, miniature paintings, and wall tiles. Sufis come from all walks of life, crossing boundaries of class, ethnicity, and region. The display explores themes like mystical love, poverty, and self-denial, as well as the influence Sufism has had on the wider societies in which it exists.

CuratorialText: Hawaiʻi: a kingdom crossing oceans is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. The exhibition brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations. Building on years of collaboration with Native Hawaiian artists, practitioners and scholars, the show centres on indigenous knowledge, shining new light on exceptional objects and extraordinary stories. Shaped together with Hawaiian knowledge-bearers, the exhibition showcases seldom-seen international loans alongside the remarkable collections at the British Museum – one of the largest in the world outside of Hawaiʻi.
persona2_adapted: Hawaiʻi: a kingdom crossing oceans is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. The exhibition brings together remarkable objects—from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved figures of gods, to powerful weapons edged with shark teeth and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu traveled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture by exploring the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations. Building on years of collaboration with Native Hawaiian artists, practitioners, and scholars, the show centers on indigenous knowledge, offering fresh perspectives on exceptional objects and extraordinary stories. Shaped together with Hawaiian knowledge-bearers, the exhibition features rarely-seen international loans alongside the remarkable collections at the British Museum—one of the largest in the world outside of Hawaiʻi.

CuratorialText: Explore prints by Scottish poet, writer, artist and gardener Ian Hamilton Finlay (1925–2006) on the theme of revolution and its relationship to contemporary society. Finlay became known from the late 1950s for his poetry but in the 1960s his output expanded to include the production of prints and three-dimensional works of art in stone, metal, neon and glass, which he produced in collaboration with various craftspeople. He also became a dedicated gardener, developing a conceptual garden at his remote home on the edge of the Pentland Hills, southwest of Edinburgh, which incorporated many of his works of art. He named his garden Little Sparta after the ancient Greek city, a militaristic rival to Athens, reflecting his deep interest in classical history.
persona2_adapted: Explore prints by Scottish poet, writer, artist, and gardener Ian Hamilton Finlay (1925–2006) that focus on the idea of revolution and how it connects to modern society. Finlay first gained attention in the late 1950s for his poetry, but by the 1960s his work had expanded to include prints and three-dimensional pieces made from materials like stone, metal, neon, and glass. He created these works in partnership with various skilled artisans. Finlay was also a devoted gardener, and he designed a garden with a strong conceptual theme—meaning it was built around ideas and artistic expression—at his remote home on the edge of the Pentland Hills, southwest of Edinburgh. This garden incorporated many of his artworks. He named it Little Sparta, after the ancient Greek city that was a military rival to Athens, a choice that reflects his deep interest in classical history.

CuratorialText: In the 1980s, Finlay's interest in the French Revolution began to dominate his work. He was fascinated by revolutions and ruptures, and how high ideals could easily lead to violence and corruption. The 12 prints in this display, made between 1984 and 1992, form part of Finlay's engagement with the French Revolution as a subject. They are from a group of 26 prints donated in 2025 by Prof Stephen Bann, an art historian who has written extensively on Finlay and who corresponded with the artist for many years.
persona2_adapted: In the 1980s, Finlay's work became increasingly focused on the French Revolution. He was drawn to the ideas of revolution and sudden change, and to how noble intentions could easily turn into violence and corruption. The 12 prints in this display, created between 1984 and 1992, are part of Finlay's exploration of the French Revolution as a theme. They come from a set of 26 prints donated in 2025 by Professor Stephen Bann, an art historian who has written extensively about Finlay and corresponded with the artist over many years.

CuratorialText: In a career that lasted from the 1760s to the 1830s, Pierre Alexandre Wille (1748–1837) experienced some of the most turbulent events in French history. A pupil of Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805), Wille established himself by imitating the genre scenes that won Greuze renown at the Salons of the 1760s, showing an inclination for delicacy that suited the tastes of the ancien régime.
persona2_adapted: In a career spanning the 1760s to the 1830s, Pierre Alexandre Wille (1748–1837) lived through some of the most dramatic upheavals in French history. Trained by the celebrated artist Jean Baptiste Greuze (1725–1805), Wille first made a name for himself by copying the kinds of everyday-life scenes that had brought Greuze fame at the official art exhibitions of the 1760s. His work showed a preference for gentle, refined details that appealed to the tastes of the French royal court and aristocracy before the Revolution.

CuratorialText: Yet Wille the man embraced the Revolution, even joining the republican army, and he busied himself chronicling the personalities of the day. Ultimately, however, the Revolution proved more of a challenge than an opportunity. The younger Wille had embraced a variety of styles but, in later life, he found it difficult to adapt to the more austere taste of Napoleonic France. By the time he died, he had long been reduced to poverty. During his lifetime and since, Wille was renowned more for his drawings than his paintings. The British Museum has been acquiring his work since the 19th century, and its collection has recently doubled in size following a bequest from the dealer and philanthropist Colin Clark (1935–2020). This gift included some of the finest finished drawings by Wille; one of the most powerful is the portrait which claims to show the 'Angel of Assassination' Charlotte Corday, made in her prison cell days or even hours before her execution. This celebration of Clark's bequest illustrates Wille's world through his drawings and those of his peers, revealing him to have been a talented draughtsman whose works offer a fascinating insight into French society before, during and after the Revolution.
persona2_adapted: Yet Wille the man embraced the Revolution, even joining the republican army, and he kept himself busy recording the notable figures of his time. Ultimately, however, the Revolution proved more of a challenge than an opportunity. The younger Wille had experimented with many different artistic approaches, but in later life he found it difficult to adapt to the more restrained and simple tastes of Napoleonic France. By the time he died, he had long been living in poverty. During his lifetime and since, Wille has been more famous for his drawings than his paintings. The British Museum has been acquiring his work since the 19th century, and its collection has recently doubled in size thanks to a gift from the dealer and philanthropist Colin Clark (1935–2020). This gift included some of Wille's finest completed drawings; one of the most powerful is the portrait that claims to show Charlotte Corday, the so-called 'Angel of Assassination,' made in her prison cell days or even hours before her execution. This exhibition celebrating Clark's gift illustrates Wille's world through his drawings and those of his peers, revealing him to have been a highly skilled artist whose works offer a fascinating window into French society before, during, and after the Revolution.

CuratorialText: Made in England between 1340 and 1405, the Asante Ewer later travelled to West Africa. In the 1880s it was photographed in a courtyard of the royal palace of the Asantehene, king of the Asante people, in Kumasi (in present-day Ghana). During the Anglo-Asante War of 1896 it was looted by British forces and subsequently purchased by the British Museum. A second ewer, also taken in 1896 and shown in the same photograph, was presented to the Prince of Wales's Own (West Yorkshire) Regiment. This display reunites these two jugs. A major new research project into the Asante Ewer – one of the finest examples of late medieval English bronze casting – is helping to further trace the story of its journey to Ghana, and its status and significance in West Africa. Its location in the centre of the palace courtyard, in what appears to be a shrine, embedded in the roots of sacred trees, suggests it was an object of significant cultural or ritual value. Remarkable for surviving the long-distance journey from Europe to West Africa and back, the Asante Ewer has had vastly different lives, from a luxury English jug to sacred West African vessel, to colonial loot taken by British troops.
persona2_adapted:
Made in England between 1340 and 1405, the Asante Ewer later traveled to West Africa. In the 1880s, it was photographed in a courtyard of the royal palace of the Asantehene—the king of the Asante people—in Kumasi (in present-day Ghana). During the Anglo-Asante War of 1896, it was taken by British forces and later bought by the British Museum. A second jug, also taken in 1896 and shown in the same photograph, was given to the Prince of Wales's Own (West Yorkshire) Regiment. This display brings these two jugs back together.

A major new research project into the Asante Ewer—one of the finest surviving examples of late medieval English metalwork made by pouring molten bronze into a mold—is helping to trace more of its journey to Ghana, as well as its meaning and importance in West Africa. Its placement in the center of the palace courtyard, inside what appears to be a shrine, embedded in the roots of sacred trees, suggests it was an object of significant cultural or ritual value. Remarkable for surviving the long journey from Europe to West Africa and back, the Asante Ewer has had vastly different lives: from a luxury English jug, to a sacred West African vessel, to colonial loot taken by British troops.

CuratorialText: This display forms part of a three-year research project, The age of copper, ivory and gold, supported by The British Academy and Wolfson Foundation, exploring the connections between Western Europe and West Africa during the Middle Ages and Early Modern period. It is the result of close collaboration with colleagues at the Manhyia Palace Museum in Kumasi, York Army Museum and many others across Ghana, the US and the UK.
persona2_adapted: This display is part of a three-year research project called *The Age of Copper, Ivory and Gold*, funded by The British Academy and Wolfson Foundation. The project investigates the links between Western Europe and West Africa during the Middle Ages and the Early Modern period (roughly the 5th to 18th centuries). It was developed through close collaboration with colleagues at the Manhyia Palace Museum in Kumasi, the York Army Museum, and many other institutions across Ghana, the United States, and the United Kingdom.

CuratorialText: In the 1400s and 1500s, the Low Countries were a powerhouse of artistic innovation. This was a transformative period for Netherlandish drawings, as they evolved from preparatory studies to works of art in their own right.
persona2_adapted: In the 1400s and 1500s, the region now known as the Low Countries (roughly modern-day Belgium, Netherlands, and Luxembourg) became a major center of artistic creativity. During this time, drawings in this area underwent a significant change: they were no longer just rough sketches made in preparation for paintings or other finished works, but began to be valued as independent artworks in their own right.

CuratorialText: Featuring around 120 works by artists including Rogier van der Weyden, Lucas van Leyden, Pieter Bruegel the Elder and Hendrick Goltzius, this exhibition charts the development of drawing in the Low Countries (present-day Belgium, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) before 1600. The exceptional breadth and quality of the British Museum's holdings provides a unique opportunity to present a comprehensive account of drawing from this region.  Early Netherlandish drawings are extremely rare, as they were made as functional objects in the workshop and were not typically retained. This was unlike Italy or the German-speaking lands, where the taste for collecting drawings in the 1500s ensured the survival of a higher number of works from this period. Drawings were central to the design and production of works of art in different media, including tapestries, paintings, stained glass and prints – and surviving examples shed light on the creativity and collaboration of Netherlandish artists.
persona2_adapted: Featuring around 120 works by artists such as Rogier van der Weyden, Lucas van Leyden, Pieter Bruegel the Elder, and Hendrick Goltzius, this exhibition traces how drawing developed in the Low Countries—the region now known as Belgium, Luxembourg, and the Netherlands—before 1600. The British Museum’s collection is unusually broad and high-quality, offering a rare chance to present a thorough overview of drawing from this area. Drawings from the early Netherlandish period are extremely scarce because they were created as practical tools within artists’ workshops and were not usually kept for display. This contrasts with Italy and German-speaking regions, where a growing interest in collecting drawings during the 1500s helped preserve many more works from that time. In the Low Countries, drawings played a central role in designing and producing art across different forms—including tapestries, paintings, stained glass, and prints. The examples that survive give us valuable insight into the creativity and collaborative work of Netherlandish artists.

CuratorialText: Based on a research project and supported by a grant from the International Music and Art Foundation, the exhibition highlights new findings that have come from combining curatorial and conservation expertise along with scientific analysis. New research into drawings previously only connected to Rogier van der Weyden and his circle, for instance, reveals they originate directly from Rogier's workshop and must have been drawn by his pupils as part of their training.
persona2_adapted: Based on a research project and funded by a grant from the International Music and Art Foundation, this exhibition presents new discoveries made possible by combining curatorial knowledge, conservation work, and scientific analysis. For example, recent research into drawings that were once only linked to the artist Rogier van der Weyden and his close associates now shows that these works actually came directly from his workshop. This means they were likely created by his students as part of their training.

CuratorialText: Be among the first to view designs for a permanent memorial to Queen Elizabeth II in central London.
persona2_adapted: Be among the first to see the proposed designs for a permanent memorial to Queen Elizabeth II, to be located in central London.

CuratorialText: Featuring a 10ft bronze statue of Elizabeth as a young Queen, on a 14ft plinth, the memorial in St James' Park will commemorate the late monarch and her lifelong commitment to public service. The memorial is expected to be completed in 2028. Designed by acclaimed sculptor Martin Jennings, to be sculpted in bronze, the statue will show Her Majesty in an early stage of her reign, dressed in the robes of the Order of the Garter, using a famous painting by Pietro Annigoni as inspiration.
persona2_adapted: A memorial in St James' Park will honor the late Queen Elizabeth and her lifelong dedication to public service. The centerpiece will be a 10-foot bronze statue of the Queen as a young monarch, set atop a 14-foot stone base. The memorial is expected to be finished in 2028. The statue, designed by the well-known sculptor Martin Jennings, will be cast in bronze. It will depict Her Majesty early in her reign, wearing the ceremonial robes of the Order of the Garter—a prestigious British knighthood order. The artist drew inspiration from a famous portrait of the Queen by the painter Pietro Annigoni.

CuratorialText: Mr Jennings will also sculpt a statue of Prince Philip, the country's longest-serving consort, at a similar age wearing a uniform of Admiral-of-the-Fleet. His statue will be located close to Queen Elizabeth's, in recognition of the support he gave her during her 70-year reign. Other elements of the memorial have been designed by Foster + Partners in collaboration with Michel Desvigne Paysagiste. The British Museum display features maquettes of the statues and a scale model of the wider memorial park. The King and Queen, other Members of the Royal Family and the Prime Minister viewed the designs at the Museum on 21 April to mark the 100th anniversary of Queen Elizabeth II's birth. The permanent memorial will also feature: a new cast-glass bridge inspired by Queen Mary's Fringe Tiara, which was worn by Queen Elizabeth on her wedding day; a bust of Her Majesty in her later years by sculptor Karen Newman; and The Commonwealth Wind Sculpture, a new abstract work by Yinka Shonibare.
persona2_adapted: Mr. Jennings will also create a statue of Prince Philip, the country's longest-serving royal spouse, shown at a similar age and dressed in the uniform of an Admiral of the Fleet. This statue will be placed near Queen Elizabeth's, as a tribute to the support he gave her during her 70-year reign. Other parts of the memorial have been designed by Foster + Partners, working together with Michel Desvigne Paysagiste. The display at the British Museum includes small-scale models of the statues and a miniature version of the entire memorial park. The King and Queen, other members of the Royal Family, and the Prime Minister viewed the designs at the Museum on April 21, marking the 100th anniversary of Queen Elizabeth II's birth. The permanent memorial will also include: a new bridge made of cast glass, inspired by Queen Mary's Fringe Tiara—the headpiece Queen Elizabeth wore on her wedding day; a sculpture of Her Majesty's head and shoulders in her later years, created by artist Karen Newman; and The Commonwealth Wind Sculpture, a new abstract artwork by Yinka Shonibare.

CuratorialText: The design also includes gardens dedicated to the Commonwealth and to the nations of the United Kingdom, creating spaces for relaxation and reflection. The memorial has been developed in consultation with the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee following a design competition held last year. As well as the memorial park, recommendations made by the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee include a new charitable trust for regenerating community assets across the UK, and an online Digital Memorial(Opens in new window) to allow people to submit their own memories of the late Queen.
persona2_adapted: The design also includes gardens dedicated to the Commonwealth and to the nations of the United Kingdom, offering spaces for both relaxation and quiet reflection. The memorial was developed in consultation with the Queen Elizabeth Memorial Committee, following a design competition held last year. In addition to the memorial park, the Committee has recommended establishing a new charitable trust to help regenerate community assets across the UK, as well as an online Digital Memorial (opens in a new window) where people can share their own memories of the late Queen.

CuratorialText:
Discover how people lived and died in ancient Egypt and explore their hopes and aspirations for the afterlife. 
From human remains, painted coffins, amulets, shabtis and tomb models of daily life to spectacular wall paintings and papyri; investigate what tombs, human remains and burial goods tell us about the lives and afterlives of ancient Egyptian people. The tour will explore the preparation of the deceased for burial, including mummification, the furnishing of the tomb for an eternal restful existence, and the use of magic to guide and protect people on their perilous journey to the afterlife. It will also show how funerary remains can tell us about the lives of the ancient Egyptians. This tour visits displays that include human remains. The British Museum is committed to curating human remains with care, respect and dignity. Find out more about our principles governing the holding, display and study of human remains.
persona2_adapted:
Discover how people lived and died in ancient Egypt, and what they hoped for in the afterlife. This tour examines human remains, painted coffins, protective charms, small servant statues called *shabtis*, and miniature models of daily life, as well as stunning wall paintings and ancient documents written on papyrus. Together, these objects reveal what tombs, bodies, and burial items can tell us about the lives and beliefs of ancient Egyptians.

You will learn how the dead were prepared for burial—including the process of mummification—how tombs were furnished to provide an eternal, peaceful existence, and how magic was used to guide and protect people on their dangerous journey to the afterlife. The tour also shows how these funerary objects offer insights into everyday life in ancient Egypt.

Please note that this tour includes displays of human remains. The British Museum is committed to caring for these remains with respect and dignity. You can find more about our principles for holding, displaying, and studying human remains on our website.

CuratorialText:
Discover the fascinating history of ancient Egypt and explore one of the most iconic spaces in the Museum before it opens to the public.
In the Egyptian sculpture gallery (Room 4), you can see impressive statues of kings and gods, monumental tomb architecture and ancient tomb reliefs spanning 3,000 year. These include the imposing colossal bust of Ramesses II and the Gayer-Anderson Cat. Learn about the importance of large-scale sculpture in ancient Egyptian temples, tombs and much more in this special guided tour.
persona2_adapted:
Discover the fascinating history of ancient Egypt and explore one of the Museum’s most iconic spaces before it opens to the public.

In the Egyptian sculpture gallery (Room 4), you will see impressive statues of kings and gods, monumental tomb structures, and carved stone panels from ancient tombs—works that span 3,000 years. Among these are the towering bust of Ramesses II and the famous Gayer-Anderson Cat. This special guided tour will explain why large-scale sculpture played such a central role in ancient Egyptian temples and tombs, and what these works reveal about the beliefs and practices of the time.

CuratorialText: Founded in 1753, the British Museum opened its doors to visitors in 1759. The Museum tells the story of human cultural achievement through a collection of collections. This room celebrates some of the collectors who, in different ways, have shaped the Museum over four centuries, along with individuals and organisations who continue to shape its future. The adjoining galleries also explore aspects of collecting. Room 1: Enlightenment, tells the story of how, in the early Museum, objects and knowledge were gathered and classified. Room 2a: The Waddesdon Bequest displays the collection of Renaissance and Baroque masterpieces left to the British Museum by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild MP at his death in 1898.
persona2_adapted: Founded in 1753, the British Museum first opened its doors to the public in 1759. The Museum tells the story of human cultural achievement through a collection of collections. This room celebrates some of the collectors who, in different ways, have shaped the Museum over four centuries, as well as the individuals and organizations who continue to shape its future. The adjoining galleries also explore different aspects of collecting. Room 1: Enlightenment, tells the story of how, in the Museum’s early days, objects and knowledge were gathered and organized into categories. Room 2a: The Waddesdon Bequest displays the collection of masterpieces from the Renaissance and Baroque periods—eras of great artistic flourishing in Europe—left to the British Museum by Baron Ferdinand Rothschild MP upon his death in 1898.

CuratorialText: The British Museum has successfully raised £3.5 million, which means the Tudor Heart Pendant will remain on public display for generations to come.  In December 2019 a metal detectorist in Warwickshire found a spectacular Tudor Heart jewel associated with Henry VIII and his first wife Katherine of Aragon in the ground after centuries of rest. The Tudor Heart Pendant is unlike any object in the British Museum collection or elsewhere in the UK. This highly accomplished jewel with enamelled motifs is attached to a 75-link gold chain by a suspension clasp in the form of a hand emerging from a cloud.
