Few objects survive to tell the story of Henry's life with the devoutly Catholic Katherine, a consequence of the annulment of their marriage in 1533. Their union was the longest of all of Henry's, lasting 24 years it decisively shaped the first three decades of his reign.
persona2_adapted: The British Museum has successfully raised £3.5 million, ensuring that the Tudor Heart Pendant will remain on public display for generations to come. In December 2019, a metal detectorist in Warwickshire discovered a remarkable Tudor heart-shaped jewel, buried in the ground for centuries, linked to Henry VIII and his first wife, Katherine of Aragon. This pendant is unlike any other object in the British Museum’s collection—or anywhere else in the UK. The highly skilled craftsmanship is evident in its enameled designs, and it hangs from a 75-link gold chain by a clasp shaped like a hand emerging from a cloud. Few objects survive to tell the story of Henry’s life with the devoutly Catholic Katherine, a consequence of the annulment of their marriage in 1533. Their union was the longest of all Henry’s marriages, lasting 24 years, and it decisively shaped the first three decades of his reign.

CuratorialText: In the 18th century, the collections were divided into 'Natural and Artificial Rarities' – objects found in nature or made by people. Only a few rooms in the early Museum had manmade objects but case after case was filled with natural specimens from Sloane's collection and his library which included the 265 volumes of his herbarium. In 1735, the Swedish botanist Carl Linnaeus revolutionised the way plants, animals and other objects from the natural world were named and classified. His pupil, Daniel Solander, was a curator in the Museum and applied the system to Sloane's collections and to the natural history specimens Solander himself collected with Sir Joseph Banks on the first Cook voyage.  Many of these original specimens are included in the exhibition, as well as a number of fossils. By 1880 there were so many natural history specimens that they needed a museum of their own – the Natural History Museum in South Kensington.
persona2_adapted: In the 18th century, the collections were divided into 'Natural and Artificial Rarities'—objects found in nature or made by people. Only a few rooms in the early Museum contained manmade objects, but case after case was filled with natural specimens from Sloane's collection, along with his library, which included the 265 volumes of his herbarium (a collection of dried plant specimens). In 1735, the Swedish botanist Carl Linnaeus transformed how plants, animals, and other natural objects were named and organized. His student, Daniel Solander, worked as a curator at the Museum and applied this system to Sloane's collections, as well as to the natural history specimens Solander himself gathered with Sir Joseph Banks during the first Cook voyage. Many of these original specimens are included in the exhibition, along with a number of fossils. By 1880, there were so many natural history specimens that they needed a museum of their own—the Natural History Museum in South Kensington.

CuratorialText: Antiquaries collected books, manuscripts, drawings and artefacts from the past, and travelled the country studying ruins in order to learn more about Britain's Roman past and its earlier history. It was fairly easy to learn about medieval times because many of these objects had survived, including religious artefacts, armour, books and buildings. But antiquaries also began to survey and map Roman and earlier sites, such as Stonehenge, in a more scientific and systematic way and to learn more about the earliest Britons.  This knowledge, combined with the new study of rock strata, led archaeologists to question the accepted date of the world's creation – 4004 BC – calculated from readings of the Bible.
persona2_adapted: Antiquaries were early historians and collectors who gathered books, manuscripts, drawings, and objects from the past. They traveled across the country studying ruins to learn more about Britain's Roman period and its even earlier history. Understanding medieval times was relatively easy because many items from that era had survived, including religious objects, armor, books, and buildings. However, antiquaries also began to survey and map Roman and prehistoric sites—like Stonehenge—in a more scientific and organized way. This allowed them to learn more about the earliest inhabitants of Britain. This growing knowledge, combined with the emerging study of rock layers (known as rock strata), led archaeologists to question the widely accepted date of the world's creation: 4004 BC, a date calculated from readings of the Bible.

CuratorialText: In 1824, Charles Townley's famous collection of Greek and Roman sculptures, acquired on his Grand Tours in Italy, came to the British Museum. They joined the collections of prints and drawings, gems, coins, bronzes, vases and other classical antiquities from fellow connoisseurs such as Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight. These men studied each other's collections to learn about the 'progress' of art, from 'primitive' beginnings in early civilisations to what they considered to be the height of artistic achievement – the sculpture and architecture of classical Greece. This became the standard against which to measure all art and led, in late 18th-century Britain, to a classical revival in architecture and the decorative arts.
persona2_adapted: In 1824, Charles Townley’s renowned collection of Greek and Roman sculptures—pieces he had acquired during his educational travels across Italy—arrived at the British Museum. These works joined the existing holdings of prints, drawings, engraved gems, coins, bronze objects, vases, and other ancient artifacts from fellow collectors like Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight. These men studied one another’s collections to understand what they saw as the “progress” of art: a journey from what they considered the crude beginnings of early civilizations to the peak of artistic achievement, which they believed was found in the sculpture and architecture of classical Greece. This Greek ideal became the standard by which all art was judged, and in late 18th-century Britain, it sparked a revival of classical styles in both architecture and decorative arts.

CuratorialText: Everything gathered in the 18th century had to be classified and organised so that it could be more easily understood and used.  King George III's library was presented to the nation by George IV and was housed in the room that is now the Enlightenment Gallery (his library is now in the British Library). The coins and medals collection was among the first part of the library to arrive at the Museum and they were arranged by country and ruler to help bring past history alive. George III was also keenly interested in the navy, navigation and scientific discoveries and collected instruments and working models. Sir William Hamilton arranged his vases found in the area around Naples according to the styles of painting, attempting to establish a chronology for them. He later published them in beautifully illustrated folios. In the early part of the century, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious 'artificial' (manmade) objects from around the world, including those collected from enslaved peoples in Jamaica. These objects were catalogued and displayed in many different ways – by type, material, date or purpose.
persona2_adapted: In the 18th century, everything that was collected had to be sorted and organized so it could be more easily understood and used. King George III’s library was given to the nation by his son, George IV, and was originally kept in the room that now houses the Enlightenment Gallery (the library itself is now at the British Library). The collection of coins and medals was among the first parts of the library to arrive at the Museum, and they were arranged by country and ruler to help bring past history to life. George III was also very interested in the navy, navigation, and scientific discoveries, and he collected instruments and working models. Sir William Hamilton organized the vases he found near Naples according to their painting styles, trying to establish a timeline for them. He later published these vases in beautifully illustrated large-format books. In the early part of the century, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious manmade objects from around the world, including items collected from enslaved people in Jamaica. These objects were catalogued and displayed in many different ways—by type, material, date, or purpose.

CuratorialText: The Enlightenment led to a fevered interest in deciphering the mysterious forms of Egyptian hieroglyphs and the cuneiform inscriptions found in lands described in the Bible as well as early forms of Sanskrit in India. Scholars 'cracked the codes' in the 19th century, opening up thousands of years of history. At the same time, antiquaries deciphered runes and other early forms of writing, revealing more of our own history. This was a period when significant numbers of antiquities, acquired from collectors, diplomats and through excavation, entered the British Museum.
persona2_adapted: The Enlightenment sparked a widespread fascination with decoding the mysterious symbols of Egyptian hieroglyphs, the wedge-shaped writing (cuneiform) found in lands mentioned in the Bible, and early forms of Sanskrit in India. In the 19th century, scholars successfully "cracked the codes" of these ancient scripts, unlocking thousands of years of previously inaccessible history. At the same time, antiquaries—early historians and archaeologists—deciphered runes and other early writing systems, revealing more about our own past. This era also saw a large number of ancient objects, or antiquities, entering the British Museum, acquired from collectors, diplomats, and through archaeological excavations.

CuratorialText: Christian mistrust of superstition and idols, rituals and magic led to ignorance about other religions. Enlightenment scholars attempted to dispel this by studying the ancient and modern religions they encountered. They researched rituals and cults in different cultures, collected religious artefacts, grouped gods into families and examined their attributes and functions. They wanted to find similarities that might shed light on the origins of the world's religions. Much of this activity took place within the context of colonialism.
persona2_adapted: Christian suspicion of superstition, idols, rituals, and magic led to a lack of understanding about other religions. Scholars during the Enlightenment period tried to correct this by studying both ancient and modern religions they came across. They examined religious practices and worship in different cultures, gathered religious objects, organized gods into groups, and looked at their characteristics and roles. Their goal was to find commonalities that might explain how the world's religions began. Much of this work happened within the framework of colonialism.

CuratorialText: In this era of colonialism and global trade, travellers, traders, scientists, explorers and diplomats created collections of ethnographic objects, including ceremonial and everyday items, thought representative of customs and cultures of people around the globe. They collected artefacts and published illustrated accounts about the lives and cultures of people around the world, from a European perspective. Alongside Sir Hans Sloane's collection, the British Museum also houses objects from the voyages of Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks to Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti and other Pacific islands. A significant number of objects were acquired through employees of the East India and Royal Africa Companies, whose wealth was made possible through the use of enslaved labour and the exploitation of people and resources.
persona2_adapted: In this period of colonialism and global trade, travelers, traders, scientists, explorers, and diplomats put together collections of objects from different cultures—both items used in ceremonies and in daily life. These objects were thought to represent the customs and ways of life of people around the world. Collectors also published illustrated accounts describing the lives and cultures of these peoples, all from a European point of view. Alongside Sir Hans Sloane's collection, the British Museum also holds objects from the voyages of Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks to Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti, and other Pacific islands. A significant number of these objects were acquired through employees of the East India Company and the Royal Africa Company—companies whose wealth came from the use of enslaved labor and the exploitation of people and natural resources.

CuratorialText: During his leadership, from 336–323 BC, Alexander the Great conquered the Greek city-states, the Persian Empire and founded more than 70 cities creating an empire that stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles.  The Hellenistic period dates from the death of Alexander the Great in 323 BC to the defeat of Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony at Actium in 31 BC. After his death, Alexander's empire was divided up into kingdoms, ruled by his generals. The major dynasties sponsored major architectural programmes and were great patrons of the arts and culture. Objects on display in Room 22 include a colossal carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World), the Demeter from Knidos in south-west Turkey and Greek bronzes. Displays of smaller objects show the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters and coroplasts (makers of clay or terracotta figurines) and gem cutters.
persona2_adapted: During his reign from 336 to 323 BC, Alexander the Great conquered the Greek city-states and the Persian Empire, and founded more than 70 cities. In doing so, he created an empire that stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles. The period that followed—known as the Hellenistic era—began with Alexander’s death in 323 BC and ended with the defeat of Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony at the Battle of Actium in 31 BC. After Alexander died, his empire was divided into separate kingdoms, each ruled by one of his former generals. These ruling dynasties sponsored large-scale building projects and became major supporters of the arts and culture. Among the objects displayed in Room 22 are a massive carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World), a statue known as the Demeter of Knidos from southwestern Turkey, and several Greek bronze works. Smaller exhibits highlight the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters, and makers of clay or terracotta figurines, as well as gem cutters.

CuratorialText: The exhibition is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.
persona2_adapted: This exhibition celebrates the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together remarkable objects—from feathered cloaks once worn by chiefs and finely carved figures of gods, to powerful weapons edged with shark teeth and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu traveled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this fateful royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture by exploring the complex and lasting relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.

CuratorialText: This fashion show presents contemporary clothing of the Moana-Nui-a-Kiwa (the great ocean of Kiwa/the Pacific Ocean), which has been facilitated in the UK by Interisland Collective and curated by Lyall Hakaraia. The collection has been conceived and made by Māori designer Lyall Hakaraia in collaboration with venerated practitioners from across the Pacific. These practictioners are tohunga (experts) who are building and continue to grow Pacific cultural knowledge. They consider themselves as guides and strive to be 'good ancestors' through their daily practices, powerful intentions and vision. They are building resilient futures for mokopuna (grandchildren) and generations to come. The show will act as a focal point for the Pacific diaspora community as they activate a VĀ (portal) to their ancestors. The show is an offering to those ancestors that remain at the British Museum and are embodied through Pacific collections. It aims to 'warm' them in slumber. The collective will sing, chant and offer prayers to them during the show with Aroha (love) from across the Pacific.
persona2_adapted:
This fashion show features contemporary clothing from Moana-Nui-a-Kiwa—the vast Pacific Ocean region. The event has been organized in the UK by Interisland Collective and curated by Lyall Hakaraia. The collection was designed by Hakaraia, a Māori creator, working alongside highly respected practitioners from across the Pacific. These practitioners are *tohunga* (experts) who are actively building and expanding Pacific cultural knowledge. They see themselves as guides and aim to be 'good ancestors' through their daily actions, strong intentions, and long-term vision. Their work focuses on creating resilient futures for *mokopuna* (grandchildren) and future generations.

The show will serve as a gathering point for the Pacific diaspora community as they open a *vā* (a portal or sacred space) to connect with their ancestors. It is an offering to those ancestors who remain at the British Museum, embodied in the Pacific collections there. The intention is to 'warm' them in their rest. During the show, the collective will sing, chant, and offer prayers to them with *aroha* (love) from across the Pacific.

CuratorialText: One half of the gallery presents the histories of China from 5000 BC to the present: from iconic Ming dynasty blue-and-white porcelain to delicate handscrolls, from magnificent Tang dynasty tomb figurines to modern works of art. The displays feature the richness of art and material culture in China, including painting, prints, jade, bronze, lacquer and ceramics.
persona2_adapted: One half of the gallery traces China's history from 5000 BC to the present day, showcasing objects that range from the famous blue-and-white porcelain of the Ming dynasty to delicate handscrolls (paintings on long rolls of silk or paper), from impressive Tang dynasty tomb figurines to modern artworks. The displays highlight the richness of China's artistic and material culture, including painting, prints, jade, bronze, lacquer, and ceramics.

CuratorialText: The other half of the gallery presents South Asia's many histories chronologically and by region, from early human occupation to the present. Highlights include seals from the Indus civilisation, superb south Indian sculptures of Shiva and one of the finest statues of the goddess Tara from Sri Lanka. Sophisticated paintings and objects from the courts of the Mughal emperors can be seen alongside 20th-century paintings, including by the Nobel laureate Rabindranath Tagore.
persona2_adapted: The other half of the gallery arranges South Asia's many histories in chronological order and by region, covering from early human settlement to the present day. Key highlights include engraved stone seals from the Indus Valley civilization, remarkable sculptures of the Hindu god Shiva from southern India, and one of the finest statues of the goddess Tara, originating from Sri Lanka. Intricate paintings and objects created for the courts of the Mughal emperors are displayed alongside 20th-century works, including those by the Nobel Prize-winning writer and artist Rabindranath Tagore.

CuratorialText: China is one of the world's oldest civilisations. Today it covers a vast territory the size of Europe and is home to a quarter of the world's population. China has produced a highly distinctive culture with beautifully crafted objects made on an industrial scale from the earliest times. Room 33 examines the past 7,000 years of China's history, exploring the themes of writing systems, rituals, beliefs, war, international trade and more. Room 33 also explores South Asia's history, from 1.5 million years ago to the present day. Trade, exchange, migration and political power are some of the themes examined. South Asia spans India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, the Tibetan plateau and Sri Lanka. The region is home to numerous ancient and modern languages, such as Sanskrit, Tamil, Hindi and Urdu. The Buddhist, Hindu, Jain, Sikh and Islamic faiths have inspired magnificent architecture, sculpture, painting, literature and music.
persona2_adapted: China is one of the world's oldest civilizations. Today, it covers a vast area roughly the size of Europe and is home to a quarter of the world's population. From very early on, China developed a highly distinctive culture, producing beautifully made objects on a large, almost industrial scale. Room 33 looks back over 7,000 years of Chinese history, exploring topics such as writing systems, rituals, beliefs, warfare, and international trade. The gallery also examines the history of South Asia, from 1.5 million years ago up to the present day. Key themes include trade, exchange, migration, and political power. South Asia includes India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, the Tibetan plateau, and Sri Lanka. The region is home to many ancient and modern languages, including Sanskrit, Tamil, Hindi, and Urdu. Major religions—Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, Sikhism, and Islam—have inspired remarkable architecture, sculpture, painting, literature, and music.

CuratorialText:
Africa's rich cultural heritage and histories are represented at the Museum both in Africa (Room 25), The Sainsbury Galleries, and across the galleries. Join our volunteer-led tours, developed in collaboration with community members, to explore their personal selection of objects ranging from the ancient world to the present day. Each guide will deliver their own personal tour exploring one of the following themes:
Out of Africa: stories of migration, materials and mythology
The Igbo worldview: artefacts from the past in the British Museum
African metal artefacts: tradition, technique and trade
Ancient objects from African kingdoms
persona2_adapted:
Africa’s rich cultural heritage and histories are represented at the Museum in several gallery spaces, including Africa (Room 25), The Sainsbury Galleries, and others throughout the building. Join our volunteer-led tours, created in partnership with community members, to explore their personal selection of objects—from ancient times to the present day. Each guide will offer their own unique tour, focusing on one of the following themes:

- **Out of Africa:** stories of migration, materials, and mythology  
- **The Igbo worldview:** historical objects from the Igbo culture, now housed in the British Museum  
- **African metal artefacts:** traditions, techniques, and trade in metalworking across Africa  
- **Ancient objects from African kingdoms**

CuratorialText:
Step into spring and the month of May in this creative play session for children aged five and under.
Meet May Queens, Green Men and woodland creatures in this child-led, open-ended creative session. Stop and smell some floral scents, draw and scribble with a range of art materials and make crowns and masks to wear for spring festivities! Working at your child's pace, together we'll explore textures, colours and scents through a range of materials.
persona2_adapted: Step into the season of spring and the month of May with this imaginative play session designed for children aged five and under. You’ll encounter figures like May Queens, Green Men, and woodland creatures in a creative experience that is child-led and open-ended—meaning your child decides how to explore and engage. Take a moment to enjoy floral scents, draw and scribble with a variety of art supplies, and craft crowns and masks to wear for spring celebrations. Moving at your child’s own pace, together we’ll discover different textures, colors, and scents using a range of materials.

CuratorialText: Sennacherib was the king of Assyria from 704–681 BC and was famous for his building projects. The rooms and courtyards of his Neo-Assyrian Southwest Palace at Nineveh were decorated with a series of detailed carved stone panels. Many of these stone panels are on display in Room 9. The panels depict a variety of scenes, including the transport of huge sculptures of human-headed winged bulls (lamassu) that weigh up to 30 tons and were intended for the main entrances to the palace. These illustrations provide an insight into ancient quarrying and transport techniques, as well as Sennacherib's keen interest in his building projects. Other panels on display depict the king's military campaigns.
persona2_adapted: Sennacherib ruled Assyria from 704 to 681 BC and is best known for his ambitious construction projects. The rooms and courtyards of his Southwest Palace at Nineveh—built during the Neo-Assyrian period—were lined with intricately carved stone panels. Many of these panels are now displayed in Room 9. They show a variety of scenes, including the transport of enormous stone sculptures: human-headed winged bulls, known as *lamassu*, which weighed up to 30 tons and were designed to guard the palace’s main entrances. These images offer a window into ancient methods of quarrying and moving heavy materials, as well as Sennacherib’s deep personal involvement in his building works. Other panels on display depict the king’s military campaigns.

CuratorialText: Split across three rooms, these galleries display life during this period in Assyrian culture. On display are lion hunting reliefs, the siege of the city of Lachish and scenes from inside the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad.  Visitors to Room 10a are greeted by a pair of colossal human-headed winged bulls. Intended as a supernatural guardians, these gigantic figures guarded the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad, and were intended to deter evil of all kinds from entering.
persona2_adapted: Spread across three rooms, these galleries show what life was like during this period in Assyrian culture. On display are stone carvings showing lion hunts, the attack on the city of Lachish, and scenes from inside the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad. Visitors to Room 10a are greeted by a pair of enormous winged bulls with human heads. These giant figures were meant to serve as supernatural protectors, guarding the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad and warding off all kinds of evil from entering.

CuratorialText: In ancient Assyria, hunting lions was considered the sport of kings, symbolic of the ruling monarch's duty to protect and fight for his people. The sculpted reliefs in Room 10a illustrate the sporting exploits of the last great Assyrian king, Ashurbanipal (668–631 BC) and were created for his palace at Nineveh (in modern-day northern Iraq). The hunt scenes, full of tension and realism, rank among the finest achievements of Assyrian Art. They depict the release of the lions, the ensuing chase and subsequent killing.
persona2_adapted: In ancient Assyria, hunting lions was seen as the king's sport—a symbol of his responsibility to protect and fight for his people. The carved stone panels displayed in Room 10a show the hunting achievements of Ashurbanipal, the last great Assyrian king, who ruled from 668 to 631 BC. These panels were made for his palace in Nineveh, located in what is now northern Iraq. The hunting scenes are filled with tension and a lifelike quality, and they are considered some of the finest examples of Assyrian art. They show the lions being released, the chase that follows, and the final killing.

CuratorialText: Lachish was one of the chief cities of the kingdom of Judah in the southern Levant and in 701 BC it was captured by the Assyrian King Sennacherib (704–681 BC). The siege followed the refusal of Lachish to pay tribute to the Assyrian Empire (based in modern northern Iraq) and is mentioned in the Bible. Many of the relief sculptures on display in Room 10b depict the capture of the city, alongside a selection of items and weaponry used in the siege. A 'prism' inscribed with an Assyrian account of the campaign is also on show.
persona2_adapted: Lachish was one of the major cities of the kingdom of Judah, located in the southern part of the ancient Near East (roughly modern-day Israel and Palestine). In 701 BC, it was conquered by the Assyrian King Sennacherib (who ruled from 704 to 681 BC). The attack happened because Lachish refused to pay tribute—essentially, a form of enforced payment or tax—to the Assyrian Empire, which was based in what is now northern Iraq. This event is also mentioned in the Bible. Many of the carved stone panels, or relief sculptures, displayed in Room 10b show scenes from the city's capture, along with examples of the weapons and other equipment used during the siege. A clay prism, inscribed with the Assyrian version of the campaign, is also on view.

CuratorialText: The city and palace at Khorsabad (in modern northern Iraq), was built for the Assyrian King Sargon II (721–705 BC). The palace entrances were originally dominated by pairs of colossal human-headed winged bulls, which were intended as guardians, accompanied by protective spirits with magical powers. Two of these impressive statues now stand in Room 10c, along with carvings depicting the king and crown prince, royal courtiers and hunting scenes. Inscriptions on display in the gallery come from a similar winged bull from the palace of Sennacherib (704–681 BC) at nearby Nineveh and were badly burnt when the city was destroyed in 612 BC.
persona2_adapted: The city and palace at Khorsabad, located in what is now northern Iraq, were built for the Assyrian King Sargon II, who ruled from 721 to 705 BC. The entrances to the palace were originally flanked by pairs of enormous statues: human-headed winged bulls. These figures were meant to serve as protectors, accompanied by guardian spirits believed to possess magical powers. Two of these impressive statues are now displayed in Room 10c, alongside carvings that show the king, the crown prince, royal courtiers, and scenes of hunting. Inscriptions in the gallery come from a similar winged bull that once stood in the palace of Sennacherib, a later Assyrian king who ruled from 704 to 681 BC, at the nearby city of Nineveh. These inscriptions were badly burned when Nineveh was destroyed in 612 BC.

CuratorialText: Rooms 57–59 present the material culture of the region from the Neolithic farmers of the eighth millennium BC to the fall of the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BC, within the context of major historical events. Objects on display illustrate the continuity of the Canaanite culture of the southern Levant throughout this period. They highlight the indigenous origins of both the Israelites and the Phoenicians. The display compares this culture with that of the people of central inland Syria, the Amorites and the Aramaeans.
persona2_adapted: Rooms 57–59 showcase the everyday and ceremonial objects of the region, spanning from the first farming communities of the Neolithic period (around 8000 BC) to the collapse of the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BC. These items are presented within the framework of major historical events. The objects on display reveal how the Canaanite culture of the southern Levant—an area roughly covering modern-day Israel, Palestine, and Jordan—remained remarkably consistent over thousands of years. They also highlight the local roots of both the Israelites and the Phoenicians. The exhibition draws a comparison between this culture and that of the people from central inland Syria, specifically the Amorites and the Aramaeans.

CuratorialText: Ancient Anatolia and Urartu form an important land link between Europe and Asia. They lie where the modern Republic of Turkey, Armenia, Georgia and north-west Iran are located today.  Examples of Early Bronze Age craftsmanship on display include silver bull and cup. Business archives of Middle Bronze Age merchants illustrate trading between central Anatolia and Mesopotamia (modern Iraq). Delicate gold jewellery and figurines date from the Hittite period. Iron Age objects from Urartu, include winged bulls and griffins used to decorate furniture.
persona2_adapted: Ancient Anatolia and Urartu served as a vital land bridge connecting Europe and Asia. These regions are located in what is now the modern Republic of Turkey, Armenia, Georgia, and northwestern Iran. Among the objects on display from the Early Bronze Age are a silver bull and a cup, showcasing the skilled metalworking of the time. Business records from Middle Bronze Age merchants reveal the trade networks that linked central Anatolia with Mesopotamia (present-day Iraq). Delicate gold jewelry and small human or animal figures date from the Hittite period. Objects from the Iron Age kingdom of Urartu include winged bulls and griffins—mythical creatures with the body of a lion and the head of an eagle—that were used to decorate furniture.

CuratorialText: Reaching back more than 2,000 years, this exhibition explored the origins of Hindu, Jain and Buddhist sacred art in the ancient and powerful nature spirits of India, and the spread of this art beyond the subcontinent.  One of the first major exhibitions in the world to look at the early devotional art of India from a multi-faith, contemporary and global perspective, it highlighted the inspiration behind now-familiar depictions of the deities and enlightened teachers of these world religions – and how they were shared across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and along the Silk Roads to East Asia.
persona2_adapted: Reaching back more than 2,000 years, this exhibition explored the origins of sacred art in Hinduism, Jainism, and Buddhism. It began with the ancient and powerful nature spirits of India—beings that were worshipped long before these religions took shape—and traced how this art spread beyond the subcontinent. As one of the first major exhibitions worldwide to examine early devotional art in India from a multi-faith, contemporary, and global perspective, it revealed the inspiration behind now-familiar images of the deities and enlightened teachers of these world religions. The exhibition also showed how these images and ideas were shared across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and along the Silk Roads to East Asia.

CuratorialText: Colourful, multi-sensory and atmospheric, this exhibition was developed in collaboration with an advisory community panel of practising Hindus, Buddhists and Jains. These living religious traditions and their sacred art are now integral to the daily lives of almost two billion people around the world including in the UK. Key loans from our community partners helped to tell this contemporary story.
persona2_adapted: Vibrant, immersive, and rich in sensory detail, this exhibition was created in partnership with a community advisory panel made up of practicing Hindus, Buddhists, and Jains. These living faith traditions—and the sacred artworks central to them—are now a part of everyday life for nearly two billion people worldwide, including here in the UK. Important objects lent by our community partners helped bring this contemporary story to life.

CuratorialText: The exhibition showcased more than 180 objects – including sculptures, paintings, drawings and manuscripts – from the South Asian collection at the British Museum as well as generous loans from national and international partners. It highlighted provenance, examining the stories, from creation to acquisition by museums, of every object in the show.
persona2_adapted: The exhibition featured over 180 items—such as sculptures, paintings, drawings, and handwritten documents—drawn from the British Museum's South Asian collection, along with significant loans from institutions in the UK and abroad. A central focus was on each object's history, tracing its journey from the time it was made to how it eventually came to be owned by museums.

CuratorialText: From the symbolic footprints which preceded portrayals of the Buddha in human form to the cosmic serpents incorporated into Hindu art and the nature spirits who attend Jain enlightened teachers, this compelling exhibition told the ancient stories behind these living traditions.
persona2_adapted: From the symbolic footprints that once stood in for the Buddha before artists began showing him in human form, to the cosmic serpents woven into Hindu art and the nature spirits that accompany Jain enlightened teachers, this compelling exhibition revealed the ancient stories behind these still-living traditions.

CuratorialText: The first exhibition on Hiroshige to be held at the British Museum, and the first on the artist in London for more than a quarter of a century, this was a visually stunning portrait of a country about to change forever. Born during an unsettled time in Japan's history, Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) went on to become one of the country's most talented, prolific and popular artists. As Japan confronted the encroaching outside world, Hiroshige's calm artistic vision connected with – and reassured – people at every level of society. From fashionable figures and energetic city views to remote landscapes and impressions of the natural world, Hiroshige captured many aspects of life in the Japan of his time. Stunning bird-and-flower prints reveal his poetic feeling for nature while his evocative landscapes reflected the growing interest in travel across Japan. Hiroshige portrayed his world sometimes as it was, but often the way he imagined it could be.
persona2_adapted: The first exhibition on Hiroshige at the British Museum—and the first in London in over twenty-five years—offered a visually striking portrait of a country on the verge of profound change. Born during a turbulent period in Japan’s history, Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) became one of the nation’s most gifted, productive, and beloved artists. As Japan faced growing pressure from the outside world, Hiroshige’s calm artistic vision connected with—and offered reassurance to—people across all levels of society. His work captured many facets of life in his time: fashionable figures, lively city scenes, remote landscapes, and impressions of the natural world. Stunning prints of birds and flowers reveal his poetic sensitivity to nature, while his evocative landscapes reflect the era’s growing interest in travel across Japan. Hiroshige portrayed his world sometimes as it was, but often as he imagined it could be.

CuratorialText: Possessed of remarkable technical skills, both as a colourist and draftsman, Hiroshige had a sympathetic regard for people from all walks of life. Unlike most other print designers of his day, he came from a samurai family, but crossed social boundaries to devote himself to depicting popular customs. His work was affordable, too – along with celebrated landscape prints, he also designed hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that were available to all.
persona2_adapted: Possessing extraordinary technical ability, both in his use of color and his skill as a draftsman, Hiroshige showed a warm and understanding interest in people from every level of society. Unlike most other print designers of his time, he was born into a samurai family—a class of warriors and officials—yet he crossed social boundaries to focus his work on depicting everyday customs and ordinary life. His art was also affordable: in addition to his famous landscape prints, he created hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that were available to a wide range of people.

CuratorialText: The exhibition featured prints, drawings, illustrated books and paintings from the British Museum collection, as well as a significant gift and loan of prints from Alan Medaugh, a major US collector of Hiroshige's work, and other important loans. As well as exploring Hiroshige's incredible body of work, this show considered his global legacy, which spans from Japan's Edo period (1615–1868) through to Vincent van Gogh and contemporary artists such as Julian Opie.
persona2_adapted: The exhibition includes prints, drawings, illustrated books, and paintings from the British Museum’s collection. It also features a major gift and loan of prints from Alan Medaugh, a prominent American collector of Hiroshige’s work, along with other important loans. Beyond showcasing Hiroshige’s remarkable range of work, the exhibition examines his lasting influence around the world—from Japan’s Edo period (1615–1868) to artists like Vincent van Gogh and contemporary figures such as Julian Opie.

CuratorialText: A stone figure of 'Hara-Pārvatī' (Śiva seated with Pārvatī on his knee). Also known as 'Umamahesvaramurti'. Accompanied by a lion and a bull (Nandi) and surrounded by many flying and standing attendants (musicians, garland-bearers), there is also a row of ritual equipment: two conch-shell trumpets on stands, a lamp and a bell.
persona2_adapted: A stone sculpture depicts the Hindu deities Śiva and Pārvatī, shown here in a tender pose with Pārvatī seated on Śiva's knee. This type of image is also referred to as 'Umamahesvaramurti.' The couple is accompanied by a lion and a bull (the bull is named Nandi, a traditional companion of Śiva). Surrounding them are many attendants, some flying and others standing, including musicians and figures carrying garlands. Below them, a row of ritual objects is visible: two conch-shell trumpets placed on stands, a lamp, and a bell.

CuratorialText: Pair of bride's slippers (tarkasin) made of red-dyed leather embroidered in silk thread and decorated with metal studs. Tongues cut in the shape of the khamsa ('five') or 'Hand of Fatima'.
persona2_adapted: This pair of bride's slippers, called *tarkasin*, is made from red-dyed leather. They are embroidered with silk thread and decorated with small metal studs. The tongues of the slippers are cut in the shape of the *khamsa* (meaning "five" in Arabic), also known as the "Hand of Fatima"—a symbol often used for protection.

CuratorialText: Head and upper body of pink/grey granite monumental statue of Ramses II (one of a pair placed before the door of the Ramesseum) wearing nemes head-cloth and circlet of uraei (about half now lost), the sculptor has exploited the bichrome nature of the stone to emphasise the division between body and face; the dorsal pillar is inscribed with vertical registers of hieroglyphs - giving the name and titles of the king and part of a dedication to Amun-Ra; in 1817 it was noted that there were traces of colour upon the statue and it may have, therefore, been painted red in antiquity.
persona2_adapted: This head and upper body of a monumental statue of Ramses II is carved from pink and grey granite. It was originally one of a pair placed in front of the entrance to the Ramesseum, the king’s mortuary temple. The statue wears the royal headcloth, known as a *nemes*, and a circlet of sacred cobras, called *uraei*, though about half of that crown is now lost. The sculptor made deliberate use of the stone’s two-tone coloring to draw a clear visual line between the body and the face. On the pillar running down the statue’s back, vertical columns of hieroglyphs record the king’s name and titles, along with part of a dedication to the god Amun-Ra. In 1817, observers noted traces of color on the statue, suggesting it may originally have been painted red in ancient times.

CuratorialText: Head from monumental red granite statue of Amenhotep III(?).
persona2_adapted: Head from a large-scale statue of Amenhotep III (possibly), carved from red granite.

CuratorialText: Base and lid of the anthropoid wooden inner coffin of Hornedjitef, son of Nekhthorheb, who held a large number of priestly offices. Polychrome painted and gilded face, wig, collar and pectoral, winged scarab across breast, body inscribed with vertical registers of painted hieroglyphs, flanked by deities, the rest of the surface is unelaborated. Interior of lid decorated with a figure of Nut, surrounded by astronomical images of decan stars and constellations. Interior of base also decorated.
persona2_adapted: This is the inner coffin of Hornedjitef, the son of Nekhthorheb. Hornedjitef held many important roles as a priest. The coffin is made of wood and shaped like a human body. Its face, wig, and collar are painted in bright colors and covered with gold. A winged scarab beetle—a symbol of rebirth—is painted across the chest. The body is covered with vertical rows of painted hieroglyphs, with gods and goddesses shown on either side. The rest of the surface is left plain. Inside the lid is a painting of the sky goddess Nut, surrounded by images of stars and constellations that the ancient Egyptians used to track time. The inside of the base is also decorated.

CuratorialText: Brass (heavily leaded zinc-brass) head cast using the lost wax (cire perdue) technique. The head is a little under life size and is made in a naturalistic style. It has a headdress, suggesting a crown, of complex construction. The main tubular section of the crown runs around the head in a three-layer composition. The upper layer has a band of four horizontal rectangles representing flat, discoidal beads surmounted by a red-painted tubular bead and a tassel. The central layer has a row of vertical rectangles representing tubular beads with tassels. The lower layer has a row of rosettes painted in red. The main tubular section of the crown also has a projecting arc around the forehead comprising small tubular beads edged with a row of red-painted feathers. At the back of the crown is a neck cover. The central part of the cover has eighteen vertical elements incised to indicate plaitwork with traces of black paint. The bottom and sides have a row of red-painted rosettes. A crest rises above the central crown at the front. It comprises a conical roundel with central boss surrounded by seven concentric rings, probably representing beads. A plaitwork element rises behind the roundel, it terminates in a pointed ovoid tip; both elements bear traces of black paint. The face is slightly elongated and has vertical incised markings (striations). Two lines of holes run between the lobes of the ears, one going through the angle of the neck and the jaw, the second crossing the jaw below the lower lip. A double line of holes runs across the upper lip. The almond-shaped eyes are small in proportion to the head and the eyebrow-ridge is sharply defined. The lips are not striated. There are grooves round the front of the neck representing skin creases. There are holes pierced through the neck at the front and at both sides. There is a large irregular hole on the right proper side of the jaw.
persona2_adapted:
This head was cast in brass—a metal alloy containing a high amount of lead and zinc—using a method called lost-wax casting. In this technique, a model is first made in wax, then covered in clay or another heat-resistant material. When heated, the wax melts and drains out, leaving a hollow mold that can be filled with molten metal. The result is a one-of-a-kind sculpture.

The head is slightly smaller than life-size and is rendered in a naturalistic style, meaning it aims to look realistic rather than abstract or symbolic. It wears an elaborate headdress that suggests a crown, built in several layers. The main band of the crown wraps around the head in three tiers. The top tier features a row of four horizontal rectangles that represent flat, disc-shaped beads, each topped with a red-painted tubular bead and a tassel. The middle tier shows a row of vertical rectangles, which stand for tubular beads also with tassels. The bottom tier has a row of rosettes painted in red.

Above the forehead, the crown extends outward in a curved arc made of small tubular beads, edged with a row of red-painted feathers. At the back of the crown, there is a neck cover. The central part of this cover has eighteen vertical lines carved into it to suggest braided or woven work, with traces of black paint still visible. The bottom and sides of the neck cover are decorated with a row of red-painted rosettes.

At the front of the crown, a crest rises above the central band. It consists of a cone-shaped roundel with a central knob, surrounded by seven concentric rings—likely representing beads. Behind this roundel, a braided element rises and ends in a pointed, egg-shaped tip. Both the roundel and the braided element still show traces of black paint.

The face is slightly elongated and has vertical incised lines—fine grooves cut into the surface. Two lines of small holes run between the earlobes: one line passes through the angle of the neck and jaw, and the other crosses the jaw below the lower lip. A double line of holes runs across the upper lip. The eyes are almond-shaped and small compared to the size of the head, and the brow ridge is sharply defined. The lips are smooth, without incised lines. Grooves around the front of the neck suggest skin creases. There are holes pierced through the front and both sides of the neck. On the right side of the jaw, there is a large, irregular hole.

CuratorialText: Goddess. A standing figure of a female deity (Tārā) solid cast in bronze and gilded. The figure has a high tubular coiffure (jatamukuta) held in place by a medallion flanked by makara-s (water creatures). The medallion was probably set with stones, as were the eyes. The earlobes are elongated and the lower body dressed in a tight-fitting cloth knotted at the hips. The upper body is uncovered. The proper right hand is in varadamudrā (gesture of giving), the left hand in katakahastamudrā (the hand pose wherein the tips of the fingers are applied to the thumb so as to form a ring, as if to hold a flower). The two middle fingers of the right hand are missing as are toes from both feet.
persona2_adapted:
**Goddess**

This is a standing figure of a female deity, known as Tārā, made from solid bronze and covered with a layer of gold. Her hair is styled into a tall, tube-like topknot, held in place by a medallion. On either side of this medallion are mythical water creatures called *makara-s*. The medallion itself was likely set with precious stones, as were the figure’s eyes. Her earlobes are stretched long, and her lower body is wrapped in a tight-fitting cloth, knotted at the hips. Her upper body is bare.

Her right hand is held in a specific gesture known as *varadamudrā*, which symbolizes giving or blessing. Her left hand forms a pose called *katakahastamudrā*, where the tips of the fingers touch the thumb to create a ring, as if she were holding a flower. The two middle fingers of her right hand are missing, as are some toes from both feet.

CuratorialText:
The Virgin and child: cartoon; the Virgin half-length, Christ standing with his arms around his mother
Black chalk and/or charcoal, touches of white chalk, pricked and indented for transfer, on two conjoined sheets; framed
persona2_adapted: The Virgin and Child: A full-scale preparatory drawing (called a cartoon) showing the Virgin Mary from the waist up, with the infant Christ standing and wrapping his arms around her. The artist used black chalk and charcoal, with small highlights in white chalk. The surface has been pricked and indented with tiny holes and lines—techniques used to transfer the design onto another surface, such as a wall or panel for painting. The drawing is on two sheets of paper joined together, and it is presented in a frame.

CuratorialText: Marble torso of Nike, originally winged, wearing a sleeveless chiton; probably part of an akroterion.
persona2_adapted: Marble torso of Nike, the Greek goddess of victory, originally depicted with wings. She wears a sleeveless garment called a chiton. This sculpture was likely part of an akroterion—an ornament placed at the peak or corners of a temple roof.

CuratorialText:
Fragment of a marble corner palmetto with bird foot.
The small fragment originates from the standing, rectangular snapped off corner palmetto above a volute acroterion. One both sides of the middle leave, which protrudes from the corner, three lightly curved and hollow shaped leaves are visible and whose edges are accompanied by a bar. The round endings were emphasised on the outer side by tubercle. Darts appear between the leaves. On the left side of the fragment remains of a curved ovolo moulding of the volute is preserved. The upper and back side show a small pentagonal block with a 45^ (degrees) outstanding, discs-like tunnel. Leaves with a convex back site seem to grow out of the tunnel. On the block and on both sides of the tunnels two claws are visible with bird legs on each the edges
On the darts on the left side traces of red paint was found on the uppermost and the third. Traces of red and blue paint.
The light crystalline marble is fairly sanded on the surface, however, the underside is fractured.
persona2_adapted:
Fragment of a marble corner ornament shaped like a palm leaf, with a bird's foot attached.

This small piece comes from a larger, rectangular ornament that once stood at the corner of a roof or building. The ornament was attached above a spiral-shaped decorative element called a volute. On both sides of the central leaf—which sticks out from the corner—there are three gently curved, hollowed leaves. Each leaf is outlined by a raised ridge. The rounded tips of the leaves are emphasized on the outer side by small bumps. Pointed, arrow-like shapes appear between the leaves.

On the left side of the fragment, part of a curved, egg-shaped molding from the volute is still visible. The top and back of the piece show a small, five-sided block with a tunnel-like opening that angles outward at 45 degrees. Leaves with rounded backs seem to grow out of this tunnel. On the block and on both sides of the tunnel, there are two claw-like shapes, each with bird legs along the edges.

On the pointed shapes on the left side, traces of red paint were found on the topmost and third ones. There are also traces of red and blue paint elsewhere.

The light, crystal-like marble is fairly smooth on the surface, but the underside is rough and broken.

CuratorialText: Chess piece; walrus ivory; king: bearded; wearing floriated crown, tunic, mantle and brooch; seated upon high-backed chair holding sheathed sword; chair back: two horizontal compartments separated by zigzag band; upper filled with interlaced arcading of round arches; lower filled with symmetrical foliate ornaments; chair sides decorated with interlaced patterns.
persona2_adapted: This chess piece, carved from walrus ivory, represents a king. He is bearded and wears a crown decorated with floral designs, along with a tunic, a cloak, and a brooch. He sits on a high-backed chair, holding a sheathed sword. The back of the chair is divided into two horizontal sections by a zigzag band. The upper section is filled with a pattern of interlocking rounded arches, while the lower section features symmetrical leaf-like ornaments. The sides of the chair are decorated with interwoven patterns.

CuratorialText: Egyptian terracotta model palmette acroterion. The upper corner broken from a miniature altar, decorated on each side with a half-palmette. The altar was made in a multiple mould, probably of two pieces, but perhaps more. Traces of a matt red slip survive, inside and out and signs of burning within. Brown Nile silt with a light-brown core, abundant fine gold mica and some white inclusions.
persona2_adapted:
This small decorative piece, made from Egyptian terracotta (a type of baked clay), was originally the upper corner of a miniature altar. It is shaped like a half-palmette—a fan-like pattern inspired by palm leaves—and was designed to sit at the top or edge of the altar as an ornament.

The altar itself was made using a multi-part mold, likely with two pieces, though possibly more. Traces of a matte red coating, known as a slip, remain on both the inside and outside surfaces. There are also signs of burning inside, suggesting it may have been used in rituals involving fire.

The clay used is brown Nile silt, with a lighter brown core, and it contains abundant fine gold mica (a shiny mineral) along with some white inclusions (small embedded particles).

CuratorialText: Indigo resist-dyed cloth (adire eleko); on factory-made cotton; two panels machine sewn tgether. Each panel is divided into four by seven squares. Hand-painted patterns include two squares with the pillars of Mapo Hall (in Ibadan) between which are four pestles (or spoons).
persona2_adapted: This cloth is a type of indigo-dyed fabric known as *adire eleko*, a traditional Nigerian textile. It is made from factory-produced cotton, with two panels machine-stitched together. Each panel is divided into a grid of four rows by seven squares. The patterns are hand-painted, and include two squares featuring the pillars of Mapo Hall, a historic building in Ibadan, Nigeria. Between these pillars are four shapes that may represent pestles or spoons.

CuratorialText:
Ticket addressed to A. W. Franks to see Falero's painting 'Nightmare' at the Gainsborough Gallery, with image of a creature behind the ticket, with a bat's wing, claw and bone rib cage. 1888
Wood-engraving and letterpress
persona2_adapted:
Ticket addressed to A. W. Franks for viewing Falero's painting "Nightmare" at the Gainsborough Gallery. The ticket features an image of a creature with a bat's wing, claw, and skeletal rib cage. 1888
Wood engraving (a technique where the design is carved into a block of wood and then printed) and letterpress (a method of printing using raised type).

CuratorialText:
Ticket to see Frith's painting 'The Railway Station', with central image of a steam train arriving at the station with figures; empty space at the top to add name. 1865
Lithograph
persona2_adapted:
**Ticket to see Frith's painting 'The Railway Station'**  
This ticket was printed in 1865 for visitors to view William Powell Frith's large, detailed painting *The Railway Station*. The artwork's central scene shows a steam train pulling into a busy station, surrounded by many figures—passengers, workers, and onlookers. The empty space at the top of the ticket was left blank so that the owner's name could be written in.  
The ticket is a lithograph, meaning it was created using a printing method where an image is drawn on a flat stone or metal plate with a greasy substance, then inked and pressed onto paper. This technique allowed multiple copies to be made efficiently.

CuratorialText:
Paper admission ticket. (whole)
Photographs of, left to right, Klimt painting, the Leopold Museum and Shiele painting; inscription in black above: multicolour print on white background.
Inscription, in black print on white background.
persona2_adapted: This is a paper admission ticket. It features photographs of, from left to right, a painting by Gustav Klimt, the Leopold Museum building, and a painting by Egon Schiele. Above these images, there is an inscription printed in black on a white background, using multiple colors. The ticket also includes additional text printed in black on white.

CuratorialText:
Paper admission ticket. (whole)
Photograph, view of the Basilica Cistern in Istanbul showing columns and arched roof; inscription to right in black: multicolour print on pink and white background.
Unidentified historical painting showing forces gathered, tree to left: multicolour print on white background.
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This object is a paper admission ticket. It features a photograph of the Basilica Cistern in Istanbul, highlighting its rows of columns and arched ceiling. To the right of the image, there is an inscription printed in black. The ticket is printed in multiple colors on a pink and white background.

The ticket also includes a reproduction of an unidentified historical painting. This artwork shows a gathering of military forces, with a tree visible on the left side of the scene. Like the photograph, this image is printed in multiple colors on a white background.

CuratorialText:
View of the interior of a tent with visitors gathered before Copley's huge history painting (Guildhall Art Gallery), elaborately framed with pillars and swags, including two young men at left flirting with young women, and two older men at right seated with papers, figures at a desk behind at left; facsimile of the admission ticket to the exhibition, from an engraving by Bartolozzi. c.1850s-60s
Photomechincal reproduction
persona2_adapted: This scene shows the inside of a tent where visitors are gathered in front of a massive history painting by Copley (now in the Guildhall Art Gallery). The painting is framed in an elaborate style, with columns and decorative fabric swags. On the left, two young men are flirting with young women; on the right, two older men sit with papers, and there are figures at a desk behind them on the left. The image also includes a copy of the admission ticket to the exhibition, taken from an engraving by Bartolozzi. This reproduction was made around the 1850s–1860s using a photomechanical process, meaning it was created by transferring a photographic image onto a printing surface.

CuratorialText:
Admission ticket to a performance organised by the Guild of Literature and Art, with figure representing the painter Richard Wilson at left, taking a painting to a pawn shop, and at right figure representing Daniel Defoe, with a manuscript of 'Robinson Crusoe' in his pocket, leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll with a sad expression on his face, met by his worried wife and daughter; books and pallette with paintbrushes lying in foreground; after Ward. 1851
Mixed method, including etching, aquatint and engraving, on chine collé
persona2_adapted:
This admission ticket was created for a performance organized by the Guild of Literature and Art. On the left side, a figure representing the painter Richard Wilson is shown taking a painting to a pawn shop. On the right, the writer Daniel Defoe—with a manuscript of *Robinson Crusoe* in his pocket—is leaving the office of publisher Edmund Curll. His expression is sad, and he is met by his worried wife and daughter. In the foreground lie books and a palette with paintbrushes. The image was made after a design by Ward in 1851.

The print was created using a combination of techniques: etching, aquatint, and engraving. It is printed on *chine collé*, a method where a thin sheet of paper is bonded to a thicker backing paper.

CuratorialText:
Admission ticket to a performance organised by the Guild of Literature and Art, with figure representing the painter Richard Wilson at left, taking a painting to a pawn shop, and at right figure representing Daniel Defoe, with a manuscript of 'Robinson Crusoe' in his pocket, leaving the office of the publisher Edmund Curll with a sad expression on his face, met by his worried wife and daughter; books and pallette with paintbrushes lying in foreground; after Ward. 1851
Mixed method, including etching, aquatint and engraving, on chine collé

On the right, the writer Daniel Defoe is depicted leaving the office of publisher Edmund Curll, with a sad expression on his face. A manuscript of his famous novel *Robinson Crusoe* can be seen in his pocket. Defoe is met by his worried wife and daughter. In the foreground, books and an artist’s palette with paintbrushes lie scattered. The image was made after an earlier design by Ward, in 1851.

The print was created using a combination of techniques: etching, aquatint, and engraving. It was printed on a type of paper called *chine collé*, where a thin sheet of paper is bonded to a thicker backing sheet.

CuratorialText:
