CuratorialText:
Ticket to a special exhibition at the South Kensington Museum, with signature by James Beck; am emblem on the top left with the letters VR and a crown above. 1862
Woodcut and letterpress
persona2_adapted: Ticket for a special exhibition at the South Kensington Museum, signed by James Beck. In the top-left corner, there is an emblem featuring the letters "VR" with a crown above it. 1862. This item was made using woodcut (a printing technique where the image is carved into a block of wood) and letterpress (a method of printing with movable type).

CuratorialText:
Advertisement for "Aspinall's Enamel"; Princess Alexandra, full length, seated to right, painting a stool with Aspinall's Enamel; illustration to "The Queen, the Lady's Newspaper" (3 December, 1887).
Wood engraving
persona2_adapted: This advertisement for "Aspinall's Enamel" shows Princess Alexandra seated, facing to the right, as she paints a stool with the product. The image was originally published as an illustration in *The Queen, the Lady's Newspaper* on December 3, 1887. The print is a wood engraving—a technique where the design is carved into a block of wood, inked, and pressed onto paper to create the image.

CuratorialText: Large glazed and framed oil painting on canvas depicting a swirling abstract composition dominated by crimson on a dark background. The central bright design has areas of thickly applied paint while in the black background the contracting of the paint surface has produced a textured effect.
persona2_adapted: A large oil painting on canvas, framed and covered with glass, shows a swirling abstract scene. The main colors are deep red against a dark background. In the bright center, the paint is applied in thick layers, creating a raised, textured surface. In the black areas around it, the paint has shrunk as it dried, producing a cracked or wrinkled effect.

CuratorialText: Painting. Oil painting on canvas. The adoration of the Śālagrāma is shown in the middle of the picture. To the left, is Balakṛṣṇa holding the butter ball. On the right, a female figure holds a parasol over a central throne which suggests an aniconic reverence to Viṣṇu, also suggested by the large conch positioned centrally in the foreground. Other ritual objects typically used in 'puja' can be seen too: a copper water-pourer and a bell.
persona2_adapted:
**Painting. Oil on canvas.**  
At the center of the scene, we see the worship of the Śālagrāma—a sacred stone believed to embody the presence of the god Viṣṇu. To the left stands Balakṛṣṇa, the child form of the god Kṛṣṇa, holding a ball of butter. On the right, a female figure holds a parasol over a central throne. This arrangement suggests a form of reverence that does not depict Viṣṇu directly, but instead honors him through symbolic objects—a practice known as aniconic worship. That idea is reinforced by the large conch shell placed prominently in the foreground, a traditional emblem of Viṣṇu. Other ritual items commonly used in *puja* (Hindu devotional worship) are also visible, including a copper water-pourer and a bell.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Lady Layard, wife of Henry Layard; oil painting on canvas in a carved and gilded wood frame. Lady Layard is seated on the balcony of Ca Capello, that Layards' house in Venice, overlooking the Grand Canal. She wears a closely-fitting belted red jacket and a black skirt with an enormously long train; she holds a fan in her right hand and leans on the balcony balustrade. Open French doors set with glass panels give onto an interior with potted plants; in the distance is a view across the Grand Canal.
persona2_adapted: Portrait of Lady Layard, wife of Henry Layard; an oil painting on canvas set in a carved and gilded wooden frame. Lady Layard is seated on the balcony of Ca' Capello, the Layards' home in Venice, looking out over the Grand Canal. She wears a close-fitting red jacket with a belt and a black skirt that trails into an extremely long train behind her. In her right hand, she holds a fan, and she leans against the balcony railing. Behind her, open French doors with glass panels lead into an interior space filled with potted plants. In the distance, the view opens across the Grand Canal.

CuratorialText: Painting in mixed media (?oil pencil) of a bearded head and shoulders of a male figure. This is one from the series 'Telingana Faces'. The figure wears a square amulet around his neck. Signed and dated by the artist. Mounted
persona2_adapted: This painting, created in mixed media (possibly oil pencil), shows the head and shoulders of a bearded man. It is part of a series called *Telingana Faces*. The figure wears a square amulet around his neck. The work is signed and dated by the artist, and it has been mounted for display.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Voltaire (1694-1778), T.Q.L., standing, body nearly in profile to r., head turned half r., and eyes looking full, wearing dark green coat trimmed with gold lace, and holding book in his l. hand.
persona2_adapted: This portrait depicts Voltaire (1694–1778), the famous French writer and philosopher. He stands with his body turned mostly to the right, his head angled slightly toward the viewer, and his eyes looking directly at us. He wears a dark green coat trimmed with gold lace and holds a book in his left hand.

CuratorialText:
Painting; portrait of Se-Tan-Si-Tan, also known as Yellow Hawk a Dakota Sans Arc Chief, sitting. Wearing three feathers in his hair, ear and neck -ornaments, decorated shirt with feathers on upper sleeves and blanket wrap; holding a Pipe-tomahawk.
Oil on canvas
persona2_adapted: This is a painted portrait of Se-Tan-Si-Tan, also known as Yellow Hawk, a chief of the Dakota Sans Arc people. He is shown seated, wearing three feathers in his hair, along with ear and neck ornaments. His shirt is decorated, with feathers adorning the upper sleeves, and he is wrapped in a blanket. In his hands, he holds a pipe-tomahawk—a combined ceremonial pipe and weapon. The work is an oil painting on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Johann Gutenberg (c. 1398-1468), H.L., turning half-r., the eyes looking full, wearing a cap, holding a wood block with the letters of the alphabet A-I.
Oil painting on canvas
persona2_adapted:
**Portrait of Johann Gutenberg (c. 1398–1468)**  
The subject is shown from the waist up, his body turned slightly to the right, while his eyes meet the viewer’s gaze directly. He wears a cap and holds a wooden block carved with the letters A through I—a clear reference to his invention of movable type, which revolutionized printing in Europe.  
Oil on canvas

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Isabella Clara Eugenia of Austria (1566-1633), Infanta of Spain, half-length turned slightly to left, wearing white dress trimmed with red rosettes etc., large ruff, and jewelled headdress; after Frans Pourbus II
Oil painting on canvas
persona2_adapted:
**Portrait of Isabella Clara Eugenia of Austria (1566–1633), Infanta of Spain**

This painting shows Isabella Clara Eugenia from the waist up, her body turned slightly to the left. She wears a white dress trimmed with red rosettes, a large, stiff lace collar known as a ruff, and a jeweled headdress. The work is a copy made after an original by the Flemish artist Frans Pourbus the Younger, who was known for his detailed portraits of European royalty. Painted in oil on canvas, the portrait reflects the formal style and rich symbolism of court portraiture in the late 16th and early 17th centuries.

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Augustus II ('The Strong') (1670-1733), King of Poland and Elector of Saxony, three-quarter length, standing, body turned half-right, head half-left, wearing armour and ermine-trimmed cloak, and holding sceptre.
Oil painting on canvas
persona2_adapted:
**Portrait of Augustus II ('The Strong') (1670–1733), King of Poland and Elector of Saxony**

In this painting, the king is shown from the chest down to just above the knees—a format often used to convey both authority and presence. He stands with his body angled slightly to the right, while his face turns gently to the left, a pose that suggests both readiness and reflection. He wears armor, a symbol of military strength, and a cloak trimmed with ermine—a type of fur historically associated with royalty and high status. In his hand, he holds a scepter, an emblem of sovereign power.

This is an oil painting on canvas, a technique that allows for rich color and detailed texture.

CuratorialText:
Portrait said to be of Mary Tudor (1516-58) as Princess; Head and shoulders, 3/4 face, eyes looking full. Wearing ruff, earring, embroidered dress, ?chain, headdress.
Oil painting on canvas
persona2_adapted: This portrait is believed to show Mary Tudor (1516–58) before she became queen, when she was still a princess. The painting captures her from the chest up, with her body turned slightly to the side while her eyes meet the viewer’s gaze directly. She wears a ruff—a stiff, pleated collar popular in the 16th century—along with an earring, an embroidered dress, and what may be a chain or necklace. Her hair is covered by a headdress. The work is an oil painting on canvas.

CuratorialText: Portrait of Jacques (aka Jean, John, James) Rousseau (1630-93), Oval, head and shoulders turned half-r., eyes looking full, wearing dark coat and lace cravat. Oil on canvas.
persona2_adapted: Portrait of Jacques Rousseau (1630–93), shown in an oval frame from the chest up, his body turned slightly to the right while his eyes meet the viewer’s gaze directly. He wears a dark coat and a lace cravat. Painted in oil on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Portrait of Dr Robert G W Anderson. Head, profile, facing to sitter's right
Oil painting on panel. The subject is looking out of a window in the artist's studio at Talfourd Road, Peckham. Superimposed on the window is a section of the Sloane astrolabe. The tie is a version of the Great Court ties sold at the British Museum around the end of 2000, to mark the opening of the Great Court.
persona2_adapted: Portrait of Dr. Robert G. W. Anderson. The painting shows his head in profile, facing to the right. It is an oil painting on a wooden panel. The subject is looking out of a window in the artist's studio on Talfourd Road in Peckham. Overlaid on the window is a section of the Sloane astrolabe—a historical instrument used for measuring the positions of stars and planets. The tie he wears is a version of the Great Court ties that were sold at the British Museum around the end of 2000, to celebrate the opening of the Great Court.

CuratorialText: Oil painting on canvas stretched and glued to back of stained wooden frame. Painting shows scene of an imaginary beach side village with huts and trees in the foreground, ocean and sky with full moon behind. Painting is signed.
persona2_adapted: Oil painting on canvas, which has been stretched and glued to the back of a stained wooden frame. The painting depicts an imaginary beachside village, with huts and trees in the foreground, and the ocean and sky behind, where a full moon shines. The painting is signed.

CuratorialText: Oil painting; commercial sign-board made of plywood, used to advertise barbers' shop hairstyles. Six individual heads are depicted in profile, each numbered and displaying a different haircut. The heads face in different directions and are painted in bold black outlines on a white background; each head has a blue collar.
persona2_adapted: Oil painting on a plywood sign-board, originally used to advertise hairstyles at a barber shop. It shows six individual heads in profile view, each numbered and featuring a different haircut. The heads face various directions and are painted with strong black outlines against a white background; each head includes a blue collar.

CuratorialText: A large oil painting on canvas depicting the slaying of the dragon by St George who is the central figure of the painting, mounted on a white horse and carrying a circular shield in one hand, a spear in the other. The dragon, with striped skin and small spotted wings, lies on its back beneath the horse's hooves, together with a little blue vanquished devil, his face in profile. To the right of the painting Biruwit is depicted in the branches of a tree; to the left stand a group of soldiers armed with spears and shields.
persona2_adapted: A large oil painting on canvas shows Saint George killing a dragon. George is the main figure in the scene, riding a white horse. He holds a round shield in one hand and a spear in the other. The dragon, which has striped skin and small spotted wings, lies on its back beneath the horse's hooves. Next to the dragon is a small blue defeated devil, shown in profile, or from the side. On the right side of the painting, a figure named Biruwit appears in the branches of a tree. On the left, a group of soldiers stands holding spears and shields.

CuratorialText: Oil painting; wooden frame painted brown; matte of white mesh; at centre is miniature oil painting on white background with brown matte around it; painting shows a matador waving a red cape in front of a bull. Written on back: "El Paso del Desden: 1.8 cm x 2 cm ozeo (?) s/ P. Ingres Fabr. Guillermo Calaveo Lazaro 1 217 Oct de 1985"
persona2_adapted: This small oil painting is set within a brown-painted wooden frame. A white mesh matte surrounds the central image, which is a miniature oil painting on a white background, framed by a brown border. The scene depicts a matador waving a red cape in front of a bull. On the back of the piece, an inscription reads: "El Paso del Desden: 1.8 cm x 2 cm ozeo (?) s/ P. Ingres Fabr. Guillermo Calaveo Lazaro 1 217 Oct de 1985."

CuratorialText:
Painting; pouches made from the skin of small animals; used for carrying tobacco, smoking barks and weeds. plate 13.
Oil on cardboard
persona2_adapted: This small painting shows pouches made from the skin of small animals. These pouches were used for carrying tobacco, as well as smoking barks and weeds. The work is painted in oil on cardboard. It is listed as plate 13 in the series.

CuratorialText:
Painting; three Javanese men; wearing head-dress, shirt and sarong. Man at the front has a kris or sword, secured to his back with a waist wrap. House and trees are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona2_adapted: Three Javanese men are depicted in this oil painting on canvas. They wear traditional headdresses, shirts, and sarongs—a type of wrapped garment. The man in the foreground carries a kris, a distinctive Indonesian sword, secured to his back with a cloth wrapped around his waist. In the background, a house and trees complete the scene.

CuratorialText:
Painting; full length portrait a Javanese woman; wearing head, neck, arm ornament and designed costume. People, trees and two thatched buildings are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona2_adapted: This full-length portrait depicts a Javanese woman adorned with elaborate head, neck, and arm ornaments, as well as a richly designed costume. In the background, we see figures, trees, and two thatched buildings, which together suggest a specific cultural setting. The work is executed in oil on canvas, a common painting technique that uses pigment mixed with oil to create depth and texture.

CuratorialText:
Painting; full length portrait of a Javanese man holding a spear; wearing a helmet, striped shirt and leggings and sarong with waist wrap. He carries a kris either side of his waist wrap and a sword hanging on his left side. Trees and buildings, one with a large flag are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona2_adapted: A full-length portrait of a Javanese man, standing with a spear in hand. He wears a helmet, a striped shirt and leggings, and a sarong—a traditional wrapped skirt—secured with a waist wrap. Tucked into either side of the waist wrap are two kris, distinctive Javanese daggers with wavy blades, and a sword hangs from his left side. In the background, trees and buildings are visible, one of which displays a large flag. The work is painted in oil on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; portrait of Wunagisa, also known as Moses Keokuk, Jr; was principal Chief of the Sacs & Fox Nation; wearing headdress with feathers, ear-rings, necklaces, one made of bear claws, shirt and a decorated blanket wrap; holding a Pipe-tomahawk and fan made of feathers.
Oil on canvas
persona2_adapted: This portrait shows Wunagisa, also known as Moses Keokuk, Jr., who served as the principal chief of the Sac and Fox Nation. He is depicted wearing a feathered headdress, earrings, and several necklaces—including one made from bear claws—along with a shirt and a decorated blanket wrapped around him. In his hands, he holds a pipe-tomahawk (a ceremonial weapon that combines a pipe for smoking with a small axe) and a fan made of feathers. The work is painted in oil on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; from a collection of seventeen. An Aboriginal Australian woman from Spring Creek, near Port Fairy, Victoria; wearing head-band and possum skin cloak.
Oil on canvas.
persona2_adapted: A painting from a set of seventeen. It depicts an Aboriginal Australian woman from Spring Creek, near Port Fairy, in Victoria. She is shown wearing a headband and a cloak made from possum skin. The work is painted in oil on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; portrait of a Native North American Chief, name unknown; wearing two feathers in his hair, neck ornament, decorated frill shirt, blanket wrap and holding a Pipe-tomahawk and tobacco pouch.
Oil on canvas
persona2_adapted: This is a painted portrait of a Native North American chief, whose name is not known. He wears two feathers in his hair, a neck ornament, a shirt with a decorated frill, and a blanket wrapped around him. In his hands, he holds a pipe-tomahawk—a combined weapon and smoking pipe—along with a tobacco pouch. The work is an oil painting on canvas.

CuratorialText:
Painting; side view of a Javanese soldier holding a kris and circular shield; wearing head-dress, top garment and serong, with a kris-sheath secure to his back with his waist wrap. People, trees and the top of a building are in the background.
oil on canvas
persona2_adapted: A painting shows a Javanese soldier from the side. He holds a kris—a traditional Southeast Asian dagger with a distinctive wavy blade—and a round shield. He wears a headdress, an upper garment, and a *serong* (a type of cloth draped over the shoulder). A sheath for his kris is secured to his back by the cloth wrapped around his waist. In the background, there are people, trees, and the top of a building. The work is painted in oil on canvas.

Oil on cardboard
persona2_adapted: Five painted pipe bowls, each decorated with images of men, bears, and a woman, are shown above a similarly decorated tomahawk pipe. This work is painted in oil on cardboard.

CuratorialText:
Framed painting; portrait of Sina Kavaliku, seated on the ground, wearing a western-style dress, her hair in plaits and a flower in her hair. Tonga. 1951.
Oil on canvas.
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This framed oil painting, created in 1951, depicts a woman named Sina Kavaliku seated on the ground. She wears a Western-style dress, and her hair is styled in plaits with a flower tucked into it. The work is from Tonga and was painted using oil on canvas—a technique in which pigments are mixed with oil (typically linseed) and applied to a fabric surface, allowing for rich color and texture.

CuratorialText: Pottery Protocorinthian alabastron decorated with a lion and two griffins; wheel-made; depressed-ovoid body on a rounded base; small mouth with a sloping rim; pierced lug handle at the top; made of fine buff clay and slip, with painted decoration which has fired black; also added purple paint on the animal bodies; details of the figures are incised. The main frieze shows two griffins separated by stylised tree-like motif; a lion occupies the space between the two mythical beasts; filling motifs in the field. Above the main scene is a frieze of stylised floral designs; below simpler motifs composed of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. Simple rays on the vase, and tongues on the lip.
persona2_adapted:
This small pottery container, known as an alabastron, was made in the ancient Greek region of Corinth. It was shaped on a potter's wheel, with a rounded, egg-like body, a narrow mouth, and a small handle at the top. The artist used fine, light-colored clay and a thin coating called slip, then painted the surface with black pigment that turned dark during firing. Purple paint was also added to the animals' bodies, and details were scratched into the surface.

The main band of decoration shows two griffins—mythical creatures with the head and wings of an eagle and the body of a lion—separated by a stylized, tree-like design. A lion stands between these two beasts. The background is filled with smaller decorative shapes. Above this scene is another band of stylized floral patterns, and below it, simpler motifs made of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. The vase itself has simple ray-like lines, and the rim is decorated with tongue-shaped patterns.

CuratorialText: IWM holds approximately 11 million photographs of modern conflict from the First World War to present day.
persona2_adapted: The IWM’s collection includes around 11 million photographs documenting modern conflict, from the First World War to the present day.

CuratorialText: The collection covers the activities of British and Commonwealth forces and international perspectives. Photographs from official, press, and private perspectives such as soldiers, nurses and civilians who participated in or witnessed conflict reveal the human impact of war. The collection includes notable photographers such as Olive Edis, Bill Brandt and Tim Hetherington.
persona2_adapted: This collection documents the actions of British and Commonwealth forces, as well as offering viewpoints from around the world. Photographs taken by official military sources, the press, and private individuals—including soldiers, nurses, and civilians who took part in or observed conflict—show us the human toll of war. Among the photographers featured are well-known figures such as Olive Edis, Bill Brandt, and Tim Hetherington.

CuratorialText: The photographs can be viewed alongside original documents which help us understand how and why these photographs were taken.
persona2_adapted: The photographs are presented together with original documents, which offer insight into the methods and motivations behind their creation.

CuratorialText:
Uniforms, Flags, Badges and Insignia
An important and international collection of flags and uniforms, the latter representing all of the armed services and institutions of not only Great Britain and the Commonwealth, but Allies as well as former enemies along with their associated badges and insignia.
persona2_adapted:
**Uniforms, Flags, Badges, and Insignia**

This is a significant international collection of flags and uniforms. The uniforms represent all branches of the armed forces and official institutions—not only from Great Britain and the Commonwealth, but also from Allied nations and former enemy countries. The collection also includes the badges and insignia (the emblems or symbols worn on uniforms to show rank, unit, or affiliation) associated with these groups.

CuratorialText:
Mementoes and Miscellanea
A diverse collection containing objects of personal significance, including escape and evasion items, prisoner of war and civilian internee items, trench art, battlefield souvenirs, commemorative objects, foodstuffs, currency, tobacciana, toys and games, Kindertransport and Holocaust related items, home-front and peace-time items.
persona2_adapted:
**Mementoes and Miscellanea**

This varied collection brings together objects that held deep personal meaning for those who owned them. It includes items used for escape and avoiding capture, belongings from prisoners of war and civilians held in internment camps, and "trench art"—objects crafted by soldiers from wartime materials like shell casings. You will also find souvenirs collected from battlefields, objects made to commemorate people or events, preserved food and currency, tobacco-related items, toys and games, and objects tied to the Kindertransport (the rescue of mostly Jewish children before the war) and the Holocaust. Finally, the collection includes items from life on the home front and from peacetime.

CuratorialText:
Orders, Medals and Decorations
An important collection comprising orders, medal and decorations issued to service personnel and civilians.
persona2_adapted:
Orders, Medals and Decorations  
This significant collection includes orders, medals, and decorations awarded to both military personnel and civilians.

CuratorialText:
Equipment
An extensive collection of equipment used in twentieth and twenty-first-century warfare, including radios, cipher machines, radars, cameras, medical equipment and even pigeon baskets.
persona2_adapted:
Equipment  
A wide-ranging collection of tools and devices used in warfare from the 1900s to the present day. This includes radios, code-making and code-breaking machines, radar systems, cameras, medical supplies, and even baskets designed to carry carrier pigeons.

CuratorialText:
Weapons and Ammunition
A major reference collection that includes firearms, edged weapons, trench clubs or concussive weapons, ordnance and munitions.
persona2_adapted:
**Weapons and Ammunition**  
This is a key study collection, containing firearms, bladed weapons, trench clubs (used for blunt-force impact), and various types of artillery and ammunition.

CuratorialText:
Vehicles, Aircraft and Ships
An internationally renowned collection including models, battle tanks, maritime equipment and aircraft components. Most of the collection of artillery and large military equipment is on show at IWM London or IWM Duxford.
persona2_adapted:
**Vehicles, Aircraft and Ships**  
This world-famous collection features models, battle tanks, maritime equipment, and aircraft parts. Most of the artillery and large military machinery is displayed at IWM London or IWM Duxford.

CuratorialText: IWM’s Sound Collection is home to over 35,000 recordings relating to conflicts since 1914.  It is the largest oral history archive of its kind in the world, with more than 60,000 hours of material and interviews in over 15 different languages.  Beyond interviews it also includes speeches, sound effects, radio broadcasts and music. The collection covers the wartime experiences of ordinary men and women- from soldiers and activists, to factory workers and journalists. It also includes notable figures such as Field Marshal Bernard Montgomery and Vera Lynn.  Our unique collection of oral history interviews has formed the basis for significant academic research as well as featuring heavily in bestselling books, films, TV and radio broadcasts and podcasts. Many thousands of our audio recordings can be listened to via the IWM website, which is regularly updated with new additions.
persona2_adapted: IWM’s Sound Collection holds more than 35,000 recordings related to conflicts since 1914. It is the largest archive of its kind in the world, containing over 60,000 hours of material and interviews in more than 15 languages. In addition to interviews, the collection includes speeches, sound effects, radio broadcasts, and music. These recordings capture the wartime experiences of ordinary people—soldiers and activists, factory workers and journalists—as well as notable figures like Field Marshal Bernard Montgomery and Vera Lynn. This unique archive of oral history interviews has supported major academic research and has been widely used in bestselling books, films, television and radio programs, and podcasts. Thousands of these audio recordings are available to listen to on the IWM website, which is regularly updated with new material.

CuratorialText:
IWM has managed a Film Archive for over a century. From its very beginnings as an institution IWM understood the importance of film as primary source material and its extensive collection illustrates the causes, course and consequences of conflict from the First World War to the present day. A remarkable resource for researchers and film-makers
Our constantly growing collection now extends to over 25,000 hours of moving images, representing a wide and diverse range of material from public and service information films, documentaries and unedited combat film, through to official newsreels and amateur films. Two substantial collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia are also held. An ongoing film digitisation programme provides free online access, with newly-digitised media added as soon as it becomes available.
persona2_adapted:
IWM has been managing a Film Archive for over a hundred years. From the very beginning, the institution recognized that film could serve as a primary source—a firsthand record of events—and its vast collection shows the causes, course, and consequences of conflict from the First World War to today. This makes it an invaluable resource for researchers and filmmakers alike.

Our constantly growing collection now includes more than 25,000 hours of moving images. It covers a wide and diverse range of material: public information films and service films (made to inform or instruct), documentaries, unedited combat footage, official newsreels, and amateur films. We also hold two substantial collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia. An ongoing film digitization program provides free online access, with newly digitized media added as soon as it becomes available.

CuratorialText:
Private Papers
IWM holds over 20,000 individual collections of private papers, principally comprising unpublished diaries, letters and memoirs written primarily by British and Commonwealth servicemen and women since 1914 and by civilians mainly during the two world wars. We also hold many important documents from victims and survivors of the Holocaust.
persona2_adapted:
**Private Papers**

IWM holds over 20,000 individual collections of private papers. These are mainly unpublished diaries, letters, and memoirs written by British and Commonwealth service members since 1914, as well as by civilians, mostly during the two world wars. The collection also includes many important documents from victims and survivors of the Holocaust.

CuratorialText:
Foreign and Related Documents
We also look after a large collection of foreign documents, including the official British records of the major war crimes trials conducted at Nuremberg and Tokyo, together with related documents from the Second World War which mainly concern the German and Japanese war efforts.
persona2_adapted:
Foreign and Related Documents

Our collection also includes a large number of documents from outside the United Kingdom. Among these are the official British records of the major war crimes trials held in Nuremberg and Tokyo, as well as related materials from the Second World War. These documents primarily focus on the German and Japanese war efforts.

CuratorialText: This exceptional collection is one of the most important representations of twentieth century British art in the world. It includes many great works of art from the British government war art schemes of the First and Second World Wars, which employed the greatest artists of their day, including leaders of the avant garde. These included Paul Nash, C R W Nevinson, John Singer Sargent and Sir William Orpen.  IWM also holds the unique War Artists Archive, which reveals the day-to-day running of the war art schemes and gives fascinating glimpses of the artists' experiences.
persona2_adapted: This exceptional collection is one of the most important displays of twentieth-century British art anywhere in the world. It includes many major works created through British government programs that commissioned artists to document the First and Second World Wars. These programs hired the most celebrated artists of the time, including leaders of the avant garde—those who were pushing the boundaries of traditional art with new, experimental styles. Among them were Paul Nash, C R W Nevinson, John Singer Sargent, and Sir William Orpen. IWM also holds the unique War Artists Archive, which reveals how these government art programs operated day to day and offers fascinating insights into the artists' own experiences.

CuratorialText: Our Library collection consists of over 150,000 items covering twentieth and twenty-first century conflict involving British and Commonwealth countries. Along with material on military units and campaign histories, we have biographies, manuals, items relating to the economic, social, and cultural aspects of war, journals, newspapers, ephemera, propaganda, and e-resources.
persona2_adapted: Our Library holds more than 150,000 items that document conflicts from the twentieth and twenty-first centuries involving Britain and Commonwealth countries. In addition to records about military units and the history of specific campaigns, the collection includes biographies, training manuals, and materials exploring the economic, social, and cultural dimensions of war. You will also find journals, newspapers, short-lived printed items (known as ephemera), propaganda materials, and digital resources.

CuratorialText: image: HMS Laurentic is seen sinking in the background, with half of the ship submerged and her bow rising up out of the water. In the foreground is a lifeboat full of sailors illuminated against the dark by a small light. Another lifeboat is visible on the right and small dots of light in the background indicate further lifeboats. In the background on the left, the top of the German U-boat is visible. HMS Laurentic, originally a P&O liner, was equipped with 6" guns in 1939 and converted into an armed merchant cruiser. She was Worsley's first ship and he remained with her until she was sunk off Foreland, Ulster by the U-boat captain Otto Kretschmer (U-99) on the night of 3 November 1940. Worsley painted the original immediatly afterwards, while on survivors leave. The WAAC bought the picture and in August 1942 sent it to South America in a touring exhibition of war art. On the way, however, the ship was torpedoed and the present picture was painted as a replacement, at the request of the WAAC. (From exhibition catalogue, Burstow Gallery, Brighton, 1995)
persona2_adapted:
In this painting, we see HMS *Laurentic* sinking in the background, half-submerged with its bow rising out of the water. In the foreground, a lifeboat filled with sailors is lit against the darkness by a small light. Another lifeboat appears on the right, and tiny points of light in the distance suggest more lifeboats. On the left side of the background, the top of a German U-boat is visible.

Originally a passenger liner for the P&O shipping company, HMS *Laurentic* was fitted with 6-inch guns in 1939 and turned into an armed merchant cruiser—a civilian ship armed for military use. This was the first ship the artist, Worsley, served on, and he stayed with her until she was sunk off the coast of Foreland, Ulster, by German U-boat captain Otto Kretschmer (U-99) on the night of 3 November 1940.

Worsley painted the original version immediately afterward, while on leave as a survivor. The War Artists Advisory Committee (WAAC) purchased the painting and, in August 1942, sent it to South America as part of a traveling exhibition of war art. However, the ship carrying the painting was torpedoed en route. At the WAAC’s request, Worsley painted this version as a replacement. (From exhibition catalogue, Burstow Gallery, Brighton, 1995)

CuratorialText: image: A view across a tree-lined road to a high wall bordering a railway cutting. The wall has grafitti on it which reads 'Nuclear state = Police state' followed by an anarchy symbol and then 'One World'. Behind the wall to the right is a large industrial building. Further to the right is a junction with a road leading off to the left.'In the summer of 1983 ... I discovered the old train station in Mildmay Grove and started what became a set of ten drawings of the section of line between Canonbury and Kingsland Road. It was while doing the second drawing that I realized the line I was looking at was used several times a week to take nuclear waste from Sizewell [nuclear power station] to Sellafield [nuclear fuel reprocessing plant]. Over the next six weeks, I found it impossible to escape that realization, and it inextricably meshed with every mark I made.' [Eric Rimmington, from the exhibition catalogue 'A World's Waste: Cumbria, Sellafield and Nuclear Reprocessing, A Brewery Arts Centre Touring Exhibition' 1987.] The artist made this series of drawings at a time of intense anti-nuclear protests against both nuclear weapons and the nuclear power industry. The 1979 decision by the British Government to allow American Cruise Missiles to be sited in the UK sparked a series of marches and protests. These included the Greenham Common Women's Peace Camp, started in 1981 and which was at its height in 1983 when the missiles finally arrived at the Berkshire base. The accident at Three Mile Island, a nuclear power station in Pennsylvania, USA, also in 1979, heightened fears about the safety of nuclear power. Nuclear waste and its transport and storage continues to be a focus of protest and anxiety. The Greater London Assembly commissioned a report into the safety of nuclear waste trains in 2001 for this reason.
persona2_adapted:
In the summer of 1983, the artist Eric Rimmington discovered an old train station in Mildmay Grove, which led him to create a series of ten drawings of the railway line between Canonbury and Kingsland Road. While working on the second drawing, he realized that this same line was used several times a week to transport nuclear waste from the Sizewell nuclear power station to the Sellafield nuclear fuel reprocessing plant. Over the next six weeks, he found this knowledge impossible to ignore, and it became deeply woven into every mark he made on paper.

This series of drawings was created during a period of intense public opposition to nuclear weapons and nuclear power. In 1979, the British government decided to allow American Cruise Missiles to be stationed in the UK, which sparked a wave of marches and protests. One notable example was the Greenham Common Women's Peace Camp, established in 1981 and reaching its peak in 1983 when the missiles finally arrived at the Berkshire base. That same year, the accident at Three Mile Island, a nuclear power station in Pennsylvania, USA, further intensified fears about the safety of nuclear energy. The issue of nuclear waste—including how it is transported and stored—remains a source of protest and concern. For instance, in 2001, the Greater London Assembly commissioned a report specifically on the safety of trains carrying nuclear waste.

The image shows a view across a tree-lined road toward a high wall bordering a railway cutting. The wall is covered in graffiti that reads "Nuclear state = Police state," followed by an anarchy symbol and then "One World." Behind the wall to the right stands a large industrial building, and further to the right is a junction where a road leads off to the left.

CuratorialText:
Object description
image: Five children are seated around a table in room lit by a large window. An elderly woman sits at the centre with the youngest child on her lap. A large 'Spotted-Dick' pudding is placed on the table with several bowls and cutlery set around it. The woman serves a boy on the far right who holds out a bowl for his portion.
Label
'Mrs Norris, of Cookham, with five of her war family of seven children from London who had by January of 1941 been with her for sixteen months. Mrs Norris is the ideal foster-parent, and there are very many other householders like her. Over a million children have been moved in school parties from the evacuation areas, and with such large numbers, boarding out in billets must always be the principal means of finding homes for them. Given Mrs Norris' friendliness and care, town children readily adapt themselves to their new surroundings.' (title from folder cover, Children in Wartime).
persona2_adapted:
**Label**

In this photograph, five children are seated around a table in a room lit by a large window. An elderly woman sits at the center with the youngest child on her lap. A large pudding—a traditional British "spotted dick"—rests on the table, surrounded by bowls and cutlery. The woman is serving a boy on the far right, who holds out his bowl for his portion.

The original caption, taken from the folder cover titled *Children in Wartime*, reads:

> "Mrs Norris, of Cookham, with five of her war family of seven children from London who had by January of 1941 been with her for sixteen months. Mrs Norris is the ideal foster-parent, and there are very many other householders like her. Over a million children have been moved in school parties from the evacuation areas, and with such large numbers, boarding out in billets must always be the principal means of finding homes for them. Given Mrs Norris' friendliness and care, town children readily adapt themselves to their new surroundings."

This image captures a quiet domestic scene during a time of national upheaval. During World War II, the British government organized the evacuation of over a million children from cities considered at risk of bombing. They were sent to safer rural areas and housed with local families—a system known as "billeting." Mrs. Norris is one such host, caring for seven children from London. The photograph presents her as a model of the calm, nurturing care that officials hoped would help urban children adjust to life away from home. The scene is deliberately ordinary: a shared meal, a familiar pudding, a moment of everyday kindness. Yet it also reflects a massive social experiment, in which private homes became part of the national war effort.

CuratorialText: image: portrait of a female factory worker operating a lathe. Miss Loftus had been brought to the attention of the War Artist's Advisory Committee as 'an outstanding factory worker'. Knight expected to do a studio portrait but the Ministry of Supply requested that she be painted at work in the Royal Ordnance Factory in Newport. Making a Bofors Breech ring was considered the most highly skilled job in the factory, normally requiring eight or nine years training. Loftus was aged 21 at the time of the painting and had no previous factory experience. Her ability to operate the machine presented a considerable publicity coup at the time. However, it has been suggested that she was placed at the machine precisely for this reason. Industrial machinery was a wholly new element in Knight's work but her technical accuracy was praised in contemporary reports: Knight, like Loftus, was proving herself in a traditionally male environment. Loftus was an outstanding factory worker who had mastered complex engineering skills in a very short space of time, and Knight was commissioned to paint her at work in the factory. Knight was fascinated by circus artistes and dancers, and she emphasises the balance and posture of her subject at work. Industrial machinery was a wholly new element in Knight's work but her technical accuracy was praised in contemporary reports: Knight, like Loftus, was proving herself in a traditionally male environment.
persona2_adapted:
In this portrait, we see a young woman named Miss Loftus operating a lathe—a machine used for shaping metal. She had been recommended to the War Artist's Advisory Committee as an exceptionally skilled factory worker. The artist, Laura Knight, originally planned to paint a formal studio portrait, but the Ministry of Supply asked that Loftus be shown at work in the Royal Ordnance Factory in Newport. The task she is performing—making a Bofors Breech ring—was considered the most technically demanding job in the factory, one that normally required eight or nine years of training. Loftus was just 21 at the time and had no previous factory experience. Her ability to operate the machine was a major public relations success for the war effort. However, some have suggested that she may have been placed at the machine specifically for this reason—to create a powerful image of a young woman mastering advanced industrial work.

Industrial machinery was a completely new subject for Knight, but contemporary reports praised the technical accuracy of her painting. In this way, Knight, like Loftus, was proving herself in a field traditionally dominated by men. Knight had long been fascinated by circus performers and dancers, and here she draws attention to the balance and posture of her subject as she works. The portrait captures not only Loftus’s skill but also the grace and concentration required to perform such a demanding job.

In the center, a group of soldiers in overcoats includes several with bandaged limbs. The upper half of the scene shows the station roof filled with steam and smoke rising from the train below.

CuratorialText:

CuratorialText:
image: a skeletal Death drives a bulldozer through the rubble, debris and detritus of war. Bricks, metal struts and tyres litter the ground and there is a flaming object on the right.
Label
This is a fantasy of utter destruction, as death rides in on a bulldozer. Only the flags stand tall, criss-crossing but ultimately adding to the confusion. The only architectural remains are bricks and girders. Michael Sandle is primarily a sculptor and has produced a number of public monuments. A key theme of his work is warfare, influenced by his early experiences of air raids in Plymouth and subsequent National Service. This is one of a series of six prints entitled 'The Driver'.
persona2_adapted:
In this imagined scene of total devastation, Death—shown as a skeleton—drives a bulldozer through the wreckage left by war. The ground is covered with bricks, metal beams, and tires, while a burning object glows on the right. Only flags remain standing, crossing each other but only adding to the chaos. The few surviving structures are just bricks and girders.

Michael Sandle is mainly a sculptor, and he has created several public monuments. A central theme in his work is warfare, shaped by his early memories of air raids in Plymouth and his later time in National Service. This print is part of a series of six called *The Driver*.

CuratorialText: image: a view through a few trees across the large field of crosses of the British military cemetery at Etaples on the French coast. The coastline is bordered by trees and a sailing boat is visible in the English Channel.

CuratorialText:
image: The Royal Navy destroyer HMS Eclipse in the process of sinking in the Aegean Sea. The ship appears to have broken in two, with only the stern and bow sections above water. The bow section is on fire, with a bright cloud of flames and smoke billowing up into the night sky. There is another ship in the background and two Greek islands are visible to the left and right.
Label
On 23 October 1943 the two destroyers HMS Eclipse and HMS Petard sailed along the Turkish coast bound for Leros, one of the Greek Dodecanese Islands, which was occupied by a British garrison. On board the Eclipse was Brigadier-General George Davy, the Director of Military Operations, Middle East, who was enroute to inspect the defences of Leros. The ships were also carrying troops, ammunition and supplies for the garrison. At 11.45pm the Eclipse struck a mine between Kos and Kalymnos, blew up and sank in only two minutes. In addition to the ship's complement of around 200 men, she was carrying another 200 soldiers, including a company of the Buffs. Only around 100 men were picked up by an air-sea rescue launch of the RAF dispatched from Leros and boats sent from HMS Petard, after spending over three hours in the water. Davy was amongst the survivors. He went onto become the Commander of Land Forces, Adriatic, retiring from the Army in 1949. He recorded this traumatic event 24 years after it had occurred.
persona2_adapted:
On the night of October 23, 1943, two British warships—the destroyers HMS *Eclipse* and HMS *Petard*—were sailing along the coast of Turkey, heading for the Greek island of Leros. At the time, Leros was one of the Dodecanese Islands and was held by a British garrison. Aboard the *Eclipse* was Brigadier-General George Davy, a senior military officer responsible for overseeing operations in the Middle East. He was traveling to inspect the island’s defenses. The ships were also carrying troops, ammunition, and supplies for the garrison.

Just before midnight, at 11:45 PM, the *Eclipse* struck a mine in the sea between the islands of Kos and Kalymnos. The explosion tore the ship apart, and it sank in only two minutes. In addition to its regular crew of about 200 men, the destroyer was carrying another 200 soldiers, including a company from the British infantry regiment known as the Buffs. Only around 100 men survived. They were rescued after spending more than three hours in the water, picked up by an air-sea rescue launch sent from Leros and by boats from the *Petard*.

Brigadier-General Davy was among the survivors. He later became the Commander of Land Forces in the Adriatic region and retired from the army in 1949. He recorded this traumatic event 24 years after it happened, describing the ship breaking in two, with its bow on fire and smoke rising into the night sky, while two Greek islands were visible in the distance.

CuratorialText: image: An abstract composition of coiled and twisted girders of all sizes. This drawing explores the distortions made by bombs to building structures. The violence of the blast is evident in the rhythm and the form of the girders. Paule Vézelay was born in Bristol and returned there during the Second World War after over 10 years as an artist in Paris. She never gained an official war artist commission due to her left wing associations and the abstract nature of her work. However with the help of Bristol Museum and Art Gallery she gained a permit to draw the ruins in Bristol.
persona2_adapted:
This drawing shows a tangle of twisted metal beams, all different sizes, coiled together in an abstract way. It captures how bombs distort the structures of buildings. The violence of the explosion comes through clearly in the rhythm and shape of the beams.

Paule Vézelay was born in Bristol and returned there during the Second World War, after spending more than ten years working as an artist in Paris. She never received an official commission as a war artist—likely because of her left-wing political views and the abstract style of her work. However, with help from Bristol Museum and Art Gallery, she was able to get a permit to draw the city's ruins.

CuratorialText: Acknowledged as one of the greatest landscape artists of all time, Joseph Mallord William Turner (1775–1851) knew Oxford extremely well. From childhood, he made numerous drawings and watercolours of the streets and colleges and the picturesque views surrounding the city. His celebrated oil painting of the High Street, Oxford is on display in the 19th-Century Art Gallery. To mark the 250th anniversary of J.M.W. Turner's birth on 23 April 1775, explore this selection of his depictions of Oxford from the Ashmolean's collections. The Museum's Western Art Print Room houses just over 100 watercolours and drawings by Turner, as well as many landscape etchings. There are lively studies of animals, birds and fish, as well as evocative views of places that the artist visited on his travels in Britain and Europe, from Margate to Venice.
persona2_adapted: Considered one of the greatest landscape painters of all time, Joseph Mallord William Turner (1775–1851) knew Oxford very well. From childhood, he created many drawings and watercolours—paintings made with pigments mixed with water—of the city’s streets, colleges, and the charming views around it. His famous oil painting of Oxford’s High Street is now on display in the 19th-Century Art Gallery. To celebrate the 250th anniversary of Turner’s birth on 23 April 1775, explore this selection of his Oxford scenes from the Ashmolean’s collections. The Museum’s Western Art Print Room holds just over 100 watercolours and drawings by Turner, along with many landscape etchings—prints made by carving a design into a metal plate. There are lively studies of animals, birds, and fish, as well as evocative views of places the artist visited during his travels in Britain and Europe, from Margate to Venice.

CuratorialText: In the second millennium BCE the island of Crete was home to a complex pre-Greek society known as ‘the Minoans’. When archaeologist Sir Arthur Evans started to excavate at the site of Knossos on Crete, in 1900, he frequently connected the things he found with ancient Cretan myths. He named the building at Knossos the ‘Palace of Minos’, after the mythical king of Crete, and connected it to the story of the Labyrinth. The Labyrinth was an ingenious maze commissioned by King Minos to imprison the ferocious Minotaur, a creature with the head of a bull and the body of a man. In the myth, the Minotaur is killed by Theseus, the famous semi-divine hero. Following Theseus's victory, he returned to Greece, and became king and the founder of Athens. The Cretan myths originated in stories which the Greek and Roman inhabitants of the city of Knossos would have told long after the Palace had been destroyed.
persona2_adapted: In the second millennium BCE—roughly 4,000 years ago—the island of Crete was home to a sophisticated pre-Greek society that archaeologists call 'the Minoans.' When the archaeologist Sir Arthur Evans began digging at the site of Knossos on Crete in 1900, he often linked the objects he uncovered to ancient Cretan myths. He named the large building he found there the 'Palace of Minos,' after the legendary king of Crete, and connected it to the story of the Labyrinth. According to the myth, the Labyrinth was an elaborate maze built for King Minos to hold the terrifying Minotaur—a creature with the head of a bull and the body of a man. In the story, the Minotaur is killed by Theseus, a famous hero who was part human and part divine. After his victory, Theseus returned to Greece, became king, and was later regarded as the founder of Athens. These Cretan myths originated in stories that the Greek and Roman inhabitants of Knossos would have told long after the Palace had fallen into ruin.

CuratorialText:
Landscape painting has a long tradition in East Asian art. However, landscape did not become an important feature of Japanese woodblock prints until the early 1800s. By this time, Japanese citizens were taking part in more outdoor sightseeing activities, such as temple visits and boat trips, and there was a growing demand for souvenir images. In the competitive world of the publishing industry, publishers were constantly trying to develop new products; landscape prints were an appealing novelty.

Technical factors also played an important part in the emergence of the landscape print. Particularly significant was the introduction of the new imported pigment Prussian Blue. Unlike earlier Japanese blues, which faded very quickly, this brilliant colour gave artists more freedom of expression, especially in the depiction of sky and water

Here we feature a selection of highlights from the master of landscape prints, Utagawa Hiroshige, as well as selected works by Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.
persona2_adapted:
Landscape painting has a long history in East Asian art. However, it wasn’t until the early 1800s that landscapes became a major subject in Japanese woodblock prints. Around this time, more Japanese people were enjoying outdoor sightseeing—like visiting temples or taking boat trips—and there was a growing demand for souvenirs that captured these experiences. In the competitive world of publishing, companies were always looking for new ideas, and landscape prints offered something fresh and appealing.

Technical developments also helped make landscape prints possible. One key factor was the introduction of a new imported blue pigment called Prussian Blue. Unlike earlier Japanese blues, which faded quickly, this vivid color gave artists more freedom to express themselves, especially when depicting sky and water.

Here we present a selection of highlights from Utagawa Hiroshige, a master of the landscape print, along with selected works by Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.

CuratorialText:
The collection of antiquities includes internationally renowned archaeological collections from Prehistoric Europe, the Ancient Near East, Ancient Egypt and Sudan, Minoan Aegean, Mycenaean and Classical Greece, Ancient Cyprus, the Roman world, Medieval and later Europe, as well as the museum's Founding Collection and historic archives.

It also includes one of the oldest, largest and best-preserved collections of casts of Greek and Roman sculpture in the UK.
persona2_adapted:
The collection of antiquities spans internationally celebrated archaeological finds from prehistoric Europe, the ancient Near East, ancient Egypt and Sudan, the Minoan and Mycenaean civilizations of the Aegean, classical Greece, ancient Cyprus, the Roman world, medieval and later Europe, as well as the museum's original founding collection and historical archives.

It also holds one of the oldest, largest, and best-preserved collections of plaster casts—reproductions of Greek and Roman sculptures—in the UK.

CuratorialText: In our Eastern Art collections you will find sculpture, textiles, ceramics and paintings from the Islamic Middle East, China, Japan, Southeast Asia, India and the Himalayas.

CuratorialText: The Museum’s Heberden Coin Room is one of the leading international coin cabinets with particular strengths in the fields of Greek, Roman, Celtic, Byzantine, Medieval, Islamic and Chinese coinages. It also holds collections of paper money, tokens, jetons and commemorative art medals.

CuratorialText: The Western Art collections contain outstanding European fine and decorative arts from the Renaissance to the present day. They include Old Master and later paintings, drawings, watercolours, and prints; English and continental ceramics, sculpture, silver, watches, rings and musical instruments. The Ashmolean's exceptional print collection contains around 25,000 drawings and over 250,000 prints and many are accessible through our Western Art Print Room.
