These include paintings by earlier masters and later artists, along with drawings, watercolours, and prints. The collections also feature English and European ceramics, sculpture, silverware, watches, rings, and musical instruments. The Ashmolean’s remarkable print collection holds around 25,000 drawings and over 250,000 prints, many of which can be viewed in our Western Art Print Room.

CuratorialText: The painting resulted from an important early commission for two watercolours to be engraved for the annual calendar published by the University Press, the Oxford Almanack. They were so successful that Tuner eventually completed ten watercolours between 1798 and 1804. This work and one other were later painted in oils for the local print-seller and frame-maker, James Wyatt. Turner consulted Wyatt on the details of the architecture included in the view. He also introduced figures, members of the University and clergy, and some women ‘for the sake of colour’. The painting is one of the most fully documented of all Turner’s works because Wyatt kept his correspondence with the artist which was included in his posthumous sale. The original purpose of the commission was to have the design engraved. Wyatt settled on an oil painting, instead of a watercolour, half the size of Turner’s normal canvases, at a cost of 100 guineas. The painting was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1812. When, in the 1830s, Turner was choosing views of Oxford for his greatest series of watercolours, he rejected the High Street. He felt that in this painting he had achieved an unparalleled view of technical mastery that he could not repeat.
persona2_adapted:
This painting came from an early, important job Turner received: he was asked to create two watercolors that would be turned into engravings—printed images—for the *Oxford Almanack*, a yearly calendar published by the University Press. The watercolors were so successful that Turner eventually made ten of them between 1798 and 1804. Later, this work and one other were painted in oils for a local print-seller and frame-maker named James Wyatt.

Turner asked Wyatt for advice on the architectural details shown in the scene. He also added figures—members of the university and clergy—along with some women, as he put it, “for the sake of colour.” This painting is one of the best-documented of all Turner’s works, because Wyatt kept the letters Turner sent him, and those letters were later sold after Wyatt’s death.

The original goal of the commission was to have the design turned into an engraving. But Wyatt decided on an oil painting instead of a watercolor, half the size of Turner’s usual canvases, for a fee of 100 guineas. The painting was shown at the Royal Academy in 1812. Later, in the 1830s, when Turner was choosing views of Oxford for his most famous series of watercolors, he decided not to paint the High Street again. He felt that in this painting he had achieved a level of technical skill that was so remarkable he could not match it.

CuratorialText: Together with a St Francis (Christ Church, Oxford) and a Risen Christ (National Gallery of Wales), WA1850.13 and WA1850.13.1 come from a lost altar-piece by Granacci, painted around 1520. The four panels may have been placed in the pilasters. Granacci was a follower of Ghirlandaio who was particularly interested in the art of Fra Bartolomeo and Raphael.
persona2_adapted: These four panels—including this one and its companion pieces at Christ Church, Oxford, and the National Gallery of Wales—once belonged to a larger religious painting, or altarpiece, created by the artist Granacci around 1520. The original work is now lost, but these surviving sections may have been set into the vertical columns, or pilasters, that framed the main image. Granacci was a student of the painter Ghirlandaio, and his work shows a strong interest in the styles of two other artists: Fra Bartolomeo and Raphael.

CuratorialText: These are pinnacle panels that once belonged to the same unknown altar-piece, which may have been crowned with a central pinnacle panel showing Christ on the Cross. The artist was Florentine, working in a late Gothic manner and an attribution to Bartolomeo di Fruosino has recently been suggested. A painter and miniaturist, Bartolomeo was a pupil of Agnolo Gaddi.
persona2_adapted: These are the topmost panels from a single, unknown altarpiece. The central panel, now lost, may have shown Christ on the Cross. The artist was from Florence and worked in a late Gothic style—meaning the painting still followed the decorative, elegant traditions of the Middle Ages, just before the more naturalistic Renaissance took hold. Recently, experts have suggested that the artist might be Bartolomeo di Fruosino, a painter and manuscript illustrator who studied under Agnolo Gaddi.

CuratorialText: As a nocturnal landscape and as a brilliantly structured composition, The Hunt in the Forest is a highly original painting. Hunting was an aristocratic pastime with its own rituals, but the idea of a hunt at night is playful and may be symbolic rather than realistic. In preparing this work, the artist Paolo di Dono first mapped out a grid on the panel's surface, fixing a central vanishing point to draw our gaze deep into the composition. In painting the subject, he incorporated details including the huntsmen's spears, the cut branches, logs and the water in the foreground as devices to bring focus to this space and to the receding forms of men and animals. Gold flecks in the foliage of the trees would have complemented the bright colours of the figures against the dark forest, and would have glistened and become animated when illuminated by candlelight. Paolo di Dono (1397-1475) was celebrated in his lifetime as a master of perspective, animals and landscape; his nickname, Uccello (‘Bird’), alludes to his depictions of the natural world. He was a versatile designer, working at times on mosaic and stained-glass commissions and all of these skills have been cleverly combined to create this masterpiece. The Hunt is what is known as a spalliera painting, which would have been mounted at shoulder height – whether as a backboard of a decorated chest or set into the wooden panelling of a room. The original client is unknown, but the painting was clearly intended for a Renaissance palace, perhaps in Urbino where Uccello worked for a time from 1465, or in Florence about 1470.
persona2_adapted:
In this adaptation, the original text is rephrased to be clear and engaging for a native English speaker without specialized art knowledge, while preserving the depth and nuance of the original.

---

As a night-time scene and a carefully structured composition, *The Hunt in the Forest* is a highly original painting. Hunting was an aristocratic pastime with its own traditions, but the idea of a hunt at night is playful and may be more symbolic than realistic. To prepare this work, the artist Paolo di Dono first drew a grid on the panel's surface, fixing a central point where lines of perspective meet—called a vanishing point—to draw our gaze deep into the scene. In painting the subject, he included details such as the hunters' spears, cut branches, logs, and the water in the foreground as ways to focus attention on this space and on the receding figures of men and animals. Gold flecks in the leaves of the trees would have complemented the bright colors of the figures against the dark forest, and would have glittered and seemed to come alive when lit by candlelight. Paolo di Dono (1397–1475) was famous in his lifetime as a master of perspective, animals, and landscape; his nickname, Uccello (meaning "Bird"), refers to his depictions of the natural world. He was a versatile designer, sometimes working on mosaics and stained-glass projects, and all of these skills have been cleverly combined to create this masterpiece. *The Hunt* is what is known as a *spalliera* painting—a type of artwork that would have been mounted at shoulder height, either as the backboard of a decorated chest or set into the wooden paneling of a room. The original buyer is unknown, but the painting was clearly intended for a Renaissance palace, perhaps in Urbino, where Uccello worked for a time starting in 1465, or in Florence around 1470.

CuratorialText: The portrait corresponds generally to a number of paintings and prints of Louix XIII of France on a prancing horse. From the type and handling of the paint, it seems to be either by or after a Flemish painter. The appearance of the king suggests a date of about 1622.
persona2_adapted: This portrait resembles several paintings and prints that show King Louis XIII of France riding a horse that is rearing up, or prancing. Based on the style and technique used to apply the paint, the work appears to be either by a Flemish painter or a copy made after such an artist. The king's appearance in the image suggests it was created around 1622.

CuratorialText: The Magdalen is recognisable by her jar of ointment, a reference to her traditional identification with the woman who anointed the feet of Christ and dried them with her hair (Luke 7: 36-50). Possibly cut from a larger composition, this small panel has been enlarged by being fitted into another and the figure extended. The artist may have worked in Parma in the early 17th century in the circle of Bartolomeo Schedoni.
persona2_adapted: Mary Magdalene is identified by the jar of ointment she holds, which refers to the biblical story of the woman who anointed Christ’s feet and dried them with her hair (Luke 7:36–50). This small painting may have originally been part of a larger scene; it was later set into another panel and the figure was extended to fill the new space. The artist was likely active in Parma in the early 1600s, working among the followers of Bartolomeo Schedoni.

CuratorialText: The landscape is reminiscent of Éragny. It may have been painted in the summer of 1893 or 1894, before Félix Pissarro left Éragny for Belgium. In: Jon Whiteley, Nineteenth-century French Paintings in the Ashmolean Museum, (Oxford: Ashmolean Museum 2022)
persona2_adapted: The landscape shown here resembles the area around Éragny, a village in northern France. It was likely painted in the summer of either 1893 or 1894, just before Félix Pissarro moved from Éragny to Belgium. This description is based on the catalog entry by Jon Whiteley in *Nineteenth-century French Paintings in the Ashmolean Museum* (Oxford: Ashmolean Museum, 2022).

CuratorialText: A free copy, dating from the late 17th or early 18th century, of the fresco in one of the spandrels of the painted Loggia di Psiche in the Villa Farnesina, Rome. Raphael and his studio decorated this space in 1518-19 with scenes from the story of Cupid and Psyche. The spandrel from which WA1859.1 was copied may have been painted by Raphael's senior assistant, Giulio Romano. The copyist has altered the format to a square shape.
persona2_adapted: A freehand copy, made in the late 1600s or early 1700s, reproduces a section of a painted ceiling in the Villa Farnesina in Rome. The original painting is part of a series of scenes from the myth of Cupid and Psyche, which the artist Raphael and his workshop created in 1518-19. The specific section copied here comes from one of the curved, triangular spaces between the arches of the ceiling—an area that may have been painted by Raphael's senior assistant, Giulio Romano. The copyist changed the original shape, turning it into a square.

CuratorialText: Three of the objects in this still life are found elsewhere in Renoir’s late work: the silver beaker appears alongside a sugar bowl and knife in a small still life said to have been painted at Cagnes in 1910 and again in a similar composition without the knife, also said to have been painted in 1910; the bronze-coloured child on the column was included in a small study, dated c.1905 by the Daubervilles. The faience double salt, a type produced in quantity in the nineteenth century at Desvres in the Pas-de-Calais and at Quimper in Brittany, appears in at least four other sketches. A disembodied image of the same figure, seen from the back, hovers in upper right, probably a reflection in a mirror. Renoir, who worked as a painter at a ceramic manufactory in his early days, seems to have owned a number of pieces of faience of this popular type⁵ and used a variety of coloured and decorated ceramic containers in his flower paintings.
persona2_adapted: Three of the objects in this still life also appear in other works from Renoir’s later career. The silver beaker, for example, is shown with a sugar bowl and knife in a small still life said to have been painted at Cagnes in 1910, and again in a similar composition without the knife, also dated to 1910. The bronze-colored child figure on the column was included in a small study dated around 1905 by the Daubervilles. The double salt container made of faience—a type of glazed earthenware that was mass-produced in the 19th century in the French towns of Desvres and Quimper—appears in at least four other sketches. A faint, bodiless image of the same figure, seen from behind, hovers in the upper right corner, likely a reflection in a mirror. Renoir, who worked as a painter at a ceramics factory early in his career, seems to have owned several pieces of this popular type of faience and used a variety of colorful, decorated ceramic containers in his flower paintings.

CuratorialText: Although damaged, this portrait of an unknown woman is of fine quality and has affinities with the work of Andrea in the 1520s. Apart from a short stay in Rome c.1516, Andrea spent most of his career in Siena and in Florence, where he collaborated with his brother Raffaello Piccinelli.
persona2_adapted: Although this portrait of an unidentified woman is damaged, it is still of high quality and shows similarities to the work of the artist Andrea in the 1520s. Aside from a brief visit to Rome around 1516, Andrea worked mainly in the cities of Siena and Florence, where he collaborated with his brother Raffaello Piccinelli.

CuratorialText: After 1739, Ruysch painted mainly small-scale paintings of bouquets and posies, inscribed with her age, as here. The arrangement of curving stems, full, rounded roses and a number of smaller flowers looks back to the work of Willem van Aelst. This painting was stolen from the Museum in March 1949 and has not been recovered
persona2_adapted: After 1739, Ruysch focused mainly on small paintings of bouquets and small bunches of flowers, often noting her age on the work, as she does here. The composition—with its gently curving stems, full, rounded roses, and several smaller flowers—echoes the style of an earlier artist, Willem van Aelst. This painting was stolen from the Museum in March 1949 and has never been recovered.

CuratorialText: This panel comes from the predella of a large altar-piece of 1433-35 for S. Niccolò in Cafaggio, Florence, whose parts are now scattered in various collections. The Virgin and Child with Four Angels in the centre was flanked by pairs of saints (Sts Benedict and Nicholas of Bari and St John the Baptist and an Evangelist, probably St Matthew). The predella showed scenes from the life of St Nicholas, of which five are known: the birth of the saint; pilgrims at the shrine of the saint; St Nicholas resuscitating three youths; the saint providing dowries for three sisters (three gold balls, his traditional attribute); and banishing the storm. The panels probably originally had quatrefoil frames. Bicci based the design of WA1850.26 on Gentile da Fabriano's Quaratesi altar-piece of 1425 for S. Niccolò oltr'Arno.
persona2_adapted: This panel was originally part of the lower section, called the predella, of a large altarpiece created between 1433 and 1435 for the church of San Niccolò in Cafaggio, Florence. Today, the different parts of the altarpiece are scattered across various museum collections. The central panel showed the Virgin and Child with Four Angels, and on either side were pairs of saints: Benedict and Nicholas of Bari, along with John the Baptist and an Evangelist (likely Matthew). The predella illustrated scenes from the life of Saint Nicholas, and five of these scenes are still known today: his birth; pilgrims visiting his shrine; the saint bringing three youths back to life; his providing dowries for three sisters (symbolized by three gold balls, his traditional emblem); and his calming a storm. The panels were probably originally set in frames shaped like four-leaf clovers, known as quatrefoils. The artist, Bicci, based the design of this panel on an earlier altarpiece by Gentile da Fabriano, created in 1425 for another Florentine church, San Niccolò oltr'Arno.

CuratorialText: This is clearly the portrait of a professional sculptor, shown in his studio with some of his work on the shelf behind. He holds a terracotta figure of a saint, perhaps the Virgin Mary. The painting has been tentatively attributed to the circle of the de Critz family.
persona2_adapted: This is clearly the portrait of a professional sculptor, shown in his studio with some of his work on the shelf behind him. He holds a small clay figure of a saint, possibly the Virgin Mary. Experts have tentatively attributed the painting to the circle of the de Critz family—meaning it was likely created by an artist working in their style or close to their workshop.

CuratorialText: The pages of calligraphy, all by one hand, mostly include kanshi (Chinese poems), and are signed with variations of the name Sessō Keigi. Two of the poems bear dates of Meiji 10 (1878) and Meiji 12 (1880), and a few identify the source from which the poem came or where the poem was composed. If the calligraphy was done at the same time as the paintings, this album would be an early work by Sobun before he began participating in the national and international expositions.
persona2_adapted: The calligraphy pages in this album were all written by the same person. Most of them feature *kanshi*—a type of Chinese-style poem that was popular among educated people in Japan. The pages are signed with different versions of the name Sessō Keigi. Two of the poems include dates: 1878 and 1880. A few others mention where the poem came from or where it was written. If the calligraphy was created around the same time as the paintings in the album, then this work would be an early piece by Sobun, made before he started showing his art in major national and international exhibitions.

CuratorialText: The genesis of this painting began in 1857, when it was exhibited as The Mother's Grave. The original composition, known from a drawing in the Ashmolean, showed the boy desolate over the grave of his mother. The landscape was begun in the summer of 1856 in the old churchyard at Chingford, Essex. Around 1862, Hughes altered the background and added the figure of the sister, for which the artist's wife, Tryphena, posed. The detail is used to reinforce the pathos of the subject, so that the ephemeral nature of spider's webs, dew drops, dog roses and dandelion seeds all emphasise the theme of transience. The boy's loss is retold in the lamb separated from its mother by the barrier of the tomb.
persona2_adapted: This painting first came into being in 1857, when it was shown under the title *The Mother’s Grave*. An early version of the scene, known from a drawing now held at the Ashmolean Museum, showed a young boy grieving alone at his mother’s grave. The landscape background was begun in the summer of 1856, painted in an old churchyard at Chingford, Essex. Around 1862, the artist, Arthur Hughes, reworked the background and added the figure of the sister, for whom his wife, Tryphena, posed. The carefully painted details heighten the emotional weight of the scene. Elements like spiderwebs, dew drops, wild roses, and drifting dandelion seeds all reinforce the theme of life’s fleeting nature. The boy’s loss is echoed in the image of a lamb separated from its mother by the stone barrier of the tomb.

CuratorialText: John Tradescant the Younger (1608-1662) succeeded his father as Keeper of the King's Gardens at Oatlands Palace and further enriched the family collection of specimens of natural and man-made curiosity. He is shown here with his friend, Roger Friend, a brewer (for which Zythepsa is the Greek) from Lambeth, who may have contributed the shells to Tradescant's museum.
persona2_adapted: John Tradescant the Younger (1608-1662) took over his father's role as the head of the King's Gardens at Oatlands Palace. He continued to expand the family's collection of natural and man-made curiosities—objects that were rare, unusual, or fascinating. In this portrait, he is shown with his friend Roger Friend, a brewer from Lambeth. (The Greek word for brewing, *Zythepsa*, appears in the painting.) It is possible that Friend contributed the shells displayed in Tradescant's museum.

CuratorialText: As one of the leading members of post-war generation of St Ives artists, Lanyon was primarily interested in the representation of landscape. This work epitomises the tension between abstraction and figuration in an otherwise carefully descriptive work.
persona2_adapted: As a key figure among the artists working in St Ives after the Second World War, Lanyon was mainly concerned with how to depict the landscape. This piece captures the push-and-pull between abstract form and recognizable imagery, even though the work is otherwise carefully detailed and descriptive.

CuratorialText: Bundi, Rajasthan, c.1760-70. Gouache with gold on paper. Attended by a drowsy maid, a young noble couple sleep in close embrace on a palace terrace, sheltered by a shamiana or awning. The young prince’s sword and arrows lie close to hand.
persona2_adapted: Bundi, Rajasthan, around 1760-70. This painting, made with opaque watercolor and gold on paper, shows a young noble couple sleeping in a close embrace on a palace terrace. A drowsy maid attends to them, while they are sheltered by a decorative canopy or awning. The prince’s sword and arrows lie nearby, ready at hand.

CuratorialText: This painting was described as A Nun Playing a Piano when it appeared at Christie’s in 1919 and when it was sold by Colnaghi’s to Mrs Weldon in 1936, no doubt because the sitter is wearing a long veil. However, there is no reason otherwise to suppose the sitter is a nun. It is almost certainly a portrait of Desirée-Marie Ribot, known as Louise, the artist’s daughter whom he often used as a model. Born in 1857, her youthful appearance supports the evidence of style which suggests a date in the late 1860s or early 1870s before Ribot’s touch became thicker and more painterly. The dark background and domestic theme recall the work of François Bonvin (1817–1887), an early supporter of Ribot and of Alphonse Legros (1837–1911), who shared Bonvin’s interest in Spanish art.
persona2_adapted: This painting was once called *A Nun Playing a Piano* when it appeared at auction in 1919 and again when it was sold to a collector in 1936. The title likely came from the long veil the woman is wearing. However, there is no other reason to think she is a nun. In fact, this is almost certainly a portrait of the artist’s daughter, Desirée-Marie Ribot, known as Louise, whom he often used as a model. She was born in 1857, and her youthful appearance—combined with clues in the painting’s style—suggests it was made in the late 1860s or early 1870s. This was before the artist’s brushwork became thicker and more visibly textured. The dark background and the intimate, domestic scene recall the work of François Bonvin (1817–1887), an early supporter of Ribot, and of Alphonse Legros (1837–1911), who shared Bonvin’s interest in Spanish art.

CuratorialText: The inscription on this painting reads ‘Wind from the west is blowing through layers of curtains on doors, the proud [chrysanthemum] are falling but their beauty remains. In fading moonlight I [stayed] with these flowers while they cooled, versing through this cool evening in autumn.’
persona2_adapted: The inscription on this painting reads: 'A wind from the west blows through the layered curtains on the doors. The proud chrysanthemums are falling, yet their beauty endures. In the fading moonlight, I stayed with these flowers as they cooled, composing verses through this cool autumn evening.'

CuratorialText: Sinnott is well known for his complex figurative compositions. He often deals with the human figure in a context. Although the same fluid brush-strokes can be seen in the background and in the figures, the two wrestling men can be distinguished by a lighter touch and a slight variation in the use of paint. The underpainting, in places, appears through the upper layers, accentuating the sense of speed in the painting process.
persona2_adapted: Sinnott is well known for his complex paintings of the human form. In this work, the same flowing brushwork appears in both the background and the figures, but the two wrestling men stand out because of a lighter, more delicate handling and a subtle difference in how the paint is applied. In some areas, the initial layer of paint shows through the top layers, which adds to the feeling that the painting was made quickly and energetically.

CuratorialText: The only securely documented painting by the artist is an altar-piece of 1447 now in the Museo Civico, Padua. A follower of Michele Giambono, Francesco may have collaborated with Antonio Vivarini in Venice. Together with WA1850.10, this panel was once part of an unknown altar-piece possibly for a church in Padua or Venice. St Catherine, of royal birth, wears a crown and carries her martyr's palm; the wheel refers to earlier attempts at her martyrdom.
persona2_adapted: The only painting that can be confidently attributed to this artist is an altar-piece from 1447, now housed in the Museo Civico in Padua. Francesco was a follower of Michele Giambono, meaning he worked in a similar style, and he may have collaborated with Antonio Vivarini in Venice. Along with another panel (WA1850.10), this piece was once part of an unknown altar-piece, possibly created for a church in Padua or Venice. Saint Catherine, who was of royal birth, is shown wearing a crown and holding a martyr's palm—a symbol of her faith and sacrifice. The wheel beside her refers to earlier attempts to kill her, a common detail in stories of her life.

CuratorialText: The grief-stricken figures of the Virgin and St John the Evangelist make this small panel a powerful devotional image; it may have been the right wing of a portable diptych, although no hinge-marks are now visible. The anonymous artist was very close to Jacopo di Cione (to whom WA1921.3 has also been attributed) and would have trained in the large workshop of Orcagna, the leading artist in mid 14th-century Florence
persona2_adapted: The sorrowful figures of the Virgin Mary and St John the Evangelist make this small panel a deeply moving image meant for personal prayer. It may have been the right half of a portable two-panel painting, though no hinge marks are visible today. The unknown artist was closely connected to Jacopo di Cione (another work in the collection has also been credited to him) and would have trained in the large workshop of Orcagna, the leading painter in Florence during the mid-1300s.

CuratorialText: Veronese's celebrated Last Supper of 1573 for the refectory of SS Giovanni e Paolo, Venice, caused him to be examined by the Inquisition on the grounds of blasphemy; he changed the title to The Feast in the House of Levi, a subject which allowed for much colourful detail (Luke 6: 27-30). Numerous copies are known of this large painting, now in the Accademia, Venice. WA1855.159 is a reduced copy of fine quality by a mid-18th-century Venetian artist, probably Francesco Fontebasso.
persona2_adapted: Veronese's famous painting *The Last Supper*, completed in 1573 for the dining hall of a Venetian monastery, led to his being questioned by the Inquisition—the Church's court for investigating heresy—on charges of blasphemy. To avoid controversy, he changed the painting's title to *The Feast in the House of Levi*, a biblical story that permitted the kind of lively, colorful details he had included (based on Luke 6:27–30). Many copies of this large work, now housed in the Accademia gallery in Venice, exist. WA1855.159 is a smaller, high-quality version painted in the mid-1700s by a Venetian artist, most likely Francesco Fontebasso.

CuratorialText: A companion to Opie's portrait of the sitter's husband, Francis Smith (1764-1815), WA1934.422. Both paintings were exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1800.
persona2_adapted: This portrait is a companion piece to Opie’s painting of the sitter’s husband, Francis Smith (1764–1815), also in the museum’s collection (WA1934.422). Both works were shown together at the Royal Academy exhibition in 1800.

CuratorialText: Painted in 1886 at a time when both Camille and Lucien Pissarro were working with the pointillist technique. The view, taken from a bend on the path leading from the Pré du Chêne across the Epte from Éragny to Bazincourt, includes the spire of Saint-Martin in Éragny in the centre with the sixteenth-century brick manor house on the right. The Epte runs along the line of trees that crosses the composition. In 1887 Lucien painted an almost identical view in watercolour and bodycolour. The buildings seen from this stretch of the path are now largely concealed by the trees. However, it is clear that in both works Lucien has greatly enlarged the size of the church and manor and suppressed the farm buildings that lie in front of the manor. His father, as Lucien must have known, had painted the same view from the same spot in 1884, using the more improvised style of the early 1880s.
persona2_adapted:
Painted in 1886, this work dates from a period when both Camille Pissarro and his son Lucien were using the pointillist technique—a method of painting with small, distinct dots of color that blend together in the viewer's eye. The scene shows a view from a bend in the path leading from the Pré du Chêne, across the Epte River, from the village of Éragny to Bazincourt. In the center of the composition, you can see the spire of the Saint-Martin church in Éragny, with a sixteenth-century brick manor house on the right. The Epte River runs along the line of trees that cuts across the painting.

In 1887, Lucien painted a very similar view using watercolor and bodycolor (a type of opaque watercolor paint). The buildings visible from this part of the path are now mostly hidden by trees. However, it is clear that in both works, Lucien made the church and manor much larger than they actually were, while leaving out the farm buildings that stand in front of the manor. His father, as Lucien must have known, had painted the same view from the same spot in 1884, using a looser, more spontaneous style typical of his earlier work.

CuratorialText: The painting shows a distant view of the village of Lauris in the Vaucluse, with its distinctive eighteenth-century château and garden terrace on the left and the mountains of the Petit Luberon in the background. Guigou was seated or standing on one of the many sandbanks interspersed with the channels of the river Durance as it passes between Lauris and La Roque-d’Anthéron during the dry season. As in most of the artist’s Provençal landscapes, the air is clear and the sunlight strong. The example of Corot or Daubigny may have had some influence on the choice of the panoramic view that Guigou seems to have favoured above all others, but his manner of painting is distinctive. The sky, sand and river are thinly brushed and enlivened with spots of impasto and small, short strokes with the tip of the brush in the artist’s characteristic manner.
persona2_adapted:
The painting offers a distant view of the village of Lauris in the Vaucluse region of France. On the left, you can see its distinctive eighteenth-century castle and garden terrace, with the mountains of the Petit Luberon rising in the background. The artist, Guigou, was either seated or standing on one of the many sandbanks that appear among the channels of the river Durance during the dry season, as it flows between Lauris and La Roque-d’Anthéron.

Like most of Guigou’s landscapes of Provence, the air is clear and the sunlight is strong. It’s possible that the work of artists like Corot or Daubigny influenced his choice of a wide, sweeping view—a type of composition Guigou seems to have preferred above others. But his way of painting is unique. The sky, sand, and river are painted with thin layers of color, brought to life with small, raised dabs of paint and short, delicate strokes made with the tip of the brush—a technique that is characteristic of his style.

CuratorialText: This painting, which is a cosmic landscape with Daoist undertones, was produced in 1988 for Khoan Sullivan (1919-2003) who wished for something with a predominantly blue colour. The inscription reads ‘To Mr. and Mrs. Sullivan. Affectionately, Chu Teh-Chun 1988.’
persona2_adapted: This painting is a landscape that evokes a sense of the universe, with subtle references to Daoist philosophy. It was created in 1988 for Khoan Sullivan (1919-2003), who requested a work featuring mostly blue tones. The inscription on the piece reads: “To Mr. and Mrs. Sullivan. Affectionately, Chu Teh-Chun 1988.”

CuratorialText: One of Lucien's most extrovert works, painted in June 1925 and illustrating the broom in full flower at Le Brusq.
persona2_adapted: One of Lucien's most expressive and outwardly energetic works, painted in June 1925, shows the broom plant in full bloom at Le Brusq.

CuratorialText: Joseph Warton (1722-1800) was educated at Oriel College, Oxford, before entering the church. He was Headmaster of Winchester College between 1766 and 1793 and an accomplished poet. This portrait was painted and exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1777, the year in which Warton was also elected to 'The Club'. He was the brother of Thomas Warton, the Poet Laureate, whose portrait Reynolds also painted (Trinity College, Oxford; Mannings 2000, no. 1838).
persona2_adapted: Joseph Warton (1722-1800) studied at Oriel College, Oxford, before becoming a clergyman. He served as Headmaster of Winchester College from 1766 to 1793 and was also a skilled poet. This portrait was painted and shown at the Royal Academy in 1777, the same year Warton was elected to 'The Club'—a prestigious social and intellectual group founded by Samuel Johnson. He was the brother of Thomas Warton, who held the title of Poet Laureate (the official poet of the British monarchy). Reynolds also painted Thomas's portrait, which now belongs to Trinity College, Oxford (Mannings 2000, no. 1838).

CuratorialText: The influence of Vuillard is strongly marked. There is a less finished version of the same composition on the back
persona2_adapted: Vuillard’s influence is clearly visible here. On the reverse side of the canvas, there is a rougher, less detailed version of the same scene.

CuratorialText: Bollongier was one of the few painters of the first half of the 17th century in Haarlem who specialised in painting flowers. His work seems to have been influenced by the Bosschaert school but with an added naturalism in the arrangement of the flowers. The glass container in this painting was a favourite accessory. It appears first in a work of 1627 and for the last time in a flower piece of 1639 (Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam).
persona2_adapted: Bollongier was one of the few painters in Haarlem during the early 1600s who focused on flower painting. His work appears to have been influenced by the Bosschaert school—a group of artists known for their detailed flower still lifes—but he brought a more naturalistic feel to how he arranged the flowers. The glass vase shown here was a favorite object of his. It first appears in a painting from 1627 and appears for the last time in a flower piece from 1639, which is now in the Rijksmuseum in Amsterdam.

CuratorialText: When Pharaoh ordered all new-born Hebrew sons to be killed, Moses's mother put him in an ark of bulrushes in the Nile. His sister stayed to watch while Pharaoh's daughter came to the river with her maidens and found the child (Exodus 2: 2-4). WA1950.169 is striking for its emotional intensity, with the focus on Moses's grief-stricken parents. The joyful scene of the finding of Moses was a popular one, whereas this subject is far less common (Poussin earlier treated it c. 1627). Painted in 1654 for Jacques Stella, WA1950.169 was greatly admired by contemporaries for the artist's learned treatment of the subject, his skill in painting reflections and the beauty of the landscape. Pharoah's city is based on Pirro Ligorio's reconstruction of some of the most famous buildings of ancient Rome. A sphinx accompanied by a river-god and cornucopia identifies the Nile. Different explanations, based on Poussin's antiquarian interests and wide-ranging visual references, have been proposed for the significance of the pipes of Pan that hang from a tree behind this group. Two painted copies are recorded in the 17th century; other copies and variations are known (including a tapestry version of the right side) and three engraved versions were made, the first by Stella's niece, Claudine Bouzonnet Stella, in 1672.
persona2_adapted: When Pharaoh ordered the killing of all newborn Hebrew boys, Moses’s mother placed him in a basket made of reeds and set it on the Nile. His sister stayed to watch as Pharaoh’s daughter came to the river with her attendants and found the child (Exodus 2:2–4). This painting is striking for its emotional intensity, focusing on Moses’s grieving parents. While joyful scenes of Moses being discovered were common, this subject is far rarer (the artist had treated it earlier, around 1627). Painted in 1654 for Jacques Stella, the work was greatly admired in its time for the artist’s thoughtful approach to the story, his skill in painting reflections, and the beauty of the landscape. Pharaoh’s city is based on an architect’s reconstruction of some of the most famous buildings of ancient Rome. A sphinx, along with a river-god and a horn of plenty, identifies the Nile. Different explanations—rooted in the artist’s interest in ancient history and his wide-ranging visual references—have been proposed for the meaning of the Pan pipes hanging from a tree behind this group. Two painted copies were made in the 1600s; other copies and variations are known (including a tapestry version of the right side), and three engraved versions were produced, the first by Stella’s niece, Claudine Bouzonnet Stella, in 1672.

CuratorialText: The brother of Masaccio, Lo Scheggia was an eclectic artist who painted numerous works for domestic settings. This type of decorative, ceremonial subject is often found on cassone panels. Although the picture is much abraded, it has sufficient affinities with the mature work of Lo Scheggia to make the attribution convincing.
persona2_adapted: Lo Scheggia, the brother of the more famous artist Masaccio, was a versatile painter who created many works intended for private homes. This kind of decorative, ceremonial scene is commonly seen on the front panels of cassone—elaborately painted wedding chests that were popular in Renaissance households. Although the painting is heavily worn and damaged over time, it still shows enough similarity to Lo Scheggia’s later, fully developed style for art historians to be confident that he was its creator.

CuratorialText: Lemon studied in Rome and in Paris with Carolus-Duran, but spent his early years in California. He exhibited at the Royal Academy from 1880 and came into contact with Giovanni Costa and his circle of Etruscans in Italy. This slight sketch from nature probably dates from the last years of the 19th century.
persona2_adapted: Lemon trained in Rome and in Paris under the artist Carolus-Duran, but he spent his early years in California. He began showing his work at the Royal Academy in 1880, and in Italy he met Giovanni Costa and the group of artists around him, known as the Etruscans. This small, informal study made directly from nature was likely created in the final years of the 19th century.

CuratorialText: The subject, the flat and dry handling and the monumental quality of the figures show Shannon's debt to Puvis de Chavannes. The painting has been dated to c.1910 and is a variation on one of the artist's favourite themes.
persona2_adapted: The subject matter, the flat and dry painting technique, and the statuesque quality of the figures reveal Shannon's influence from Puvis de Chavannes. The work is dated to around 1910 and is a variation on one of the artist's recurring themes.

CuratorialText: Views in and around the village of Berck in northern France appear in many of the artist's paintings from 1870 until his death in 1898. This view across the dunes is precisely dated. The unbroken horizon and solitary figure in the foreground emphasize the windswept emptiness of the site.
persona2_adapted: The village of Berck, located on the northern coast of France, appears repeatedly in this artist’s paintings from 1870 until his death in 1898. This particular scene, looking across the sand dunes, is marked with a specific date. The uninterrupted horizon and the single figure in the foreground draw attention to the open, windswept emptiness of the landscape.

CuratorialText: Traditionally but wrongly said to be a portrait of Antonio Stradivari, A863a is a portrait of a musician or instrument-maker of the late 16th century and identifications of Claudio Monteverdi (1567-1643) and Gasparo da Salo (1540-1609) have been proposed.
persona2_adapted: This portrait, often mistakenly thought to show the famous violin maker Antonio Stradivari, actually depicts a musician or instrument-maker from the late 1500s. Experts have suggested it might be Claudio Monteverdi, a composer from that era, or Gasparo da Salo, a string instrument maker.

CuratorialText: During his later years, Calvert produced many small works in thin oil colours on paper, the restrained colours and muted harmonies evocative of a dream world. They are generally peopled with figures from Greek mythology. Painted on a pink ground.
persona2_adapted: In his later years, Calvert created many small works on paper using thin oil paints. The colors are subdued, and the gentle tonal blends suggest a dreamlike atmosphere. These pieces typically feature figures from Greek mythology and are painted over a pink base layer.

CuratorialText: Traditionally attributed to Guido Reni as the Head of a Sibyl, WA1926.9 was recently identified as a copy after Cerrini. The same expressive head appears in his altar-piece of The Holy Family with Sts Catherine of Alexandria and Agnes painted for San Carlo alle Quattro Fontane, Rome (consecrated in 1646) and now in the convent there.
persona2_adapted: Recently attributed to the artist Giovanni Domenico Cerrini rather than Guido Reni, this painting—once thought to be Reni’s *Head of a Sibyl*—is now recognized as a copy after Cerrini. The same expressive face appears in one of Cerrini’s altar paintings, *The Holy Family with Sts Catherine of Alexandria and Agnes*, which he created for the church of San Carlo alle Quattro Fontane in Rome (consecrated in 1646) and which now hangs in the convent there.

CuratorialText: One of Linnell's most important religious paintings, this was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1835 and sold to the Art Union for the purposes of engraving in 1838. It was based on sketches made in 1829, when Linnell was closely associated with the 'Ancients' at Shoreham. Samuel Palmer was the model for the disciple on the right. The source is Luke 24: 13-35.
persona2_adapted: One of Linnell’s most significant religious works, this painting was shown at the Royal Academy in 1835 and sold to the Art Union in 1838 so that it could be reproduced as an engraving. The composition was based on sketches made in 1829, when Linnell was closely connected with the 'Ancients'—a group of artists who worked together in Shoreham. The disciple on the right was modeled after Samuel Palmer, a fellow artist. The scene draws from a passage in the Gospel of Luke (24: 13–35), which describes a journey to the village of Emmaus.

CuratorialText: Design is an impulse that unifies us across time and culture. This installation unifies two traditionally disparate collecting fields, fashion and textiles with decorative and industrial arts to better understand what underlies our motivations and capacity for design. Ensembles from the Iris Apfel Rare Bird of Fashion collection celebrate the exuberant remixing and inventive styling of one of the world’s most prominent fashion icons, while constellations of unique and culturally significant works of design, fashion, and textiles explore distinctive and resourceful forms of creative expression. A recent gallery highlight is the display of 38 vibrant and flamboyant ensembles from the collection of Boston-based entrepreneur and fashion icon Yolanda Cellucci. Yolanda’s, based in Waltham, Massachusetts, was a hotspot for lavish evening and wedding ensembles and that became a household name throughout the region, and a shorthand for glitz and glamor in the late 20th century. Cellucci generously donated 57 works of fashion and accessories to PEM in 2021, along with archival photographs and materials related to the legacy of her bridal shop.
persona2_adapted: Design is a creative drive that connects people across different times and cultures. This exhibition brings together two areas that are usually kept separate in museums—fashion and textiles on one hand, and decorative and industrial arts on the other—to help us understand what motivates us to design and how we are capable of doing it. Outfits from the Iris Apfel Rare Bird of Fashion collection celebrate the bold mixing of styles and inventive way of dressing from one of the world’s most famous fashion figures. At the same time, groupings of unique and culturally important works of design, fashion, and textiles explore creative expression that is both distinctive and resourceful. A recent highlight in the gallery is the display of 38 vibrant and eye-catching outfits from the collection of Boston-area entrepreneur and fashion icon Yolanda Cellucci. Her store, Yolanda’s, based in Waltham, Massachusetts, was a popular destination for luxurious evening wear and wedding dresses. It became a well-known name throughout the region and a symbol of glamour and excitement in the late 20th century. In 2021, Cellucci generously donated 57 fashion pieces and accessories to the museum, along with archival photographs and materials related to the history of her bridal shop.

CuratorialText: Born in Greenbush, New York in 1844, Lewis became the first sculptor of Black and Indigenous (Mississauga) descent to achieve international recognition. Beginning her career in Boston in 1863, she traveled to Rome in 1866 to join the leading American sculptors of her generation, breaking international, racial and gender barriers. "Sometimes the times were dark and the outlook was lonesome, but where there is a will, there is a way,” Lewis recalled in 1878. “That is what I tell my people whenever I meet them, that they must not be discouraged, but work ahead until the world is bound to respect them for what they have accomplished.” Following her death in 1907, Lewis’ legacy endured in Black communities, yet her contribution to American sculpture has largely been underrecognized. Some of her great masterpieces were rediscovered decades later, while others remain lost today. Her vivid, naturalistic stone sculptures feature abolitionists and social reformers, engage with Indigenous identity and give new life to religious and mythological figures.
persona2_adapted: Born in Greenbush, New York in 1844, Lewis became the first sculptor of Black and Indigenous (Mississauga) descent to achieve international recognition. She began her career in Boston in 1863, then moved to Rome in 1866 to join the leading American sculptors of her generation, breaking through barriers of race, gender, and nationality. "Sometimes the times were dark and the outlook was lonesome, but where there is a will, there is a way," Lewis recalled in 1878. "That is what I tell my people whenever I meet them, that they must not be discouraged, but work ahead until the world is bound to respect them for what they have accomplished." After her death in 1907, Lewis’ legacy continued within Black communities, yet her contribution to American sculpture has largely gone unrecognized. Some of her greatest works were rediscovered decades later, while others remain lost today. Her stone sculptures—vivid and lifelike in style—portray abolitionists and social reformers, explore Indigenous identity, and breathe new life into religious and mythological figures.

CuratorialText: Home to 3.7 million people, 85 species of animals, 32,000 species of insects and 2 billion migratory birds, the boreal forest spans nearly the entire Northern Hemisphere, just below the Arctic Circle, and makes up one of the world’s last remaining stretches of true wilderness. Discover the biodiversity and global importance of our northernmost forests through first-person stories, commissioned objects, interactive experiences and exquisite photography and videography. The boreal forest is critical to the health of our entire planet. It helps regulate Earth’s climate, is the world’s largest land biome, is a critical water reservoir and holds two to three times as much carbon as tropical forests.
persona2_adapted: Home to 3.7 million people, 85 animal species, 32,000 insect species, and 2 billion migratory birds, the boreal forest stretches across nearly the entire Northern Hemisphere, just below the Arctic Circle. It is one of the last truly wild places left on Earth. Through firsthand accounts, specially commissioned artworks, hands-on exhibits, and stunning photography and video, this exhibition explores the biodiversity and global significance of our northernmost forests. The boreal forest is essential to the health of our planet: it helps regulate Earth’s climate, is the world’s largest land-based ecosystem, serves as a critical freshwater reservoir, and stores two to three times more carbon than tropical forests.

CuratorialText: Salem’s connections to the Declaration of Independence center around Ezekiel Russell, a local printer who played a key role in publishing and disseminating the Declaration in 1776. This exhibition coincides with the 250th anniversary of the American Revolution as well as the 400th anniversary of the European settlement of Salem. Works on view in the intimate James Duncan Phillips Trust Gallery include two of the earliest broadside editions of the Declaration of Independence alongside Revolutionary-era manuscripts, newspapers, pamphlets and broadsides that showcase the nation’s founding values of democracy: freedom, liberty and equality.
persona2_adapted: Salem’s role in the story of the Declaration of Independence is closely tied to Ezekiel Russell, a local printer who helped publish and spread the Declaration in 1776. This exhibition marks two major anniversaries: 250 years since the American Revolution and 400 years since European settlers first arrived in Salem. In the intimate James Duncan Phillips Trust Gallery, visitors can see two of the earliest printed versions of the Declaration—known as broadsides, or single-sheet posters—alongside handwritten documents, newspapers, pamphlets, and other printed materials from the Revolutionary period. Together, these items highlight the nation’s founding ideals of democracy: freedom, liberty, and equality.

CuratorialText: These films honor the lesser-known stories of Salem and offer a behind-the-scenes look at what makes this city so remarkable. Produced over the course of more than seven years by Perry Hallinan, Joe Cultrera and many other filmmakers for presentation at the Salem Film Fest, these short films span topics from breakfast at Red’s to the annual Black Picnic at Salem Willows. For longtime Salem residents, these stories might spark nostalgia; for new residents and tourists, they illuminate Salem’s vibrant communities, bygone businesses and traditions that local filmmakers have long worked to record and preserve. Beyond the Broom showcases the vibrant community of Salem, one with an identity that extends beyond its witch trials history. This exhibition is part of the 2026 celebration of Salem 400+, which commemorates the quadricentennial of the city’s settlement while honoring the preexisting Indigenous community. The initiative aims to engage the Salem community and ensure a vibrant and sustainable city for generations to come.
persona2_adapted: These films celebrate the lesser-known stories of Salem, offering a behind-the-scenes look at what makes this city so remarkable. Created over more than seven years by Perry Hallinan, Joe Cultrera, and many other filmmakers for the Salem Film Fest, these short works explore topics ranging from breakfast at Red’s to the annual Black Picnic at Salem Willows. For longtime residents, these stories may stir feelings of nostalgia; for newcomers and tourists, they reveal Salem’s lively communities, vanished businesses, and traditions that local filmmakers have long worked to document and preserve. *Beyond the Broom* showcases the vibrant community of Salem—a city with an identity that goes beyond its famous witch trials history. This exhibition is part of the 2026 celebration of Salem 400+, which marks the 400th anniversary of the city’s founding while also honoring the Indigenous community that lived here long before. The initiative aims to engage the Salem community and help ensure a vibrant, sustainable city for generations to come.

CuratorialText: Explore several hundred fascinating objects inside the historic hall, representing a sampling of the many collections that were displayed in the museum during its earliest years. This installation gives voice to early collectors, trading partners and museum visitors about these objects — with some accounts extracted from records in the museum’s vast archive — and shares perspectives from a range of people living today. These captivating stories and works that reflect local and global history, as well as the impact of maritime trade, recenter East India Marine Hall as the heart of the museum. From its inception, this space and the objects in it have elicited empathy, curiosity and wonder and transported visitors to places around the world. This project has been made possible in part by a grant from the National Endowment for the Humanities: Democracy demands wisdom. Major support is provided by Mr. Ulf B. Meibaum III, Jean Verbridge, and Connie and Stan Grayson.

CuratorialText: Renowned for its expansive holdings of decorative arts and paintings, PEM’s American art collection spans four centuries of creative expression in the United States. Rooted in our historic holdings that celebrate the rich and diverse artistic and cultural heritage of Essex County, Massachusetts, the collection has continued to grow to embrace a broader representation of the art of this country. The artists represented in this Seamans gallery use a variety of materials — wood, metal, glass, clay, paint, fiber and more — to convey their creative ambitions. Many of the artistic practices and decorative inspirations behind these works have long histories, with their origins traceable to Europe, Asia and the Americas. These examples of formal perfection, domestic utility and luxury, and innovative twists on cultural traditions tell a larger story of our global connectedness. Together, these works tell compelling stories of American life and the ongoing cultural exchanges between the people of the region, the country and the wider world. Located in the Seamans Gallery.
persona2_adapted: PEM’s American art collection is known for its wide range of decorative objects and paintings, covering four centuries of creative work in the United States. The collection began with historic pieces that celebrate the rich and varied artistic and cultural heritage of Essex County, Massachusetts, and has since expanded to include a broader view of American art. The artists featured in this Seamans gallery work with many different materials—wood, metal, glass, clay, paint, fiber, and more—to express their creative goals. Many of the techniques and design ideas behind these pieces have long histories, with roots in Europe, Asia, and the Americas. These objects, whether they show technical skill, serve everyday or luxury purposes, or offer fresh takes on cultural traditions, reveal a larger story of how we are all connected across the globe. Together, they tell compelling stories about American life and the ongoing exchange of ideas between people in this region, the country, and the wider world. Located in the Seamans Gallery.

CuratorialText: Persian and Turkish architecture, textiles and miniature paintings inspire the precise, stylized floral forms that compose Anila Quayyum Agha’s sculptural chamber of light and shadow. This luminous installation located in the Wheatland Family gallery provides an opportunity to contemplate the differences and commonalities that shape our lives and relationships. Originally from Lahore, Pakistan, and now living in the US, Agha is acutely attuned to the social codes that inform the lives of Muslim women and all immigrants. She describes this work as her effort to create a sense of how women can reclaim and safely open up private space to welcome others. Located in the Wheatland Family Gallery.
persona2_adapted: Persian and Turkish architecture, textiles, and miniature paintings inspire the carefully arranged floral shapes that make up Anila Quayyum Agha’s sculptural room of light and shadow. This glowing installation, located in the Wheatland Family Gallery, invites visitors to reflect on the differences and shared experiences that shape our lives and relationships. Originally from Lahore, Pakistan, and now living in the United States, Agha is deeply aware of the unwritten social rules that affect the lives of Muslim women and all immigrants. She describes this work as her attempt to show how women can reclaim and safely open up a private space to welcome others.

CuratorialText:
Merchants across the globe went to great lengths to acquire these spectacular commodities. Now known as Asian export art, these objects connected societies and created a complex global economy that continues to shape our world to this day.

PEM’s Asian Export Art collection, foremost in the world, explores cross-cultural exchange as a catalyst for creativity and celebrates the interplay of commerce and creative expression. The gallery features more than 200 works of art made in diverse media by artists in China, Japan, and South Asia. These transcultural objects demonstrate the beauty and ingenuity that can be created through blending artistic traditions, materials, and technologies. Porcelain, textiles, tea, ivory, and silver were the focus of intensive trade activity between Asia and the rest of the world. This installation in the Sean M. Healey Family gallery also addresses the uncomfortable truth that many of these works of art were originally purchased with profits derived from the illegal opium trade. During the 1800s, millions of Indian and Chinese lives were devastated by opium, a foreshadowing of today’s opioid crisis.
persona2_adapted:
Merchants around the world went to extraordinary lengths to obtain these remarkable goods. Today we call them Asian export art—objects made in Asia specifically for foreign markets. These items connected distant societies and helped create a complex global economy that still influences our world.

PEM's Asian Export Art collection, the finest of its kind anywhere, looks at how cultural exchange sparks creativity and celebrates the relationship between commerce and artistic expression. The gallery displays over 200 works created in many different materials by artists from China, Japan, and South Asia. These objects, which blend artistic traditions from multiple cultures, show the beauty and inventiveness that can result when different styles, materials, and techniques come together. Porcelain, textiles, tea, ivory, and silver were the main goods traded intensively between Asia and the rest of the world. This exhibition in the Sean M. Healey Family Gallery also confronts a difficult reality: many of these artworks were originally bought with money earned from the illegal opium trade. During the 1800s, opium devastated millions of lives in India and China, foreshadowing the opioid crisis we face today.

CuratorialText: This exhibition explores America as a place where disparate viewpoints are brought together: America as both longed for and greatly revered. Previously on view in the context of PEM's 2019 Jacob Lawrence: The American Struggle exhibition, Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal presents a solo exhibition of Collins’ art as an immersive experience. Explore 100 different versions of the popular patriotic anthem “My Country ‘Tis of Thee,” rewritten between the 18th and 20th centuries in support of various American causes. Versions of the lyrics are bound together chronologically in Collins’ artist book, where she burns away the musical notes with a laser — chronicling an idea of America through the history of one song’s varied verses. A range of these powerful voices and expressions are heard throughout the gallery in a sound piece, what Collins calls, “a familiar chaos of dissenting versions of what it means to be … American bound together.” The gallery is wallpapered with a newly commissioned work of flocked flower silhouettes. Each flower contains a meaning as the artist uses floriography, the symbolic language of flowers, to relay messages of hope, longing and loss to further illustrate the evolution and complexity of American experiences. In addition to the artwork on view, a study center offers deeper dives into Collins' artistic approaches and historical sources. Through interactives, PEM collection objects and media components, Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal inspires us to activate our own civic mindset. Located in the Jurrien Timmer Gallery.
persona2_adapted: This exhibition examines America as a place where conflicting perspectives come together: a nation both deeply desired and greatly honored. Previously shown as part of PEM's 2019 exhibition *Jacob Lawrence: The American Struggle*, *Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal* now presents Collins’ work as an immersive, standalone experience. Here, you can explore 100 different versions of the patriotic song “My Country ‘Tis of Thee,” rewritten between the 1700s and 1900s to support various American causes. Collins has bound these lyrics together in chronological order in an artist’s book—a handmade volume where she uses a laser to burn away the musical notes. This process creates a visual record of how one song’s changing words have shaped an idea of America over time. Throughout the gallery, a sound piece plays a range of these powerful voices and expressions. Collins describes it as “a familiar chaos of dissenting versions of what it means to be … American bound together.” The walls are covered with a newly commissioned wallpaper featuring silhouettes of flowers. Each flower carries a specific meaning, as the artist draws on floriography—the symbolic language of flowers—to convey messages of hope, longing, and loss. This adds another layer to the story of America’s evolving and complex experiences. In addition to the artwork, a study center offers deeper exploration of Collins’ creative methods and historical sources. Through hands-on activities, objects from PEM’s collection, and media components, *Bethany Collins, America: A Hymnal* encourages us to think about our own role as citizens. This exhibition is located in the Jurrien Timmer Gallery.

CuratorialText: Encompassing everyday objects and the fine arts, the museum’s Japanese collection is distinguished for its range, from paintings and sculpture to decorative arts and costumes and textiles. Spanning Hokkaido in the North to Okinawa in the South, and prehistory to the present, these objects illuminate the varied artistic and cultural traditions of Japan. Toward the end of the 19th century, Edward Sylvester Morse, director of the Peabody Academy of Science (a precursor to PEM), became one of the first Western scholars to visit modern Japan after it opened to travelers. Morse was highly influential in encouraging American interest in Japanese art and culture, and helped create this remarkable Japanese collection.
persona2_adapted: The museum’s Japanese collection brings together everyday objects and fine art, and stands out for its wide variety—from paintings and sculptures to decorative arts, costumes, and textiles. Spanning from Hokkaido in the north to Okinawa in the south, and from prehistoric times to the present day, these works reveal the rich artistic and cultural traditions of Japan. In the late 1800s, Edward Sylvester Morse, director of the Peabody Academy of Science (a predecessor of PEM), became one of the first Western scholars to visit modern Japan after it opened to foreign travelers. Morse played a key role in sparking American interest in Japanese art and culture, and helped build this remarkable collection.

CuratorialText: From the late Joseon dynasty (1392–1910) to today, PEM’s exceptional collection explores art, culture, and life in Korea during a time of significant transition and global connection. PEM’s early Korean collection was primarily formed by Edward Sylvester Morse, director of the Peabody Academy of Science (a predecessor organization to PEM), who proactively pursued Korean acquisitions during his tenure from 1880 to 1914. Among his most significant acquisitions from this period is a set of eight Korean musical instruments played at the 1893 World’s Columbian Exposition. PEM’s connection with Korea was also strengthened by Yu Kil-Chun (1856-1914), the reformist scholar and politician who came to the U.S. as a member of the first Korean delegation and lived near Salem for nearly two years. Yu Kil-Chun and other early travelers between the U.S. and Korea donated many meaningful and foundational works to the museum’s collection. PEM’s Yu Kil-Chun Gallery of Korean Art and Culture brings to life the compelling stories of these early pioneers, presented alongside the museum’s renowned Korean textile collection, exquisite 19th-century paintings — including the Welcoming Banquet of the Governor of Pyeongan screen — and inspiring contemporary works by Nam June Paik (1932–2006) and other Korean and Korean American artists.
persona2_adapted: From the late Joseon dynasty (1392–1910) to the present day, PEM’s outstanding collection explores the art, culture, and daily life of Korea during a period of major change and growing global connections. The museum’s early Korean collection was largely built by Edward Sylvester Morse, director of the Peabody Academy of Science (an organization that came before PEM), who actively sought out Korean objects during his time at the museum from 1880 to 1914. Among his most important finds from this era is a set of eight Korean musical instruments that were played at the 1893 World’s Columbian Exposition in Chicago. PEM’s ties to Korea were also deepened by Yu Kil-Chun (1856–1914), a reform-minded scholar and politician who came to the United States as part of the first official Korean delegation and lived near Salem for nearly two years. Yu Kil-Chun and other early travelers between the U.S. and Korea donated many meaningful and foundational pieces to the museum’s collection. PEM’s Yu Kil-Chun Gallery of Korean Art and Culture brings to life the compelling stories of these early pioneers, displayed alongside the museum’s renowned collection of Korean textiles, exquisite 19th-century paintings—including a large folding screen titled *Welcoming Banquet of the Governor of Pyeongan*—and inspiring contemporary works by Nam June Paik (1932–2006) and other Korean and Korean American artists.

CuratorialText: PEM’s Maritime art collection, which is the finest in the country, frames the sea as an enduring source of opportunity as well as peril, a force that inspires creativity and innovation, and encourages engagement with the wider world. This installation located in the Byrne Family gallery offers a global perspective on our creative response to the sea, from a 17th-century brass Pakistani astrolabe to Valerie Hagerty’s monumental Shipwrecked Armoire with Barnacles (2012) and artworks that reflect Salem’s rich history of maritime trade and exploration. Immersive digital media amplify compelling stories that lay behind unassuming objects, like a calendar stick from 1803 whose carved notches record the long days that Rhode Island native, James Drown, spent shipwrecked and left for dead on Tristan da Cunha, a remote speck island in the South Atlantic.
persona2_adapted: PEM’s Maritime art collection, the finest of its kind in the country, presents the sea as both a lasting source of opportunity and danger—a force that sparks creativity and innovation, and encourages people to connect with the wider world. This exhibition in the Byrne Family gallery takes a global look at how we have creatively responded to the sea, featuring works that range from a 17th-century brass astrolabe (a device used for navigation) from Pakistan, to Valerie Hagerty’s large-scale *Shipwrecked Armoire with Barnacles* (2012), along with artworks that reflect Salem’s rich history of maritime trade and exploration. Interactive digital displays bring to life the hidden stories behind seemingly ordinary objects, such as a calendar stick from 1803. Its carved notches record the long days that James Drown, a native of Rhode Island, spent shipwrecked and left for dead on Tristan da Cunha, a remote, tiny island in the South Atlantic.

CuratorialText: Bringing two extraordinary collections of Native American and American art together for the first time in our institution's history, this long-term installation celebrates artistic achievements across time, space, and worldviews. Located in the Nancy and George Putnam Gallery and Barbara Weld Putnam Gallery, the 250 artworks on display span in time from 10,000 years ago to the present, and demonstrate a range of voices, modes of expression, cultures, and media including: sculpture, paintings, textiles and costumes, furniture, decorative arts, works on paper, installations, video, and a re-envisioned period room. The gallery also includes original documents and objects from the Salem witch trials, one of the most iconic dramas in American history. Between May 1692 and March 1693, the court sentenced 25 innocent people in the Salem region to death. The new installation will be responsive to these urgent times by giving our public an opportunity to grapple with the links, continuities, and disjunctions of our complex histories in America in order to shape a more connected and empathetic present and future. By rethinking America’s histories, visitors are asked to consider how we might envision a better future together.
persona2_adapted: Bringing together two extraordinary collections—one of Native American art and one of American art—for the first time in our institution’s history, this long-term installation celebrates artistic achievements across time, place, and ways of seeing the world. Located in the Nancy and Barbara Weld Putnam Galleries, the 250 artworks on display span from 10,000 years ago to the present day. They represent a wide range of voices, forms of expression, cultures, and materials, including: sculpture, paintings, textiles and clothing, furniture, decorative objects, works on paper, video, and a reimagined period room (a room designed to evoke a specific historical era). The gallery also includes original documents and objects from the Salem witch trials, one of the most dramatic and well-known events in American history. Between May 1692 and March 1693, the court sentenced 25 innocent people in the Salem area to death. This new installation responds to our current moment by giving visitors a chance to reflect on the connections, continuities, and breaks in America’s complex histories—with the goal of shaping a more connected and empathetic present and future. By rethinking America’s past, visitors are invited to consider how we might imagine a better future together.

CuratorialText: These figures, from disparate cultures around the world and across time, embody power as both a fundamental social dynamic and a part of our innate wiring to respond to figures and faces. Curved niches encourage visitors to engage with each artwork, decluttering the field of view and focusing attention. Poetically concise labels heighten the emotional tenor of the experience. Located in the Louise Du Pont Crowninshield Gallery.
persona2_adapted: These figures, drawn from different cultures around the world and from different historical periods, represent power in two ways: as a basic force that shapes human societies, and as something rooted in our natural instinct to react to human forms and faces. Curved alcoves invite visitors to focus on each artwork individually, clearing the visual space and drawing attention to one piece at a time. Short, evocative descriptions intensify the emotional impact of the experience. This display is located in the Louise Du Pont Crowninshield Gallery.

CuratorialText: The exhibition starts at “A is for Always Indigenous” to acknowledge the Native communities who have lived for millennia on the land where the museum now sits. It ends with Z is for Zoology and coincides with the return to the galleries of a leatherback turtle specimen captured in 1885, a favorite of longtime visitors. Salem is a city with many stories of local, national and international significance. Alexander Graham Bell completed the first successful long-distance telephone call from Salem in 1877. Parker Brothers produced Monopoly here. And in 2013, President Obama signed legislation recognizing the city as the birthplace of the United States National Guard. Using selections from PEM’s vast collection, Salem Stories features more than 100 works, including paintings, sculpture, textiles, decorative arts, photographs, natural history specimens, manuscripts, posters, books, eyewitness accounts, and even a murder weapon. The A–Z structure creates an accessible and entertaining way to engage with Salem’s history, from past to present day. This is an evolving story, one that will continue to unfold throughout the life of the exhibition. Located in the Peggy and Lloyd Hilborn Gallery.

This is an evolving story—one that will continue to unfold over the course of the exhibition. Located in the Peggy and Lloyd Hilborn Gallery.

CuratorialText: Bridging myth with social and political history, the Chester and Davida Herwitz Gallery tells the story of nation-building and self-discovery through works by India's most celebrated artistic geniuses of the 20th century. Following independence from British rule in 1947, artists in India aimed to uncover a visual language that was uniquely Indian in inspiration to convey their experiences, struggles, ambitions and dreams. The Prashant H. Fadia Foundation and Deshpande Foundation Gallery features a selection of objects from PEM's extensive collection of historical material from India. Focused primarily on the 19th century, the gallery considers India's long and complex history of foreign occupation, and its troubling impact on the representation of Indian people in art. Featuring some of the earliest objects to come to PEM, including unfired clay sculpture, mica paintings and Kalighat paintings, the gallery considers and questions the timeless tropes of India that persist even today.

The Prashant H. Fadia Foundation and Deshpande Foundation Gallery features a selection of objects from PEM's extensive collection of historical material from India. Focusing mainly on the 19th century, this gallery examines India's long and complex history of foreign occupation and its troubling impact on how Indian people were depicted in art. Featuring some of the earliest objects to come to PEM—including unfired clay sculptures, paintings on mica (a thin, shiny mineral), and Kalighat paintings (a style of watercolor painting on paper that originated near a temple in Calcutta)—the gallery considers and questions the lasting stereotypes of India that persist even today.

CuratorialText: This diverse selection of contemporary glass represents the work of more than 40 of the most influential glass artists from the 20th century to today. Donated to PEM in 2022 by New York-based collectors Carl and Betty Pforzheimer, this collection is remarkable for its range of forms, scale and techniques in glass. The Pforzheimers’ gift significantly expands PEM’s noted historic glass collection while celebrating the extraordinary aesthetic possibilities that arise from the combination of sand and fire.

CuratorialText: The Pod is part of PEM's Dotty Brown Art & Nature Center, which investigates our place in nature through contemporary art, memorable objects and interactive experiences. Designed for visitors of all ages, the family-friendly center features installations, changing exhibitions, hands-on displays and art-making spaces. Grow your awareness of nature, try on new ways of being, and tap into your own creativity. Discover a life-size wire tree by sculptor Elizabeth Keithline modeled on a live tree from PEM’s campus. Watch dancer Laura Kathrein move as different animals and embody local habitats. And create your own imaginary creature inspired by real animals and fantastical beasts from around the globe. What creative sense will be sparked in you on your visit to The Pod? What comes to mind when you think of a pod? A pea pod, an iPod or maybe a futuristic orb? Jane Winchell, the Sarah Fraser Robbins Director of the Art & Nature Center and curator of The Pod, describes her idea of a pod and what you’ll encounter when you visit our new multisensory exhibition space. There is something for everyone here, ranging from fabulous, nature-inspired contemporary and historic artworks to stations where you can explore nature up close or make your own art. Families with young children will discover the Wonder Zone which includes activity bins, a light table and picture books just for them
