Fifty-eight granite architectural pieces from the 1500s are displayed in the museum gallery, arranged to resemble a temple hall from South India (known as a *mandapam*). In 1913, these pieces were lying discarded at the small Madanagopalaswamy Temple in Madurai, where a Philadelphia couple purchased them during their two-year honeymoon. However, recent research suggests that most of them were originally made for the large Kudal Alagar Temple nearby. There, they had been part of a shrine dedicated to the goddess Lakshmi, the wife of the Hindu god Vishnu. That shrine had fallen into disrepair, and temple authorities took it apart by 1907, completing its replacement by 1923.

Stone slabs placed between the lion-shaped brackets show several scenes from the *Ramayana*, the epic story of one of Vishnu's earthly incarnations. These slabs once formed part of a carved band (a frieze) that depicted the entire tale. The life-sized figures projecting from the pillars are gods and mythological characters, including some from the *Ramayana*. The many small images carved into the pillars include gods, heroes, and even human figures—such as an architect holding his measuring stick.

CuratorialText: Enslaved Black and free white craftsmen worked side by side across the southern landscape.  Over the past thirty years researchers at MESDA have identified nearly 6500 craftsmen who enslaved people.  In fact, this fact is among the most common things we know about an identified craftsman.  Often anonymous, those who were enslaved by craftsmen were often at work on building sites, at the potters’ wheels, in cabinet shops, at iron foundries, behind the counters of silversmiths’ shops, and at dozens of other trades.  Their skills are revealed in the objects that they helped to fashion.  Their identities are often lost to history. A line in a legal document, or a brief mention in an advertisement is often all we know about an enslaved craftsman – but careful reading of period documents by MESDA researchers has identified nearly 3000 enslaved craftsmen by name.  The exhibition “Black and White all Mix’d Together”: The Hidden Legacy of Enslaved Craftsmen looks at icons of southern decorative arts through the lens of research conducted by MESDA over more than thirty years.  It recovers the stories of the many named and nameless individuals whose hidden hands shaped the southern landscape.
persona2_adapted: Enslaved Black and free white craftsmen worked side by side across the southern landscape. Over the past thirty years, researchers at MESDA have identified nearly 6,500 craftsmen who enslaved people. In fact, this is one of the most common facts we know about any identified craftsman. Often unnamed, those enslaved by craftsmen were frequently at work on building sites, at potters’ wheels, in cabinet-making shops, at iron foundries, behind the counters of silversmiths’ shops, and in dozens of other trades. Their skills are revealed in the objects they helped create. Their identities, however, are often lost to history. A line in a legal document, or a brief mention in an advertisement, is often all we know about an enslaved craftsman—but careful reading of historical documents by MESDA researchers has identified nearly 3,000 enslaved craftsmen by name. The exhibition *“Black and White all Mix’d Together”: The Hidden Legacy of Enslaved Craftsmen* examines iconic examples of southern decorative arts through the lens of research MESDA has conducted over more than thirty years. It recovers the stories of the many named and unnamed individuals whose hidden hands shaped the southern landscape.

CuratorialText: North Carolina’s Piedmont region is known around the world for the manufacture of fine furniture.  However, long before the factories of Lexington, Thomasville, Hickory, and High Point came into existence, the Piedmont was home to a diverse group of cabinetmakers who crafted distinctive regional furniture. By the middle of the eighteenth century, immigrants from Pennsylvania, Maryland, and Virginia began pouring into the North Carolina Piedmont in search of inexpensive yet fertile land.  By 1800 about 250,000 Scotch-Irish, English, and German settlers called the region home.  Cabinetmakers immediately utilized the abundant walnut, cherry, pine and poplar trees to produce well-made, stylish furniture for their homes.  In most cases, these craftsmen combined national trends with local idiosyncratic preferences to produce regional furniture with a distinctive style and appearance. This exhibit is drawn from MESDA’s extensive collection of Piedmont North Carolina furniture made between 1780 and 1860.
persona2_adapted: The Piedmont region of North Carolina is famous worldwide for producing high-quality furniture. But long before the factories in towns like Lexington, Thomasville, Hickory, and High Point were built, the area was home to a wide range of skilled furniture makers who created furniture with a distinct local character. In the mid-1700s, immigrants from Pennsylvania, Maryland, and Virginia began moving into the North Carolina Piedmont in large numbers, looking for affordable, fertile land. By 1800, around 250,000 settlers—mostly of Scotch-Irish, English, and German background—had made the region their home. These craftsmen quickly made use of the plentiful walnut, cherry, pine, and poplar trees to build well-crafted, fashionable furniture for their own homes. In most cases, they blended broader national styles with local, individual preferences, resulting in furniture that had a unique look and feel. This exhibit features pieces from MESDA’s extensive collection of Piedmont North Carolina furniture, made between 1780 and 1860.

CuratorialText: Masterfully decorated slipware, sculptural bottles, refined creamware, and faience are but part of the rich artistic legacy of North Carolina’s first earthenware potters. During the last half of the eighteenth century, artisans of European descent introduced a variety of Old World ceramics traditions to the Carolina backcountry. From both an artistic and technical standpoint, the work of these craftsmen often surpassed that of their Middle Atlantic and New England contemporaries. North Carolina potters transformed the simplest of materials into vessels of practical utility, astonishing beauty, and deep cultural significance. This exhibit is the first major survey of this work an the context in which it was created.
persona2_adapted: Masterfully decorated pottery with painted designs, sculptural bottles, refined tableware with a smooth finish, and tin-glazed earthenware are just part of the rich artistic legacy left by North Carolina’s first potters who worked in earthenware—a type of pottery fired at relatively low temperatures. In the last half of the eighteenth century, artisans of European descent brought a variety of Old World ceramic traditions to the Carolina backcountry. From both an artistic and technical perspective, the work of these craftsmen often surpassed that of their counterparts in the Middle Atlantic and New England regions. North Carolina potters transformed the simplest materials into vessels that were practical, strikingly beautiful, and deeply meaningful within their culture. This exhibition is the first major survey of this work and the context in which it was created.

CuratorialText: Salem was the largest of North Carolina’s Moravian communities and from the moment of arrival the earliest settlers documented their landscape with a variety of detailed maps, surveys, drawings, and paintings. Salem was founded in 1766 as a congregation town with the Church overseeing all activities—financial, social, and spiritual. The Church owned all land and leased it to individuals who owned only the improvements. Salem was a bustling trade center with a mill, pottery, tannery, brewery, and store. But the earliest landscapes emphasize the centrality of the Church. They depict the town’s conscious design around a central square, the heart of the community. The square was home to the Gemein Haus (and later Home Church), where religious services were held; the large communal dwellings for Single Brothers and Single Sisters; the schools for young boys and girls; and the store. Gardens and fields appear nestled in and around the most prominent buildings in early views, suggesting the town’s growing success both agriculturally and commercially. As the nineteenth century progressed, the residents lobbied for more personal control. In 1849, the Church agreed to sell land to create the neighboring town of Winston, and in 1856 it abandoned the lease-hold system in favor of private land ownership. Manufacturing enterprises grew, and the railroad’s arrival in 1873 set the stage for tremendous growth in local industries. Salem—once closed to outsiders—became closely intertwined with the town of Winston. The textile and tobacco industries flourished, and by 1900 the twin cities of Winston and Salem had become the largest metropolitan area in North Carolina.
persona2_adapted:
Salem was the largest of North Carolina’s Moravian communities, and from the moment the first settlers arrived, they recorded their surroundings in great detail through maps, surveys, drawings, and paintings. Founded in 1766 as a congregation town, Salem operated under the Church’s control over all aspects of life—financial, social, and spiritual. The Church owned all the land and leased it to individuals, who owned only the buildings and improvements they made on it.

Despite being a busy trade center with a mill, pottery workshop, tannery, brewery, and store, the earliest images of Salem focus on the central role of the Church. These pictures highlight how the town was deliberately arranged around a central square, the heart of community life. The square contained the Gemein Haus (later called Home Church), where religious services were held; the large communal homes for Single Brothers and Single Sisters; the schools for young boys and girls; and the store. In these early views, gardens and fields appear nestled around the most important buildings, suggesting the town’s growing success in both farming and commerce.

As the 1800s progressed, residents pushed for more personal control. In 1849, the Church agreed to sell land to create the neighboring town of Winston, and in 1856 it abandoned the lease-only system in favor of allowing private land ownership. Manufacturing businesses grew, and when the railroad arrived in 1873, it set the stage for a huge expansion of local industries. Salem—once closed to outsiders—became closely linked with the town of Winston. The textile and tobacco industries thrived, and by 1900 the twin cities of Winston and Salem had become the largest metropolitan area in North Carolina.

CuratorialText:
James River Valley pottery has been the subject of archaeological and collector interest for over half a century.  The James River and its tributaries were home to more than a dozen potteries before the Civil War.  Potters hailing from New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Maryland, Ireland and England, created a rich  hybrid tradition with forms and decorations adapted from their regional styles and wrought in local clays dug from Kaolin to Claymount.

Transcending their status as utilitarian objects, these pots, many exhibited here for the first time, provide a remarkable record and a unique glimpse into a significant and long-lived concentration of pottery manufacturing in eastern Virginia.  Viewed collectively, they present a bold decorative aesthetic rivaling long-recognized and celebrated schools of production seen elsewhere in the commonwealth and the Nation.
Today, many of these important historic sites are threatened by development.  Fortunately, archaeologists and decorative arts scholars are working ahead of the bulldozers to document and understand the important legacy of the James River Potteries before they are lost forever.  At the end of 2013 the Chipstone Foundation journal Ceramics in America will publish an extensive article about the James River Valley stoneware potteries based on this ongoing research.
This exhibition was on view at MESDA from November 2012 through March 2012. It was co-curated by Kurt Russ, Oliver Mueller-Heubach, Marshall
persona2_adapted:
James River Valley pottery has fascinated archaeologists and collectors for more than fifty years. Before the Civil War, the James River and its connected waterways were home to over a dozen pottery workshops. Potters who came from New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Maryland, Ireland, and England created a unique blend of traditions. They shaped their pots using forms and decorations drawn from their own regional styles, but made them from local clays—ranging from a fine white clay called kaolin to a darker clay known as Claymount.

These pots are more than just everyday objects. Many are on display here for the first time, and together they offer a remarkable record and a rare look into a long-lasting and important center of pottery production in eastern Virginia. When seen as a group, they reveal a bold decorative style that stands alongside the better-known and celebrated pottery traditions found elsewhere in Virginia and across the country.

Today, many of these historic sites are at risk from development. Fortunately, archaeologists and scholars who study decorative arts are working ahead of the bulldozers to document and understand the important legacy of the James River potteries before it is lost forever. At the end of 2013, the Chipstone Foundation’s journal *Ceramics in America* will publish a detailed article about the James River Valley stoneware potteries, based on this ongoing research.

This exhibition was on view at MESDA from November 2012 through March 2012. It was co-curated by Kurt Russ, Oliver Mueller-Heubach, and Marshall.

CuratorialText:
An Irish schoolmaster. A German Jewish silversmith. An African Muslim scholar. A multi-national female pastelist. A Scottish cabinetmaker. A potter, a carpenter, an engraver. The lives of these individuals share a common thread: they were “lately from” somewhere else and came to the American South in the 18th and early 19th centuries.

This exhibition features objects created by these craftspeople whose origins spanned continents and oceans. As much as possible, it connects those objects with the words of their makers through letters, inscriptions, autobiographies, account books, advertisements, and even drawings. Together, they tell the story of journeys taken, communities forged in new homes, responses to changing political climates, and the creative blending of traditions into something new.
persona2_adapted:
An Irish schoolmaster. A German Jewish silversmith. An African Muslim scholar. A female pastel artist from multiple countries. A Scottish cabinetmaker. A potter, a carpenter, an engraver. What these individuals have in common is that they were "lately from" somewhere else—meaning they had recently arrived—and came to the American South during the 1700s and early 1800s.

This exhibition features objects made by these skilled workers, whose origins spanned continents and oceans. Where possible, it connects those objects with the makers' own words, found in letters, personal writings, financial records, advertisements, and even drawings. Together, these items and writings tell the story of journeys taken, communities built in new homes, responses to shifting political climates, and the creative mixing of traditions to form something new.

CuratorialText: Early Southerners drank a lot — nearly twenty gallons of alcohol each — every year. They drank when they woke up in the morning, as they ate and worked during the day, and when they socialized at night. But how people drank was as important as what they drank. From imported Madeira sipped at mahogany tables to local whisky out of redware jugs, this exhibit explores the decorative arts that defined the early Southern drinker.
persona2_adapted: Early Southerners consumed a great deal of alcohol—nearly twenty gallons per person each year. They drank in the morning upon waking, throughout the day while eating and working, and during evening social gatherings. Yet the manner in which they drank mattered just as much as what they drank. This exhibit examines the objects used for drinking—from imported Madeira wine, carefully poured at polished wooden tables, to locally made whisky served from simple earthenware jugs—and considers how these items helped define the identity of the early Southern drinker.

CuratorialText: this exhibit in MESDA’s Whitehall Gallery brings together rare maps from the 18th and early 19th centuries to explore how maps were made, used, and did work in the early American South. Rivers of Ink, Mountains of Type: Maps at Work in the Early American South includes maps from the MESDA and Old Salem collections as well as major loans from private collections, Salem Congregation, and the Moravian Archives of the Southern Province. The exhibit includes two early surveys of the Wachovia Tract made by Andreas Höger; a rare first-state copy of Joshua Fry and Peter Jefferson’s map of Virginia; two copies of Lewis Evans’s General Map of the Middle British Colonies produced at Ben Franklin’s press in Philadelphia; and surveys by George Washington of lands he owned along the Ohio River.
persona2_adapted: This exhibit in MESDA’s Whitehall Gallery brings together rare maps from the 1700s and early 1800s to explore how maps were made, used, and actively shaped life in the early American South. *Rivers of Ink, Mountains of Type: Maps at Work in the Early American South* includes maps from the MESDA and Old Salem collections, along with major loans from private collections, Salem Congregation, and the Moravian Archives of the Southern Province. Among the highlights are two early surveys of the Wachovia Tract by Andreas Höger; a rare first-state copy—meaning an early printing—of Joshua Fry and Peter Jefferson’s map of Virginia; two copies of Lewis Evans’s *General Map of the Middle British Colonies*, printed at Ben Franklin’s press in Philadelphia; and surveys by George Washington of lands he owned along the Ohio River.

CuratorialText: Alabama’s pottery traditions reflect the distinctive combination of people “thrown together” across its landscape. This exhibition explores the human hands that made, held, and preserved these objects. Across the present-day state of Alabama, Indigenous people including the Muscogee (Creek), Choctaw, Cherokee, and Chickasaw, practiced ceramic traditions brought by their ancestors into the region over 10,000 years ago. Colonial and American settlers were both a threat and a market for these craftspeople, who produced hybrid wares that meshed traditional techniques and designs that appealed to these foreign consumers. The geography of Alabama influenced how and where diverse potters and people came together. A tail of the Appalachian Mountains runs from south to north across the middle of the state. Most of the 19th century potters north of the mountains came from Tennessee, Virginia, and the Ohio River Valley. South of the mountains, the majority arrived from Georgia or South Carolina. The Gulf Coast was the site of early interactions between French, Spanish and British colonists and the Indigenous people of Alabama. Whether they came from the north, east, or across the Atlantic, immigrants brought with them enslaved people of African descent, including skilled ceramists. Together, their hands shaped the clay of the American South.
persona2_adapted:
Alabama’s pottery traditions reflect the unique mix of people who were “thrown together” across its landscape. This exhibition explores the human hands that made, held, and preserved these objects. Across what is now the state of Alabama, Indigenous peoples—including the Muscogee (Creek), Choctaw, Cherokee, and Chickasaw—continued pottery-making practices brought by their ancestors into the region more than 10,000 years ago. Colonial and American settlers were both a threat and a source of business for these craftspeople, who created hybrid wares that combined traditional techniques with designs that appealed to these foreign buyers.

Alabama’s geography shaped how and where different potters and communities came together. A tail of the Appalachian Mountains runs from south to north through the middle of the state. Most 19th-century potters who lived north of these mountains came from Tennessee, Virginia, and the Ohio River Valley. South of the mountains, the majority arrived from Georgia or South Carolina. The Gulf Coast was an early meeting point for French, Spanish, and British colonists and the Indigenous peoples of Alabama. Whether they came from the north, east, or across the Atlantic, immigrants brought with them enslaved people of African descent, including skilled potters. Together, their hands shaped the clay of the American South.

CuratorialText:
The Carolyn and Mike McNamara Gallery at the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts (MESDA) exhibits some of the furniture, ceramics, paintings, prints, metalwork, and textiles from the early American South.  From fine art to folk art, these objects tell the stories of the diverse craftsmen and consumers who called the South home for more than four centuries.

The early South is usually divided into three major cultural regions: the Chesapeake, the Lowcountry, and the Backcountry.  The rivers, roads, and natural resources of these regions defined their economic and settlement patterns.  Each was home to a unique blend of ethnicities and religions: Native Americans, British Anglicans and Quakers, Scotch-Irish Presbyterians, French Huguenots, Moravians, German Lutherans and Reformed, and free and enslaved peoples of African descent.  The dynamic interactions between and among these distinct groups flavored the decorative arts of the early South.
persona2_adapted:
The Carolyn and Mike McNamara Gallery at the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts (MESDA) displays furniture, ceramics, paintings, prints, metalwork, and textiles from the early American South. Ranging from fine art—works created for their beauty or intellectual value—to folk art—everyday objects made by untrained or community-based makers—these items reveal the stories of the diverse craftspeople and customers who lived in the South for over four hundred years.

The early South is typically divided into three major cultural regions: the Chesapeake, the Lowcountry, and the Backcountry. The rivers, roads, and natural resources in each area shaped how people settled and built their economies. Each region also had its own mix of ethnic and religious groups: Native Americans, British Anglicans and Quakers, Scotch-Irish Presbyterians, French Huguenots, Moravians, German Lutherans and Reformed Christians, as well as free and enslaved people of African descent. The lively exchanges and interactions among these distinct communities gave the decorative arts of the early South their distinctive character.

CuratorialText:
The William C. and Susan S. Mariner Southern Ceramics Gallery is home to the most comprehensive collection of antebellum pottery made in Maryland, Virginia, the Carolinas, Georgia, Kentucky, and Tennessee.

Among the decorative arts, pottery is the one media that is most intimately connected to place.  The southern pottery in this gallery reflects the interplay between maker, consumer, culture, and geography.  It is a tangible reflection of how potters responded to specific cultural needs to craft vessels out of local materials that were both useful and beautiful.

This virtual gallery offers two ways to explore the southern ceramics on exhibit in the Mariner Gallery.  The first is through a series of virtual galleries curated by the noted ceramics scholar Robert Hunter, Editor of Ceramics in America that explore important themes—tradition, design, expression, purpose, faith, war, and freedom—as seen through the lens of southern pottery.  You can also browse the objects in the Mariner Gallery by media: earthenware, salt-glazed stoneware, and alkaline glazed stoneware.  Within these pages you are also able to filter the objects by maker, place, and time
persona2_adapted:
The William C. and Susan S. Mariner Southern Ceramics Gallery holds the largest collection of pottery made in the American South before the Civil War—specifically from Maryland, Virginia, the Carolinas, Georgia, Kentucky, and Tennessee.

Among decorative arts—objects made to be both useful and beautiful—pottery is the type most closely tied to a specific place. The southern pottery in this gallery shows how makers, buyers, local culture, and geography all influenced one another. Each piece is a physical record of how potters met particular community needs, shaping local materials into containers that were both practical and pleasing to the eye.

This virtual gallery offers two ways to explore the southern ceramics on display in the Mariner Gallery. The first is through a series of virtual exhibits curated by Robert Hunter, a well-known ceramics scholar and editor of *Ceramics in America*. These exhibits explore important themes—tradition, design, expression, purpose, faith, war, and freedom—as seen through southern pottery. You can also browse the objects in the Mariner Gallery by type of material: earthenware (pottery fired at lower temperatures), salt-glazed stoneware (stoneware with a textured, glass-like coating created by throwing salt into the kiln), and alkaline-glazed stoneware (stoneware with a glossy finish made using ash or other plant-based materials). Within these pages, you can also filter the objects by maker, location, and time period.

CuratorialText: The Thomas A. Gray Rare Book and Manuscript Collection is home to rare and important works about the early American South. The collection represents a lifetime of connoisseurship for Tom Gray, a former director of the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and cousin of MESDA founder Frank L. Horton. What began as an collection focused on Tom’s home state of North Carolina gradually expanded to include important texts from all seven of MESDA’s southern states. With books and manuscripts spanning four centuries – from 1585 to 1865 – the Gray collection is a perfect complement to the MESDA and Old Salem collections.  Books and manuscripts from the collection are exhibited in the MESDA galleries and are available to researchers.  A selection of highlights are shown below.
persona2_adapted: The Thomas A. Gray Rare Book and Manuscript Collection holds rare and historically significant works about the early American South. The collection reflects a lifetime of careful, expert selection by Tom Gray, a former director of the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and a cousin of the museum’s founder, Frank L. Horton. What started as a collection focused on Tom’s home state of North Carolina gradually grew to include important texts from all seven southern states covered by MESDA. With books and manuscripts spanning four centuries—from 1585 to 1865—the Gray collection perfectly complements the holdings of MESDA and Old Salem. Items from the collection are displayed in the MESDA galleries and are also available for researchers to study. A selection of highlights is shown below.

CuratorialText: MESDA’s collection includes architectural woodwork collected from houses in Maryland, Virginia, North Carolina, South Carolina, and Georgia.  This collection was originally assembled by Frank L. Horton to serve as backdrops for period room installations.  Today, in addition to providing scale and context for our study galleries, MESDA’s architectural collections are also studied as important Southern objects in their own right that that can tell stories of style, craftsmanship, migration, ethnicity, and race.
persona2_adapted: MESDA’s collection includes wooden architectural pieces—such as doors, moldings, and paneling—taken from houses in Maryland, Virginia, North Carolina, South Carolina, and Georgia. These items were originally gathered by Frank L. Horton to serve as the settings for rooms arranged to look like historic interiors. Today, beyond giving a sense of size and context to our study galleries, these architectural pieces are also studied as important Southern objects in their own right. They can reveal stories about changing styles, the skill of the craftspeople who made them, the movement of people and ideas, and the roles of ethnicity and race in shaping the region.

CuratorialText: Old Salem Museums & Gardens and its Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts are home to one of country’s most important collections of early Southern longrifles, swords, and related objects.  Longrifles were some of the most complicated objects crafted in the early American South.  Artistic masterworks, they required craftsmen skilled in woodworking, silversmithing, ironworking, and the mechanical arts.  The rich tradition of early Southern rifle making is kept alive today by the talented craftsmen of the Timothy Vogler Gun Shop, located directly across the street from the Frank L. Horton Museum Center at Old Salem Museums & Gardens.
persona2_adapted: Old Salem Museums & Gardens, along with its Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts, holds one of the country’s most significant collections of early Southern longrifles, swords, and related items. Longrifles were among the most complex objects crafted in the early American South. These artistic masterpieces required craftsmen skilled in woodworking, silversmithing, ironworking, and the mechanical arts—essentially combining multiple specialized trades into a single object. The rich tradition of early Southern rifle making continues today through the talented craftsmen at the Timothy Vogler Gun Shop, located directly across the street from the Frank L. Horton Museum Center at Old Salem Museums & Gardens.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts and Old Salem Museums & Gardens is home to an exceptional collection of Southern bedcoverings including quilts and coverlets.  The collection also has a significant number of textiles made and used by the women of Salem, North Carolina.

CuratorialText: German speaking communities throughout the South created richly illuminated documents celebrating births, baptisms, marriages, and deaths.

CuratorialText: For over forty years the Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has collected many of the finest examples of southern-made furniture.  These objects demonstrate how the early South evolved, inform us about the diverse peoples who came here, and illuminate the artisans who were integral to the process. Whether they were English, Scottish, Irish, German, African, or French, Southern cabinetmakers utilized their materials at hand, both native and imported, to create furniture that speaks to us about their heterogeneous backgrounds and the multifaceted aspects of their everyday lives. These craftsmen contributed to the dynamic amalgamation of art, culture, and history that forms the basis for MESDA’s ongoing study of the early South.

Whether they were English, Scottish, Irish, German, African, or French, Southern furniture makers used the materials available to them—both local and brought from elsewhere—to create furniture that reveals their varied cultural backgrounds and the many different aspects of their daily lives. These craftspeople contributed to a rich blend of art, culture, and history that forms the foundation for MESDA’s ongoing research into the early South.

CuratorialText: MESDA’s collection of iron represents the work of furnaces and blacksmiths across all seven southern states.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens is home to one of the most important collections of southern of maps.  On the surface maps appear scientific and authoritative, useful devices for governmental officials and colonial expansionists to claim ownership of land and exert power and authority over competing nationalities.  Below the surface, however, maps often provide tremendous insights into a myriad of social, political, and economic forces that shaped Southern culture.
persona2_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens holds one of the most significant collections of maps from the American South. At first glance, maps seem like neutral, factual tools—useful for government officials and colonial expansionists to claim land and assert power over rival nations. But beneath this surface, maps often reveal a great deal about the social, political, and economic forces that shaped Southern culture.

CuratorialText: Music is an important part of Southern culture.  In Salem, North Carolina, music was intrgal to Moravian worship and daily life.  Salem had its own orchestra by the 1780s and brass bands played regularly as part of religious services and to announce births and deaths in the community.  Old Salem Museums & Gardens collection of musical instruments and related materials reflects the important role played by music in the community.  Other objects in the collection reflect musical craftsmanship elsewhere in the American South.

By the 1780s, Salem had its own orchestra, and brass bands performed regularly, not only during religious services but also to mark important life events like births and deaths. The collection at Old Salem Museums & Gardens includes musical instruments and related items that show how deeply music shaped this community. Other objects in the collection also highlight musical craftsmanship from across the American South, offering a broader view of the region's traditions.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has led the way in researching and collecting work by Southern artisans, allowing us to better understand the rich cultural complexity of artistic production in the South. While it is true that with only a handful of significant urban centers, fewer painters settled and remained in the South, the painting collection at MESDA ably demonstrates the diversity of training and styles with works by European-trained and native-born artists living in urban and rural settings
persona2_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts has been a pioneer in studying and collecting the work of Southern craftspeople, helping us better understand the rich cultural mix that shaped art-making in the South. It is true that, because the region had only a few major cities, fewer painters chose to settle and stay there. However, MESDA’s painting collection clearly shows the variety of artistic backgrounds and styles—featuring works by both European-trained artists and those born in America, living in cities as well as rural areas.

CuratorialText: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens is home to an extensive collection of prints that document the people and places of the early American south.  These views documented English perceptions of America and served as vehicles to convey a host of attitudes and values and provide tremendous insights into a myriad of social, political, and economic forces that shaped Southern culture.
persona2_adapted: The Museum of Early Southern Decorative Arts at Old Salem Museums & Gardens holds a large collection of prints that record the people and places of the early American South. CuratorialText: Pastel portrait of Frances L’Escott. Half-length pastel portrait of young woman with body turned slightly to the right and head slightly to the left. Her eyes are brown, and her hair is dark. In her right hand, she holds a reddish-brown book. She sits beside the base of a column on the right side of the image, while on the left, a red curtain with a tassel, a railing with balusters, and trees in the distance form the background.

CuratorialText: Full length portrait of a man in blue uniform with red trim at lapel and cuffs, white vest and breeches with black boots trimmed with gold at the top, white epaulets and neck piece, and black tircorner hat decorated with silver cord. He is posed with sword in right upraised hand and a dagger-like knife in the left, facing half-left, with a tree at either side to frame a fight between American troops and Indians in the background. The soldiers in the back wear round hats, short blue jackets with red facings, belly boxes, and linen overalls. The retreating Indians to the left rear of the painting appear with topknots or scalp locks, and wear battle dress of breechcloths and leggings.
persona2_adapted: A full-length portrait shows a man in a blue uniform with red trim on the lapel and cuffs. He wears a white vest and breeches, black boots with gold trim at the top, white shoulder ornaments (epaulets) and a neck piece, and a black three-cornered hat decorated with silver cord. He is posed with a sword raised in his right hand and a dagger-like knife in his left, facing slightly to the left. Trees on either side frame a battle scene in the background between American troops and Native Americans. The soldiers in the back wear round hats, short blue jackets with red trim, cartridge boxes worn on the front of the belt, and linen trousers. The retreating Native Americans, shown in the left rear of the painting, have topknots or scalp locks and wear battle dress consisting of loincloths and leggings.

CuratorialText: Charcoal drawing of the profile of Joseph Lewis, Jr., facing left on pink paper. The drawing shows a gentleman with light hair tied at the back with dark ribbon who is wearing a dark high coat with a white stock. The painting is mounted in the original gold frame with black and gold painted glass.
persona2_adapted: This charcoal portrait shows Joseph Lewis, Jr., in profile, facing left on pink paper. The subject is a gentleman with light hair tied back with a dark ribbon, wearing a high-collared dark coat and a white neckcloth. The piece remains in its original gold frame, which features black and gold painted glass.

CuratorialText: Watercolor on paper of a plantation with an inscription at the base of the painting. The house is a white frame two-story central hall structure with its chimneys on the rear elevation. A fence runs in front of the house with a bridge to the right in the background, indicating an inlet of water. An outbuilding stands behind some trees standing behind the bridge. Several large trees are located in the left foreground, along with some moss, grass and other foliage. A path leads to the house, and two men, one mounted upon a horse, the other standing below him are talking to one another. A body of water is in the background.
persona2_adapted: This watercolor painting on paper depicts a plantation estate. At the bottom of the artwork, an inscription has been added. The main house is a white, two-story wooden structure with a central hallway, and its chimneys are located on the back side of the building. A fence runs across the front of the house, and in the background to the right, a bridge suggests the presence of a narrow inlet of water. Behind the bridge, an outbuilding is partially hidden by trees. In the left foreground, several large trees are visible, along with moss, grass, and other plants. A path leads up to the house, where two men are shown talking to one another—one is mounted on a horse, while the other stands beside him. A body of water stretches across the background.

CuratorialText: Charcoal drawing, profile of man, facing right, on faded pink paper. The gentleman wears the uniform of an officer, a dark coat with light facings with buttons at the outside of long buttonholes on the lapel and collar, and epaulets of gold bullion. A black inner collar is over a white stock. The man’s hair is graying and is tied in a knot at the back with a black ribbon.
persona2_adapted: A charcoal drawing on faded pink paper shows a man in profile, facing to the right. He is dressed in an officer’s uniform: a dark coat with lighter trim, where buttons sit beside long buttonholes on the lapel and collar. His shoulders are adorned with gold-braided ornaments known as epaulets. A black inner collar rises above a white neckcloth, or stock. His graying hair is tied back in a knot with a black ribbon.

CuratorialText: Oil on canvas portrait of a gentleman with dark eyes, dark complexion and a shaven head, who is facing half-right with hands folded resting on a table . His right hand holds a quill pen and the left is holding a document with a name written thereon. He wears a white loose collar shirt with deep red robe and turban of the same red material, and sits in a chair upholstered in a red-brown fabric with brass tacks. The background is a dark green drapery to the left and books on the right.
persona2_adapted: Oil on canvas portrait of a gentleman with dark eyes, a dark complexion, and a shaven head. He faces slightly to the right, his hands folded and resting on a table. In his right hand, he holds a quill pen; his left hand rests on a document with a name written on it. He wears a loose white collar shirt, a deep red robe, and a turban made of the same red fabric. He sits in a chair upholstered in a reddish-brown material, with brass tacks visible along the edges. The background features dark green drapery on the left and books on the right.

CuratorialText: Bust portrait, oil on canvas, of woman wearing a pearl necklace and blue dress with lace trim, in mahogany architectural frame with cut-out corners.
persona2_adapted: A painted portrait, created with oil on canvas, shows a woman from the chest up. She wears a pearl necklace and a blue dress trimmed with lace. The painting is set in a dark reddish-brown wooden frame designed with architectural details and cut-out corners.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, of Richard Sprigg of Strawberry Hill, Maryland. Young man facing forward, his body is slightly to the right, his left hand in his vest, wearing a dark beige coat and light silk vest, with white stock. He has a ruddy complexion, dark eyes, brown hair. The background varies from greenish-gray to the lower right to very dark gray in upper left and has lunettes in the corners.
persona2_adapted: This is a painting in oil on canvas of Richard Sprigg, who lived at Strawberry Hill in Maryland. The young man faces forward, with his body turned slightly to the right and his left hand tucked into his vest. He wears a dark beige coat and a light silk vest, with a white neckcloth. His complexion is ruddy, his eyes dark, and his hair brown. The background shifts in color from a greenish-gray in the lower right to a very dark gray in the upper left, and the corners of the canvas are rounded into arched shapes.

CuratorialText: Oil on canvas portrait of a woman facing half-right. The subject has dark hair combed upward to expose her left ear, and pearls in her hair that hang down over her right shoulder. She wears a blue silk cross-bodice dress tied with a dark red sash. The pinned-up sleeves of the garment revealing a light under-garment. She is posed with her left arm upon a table, the right at her hip, and foliage painted in the background.
persona2_adapted: Oil on canvas portrait of a woman turned slightly to the right. Her dark hair is swept upward, revealing her left ear, and pearls woven into her hair cascade down over her right shoulder. She wears a blue silk dress with a crisscross bodice, fastened with a deep red sash. The sleeves are pinned up, exposing a lighter fabric beneath. She rests her left arm on a table, places her right hand on her hip, and foliage fills the background.

CuratorialText: A watercolor “en grisaille” of Williamsville, a home in Hanover County, Virginia. The house was built between 1794 and 1803 for William Pollard, II, the second Clerk of Court of Hanover County. It passed to his son George William Pollard in 1840 and remained in the Pollard family until after the death of Harry Todd Pollard, the amateur artist who drew and signed the watercolor.
persona2_adapted: A watercolor painting in shades of gray, known as a "grisaille," depicts Williamsville, a home in Hanover County, Virginia. The house was built between 1794 and 1803 for William Pollard, II, who served as the second Clerk of Court for the county. The property was inherited by his son, George William Pollard, in 1840 and stayed in the Pollard family until after the death of Harry Todd Pollard. Harry Todd Pollard, an amateur artist, created and signed this watercolor.

CuratorialText: Painting, done in oil on mahogany panel, of a young man facing half-right. The gentleman has dark hair and side-burns, and is dressed in a white shirt, tie and vest, and a black coat supporting a high collar. There is an inscription on the back of the panel.
persona2_adapted: This portrait, painted in oil on a mahogany panel, shows a young man facing slightly to the right. He has dark hair and sideburns, and wears a white shirt, tie, and vest, along with a black coat with a high collar. An inscription is written on the back of the panel.

CuratorialText: Oil on canvas painting of a man facing half-left. The work is a half-length portrait. The subject has gray hair, dark gray eyes, wears a dark brown coat with a high turned-down collar, a yellow vest, and white stock at the neck.
persona2_adapted: A half-length portrait in oil on canvas shows a man turned slightly to the left. He has gray hair and dark gray eyes, and is dressed in a dark brown coat with a high, turned-down collar, a yellow vest, and a white neckcloth.

CuratorialText: This painting depicts a bend in the Cumberland River near the Hermitage, the home of Andrew Jackson (1767-1845). It was a scene that the artist returned to regularly. Another version of this composition is in the collection of the Greenville County Museum of Art.
persona2_adapted: This painting shows a bend in the Cumberland River near the Hermitage, the home of Andrew Jackson (1767–1845). It was a view the artist revisited many times. Another version of this scene belongs to the Greenville County Museum of Art.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, of Charles Town showing the city waterfront as seen from across the Cooper River. A large sailing ship is in the left foreground, the opposite shore in foreground with various figures along the shore.
persona2_adapted: A painting in oil on canvas depicts the waterfront of Charles Town as seen from across the Cooper River. In the left foreground, a large sailing ship is prominent, while the opposite shore appears in the foreground with several figures scattered along the shoreline.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, waist-length portrait of a lady wearing a gray satin dress with gray stomacher in front and white lace at neck and sleeves, her hair is pulled tightly back, a loop of pearls in it on the left side of the portrait. Her right hand holds a pink rose.
persona2_adapted: This is a waist-length portrait of a woman, painted in oil on canvas. She wears a gray satin dress, with a matching decorative panel—called a stomacher—at the front, and white lace trim at her neck and sleeves. Her hair is pulled back tightly, and on the left side of the painting, a loop of pearls is woven into it. In her right hand, she holds a pink rose.

CuratorialText: Painting, oil on canvas, of a man facing half-left and seated in a green painted chair. The man has a high foreheard, dark hair, dark blue eyes, ruddy complexion, and is wearing a dark coat mounted with gray buttons, a white stock, and a yellow vest. A red and pearl pin is on his neck cloth. The painting is signed in the lower left corner.
persona2_adapted: This portrait, painted in oil on canvas, shows a man seated in a green chair, facing slightly to the left. He has a high forehead, dark hair, deep blue eyes, and a reddish complexion. He wears a dark coat with gray buttons, a white neckcloth, and a yellow vest. A small pin, red and pearl-colored, is fastened to the cloth around his neck. The artist’s signature appears in the lower left corner of the painting.
